Elizabeth Bonython and Anthony Burton

This is the first full length biography of Henry Cole, extraordinary larger-than-life 'eminent Victorian', visionary civil servant, and accomplished spin-doctor, whose pioneering ideas created the magnificent museum which is now the V&A.

By the end of his life Henry Cole was recognized as a national institution, 'Old King Cole', though he was also treated with caution as a 'playful monster'. He was a lifelong Civil Servant, in a period when Civil Servants could be buccaneering reformers rather than impassive mandarins. Described as 'a clever agitator, and a public spirited fellow', he waged many campaigns, moving from the Public Records to the Penny Post, to railway expansion, to patent law revision, to art and design reform.

With Prince Albert, he created the Great Exhibition of 185 1, and kept on organising international exhibitions for the rest of his life. He was in charge of a nationwide system of art and science education, and, again with Prince Albert, developed the South Kensington area as the intellectual quarter of London. The Victoria and Albert Museum, of which he was founding Director, remains as his best-known memorial, and is a symbol of his mission to educate through exhibiting.

This book gives a rounded picture of this extraordinary man. He is portrayed not only as exhibitor, museum director and educationalist, but as a galvanic force administering salutary shocks over wide areas of Victorian life: children's books, hackney cabs, public lavatories, choral singing, cookery, sewage disposal, to name only a few. In his own time he aroused admiration and antipathy in equal measure. For today's readers, his energy and creativity will mark him out as an inimitable Victorian phenomenon.

Kingston College Kingston Hall Road Kingston upon Thames KT1 2AQ

# ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS

First must be mentioned the Cole family. During the many years while she worked on Henry Cole's diaries, Elizabeth Bonython was indebted for help to many members of the family, especially the late Mrs Cynthia Dutnall. Readers of this book will soon see how vital to it is the existence of Cole's diary (of which, as of other unpublished Cole MSS, Elizabeth Bonython holds the copyright). The diary is not quotable at length, but the text is studded with phrases from it, and it underpins the whole book. Of living members of the family we are especially indebted to Henry Cole's first cousin many times removed, Geoff Cole and his wife Hazel of Sydney, Australia. Their work on the history of the Cole family before Henry's time was invaluable to us. We are grateful also to Leah Grillard and Judi Sheldon, both descendants of Cole. We have also profited from contacts in the past with the descendants of J. C. Robinson, and are grateful to Helen Davies and Timothy Wilson (Ashmolean Museum, Oxford), who have worked on Robinson recently.

At the V&A many colleagues gave us help, especially John Physick, now retired, and the late Clive Wainwright. This book has been produced under the aegis of the V&A Research Department. We are grateful to Paul Greenhalgh, who, as Head of the department, got it started; to his successor, Carolyn Sargentson; and to Malcolm Baker, whose questioning mind kept the intellectual senses of the department, including ours, on the alert. We are also indebted to Ann Eatwell, Diane Bilbey, Elizabeth James and Michael Snodin; to many colleagues in the Prints and Drawings Department and the National Art Library, who helped us unearth material; and to the Photographic Studio and Picture Library for help with illustrations. Particular thanks go to Mary Butler and Ariane Bankes of V&A Publications for coping with authors who, though not otherwise troublesome (we hope), took longer than they might have in writing.

Outside the museum, Professor Robert Hewison read the first draft and made many useful suggestions. Help came also from David Allan and Susan Bennett (both late of the Royal Society of Arts), Susanna Avery-Quash, James Broadbent, Duncan Bythell, John Cantwell (late of the PRO), Martin Crosfill, Oliver Davies, Kersti Habjoom, James Harrison, Hermione Hobhouse, Joan Jones (of the Minton Museum), Valerie Mars, Sarah Molloy, Michael Slater and Christopher Whitehead.

Lastly we recognise the contribution of our spouses, John Bonython and Carol Bakr Burtn, who put up wit urant nd prpinds.

## INTRODUCTION The Man and the Achievements

Henry Cole  extraordinary civil servant and cultural entrepreneur - struck some people, who felt the rough edge of his personality, as a monster. Others thought him a buffoon. If you had met him in the street, you would have noticed that he was quite a small man. His head, however, was on a large scale  or at least his features were, for he had a large nose, a firm chin, and a lofty brow. His head looked the larger because he wore his curly hair in an untidy shock. In later life it became a flowing white mane, and white hair continued round his face in the form of side-burns and under-the-chin (mandarin-style) whiskers, so that he seemed to look at you out of a white halo. He usually wore glasses, but these are rarely recorded in portraits. At least from the 18 hehabitually dressed in  frock coat. Wth bagy rusers, fap coat-tails, and handkerchief trailing from his back pocket, he must often have looked dishevelled, but he always stood up straight, perhaps to make the most of his height.

We do not know what he sounded like. When one reads the pugnacious phrases of his speeches, one cannot imagine that they were pronounced in a languid drawl. Surely his voice must have been emphatic, even abrupt. If he had had a high tenor range or a window-rattling basso profundo, it is likely that someone would have mentioned it, so probably he had a normal baritone timbre. One notices that big men often swallow their voices, while small men often project theirs strongly; Cole mastered the art of public speaking, so no doubt he spoke clearly.

His predominant qualities of personality were energy, force, dynamism; and it seems likely that these would have expressed themselves physically in a brisk, vigorous demeanour. There is no evidence of a disjunction between his mental and physical attributes, quite the contrary. His hand obeyed his mind. His penmanship was, at the start, his fortune, and he always enjoyed drawing and, later, etching. We can imagine him as physically efficient, practical, bustling.

If, as a downright man with an appropriately downright name, he got results primarily by determination, it was inevitable that he would run into opposition and clashes of will. In ruling his own staff he was regarded by some as an 'autocrat',1 a 'dictator', a 'despot'.² But he was not the kind of boss who oppressed his subordinates while crawling to his superiors. He gave trouble to superiors too, and was regarded as an 'insolent proconsul', doing what he liked in his own territory, to the chagrin of civil servants in Whitehall.3 His most explosive disputes were with his subordinate Charles Robinson, curator of the art museum at South Kensington, and with his superior, the government minister Robert Lowe: if it was necessary to fight somebody, Cole did not care whether he was senior or junior, but struck out just the same. Undoubtedly Cole was a rough, tough man to work with. One of his admirers,

Sir Henry Trueman Wood, said that 'he liked his own way, and he generally got it, though his methods were not such as endeared him either to the superiors whose orders he evaded or to the subordinates whose submission he compelled'.4

But his sheer determination could sometimes overcome its own adverse effects. The statesman Lord Granville, one of his staunchest allies, recalled in 1873 that when he first met Cole, he 'did not like him at all', but he confessed that 'by very force of character, the gentleman who had impressed him so unfavourably won his firm and lasting regard'. Granville characterised Cole as 'a kind of Palmerston without the varnish'.

If Cole had been an altogether impossible colleague, he could never have achieved so much. It has to be acknowledged that he sustained many long-term friendships and professional relationships. Most of the people with whom he worked at the Society of Arts and at the Great Exhibition, such as Charles Wentworth Dilke, Matthew Digby Wyatt, Lyon Playfair and Lord Granville, were still his allies at the end of his life. And in his department were staff who worked with him throughout his 2o-year tenure, such as Richard Redgrave, Norman MacLeod, Philip Cunliffe Owen, Richard Thompson and George Wallis, none of them fools or stooges.

It was said of Cole that 'he had many really great and many extremely disagreeable qualities'.6 What made working with him worthwhile was probably the sheer exhilaration of the bumpy ride. In the first place, he was unstoppable. It was said of the South Kensington Museum, the apple of his eye, that it 'has been brought to its present magnificent proportions in the teeth of such virulent opposition and persistent ridicule as would have discouraged and disgusted most men, and against which no ordinary man could have successfully worked.'7 Cole, however, shrugged off attacks; he was 'armed in triple brass  . . invulnerable to the quill or to the acids of satire'.8 Before him others quailed. 'Timid natures gave way before the impetuosity of a knight whose sword had no scabbard, and who left himself no retreat.'9

He did not, however, always proceed by sheer force, for he had 'a dogged inventive genius which knew how to turn difficulties into stepping stones to success, and to wear out stolid opposition by vivacious pertinacity'10 'He was constantly misjudged', commented Playfair, 'because his modes of work were not always on the surface. If he came to an obstacle, it was his delight to tunnel under it in secret, and tively, might be deprecated as deviousness  and one recent critic quotes Playfair to jy  deption  Cole s urtive shemg tculd e rearem positively as ingenuity and resourcefulness. His mischief, when applied to a good cause (as we shall see when examining his feats as a propagandist for the Penny Post), commands admiration, though tinged, perhaps, with a delighted misgiving.

T  v was undoubtedly an abrasive debater:

Like certain animals of the more formidable order in heraldry, his tongue is armed with asg; andhe s ot he man to estem it herwise than s a cpliment to e  hi it. Even in his most playful moods - and his bitterest enemies will admit that he is an amirable perorme the playful monstr e sting in fun, and ther is a keee to every word he utters.13

The monster did not often let his guard slip, but there is one little weakness that he islaye He ay have been qui pevius to rdicle',  but he was ot vious to compliments. In his diary, which is predominantly a curt summary of events, he noted down tributes that he received, presumably because he took pleasure in them. For instance, Lord Granville told him that, when he started to work on the Great Exhibition, he was regarded with suspicion by his colleagues, but once the exhibition was successfully open, suspicion was 'entirely removed' and he 'had earned great praise'. Richard Cobden, an exhibition commissioner, remarked to Cole: I always say the Exhibition is the work of you & the Prince. I put you first.' Once established in his own department, Cole attracted admiring comment for his ability to get things done. The influential John Shaw-Lefevre 'said I was a perfect Salamander in difficulties' Milner Gibson 'thought I was the most energetic man in England'. His minister in 185 3, Edward Cardwell, described him as 'a necromancer'. Sir John Shelley, a member of a select committee examining Cole, 'called me an Artful Dodger" but was 'converted'. At the Fine Arts Club, the publisher John Murray said that, in a crisis, 'we must send for you as the most powerful man who can do anything & is afraid of no responsibility'. The notoriously combative Acton Smee Ayrton respected Cole because 'I always slept with one eye open'. Cobden, again, said 'I was a general who always won!' Edward Hamilton, of the Society of Arts, remarked that 'next to Ld Palmerston I was considered to have the greatest resources at command of any one'. G. E. Hamilton, the Treasury civil servant, told Cole: 'You never take "No" & you make friends of people'. Sir William Gregory, MP, who had an inside view of one of Cole's great disputes, 'said I handled men better than any one'. The artist, Sir Francis Grant, 'complimented me as always succeeding', while Lord Ripon 'said the betting was always 3 to I on Cole'. 15

He was especially renowned for what he managed to get out of the politicians. Sir Joseph Paxton 'said I was always pulling the wires of Government'. Edmund Potter MP said 'I was too clever, & the only man the Ho[use] of Co[mmons] c[oul]d not keep in order'. William Rathbone MP 'told me I set Parliament at defiance and always had my own way'. His own minister in 1866, the duke of Buckingham, observed his persistence in tabling his proposals for further discussion if they were not at first approved, saying that I was a remarkable man, for I did not dispute with him, & took a snubbing, but he had to look sharp at the end of a fortnight to see that the thing was not up again'. Once when Cole had made some financial concessions, one of his ministers 'observed the Treasury will say that they have drawn y[ou]r tooth'. Cole answered 'But I can put in another', and: "Yes," s[aid] L[orld de Grey, "Your reproductive powers are inexhaustible".' W. E. Forster, Cole's minister in the early 187os, told Princess Louise that 'I was the person to accomplish anything against Rules successfully'.16

Sometimes people used a compliment to soften a reproof. On Cole's appointment to the Department of Practical Art in 18 5 2, Sir Charles Trevelyan congratulated him on his 'appointment to direct the Art of the Country' but 'recommended soft answers & "humility"". Lord Granville, in a cunningly balanced assessment, 'thought me a Genius & my fault was Buttings against people, but judgement sound'. Cole's political masters quite often had to urge him that 'we had better go on quietly'.17

However thick-skinned Cole was, it is clear that secretly he craved approval, and not only from his superiors. He was obviously gratified when he was told that his departmental architect, Francis Fowke, had said 'that whenever things went well I let every one have the credit & when they went ill I took all the blame, & it was when any one was in difficulties that it was best seen what kind of man I was'. He noted also that Fowke's successor Henry Scott said: 'I have always found [Cole] the best man I have ever served under'. 18

Cole undoubtedly was a playful monster - irrepressible, outrageous, rampant. Does the fact that he recorded the favourable opinions of others about him reveal only stark conceit? These notes certainly reveal his satisfaction, even his glee, at the recognition of his powers. But he also reveals in his writings, sayings and doings that he could be shrewd and objective, and generous to others. 'In private life', it was said, he was 'genial, indulgent, cordial, faithful, and possessing a happy gift of mimicry and an abounding sense of fun.'19 We aim to present in this book a larger-than-life character: strenuous, incorrigible, sometimes preposterous, often funny, occasionally harsh, always invigorating.

## \*   \* \*

Cole's biography is not worth writing, however, just because he was a colourful character. His achievements were formidable. Here they are in summary. For 2o years he was head of a department of the civil service, the Department of Science and Art, which administered artistic and technical education, at secondary level and beyond, in Britain. Cole started the department from almost nothing, and presided over the establishment, through the length and breadth of the country, of a chain of art colleges (science caught up later, under his successors), many of which survive today as part of our university and further education systems. The department also introduced art classes into elementary education. The part of the department's work with the highest public profile was its museum in London, which eventually grew and split to become the Victoria and Albert Museum and the Science Museum. It had satellite museums in London, Edinburgh and Dublin, fostered exhibitions and museums throughout the country, and had potent influence abroad. The central museum was on a site in South Kensington, which Albert, the Prince Consort, set up as a cultural quarter in London. After Prince Albert's death, Cole was the principal force behind the development of this site, and was directly influential in the creation of the Royal Horticultural Society's gardens, which have now disappeared, and the splendidly surviving Royal Albert Hall, Albert Memorial, Royal College of Science (now Imperial College) and Royal College of Music.

Cole came to his post as 'Plenipotentiary' of South Kensington as a result of his succss in the Great Exhibition of 185 1. Though this owed an incalculable amount te     b i C h other individual who supplied the executive drive that brought the project to fruition despite many diffulties. Thereafter, Cole repeatedly threw his weight behind furher international exhibitions, in England in 1862 and 18714, and in Paris in 1855 and 1867. The business of exhibiting things, whether in museums or in trade fairs, could be said to be his preferred means of communication and most favoured activity.

Cole emerged as the dynamo of the Great Exhibition because he was a leading activist in the body which first suggested it, the Society for the Encouragement of Arts, Manufactures and Commerce. This private pressure group remained a power base for Cole throughout his life. He first became involved with it in the 184os when, as a small-scale entrepreneur under the characteristically jaunty trade name 'Felix Summerly', he embarked upon the improvement of design in the consumer goods market, producing cheap guides to art galleries and cultural sites, elegant and unbossy children's books (the 'Home Treasury'), and a series of domestic products ('Art Manufactures') such as a tea service, a christening mug, a bread board. He had always been interested in art and antiquities, but this was the point at which he applied himself professionally in this field.

His earlier life had been a journey of discovery in which his extraordinary talents as agitator and propagandist gradually emerged. He left school with few skills except penmanship. This got him a job copying records in the Public Record Office. By a happy chance he secured what must have been a rather better intellectual training than a conventional university education would have supplied, when he became part of the group of self-educating young men, the 'philosophic radicals', around John Stuart Mill. Here Cole imbibed utilitarian attitudes and made useful contacts, which, when he lost his job in 183 5, enabled him to mount a forcible campaign against the Public Record Office. This not only got him his job back, but also resulted in the thorough reform of the administration of the public records. After this Cole was known as an effective radical operator, and his talents were soon enlisted in the creation of the penny post, in the development of docks and railways, and in the reform of the patent laws. From here he moved on to his experiments in industrial design, and then to his career as an art administrator.

Since Cole's rise to a position of national significance was chiefly due to his personal energy and talent, he might seem to belong to that Victorian category, the selfmade man. This persona does not quite fit Cole. For his family belonged to the middle class, with members in the army and the professions, though they were going down rather than up. And Cole was educated at a reputable school, though by charity. Furthermore, like many socially rising men, he benefited from noble patronage. The favour of the Prince Consort was of fundamental importance in his life. Perhaps even more important (partly because it smoothed his path to the prince) was the more accessible friendliness of Lord Granville. A little younger than Cole, he was for long the Liberal leader in the Lords (and only just missed being prime minister): his urbanity, charm, shrewdness, diplomacy and absolutely secure social status offset some of Cole's more rebarbative qualities. While Cole never ceased to be an irritant to his society, he contrived to move from the fringes to the very centre of it.

## \* \* \*

On the strength of his achievements, both official and mutinous, Cole earned a position of eminence in Victorian Britain; indeed, he straddled an international stage. We are well aware now that eminent Victorians can be seen in various lights by their biographers. Many who led lives that attracted admiration at the time enhanced their reputations by writing self-applauding memoirs, and many were accorded the tribute of an 'official' biography that highlighted their best qualities and suppressed anything undignifed or discreditable. Henry Cole had a biography of this kind, depressingly entitled Fifty Years of Public Work and published in two volumes in 1884. Cole planned this and wrote half of it, but after his death in 1882 it was completed by his children Alan and Henrietta. It is thus part memoir and part official biography.

Victorian biography was never the same after Lytton Strachey's Eminent Victorians (19 18), in which the undignified and discreditable sides of several revered Victorian public figures were gleefully exposed. Henry Cole might have stood up to this treatment quite well. He could be as dignified, authoritarian and severe as any of his eminent contemporaries: it was said of him that 'he possessed . . . that greatest of all powers - the power of subduing other wills to his own; and this, in the opinion of many who knew him best, was the great secret of his success.'20 On the other hand, an aura of mischief and humour emanated from most of his activities, not unintentionally, but because he was, as well as a worthy, also a bit of a bounder, and enjoyed it. When he retired, it was prophesied that the comic papers 'will probably go into mourning'2' at losing one of their favourite butts. He could laugh at himself, and if Lytton Strachey had laughed at him he would probably have joined in. He was a man of both force and fun.

The tradition of the massively detailed biography still persists where eminent Victorians are concerned, for such figures usually left behind a great deal of material. Since Strachey, however, writers try to portray both the refulgent and the darker sides of their subjects. In recent years, there has been a tendency to view eminent Victorians not just as heroic, or clay-footed, individualists, but also as agents within larger political, social or cultural institutions or movements. Just as history is written nowadays not as a narrative of great men, battles and speeches, but as an analysis of forces, or an examination of institutions, or a study of representations, so biography has altered its emphasis. Henry Cole would be an apt subject for this new kind of appraisal, for almost all that he did was done in and through great Victorian institutins.2

He created and manipulated institutions, so that they exercised social control, and embodied, endorsed and promoted certain values. He might be described as an agent in the 'feld of cultural production', and a study of his 2o-year period of official life might well be entitled Henry Cole and the Exploitation of Victorian Cultural Capital'. But his work can be seen as more than an exercise of power. Since he was a 'great exhibitor', he might also be regarded as a practitioner and exponent of the art of representation.

However, to see Cole only as a surrogate for the institutions in which he was involved would be to lose something. In the last quarter of his life he concerned himself, alongside his official avocations, with rural crafts, London cabs, hymn singing, army reorm,drill in schools, music teaching, cookery and sewage disposal. It isre that these activities could be interpreted as attempts on his part to reconfigure various aspects of the established order, but they were primarily expressions of enthusiasm. It is reductive to see Cole only in ideological and institutional terms, for personality is always breaking through.Similarly, Cole' part in the Great Exhibition has several dimensions. There have been many studies of the Great Exhibition as an imperial or comercial metaphor, and Cole was as assiduous as anyone at the time in constructing such significances. But the exhibition was also for him the scene of a frantic personal scramble for success.

In this book, therefore, we have aimed to show more of Cole than was visible in the ofal FiyYears not l tey clpal e  hi h a Strachey would have relished. We have tried to illuminate the cultural fields in whic heperate butwe av foundthatismultiarius activicany bheld together by regarding him primarily as a personality. If we had tried to locate him precisely in each of the fields in which he acted as a cultural entrepreneur, we would have overrun the limits of a single volume. Cole's achievements in each such field could probably support a book-length analysis, and it may be worthwhile to contemplate for a moment the extent of an imaginable library of Henry Cole studies.

First, Cole's contribution to art education is a well-documented aspect of his work. Any survey of art education has to include him,23 and many accounts of English education in general signal the role played by his department in the tortuous formation of the English educational system.24 A number of dissertations have investigated his department's work more thoroughly.25 In setting up the department's educational system, Cole left curricular matters mainly to his associate Richard Redgrave, while himself concentrating on administration ('his will be the educational, mine the business point of view').26 So he may to some extent be able to sidestep the criticism that has been directed upon the department (even in its early days), that the education it offered - the highly disciplined learning of skills - was rigid and boring. Whether Cole's fault or not, however, the department's methods are quite out of tune with art education now, in which creative intuition and personal expressiveness are pre-eminently valued. So Cole does not earn much praise today for his propagation of art education, to which recent commentators have applied the adjectives 'militaristic' and 'maniacal'.27

The results of the work of the schools of art have been disparaged no less than the methods. The schools were intended to boost trade by improving the artistic design of the objects that were traded. It is by no means obvious that the latter will lead to the former (it might hinder as much as help), and it is almost impossible to make any measurable assessment of whether trade was boosted or design improved by the schools. The efforts of Cole and his allies have been dismissed as 'fully-fledged capitalist utopianism', an attempt 'to imagine and to produce a kind of field of force that will hold the anarchistic processes of production and consumption in an ideal order'.28 This may have seemed a mistaken enterprise in the 198os, when that comment was written, and when Marxist solutions to economic problems carried conviction; today, the Marxist solutions have receded, but the problems remain, so perhaps Cole's capitalist utopianism might seem relevant again. All the same, his achievement lies more in getting the schools set up than in their results or in their philosophy and methods of education.

Cole's memory is more cherished in the museum world. He created the V&A, which must be regarded, simply by virtue of its size and splendour, as one of the greatest museums in the world. Accordingly he is hailed in the professional literature as a 'museum master'.29 In this field, he was indubitably progressive, prescient of modern attitudes in his customer-oriented policies; and his amiable views stand up well in the face of much modern thinking on museums, which sees them as sites for social regulation and their curators as engrossed in the modalities of power. One recent critic proposes that 'when Henry Cole praises the museum for its educative potential, it is worth noting what he regards as its chief lesson. "It would teach the young child", he writes, "to respect property and behave gently." Cole did say this, but it was advanced as one among a string of good things that a museum might achieve, in a letter in which Cole was trying to persuade a provincial mayor to set up a museum. The words 'chief lesson' are supplied by the critic, 30 during a discussion that draws a good deal on Foucault. When propagandising, Cole cheerfully used sound bites' such as this, but, overall, his record as a museum professional is not easily discredited.

Cole's work might be seen in various contexts in English history in the Victorian period. He was, as we have noticed, among John Stuart Mill's group of philosophic radicals in the late 182os and early 18 30s, and was therefore more or less present at the birth of utilitarianism, the political system based on the ideas of Jeremy Bentham. Bentham was a philosopher and theorist who deliberately kept at a distance from practical politics, but his ideas were transformed into legislation through the efforts of a number of reformers who can be regarded as his disciples. That Bentham exercised a powerful influence was always accepted by twentieth-century historians, this view being put decisively by A. V. Dicey in Lectures on the relation between law and public opinion in England during the nineteenth century (1905). But exactly how Bentham's ideas were implemented was explored only in the 1950s and '6os, when historians began to develop models (involving stages such as 'irradiation', 'suscitation' and 'permeation')31 to explain the transition from theory to practice. There followed a lively debate about Bentham's influence on administrative reform in the Victorian period, which went under the banner: 'the nineteenth-century revolution in government'. Eventually it was conceded that, although the explanatory models helped to clarify the thoughts of those who used them, what actually went on could not wholly be explained by the models, but was inescapably affected by the exigencies of political bargaining, the ebb and flow of public opinion,32 the personalities of the reformers themselves,33 and the social and familial allegiances and combinations of those involved.34 All the same, much light was thrown on the contribution of utilitarianism to reform, in respect of the poor laws, education, the health of towns, the railways, emigration and conditions in factories and mines, in prisons and in lunatic asylums.35 Bentham disparaged the arts, and there has been little attempt to consider whether utilitarianism might have influenced cultural policy. Henry Cole and his work seem to present themselves as a test case to be investigated.

Col was acivi ervant ralmosthisentie workig e; he hado privatemean tspprt himohe took vy great care tense that hissalaryas a publirv was safeguarded, whatever new administrative adventure he embarked upon. From 1852 he was running his own civil service department, and can be counted therefore g he nt  what Gin Clarkcalle he er agethe  v who was al l eCarput that the vil sw we are accustomed  discreet, anonymous and working impartially and conscientiously at the bidding of ministers of any political persuasion - took some time to develop even after the Northcote-Trevelyan reforms in the 185os subjected the old, bumbling civil service (unforgettably characterised by Dickens in Little Dorrit as the 'Circumlocution Office' staffed by the Tite Barnacles) to a thorough overhaul. From the 1850s to the 1870s there was a period in which the new machinery of government was tuned up and driven by men who had missions and were not afraid to promote them: James Kay-Shuttleworth, Thomas Southwood Smith, Edwin Chadwick, Sir John Simon, Rowland Hill. Cole belongs among this group, and came to be even more infamous than they as a maverick.38 Of his relentless efforts to build up his department, a critic commented: 'Never before, probably, have the BARNAcLEs of that respectable but exasperating centre of officialism [the orthodox civil service] been so persistently and shamefully trifled with as by Mr. CoLE.'9 Cole's record as a vl rvant  s   e ment of the service as a whole.40

Cole's work in the civil service should not be viewed narrowly, as a case study in administrative practice, but should also be set in the context of the transition from what Harold Perkin has called 'the entrepreneurial ideal'of social organisation to the 'professional ideal'. In Perkin's persuasive model of modern English society, Cole seems perfectly cast as a member of the entrepreneurial tendency; but as a civil servant he was also instrumental in 'the divergence of the professional ideal from the entrepreneurial . .. which was ultimately to help to undermine the entrepreneurial society'. Again, he might be a good test case for analysis.42

A significant impetus was given to Victorian politics by ever-increasing democratic pressure. At the beginning of the nineteenth century politics was still controlled by a small oligarchy of the propertied and titled, who settled matters among themselves, respecting each others' interests. The Reform Acts of 1832, 1867 and 1884 opened governments to pressure from a wider electorate. Even in the 182os, however, the oligarchy was already feeling the force of 'public opinion', as Jonathan Parry points out in The rise and fall of Liberal government in Victorian Britain (I993). Public opinion became a ruling concept for reformers of every kind. When the Chartists published their Charter in 1839, its aim was said to be 'to create and extend, by every constitutional means, an enlightened public opinion . . . and justly and righteously impress that opinion upon the legislature'. When the Anti-Corn Law League wound itself up in 1846, John Bright said that it had demonstrated 'that there is in public opinion a power much greater than that which resides in any particular form of government'.43 It was in Cole's formative years that public opinion became a force to be reckoned with. As an administrative reformer, he believed that all management of organisations should be subjected 'systematically to the test of public opinion'.44 Not only did Cole thus defer to public opinion; he evolved into a most ingenious and mischievous manipulator of it. He discovered how to do this when he was fighting the Public Record Office for his job, and later he put his gift for opinion-forming at the disposal of the campaigns for the penny post and the unified railway gauge. He might well be regarded as a pioneer in the art of public relations, which he regarded not cynically but with fervent idealism. 'Publicity', he said, 'with its attendant free discussion and knowledge, is the mainspring of all the progressive tendencies of the present age.'45

It is possible to view him in a much wider perspective, as a combatant  a general, indeedin what Peter Gay has called the 'pleasure wars'of the nineteenth-century.

By this Gay means the often confused and contradictory attempts to satisfy the cultural appetites of the rising bourgeoisie, in the period which saw, at its start, the displacement of a rule of taste imposed by a secure élite, and ended with the apotheosis of the artist as individualist, outsider and rebel. To accommodate art to the lives of the bourgeoisie was exactly Cole's mission, and when we see his work against the shifting trends charted by Gay, it is easier to accept some of the apparent paradoxes in his attitudes  his doctrinaire authoritarianism in matters of taste as compared with his social liberalism in welcoming the working classes into his museum; his determination to make his projects self-supporting in a free market as compared with his tenacious occupancy of posts in the state service and his importunate pursuit of public money. Gay points out that increasing prosperity caused the bourgeoisie to feel the need for 'guidance in the realms of beauty; hence guardians of taste, whether well trained or just fluent, could steer public responses with unexpected authority'.46 The dimensions of Cole's authority in the taste wars is a subject worth enquiry.

Within nineteenth-century cultural life, international exhibitions (or expositions) were a novel and conspicuous phenomenon. They have been much studied recently, not only in their economic, political and social dimensions (which were exhaustively documented at the time), but because the exposition process can be now seen as a motor of modernity and a glorification of commodities. The symbolic and metaphorical significance of expositions has also been construed, not least by Walter Benjamin.47 The more such discourses are elaborated, the more salutary it becomes to relate them to what individual agents did historically, and Cole, whose interventions in five such exhibitions are recorded in great detail, must be an important casestudy.

Here, then, are a number of different ways of approaching Henry Cole, which could each be worked over at considerable length. But, we argue, what is needed as a first step in what could become a flourishing field of Henry Cole studies is a straightforward biography of the man, which will give some sense of how his manifold activities fitted together. Inevitably, public work must figure largely, though we can unveil some of his private motivations. His family life can be included to some extent, and reveals him in a rosy lght. His in motinal life,howeve, remais scure, wi to the nature of the source material.

T f in obeisance to his early training as an archivist, he himself carefully preserved the records of his life, most of which have, by various routes, reached the V&A. Most helpfully, he kept a diary, from which we know what he was doing almost every hour of every day. But the diary is predominantly an aide-mémoire about business. We learn very litte from it abou Cole'inner lfe, which, bviously by ntentin, he kept out of sight. Sometimes we can read between the clipped, factual lines of the diary (we have endeavoured to reproduce its jerky tone, with abbreviations, missing punctuation, frequent capitalisation, etc.). Occasionally there is a revealing letter. (So copious is the material at the V&A, which includes many letters to Cole, that we have not made a thorough search elsewhere for letters from Cole, a task that remains for someone else.) But, while we can know his trenchant thoughts and assertive deeds, and can analyse some published pronouncements (which he carefully preserved: see i ul er a psycho-historian. But even if our account must lack a certain inwardness, it surely cannot fail to be entertaining, since it is full of outward drama and flare.

Our biography follows a roughly chronological path. Up to 1852, however, Cole darted so fast from one thing to another, as he sought his true path in life, that we have chosen to separate out some chronologically overlapping themes. After $ 1 8 5 2 .$ when he had attained his important position as a public official, it has proved easier to hold his activities together in chronological order.

# PART ONE: THE MAKING OF A RADICAL

CHAPTER I

1808-1830

Henry Cole was born on 17 July 1808 in Bath. His parents, who were not well off, were living in odgings over ahop in Milsom Stree, the wide, fashionable, busting street near the centre of the city.l His father, an army officer, had been seriously wounded in 1803 by rioters in Dublin and was taking warm baths at the spa to help his recovery. His mother, Laetitia, who was very beautiful, had married his father in 1802. Henry was not born until six years later and he called himself their 'first living child', so perhaps other babies had died.

The unavoidable question about Cole's character in later life is: what was the origin of his exceptional drive and energy? He himself pondered on this, in a fragment of autobiography, begun in 1875 but not included in his 'official' autobiography, Fifty Years of Public Work. Here are his words.

I was prized and greatly indulged by both parents. . . Nothing was refused to me... When about two years old I cried al one night for the moon and both parents promised I should have it: but the moon went down and I was not pacified. . .  To my own perception my will has always seemed to be my most marked possession. I have cherished it: for years I have fixedon some decision until said will had its own way. There it was, before I could speak, and it was surey fostered by having its way from the beginning.

Henry said of his father that he was 'always kind and indulgent . . . hasty, capricious and over sanguine'.3 The tumbling series of adjectives suggests a man of goodnatured confusion. Henry Robert Cole, who was 27 in 1808, had wanted to train as an architect, and several members of his family could have helped him in that career. His father, Martin Cole, had come up to London from Bedford, and, with the help of his wife's money, had become a property developer. Beginning life as a carpenter, he was later a brewer, a timber merchant and a property valuer. At the age of 67 he handed over his businesses to his brothers and brother-in-law, and retired to start a bank in Peterborough. Since the Cole family had been established for several generations in Peterborough as bankers and lawyers, this final stage of Martin's career was a reasonable development. A practising architect in the family was the husband of Henry Robert's sister, Elizabeth: David Laing, who had been a pupil of Sir John Soane and worked with him on the Bank of England, and who became surveyor to the Board of Customs. He and Martin Cole were partners in a timber yard near Westminster Bridge. A further architectural connection was Charles Cole, another brother of Martin's, and thus Henry's great-uncle, an architect also from Peterborough, who had a successful practice and was the assistant surveyor to the Office of Woods and Forests until, in 179o, he was dismissed for overcharging.

Despite this he was master of the Tylers' Company in 18or. He lived in some grandeur in Park Lane, where he died in 1803.4

The Napoleonic Wars caused a slump in building work, so Henry Robert did not try for an architectural career after all. According to his son, he was caught up by military fever, and joined the army as a cornet in the I2th (Prince of Wales) Dragoons. After training at Maidstone, where he met his wife and married her, he was posted to Dublin. The city was in turmoil, threatened with a nationalist rebellion led by Robert Emmet. This hotheaded attempt to oust the English was not nearly as well organised as the rising of $1 7 9 8$ , and Emmet only had an unruly band of pro-French United Irishmen at his command. Nonetheless, a less sanguine man than Cole might have hesitated to take his wife into the streets of Dublin when riots were likely. On the fateful night of Saturday 23 July 1803, when the rising took place, Cole, with Laetitia and her little dog, was travelling in a post chaise when it was stopped in Thomas Street by the mob. These men at first good-naturedly handed him a pike and asked him to join them. When he refused, they shot a pistol in his face. Luckily it misfired, but the sparks from the pan scarred his upper lip for life. The men then attacked him with pitchforks and pikes, wounding him in 17 places. His wife and the dog were unharmed. The same mob, a few minutes later, murdered Lord Kilwarden, the Lord Chief Justice of Ireland. Cole was left for dead but he recovered. His wounds, particularly those in his back, were painful for the rest of his life, and he never saw active service again. He transferred into the Ioth Dragoons as an ensign early in 18o5, and was given the rank of lieutenant in March. After Henry's birth, the Coles stayed in Bath until after the birth of Henry's next brother, Frederick Lindsay, in April 181o. He was named after the 17th Lord Lindsay of the Byres, a regimental friend of Henry Robert, and was always called Lindsay rather than Frederick.

Henry Robert Cole was wellenough to go back to non-combatant military duty by 1811, when he was made a captain in the 82nd (Prince of Wales Volunteers) Regiment of Foot. He was appointed a recruiting officer, touring the North of England to persuade men to join the army to fight Napoleon. Little Henry and Lindsay were dressed up in miniature military uniforms and would ride with their father on his tours, sitting on the pummel of his saddle. At first Henry Robert was based in Chester, and young Henry remembered the ancient picturesque houses of the city. In 1813 Henry Robert was moved to Lincoln, where a third child, William, was born on New Year's Eve. Unlike the other children, who were baptised when they were four or six months old, he was christened within 48 hours of his birth, which could mean that he was not strong or had had a difficult birth. Whether or not the wee y cpliatis at his birh,he rew p to benstabe, ansent u of his adult life in a lunatic asylum.

In Lincoln, Captain Cole rented a house in the Vicars Close, to the south of the cathedral. The house was much altered in 1875, but it is still there, tucked under the wall of the close, near the ancient doorway leading into the Cathedral Yard. The Coles' house backed on to another where the celebrated 'Squire Osbaldeston' lived. George Osbaldeston was a hunting man of the old school, and master of the Burton Hunt. He was a Tory MP for Lincoln, and made himself famous in 183 1 by riding from York to London in Io hours. It was entirely in character for the squire to arrange a huge firework display to celebrate the victory at the battle of Waterloo. Henry Cole, who was then nearly eight years old, well remembered those fireworks, ahat  shy   h Paris fete or Crystal Palace fire works have done since'

With the war over, Captain Cole was retired on half pay, and the family moved to Lewisham, a village to the south-east of London where Laetitia had lived with her two sisters before moving to Maidstone. Her family, the Downers, were merchants in the city, where her father had been a chip hatter  a dealer in straw hats and bonnets. Her mother, who died in 18oo, was Mary Clark, from a Quaker family. At Lewisham Henry and Lindsay went to their first school. Cole remembered nearly  years later:

The real miseries of my life began here. The mistress was without feeling and a tyrant. I must have been between seven and eight years of age. I was bad at spelling. Once in reading I stumbled at the word 'verily' and was ordered to spell it  which was quite impossible for me. So I was imprisoned in a dark closet there to await a whipping. My brother courageously and tenderly stept forth and volunteered to be whipped instead. 'Can you spell the word?' He tried and failed and then we were whipped together. . . . The lesser punishments were thumping on the head with the tyrant's thimble, and solitary confinement during which learning the Prayer Book was the task. I still have a cold shudder at the collect for all sorts and conditions of men.6

Captain Cole attempted to eke out his half-pay by selling coal to his friends, and had an arrangement with the well-known coal merchants Cory & Cory. He was always short of money, plaguing Lord Lindsay with begging letters; and only presents from Laetitia's brother Richard kept them afloat. Richard had been a naval officer, but had gone to Berbice, now Guyana, facing the Atlantic in north-east South America, where he made a fortune as a merchant, dealing mainly in straw hats that he sent to his relations in London. In 1816 Richard wrote to tell Laetitia that his agent in London was sending her some money and went on: 'My dear Sister I hope fortune will begin to brighten a little with you. Such continuance of Cloud since 18oo is enough to destroy even your happy temper. How would you and Cole like to come out to the West Indies . . . in the hope of bettering your fortunes[?] . . . I hope your children are all well & that Cole bears up against these vicissitudes of fortune.' In the same letter he told her that his wife was expecting a child, and continued:

You have never heard before that I have a family, but it is very true. Oh! Two three four ive ix, seven, oll colours & descipions. What wil you sy me thee ae ling, the rest dead. This is the blood of the Downers with a vengeance. Holland will be their Country. One has been there some time. My Eldest born in 1 8oo in January is one of my Clerks. This is a pretty avowal from me who never disclosed these family appendages before.7

Laetitia might also have confessed to another family appendage, since the Coles' fourth son, Richard Lockington, was born on 29 February 1816.

Henry Cole called Richard Downer 'a stern hard uncle', and remembered how he brought a black slave boy with him when he visited England (probably in spring 18 19). The slave used to accompany Downer to Cole's father's house, where his master would 'take him into concealed spots in the garden and flog him. My spirit rebelled against this cruelty,' recalled Henry, 'and defying my uncle's threats I used to run and shout for my mother to protect the boy which sometimes stopped the punishment.'8 By this date the family had moved to Lambeth, where Charles Augustus, Henry's fourth brother, was born on 1 February 1819.

After attending the dame school at Lewisham, Henry might well have proceeded to a small private school, the sort of school attended by his near-contemporary Charles Dickens, whose family, like Cole's, clung to the impoverished lower edge of the middle class, and who several times described such schools in his novels. But Captain Cole, with poor health, five sons and an income of £22o a year, could not even afford this, and had to find a way of educating his boys at no cost. Paradoxically, this gave Henry Cole the sort of education that was normally reserved for the sons of England's elite, for he was sent to Christ's Hospital, where his family had a right to a free place.

Christ's Hospital's charter had been granted in June I 5 5 3 by King Edward VI. The school was housed in the monastic buildings of the Grey Friars to the east of the City of London, which were left empty after the dissolution of the monasteries. Like a number of other schools, it had been founded and endowed for the education of 'poor boys'. Most such schools sooner or later forgot their obligation to the poor, and by the early nineteenth century they served the rich. England's top eight boys' schools were all of this type: the boarding schools at Eton, Harrow, Winchester and Rugby; and the London schools of Westminster, Charterhouse, St Paul's and Merchant Taylors'. Christ's Hospital was regarded as the ninth in order of priority after these.9 Unlike the others it still supplied scholarships for poor boys, and in comparison with the others, fewer of its pupils went to university and more into trade. 10 In 18 17 Captain Cole succeeded in proving that through his mother's family, the Bunns, he was a direct descendant of a Thomas Lockington, treasurer at Christ's Hospital in the early eighteenth century, who had left property to provide two places in the school for his relations. So Captain Cole claimed scholarships for his sons.

On 9 January 18 17, the eight-year-old Henry was delivered by his father to the school. He was put in the charge of the matron of the 1oth Ward, whom Captain Cole tipped with a guinea. Henry was provided with the uniform of long blue coat, yellow stockings and red belt. This was a survival of Tudor boys' dress (to which breeches had been added in 18 1o). The long coat had been perpetuated through the eighteenth century as the dress of charity school boys. As it fell into disuse except at Christ's Hospital, it came to have a certain chic, but in Cole's time it probably still conveyed a mixed message: of privilege combined with poverty (plates 1,2,3). Henry wrote, I remained at the school between five and six years and had a rather unhappy time of it'. 11

The teaching at Christ's Hospital was carried on, as Cole recorded, in three schools, the Grammar School, the Writing School and the Mathematical School. The latter was used especially for boys intended for the sea, who were being trained as navigators. They were the tyrants of the School, and marked by silver badges on their shoulders. They wore the longest shoestrings and went "spadging" through the Civi all boy e he eaf eore the windle a class of 10o boys, learned little. 'I recollect a Latin grammar written in Latin being p intoy n n ilI wa ar.By the words I got the sounds more or less by heart and could repeat them. But as for the meaning of the words themselves or the sense they conveyed I had not the dimmest glimmering.'13

This feebleinteletual regime was accompanid by a spartan physical rime. Cole described the school's accommodation thus:

School was separated into some twelve wards  the beds were in boxes rather like the berts along the sides of a steamer  to boys sleeping in ne box. ..Washing andkindred arrangements were simply beastly. Forty boys used to scramble to wash their han and eetine large tb fhting over a towel to dyheelvs The litt boys habu muvy whe dred boys could each wash at a running tap. . .. Twenty boys used one comb and hairbruh. Ringwor was a chronic disorder in the School.We got up at six in the summer and at seven in the winter, and each boy made his own bed.14

Iron be we uiu o he boxson  Heycame  the ol. He continued:

The diet was as follows. The breakfast was dry bread and beer which went by the name of 'swipes' served in an old monastic leather vessel like a boot, called a Jack. If instead of fetching a supply of swipes - undrinkable and repellent  the Jack boy brought up water instead, his hands were beaten with a cane. Since I left the School this meagre fare has been changed for bread and milk. The supper in 18 17 consisted of a pennyworth of bread with a piece of single Gloster [sic] cheese which was usually divided, and half kept over for the morning: swipes again washing down the bread and cheese. The dinners were thus arranged. On Mondays milk and water called 'Sky blue' served in a wooden bowl with bread and butter. On Tuesdays roast mutton served on a square wooden trencher - certainly a monastic inheritance — and a great privilege it was for the big boys to run their bread in the dripping of the large wooden dish in which the joints were cut. On Wednesday came rice milk  supply unlimited and so it was red letter day — with a small portion of bread and butter. On Thursdays boiled beef known as 'tough Jack' and not much relished. On Fridays boiled mutton called 'Gags' still less liked except for the rub in the platter. On Saturdays Peas soup - supply unlimited and therefore a popular dish. On Sundays boiled beef again  except once a month when the beef was baked. On one festival in the year we had baked pork, on another boiled pork; on a third veal for dinner and on a fourth cream cheese for supper.

Cole added, surely in a remarkably forgiving spirit: 'Scanty as the fare seems . . . and hungry as we were the boys were healthy  and the death rate not high.'15

Conditions at Christ's Hospital were not very different from those at the other great schools. Deprivation was often exacerbated by physical abuse. At this time, bullying and violence reached extreme proportions in the great old schools — not only among pupils, as depicted searingly in Thomas Hughes's Tom Brown's Schooldays, but also between boys and masters. This was the great age of the flogging schoolmaster - the supremely notorious example being the squat, frenzied Dr Keate of Eton. Christ's Hospital had a particularly bad reputation for corporal punishment, and the chief executant there was the Rev. Dr Arthur Trollope, the headmaster. This distinguished classical scholar and churchman was a fat man of about 5o, with protruding eyes. Cole described him as having:

a round cleanly shaved face, with short red hair well powdered — soon roused to a passion. . . . He wore an old shabby shovel hat  a hat which came to be a sign that he was eager to brandish the cane. Half our parrot knowledge was frightened out of us by his impatient knock at the School door, and then his fussy entrance. What joy if he did not appear at our first hours work. In the afternoon, after luncheon he would come in a well beavered, rosetted, hat smiling and picking his teeth. He could then placidly bear many false quantities and hesitancies. Not so in the morning when at the slightest trip, he would thrust his hands into his breeches pockets hastily groping for his keys - unlock the desk in a bungling way, which increased his ire, and seizing a cane he would rush at the head of the boy before he called for the palm of his hand. We all dreaded him and hated the sight of him. He was like an ogre always thirsting for the blood of little boys. I don't remember his ever giving one a word of encouragement. What a relief it was if he left school before we broke off work. He did not flog, but caned with a ferrule well wax ended. He gave me once the longest and most ferocious caning any boy had from him in my days. We had to construe some of Ovid's Metamorphoses at an early morning's lesson. By lucky chance I was fluent and said my part rightly, but throughout the class there was so much stumbling that the Dr. gave up caning and ordered the class to come again in the afternoon and repeat the task. I felt this injustice - to have to appear again in the hated presence so I asked if I too was to come. A 'yes' was thundered at me. But the devil or an Angel prompted me to resist. I rebelled against what I thought was an unjust decree and resolved not to come . . . As soon as the class assembled the doctor exclaimed 'Where's Cole? Fetch him down.' I was fetched from the Writing School. Upon my entering the Grammar School in custody and led to execution all noise ceased . .. 'Zookers boy I'll cut you in twice one. So you would not come.' Foaming at the mouth, he often did so, and shaking with wrath he took his thickest cane. Then he threw back his serge robe to get free force and gave me six cuts on my hand with all his might. I howled out that I could not bear any more. 'But you shall.' And then he flew at me hunting me round the room and thrashed my head, arms, back legs wherever he could. I took refuge at the window. He exclaimed 'If I break it you rascal you shall pay' and then whacked my legs and ankles. At last having exhausted his strength he let me go.

Again with commendable restraint, Cole commented: 'To this day I doubt if I deser I began right  the Dr. went wrong. I went wronger and then I think the Dr. went wrongest.'16

Many Victorian worthies who endured a schooling such as Cole's managed to look back on their alma mater with sentimental affection. Cole, however, said of Christ's Hospital that or years erwars I looked back upon it as a place where all my tme ha b ws  we I ha  r han ohH have influenced him in his later career as an educational reformer. After school, Cole was unable to go to university, for he would have needed the financial support of a scholarship, and, since he was not a 'Grecian'or prefect, he was not eligible for the o He had to earn his living.

L o  S He yr ate:h I e  I   I  o al. . . I had a deep love for her mingled with respect. When she died I had a strong show that he was an assiduous attender at church on Sundays, until a change of mood in 1828. Then he began resentfully to record that he only went to church 'to keep peace' in the family, and took along something to read during the service: 'prayed most devotedly out of Don Juan'." Soon after, his church-going ceased.

After Laetitia's death, thefamily moved to lodgings in Bond Street, and discussions took place as to what profession Henry might follow. Captain Cole thought that Henry should go into a bank, but Uncle Richard Downer was against it. Then it was thought that he might become a surgeon, and Uncle Richard agreed to pay the premium to a Mr Adams, so that Henry could become his apprentice. But at Christ's Hospital Cole won a silver medal for writing (plate 4), and the school provided him, when he left in April 1823, with a recommendation from one of the masters to Francis Cohen, a sub-commissioner of the public records. Young Henry, and two other boys from Christ's Hospital, became copying clerks. 'I took off my Tudor dress on the 9th April 1823 and on the 10th presented myself in an Oxford mixture suit with tailed coat at Mr Cohen's office at King's Bench Walk, Temple at the north-east corner.'20

Francis Cohen was in his mid-3os when Henry became his clerk. He was the son of a city stockbroker, who had him carefully educated by private tutors, and he grew up with a passion for medieval history. He was of medium height, dark and handsome, dashing, intelligent and enthusiastic. It was observed, in the idiom of the time, that 'he had the air of high breeding and the show of natural dignity that are sometimes observable in Jews of the highest type and finest quality'.21 In 182 1 he was baptised a Christian, so as to be eligible to become a member of the Bar. He was elected a fellow of the Royal Society at the very early age of 3 3, and he was a welcome caller at Holland House where he met many of the leading politicians and intellectuals of the day. There he met John Allen, Lord Holland's librarian and close friend, who was a member of the board of the Record Commission. He suggested that Cohen might edit some early parliamentary rolls, which the commission wished to see published. After some protracted negotiations Cohen was appointed a sub-commissioner in March 1822.

He employed clerks to make transcripts of the documents with which he was concerned, the 'Parliamentary writs of the time of Edward I', 22 which he then, at his leisure, edited and prepared for publication. At first he employed young clerks from the Record Office at the Tower of London. But after about a year, two of them, Thomas and William Hardy, rebelled against the low pay he gave them, and so he decided to make other arrangements. He consulted the steward at Christ's Hospital, who recommended Henry and the other two other 'Blues', George Aungier and William Peacock. Henry wrote that Cohen 'set me to fish out my own way in learning the abbreviations in which the Records both in Latin and French were written, and sent me to learn French in the evenings of the Abbe de Roussigny in Great Russell Street - an emigré'.23 These lessons went on for three years, twice a week, and evidently something stuck, for Cole was able in later life to deliver a short public speech in French in Paris in 1867.24

Cole found himself in the rather odd position of working within a public institution but employed privately to serve one of its staff. The Record Commission was a slack organisation. Although a State Paper Office, with its keeper, had existed since the reign of Elizabeth I, England's public records had been allowed to pile up, without central co-ordination, in the government departments and legal institutions which had at first produced them, and continued to own them. In an attempt to bring some order, several reports and inquiries were made during the eighteenth century, without positive outcome, but in 18oo an investigation by a parliamentary select committee led to the appointment of the first of a series of six commissions, known collectively as 'The Record Commission'. The commission was eventually replaced, after the death of William IV in 1837. By then it had lost public confidence, because it concentrated on publishing editions of certain of the records, but failed to provide adequately for the storage and listing of the mass of them. Quantities of public money were spent on employing the people who edited the records. The commissioners - over 2o of them, including bishops, peers and politicians, with only the occasional scholar who knew or cared anything about history or the sources of historical research - regarded the appointment of editors as an opportunity to extend patronage to their friends. The employment of Cohen, through the influence of Allen, was typical.

The nature of the records themselves can be conveyed in Henry Cole's words, from an article he later wrote. 'In a popular sense the term is applied to all public documents preserved in a recognised repository,' he said. Britain was especially rich 'in the possession of antient written memorials of all branches of its government, constitutional, judicial, parliamentary, and fiscal, memorials authenticated by all the solemn sanctions of authority, telling truly, though incidentally, the history of our progress as a people, and handed down in unbroken series through the period of nearly seven centuries'. The records mostly took the form of 'rolls written on skins of parchment and vellum', which were 'kept in pouches or bags of leather, canvas, cordovan, and buckram' or else in 'chests, coffers, coffins, and "forcers" bound with iron and painted of different colours, cases or "scrinia", "skippets", or small turned boxes, and hanapers, or "hampers of twyggys" (fig.1).25 They were kept in various decrepit stores in central London.

In early June 1822, Henry went to work for the first time at one of these stores in the Tower of London, where he found that he and his fellows were treated as outsiders and cold-shouldered by all the established staff, including William and Thomas Hardy. This repository was headed by Henry Petrie, who was the keeper in charge of the Chancery Records, and who had beaten Cohen to this post in 1819. It cannot have helped Cole's popularity in these early days that he spilt ink on a petition to Edward I. 'It was a terrible offence and might have caused my dismissal and altered my whole career.'26 Fortunately he was not dismissed. The rule that only pencils were to be used by those working on records was not introduced until 1840. The records were kept on the upper floors of the White Tower, and the exquisite Norman chapel was filled to its vaulted roof with records belonging to the Admiralty. Their security was threatened by the fact that the cellars were used by the army for storing gunpowder, 'enough to destroy all Tower-hill, and change even the course of the Thames if an explosion took place'. 27

o R where divine service is performed; in vaults two stories underground at Somerset Hu; i ar  hu cllar  Wr Hali e abl   a

Carlton Ride; in the Chapter House of Westminster Abbey; in offces surrounded by and subject to all the accidents of private dwellings' such as the Augmentation Office in Palace Yard, Westminster, and the First Fruits Office in the Temple. Cole often worked in the Chapter House at Westminster Abbey. This was the repository of the ancient Treasury of the Exchequer, and the Domesday Book was kept there. As early as the seventeenth century it was filled with records, and to make room for the files that kept arriving Sir Christopher Wren had put in wooden floors and staircases. When Cole worked there the heating was by an inadequate stove that was either too cold, when it smoked badly, or too hot, when it glowed red. It had a metal flue that ran up through the mass of vellum and paper. Another threat came from a nearby washhouse with a copper heated by a fire; this belonged to one of the prebendal houses, and had been the subject of protests from the commission to the dean and chapter for nearly 100 years.

![](images/74eab5401a927de9586ba3ed06deb7554d801dfca63e7f95930d6d7283609639.jpg)  
Figure  'Public Records of England': illustrations from Cole's article in the Penny Magazine, 184 1. Records usually took the form of parchment scrolls (top left). They were kept sometimes in leather pouches (top right), sometimes in 'skippets' or small turned boxes (bottom right), in 'hanapers' or baskets (bottom left), and in many other kinds of box or bag.

The keeper in charge of this office was John Caley, the secretary to the Record Commission, who was also keeper of the Augmentation Office. The office hours were from ten to one o'clock and the clerks were allowed six weeks holiday a year. Though Caley drew a salary of nearly £ 1,ooo a year, he never came into the office but directed the work from his house in Clerkenwell, using his footman, Charles Gay, as a messenger. Cole described how a search for a record was made. An applicant would first call at the office, but if 'Charles Gay was not there, he would then have to come a second time.' Once had had found Gay, he would be sent to Caley's house, where he might or might not find Caley. Having reached Caley, he would be told to return after two days, to give Caley time to search his indexes for the required document. On the applicant's return, Caley would, if he had traced the document, tell the applicant to call again later to see it. 'The records were brought from the office, to Mr. Caley's house, and it very often happened that the precise document wanted was not produced. Parties have informed me', said Cole, that 'they have lost a fortnight in running backwards and forwards to his house.' This system, which had been operating for 15 years, was bad for the records, as they were toted around in a bag by Gay. 'More injury has been done to the Rolls within these few years, than in the two preceding centuries.' Caley never allowed 'any applicant, without exception to look into his Indexes, public or private'. For this service of searching and transcribing Caley charged what he liked, and he made over £1,2oo a year from these fees alone.

While Cole was able to observe these dismal practices, he was to some extent distanced from them, because he and his two fellows were outside the system, working directly on transcription for Francis Cohen. Cohen's task eventually resulted in four volumes, each weighing 7 1 lbs. 'I passed nine years of my life assisting . . . in the manufacture of enormous folio tomes, so heavy that no one would lift them if he could help it,' wrote Cole. 'Their sale was very small.'30

At the end of the summer of 1823, Cohen went to Great Yarmouth to marry Elizabeth Turner, the second daughter of Dawson Turner, the East Anglian banker. Turner was a fellow of the Royal Society (he had sponsored Cohen), and was well known as a book collector, antiquarian, naturalist and patron of artists. His eldest daughter was married to Dr William Hooker, the botanist and future director of the Royal Botanic Gardens at Kew. After the wedding of Cohen and Elizabeth Turner, the bridegroom wrote this surprising note to Henry Cole: 'Tomorrow morning, be so good as to direct Mr Egerton to paint out the name "Cohen" on the door and over the same, and to paint the name "Palgrave", do not let him alter the other lettering as it will thus better show that the name only has been altered and not the person.1 Cohen had abandoned his Jewish name and taken his mother-in-law's maiden name. Presumably because of his social standing and his reputation as a historian, the College o Arms allowed him to take his place in the Palgrave pedigree and to bear the family' ars and crest. In the same letter, the newly minted Palgrave aske Cole to order new visiting cards.

Elizabeth Palgrave and her sistr wer all rtisi. They drew weland ad which their father had commissioned. They took an interest in Cole's artistic education, introducing him to Charles Wild, who was, as Cole said, 'the first architectural p     in lessons, at 7s. a time, from the watercolourist David Cox, who gave him some sketches.Cole was able to repay his kindness by helping his son, also David, to find a post as a tutor. Cole had two of his own watercolours exhibited in 1828 at Somerset House, then the home of the Royal Academy.33 He eagerly visited such art exhibitions as there were  unconscious, as yet, that exhibitions would turn out to be his life's work.

Cole tried to improve his defective education in other ways. He took German lessons, in addition to his French lessons, and developed his slight talent, but great love, for music. He had been given a flute by his father many years before, and took lessons from a Corporal Blizzard of the Life Guards' band, who also taught him fencing. He had some singing lessons from a friend, William Kingston. Once a week he visited a Monsieur Gueraint, whom he punningly described as a 'hop merchant', for dancing lessons. When he went to church on Sunday, it was not infrequently for the music. He oten went with Lindsay to a Catholic chapel to hear the wel-known singer, Madame Malibran.

In person, Cole grew to be only about 5 feet 8 inches tall; he was slight, strong and active. He brushed his reddish hair forward and had it fashionably curled, and he wore a monocle on a ribbon, then considered very smart, though he was short-sighted and should have used spectacles. He had his clothes made to measure, and his numerous female cousins hemmed silk cravats for him. Though his diaries in early years record little except his expenses and where he got his dinner, they gradually begin to note outings to plays and the opera. Cole's social life in his early 2os was passed chiefly among his various young relations. Among these were two families of cousins, the Bonds and the Webbs, who were the children of his mother's sisters and lived near each other at Epping in Essex. Henry saw most of the Bonds, where there were four girls, Charlotte, Frances, Laura and Marian ('gentle, attractive and cultivated'), 34 besides two boys, Richard and John. There was only one child in the other family, Mary Anne, whose father Thomas Webb was a veterinary surgeon. She eventually inherited Quendon Hall, farther out in Essex, from a relation, and married Captain Henry Byng of the Royal Navy. Cole visited one or other of the families about once a month, and usually walked the 2o-odd miles from central London, as there was no question of his being able to afford the fare on the coach. His younger brothers sometimes went too, and Henry was expected to keep them in order. His Uncle Webb once wrote to him when an elderly relative was expected: 'Now my dear fellow . . . I must beg you to have great steadiness of behaviour, no leap frog or Wheelbarrow driving. . . . Joking apart, do enforce upon your brother the indispensable necessity of appearing in a decent set of breeches'. As a postscript he added: 'Be sparing of the gin and water on the road'.35

Cole had some other Bond first cousins, the children of William Bond, D.D. He was rector of Hanwell in Middlesex to the west of London, where he ran a school, and was also a chaplain to the dukes of Cambridge and Kent. Among his children, those whom Henry knew best were Edward and John. Edward made his career at the British Museum, becoming a distinguished reforming chief librarian and a knight, while John followed Henry into the record service and worked there all his life, ending up a senior assistant keeper.

Cole's Laing cousins, who lived in London, were also friends and he spent evenings with them playing music. In 1825 his Uncle David Laing was disgraced because the Custom House that he had designed and built about 1o years earlier began to fall down. Among the faults, it was discovered that he had not used sturdy enough wooden piles in the foundations, and had used defective wood elsewhere. There was even a suggestion that he and the contractors, John Miles and Henry Peto, had colluded in a fraud. 36 He was dismissed and the family moved to Clapham. Henry saw quite a lot of his cousins Elizabeth and Charlotte Laing, but he came to dislike the family, noting in 1831: 'They are a mischievous set, shallow minded and rude'.37

Cole amused himself as young men will. The theatre (of which he was 'passionately fond'),38 the opera and art exhibitions became regular treats. On 12 March 18 27 he recorded that he had been 'to what is called a Ball', from which, after spending six or eight hours devotedly attending a Julia Westby, he returned home at half past five in the morning. 'A mighty exertion for one accustomed to regularity.' He had an eye for a pretty girl, when he could see accurately. On 5 July 1829 he met two sisters. 'Without my glasses I had formed a pleasant opinion of them: which was quickly dispelled in putting on my spectacles.' He evidently had a taste for pranks. At New Year's Eve 1830, he dressed up as a female friend's female chaperon, hiring a dress, wig and prince-of-Wales feathers (fig.2). He went to a party at the house of the Hughes family in Gower Street, and survived the whole evening undetected, though, as he said, 'suspicion was resting heavily upon me'. He regarded his female cousins with interest, as emerges from his correspondence with his fellow clerk, William Peacock. Peacock was the son of the bursar at Christ's Hospital, and a close friend of Cole's already at school. William could not bear the tedium of copying and transcribing old texts, and so in 1828 he went to India under the patronage of Lord William Bentinck, the new governorgeneral o Bengal. Over he next  years he wrote Cole $7 5$ letters, which Cole kept. In one of them he says: 'You speak of the beauty of your Cousins at Epping & immediately subsequent ask me if the climate has had any physical effect upon me. Verily I suspect the beauty of Marian has had a physical effect upon you. How say you?'39 But Henry was at this time attracted in another direction. He was in love with Emma Hughes, of the Gower Street family. He proposed marriage, but was turned down by her mother. He suffered a great deal and confided in William Peacock, who was sympathetic.

![](images/00a427e32098ff313b9dd38da646f4159f31e19e281c7f289a55f8eebc224eb5.jpg)  
Figure 2 Party-going: a sketch commemorating Cole's attendance at a New Year's party in 1830, disguised as a woman.

The Cole family, with their housekeeper, a widow named Mrs Cook, changed lodgings several times during these years, which Henry disliked very much. When they moved, yet again, on 15 April 1826, he wrote sadly in his diary that they had 'moved to 18 Stamford St. Q? How long shall we remain here?' As it turned out, the family lived there for many years, and Cole made a watercolour drawing of his room. It is likely that he executed it during the spring of 1827, while he was having his second course of lessons with Charles Wild. He seems to have set himself an exercise in perspective: the drawers of the chest-of-drawers are open and a cupboard door is ajar. The room is small and contains pieces of furniture that probably belonged to his landlord. It has a fireplace that can never have been lit, as the close-carpet runs up to the grate, with no hearth showing. The pot-plants on the table by the window show Cole's interest in botany (plate ).

Cole had many quarrels with his father about the position in the household of his housekeeper. 'Mrs Cook must & will go,' he confided to his diary on 3o March 1828. But, although he expected 'some decisive measures' on I I May, he discovered on the 3 1st: 'Mrs Cook is not to go. God knows how the business will terminate.' It terminated in $\mathrm { ~ r ~ } 8 3 3$ when the captain, evidently in need of solace, married Mrs Cook.
