It was Henry who had gone: he took chambers in Buckingham Street, near the Adelphi, in March $\boldsymbol { \textbf { I 8 3 1 } }$ . The house in Stamford Street, however, had already 'led to acquaintances which have had the most important influences upon my life'.40

# CHAPTER 2 The Philosophic Radicals 1826-c.1833

The house in Stamford Street, to which the Coles moved in 1826, belonged to the writer, Thomas Love Peacock, then aged 4 1. He worked at the East India Company, one of several 'Assistants Under the Examiner' who administered the company's Indian business at long range through correspondence. He kept two rooms in his house for his own use when in London, but had sent his family to live in the country, and allowed the Coles to occupy the rest of the house. In early life Peacock won recognition as a poet and the friendship of Shelley. Later he became celebrated for brilliant, satirical novels of ideas, including Headlong Hall and Nightmare Abbey. Genial, generous and a witty conversationalist, he took an immediate fancy to Henry, who was nearly 18. Frequently Cole would walk into the city with Peacock, and 'generally about once a week I passed two hours in the evening at Stamford Street with him listening to a perpetual flow of his wit and stories, oftentimes profane'. By 'profane' Cole signifies Peacock's cheerful atheism, which unsettled Cole's faith.1

Peacock frequently gave Cole an 'order' — a free ticket — for a theatre or a concert, and sometimes he got Cole to make notes on a performance to assist him in writing a review when he was unable to go himself. This launched Cole into journalism, his first independent review being a short notice of Malibran's performance in La Cenerentola, published in The Globe in April 1830. Cole wrote: 'What emotions of pride were aroused in me by the publication of this contribution'.² Through Peacock, Cole met a number of owners and editors of newspapers and magazines, such as John Bowring, the first editor of the Westninster Review; Walter Coulson of The Globe, who had once been Jeremy Bentham's secretary; and Albany Fonblanque, the editor and later owner of the Examiner. Cole was soon writing weekly musical reviews for him.' By 1834 Cole had moved on from reviewing, and after that his journalism was mostly devoted to fighting the various causes he took up. It has been rightly said that 'Cole was not in journalism for journalism's sake'.4 All the same, the opportunity that Peacock gave him to become a writer for periodicals was an important opening for him.

An even more important favour that Peacock performed for Cole was to introduce him to John Stuart Mill and his circle. This contact directed Cole's attention to the philosophy of utilitarianism, by which all his future activities were influenced; it brought his political interests into focus; and, above al, supplied him with the sort of enlightenment that he had missed by not going to university.

When Peacock joined the East Inda Company in 1819, another new entrant was S   il son John Stuart into the company in 1823 as a clerk, John then being 17. On 7 November 1826 Peacock took Cole to the house of George Grote, the middle-aged radical politician, banker and historian, and there introduced him to John Stuart Mill and to the 'disquisitive set of young men' (as Peacock called them), who clustered around Mill, meeting twice a week early in the morning for reading and discussion.

John Mill (plate 6) was described by Thomas Carlyle, who first met him at about this time, as 'a slender rather tall and elegant youth, with small clear roman-nosed face, two small earnestly-smiling eyes; modest, remarkably gifted with precision of utterance . .  a gifted and amiable youth. We had almost four hours of the best talk I have mingled in for long'.6 Cole also fell under John Mills spell. He wrote that he 'was 2o years of age when we first met. I was two years younger. He had already become a writer in periodicals. No two courses of early training and experience could have differed more than ours did. It might seem that we had little in common but I appreciated in him what I imperfectly understood and we soon grew intimate'.7

Mill had an extraordinary upbringing. His father James, a dour and driven man, had raised himself from an obscure Scottish background to literary success in London. Through writing a History of British India, a long-drawn-out project that loomed over John's childhood, he succeeded in obtaining his senior post with the East India Company. The overwhelmingly powerful influence in James's life was the political philosopher Jeremy Bentham. They met in 1808, when Bentham was 6o and James Mill 35. Bentham was already renowned for his writings, which sought to devise a political system in which the greatest good should lie in the greatest happiness of the greatest number, a system in which deference and precedent should have no value, but in which rational efficiency should prevail. Although Bentham believed that his ideas were capable of transforming the way his country was run, he kept himself remote from practical life, working on ever more complex refinements of his system. James Mill became his disciple and interpreter, synthesising his views and trying to bring them into practical effect by promoting them among radical politicians. He also found the opportunity to apply them practically in the upbringing of his son, in which Bentham took a keen interest. John Mill was to be educated to be a demonstration of Bentham's idea of what a perfect human being should be. He was to grow up totally rational, free of the waywardness of emotion, and free of the imposed principles of religion.

This education had its quaint, even comic aspects, especially during the summers that the Bentham and Mill families spent together at Forde Abbey in Dorset. There, ignoring the large number of rooms that the house contained, they all worked together in the saloon, an enormous room hung with tapestries after designs by Raphael. The Mills sat at one table, James the father writing his History of India, and the child John working at problems of logic. At the far end of the room, Bentham, surrounded by cushions to which he pinned his notes, wrestled with his great scheme for universal education, the Chrestomathia. From an early age John, the eldest of the Mill children, was expected to superintend the lessons of his six younger siblings, three girls and three boys. He was allowcd no friends outside the family, no toys, and no games except battledore and shuttlecock in the great hall, which Bentham enjoyed and took very seriously. The children's mother was ignored. Under this regime, Mill became extraordinarily precocious. He started learning Greek at three, was doing algebra at eight, logic at twelve, and economics at thirteen.

Mill insisted that 'mine . . . was not an education of cram', because it involved continuous, searching explanation of everything from his father, rather than learning by rote. Nonetheless it was very demanding. It prevented him from 'having any great amount of intercourse with other boys',8 and from going to university, which Bentham and James Mill dismissed as dominated by the Church. John Mill's sequestered education led him, in about 1826-7, into severe depression - one of the most celebrated nervous breakdowns in English intellectual history. On recovering from depression, Mill remained faithful to the philosophy he had learned from Bentham and his father, but he softened its rigours, and he and the young friends he had by this time made became a second generation of more tolerant, liberal and realistic utilitarians.

Cole did not study Bentham's long and difficult writings - he preferred listening to lectures to hard reading - but he must have absorbed Bentham's views at second hand from John Mill, and have absorbed also Mill's admiration for Bentham. When reading Bentham, Mill recalled, 'I felt taken up to an eminence from which I could survey a vast mental domain'. Every page of Bentham 'seemed to open a clearer and broader conception of what human opinions and institutions ought to be'. Bentham's principle of utility 'fell exactly into its place as the keystone which held together the detached and fragmentary components of my knowledge', and offered a prospect of 'practicable improvement' which 'was sufficiently large and brilliant to light up my life, as well as to give a definite shape to my aspirations'. For Cole, Mill's enthusiasm must have been exhilarating, and the doctrine of utility  that all things should be valued in so far as they contributed to the good of the majority, and that practical efficiency was the prime virtue - must, for a young man of Cole's direct, impatient temperament, have offered a sharp weapon for cutting through the world's muddle.

Inclusion in Mill's circle gave Cole the equivalent of a university education. His work with the records was giving him an academic training of sorts, but hardly the kind of intellectual challenge that Mill's group could provide. Mill, barred by his father from a university education, had instituted for himself a programme of selfeducation, through a study group, started in the winter of 18223. He called this the 'Utilitarian Society', since its main aim was to foster Jeremy Bentham's ideas. It broke up in 1826, but was soon revived. It now \`assembled to the number of a dozen or more. Mr Grote lent a room of his house in Threadneedle Street for the purpose. .. We met two mornings in every week, from half past eight till ten, at which hour most of us were called off to our daily occupations.'10 This group Cole now joined. The members chose for themselves set texts, read them, and discussed them exhaustively, often continuing with the same text from meeting to meeting for several weeks, until they felt they had thoroughly analysed a subject and reached a consensus. They started with political economy, proceeding to syllogistic logic. Cole confided to his diary that he was often out of his depth, and remembered that Whately's Logic was 'diffcult to master'. 11

Within  week t eghim MillsoinviCole  me  the Lon DebaiSciy, which hend s s ha ar out a yr beore. It mt fortnightly during the winter at the Freemasons Tavern, Chancery Lane, and had about 8o members who came regularly, mainly young Benthamite radicals, though there were a few Tory speakers. Mill spoke at almost every meeting and was a good speaker, clear and unemphatic with a resonant voice. Cole was at the meeting on 30 January 1829 when Mill shocked his utilitarian friends by avowing his admiration for the romanticism of Wordsworth. He lent his speech to Cole to encourage him to speak in the debate, but as Cole wrote much later, he 'read it again and again but felt quite incompetent to answer it'.12 On 8 May 1829 Cole opened a debate on his own proposition, 'That the periodicals of this Country are detrimental to its literature'. He admitted that he took his opinions, undigested, from Peacock, and wrote that I practically made a fiasco . . . [and] in the noise of a dinner party over our heads, a most indifferent debate was produced'.13 All the same, this was excellent training for one who was to become an unstoppable controversialist. Mill recorded that, for his part, he valued discussion with his friends because it encouraged in him the habit 'of never accepting half-solutions of difficulties as complete; never abandoning a puzzle, but again and again returning to it until it was cleared up; never allowing obscure corners of a subject to remain unexplored'. 14 Cole too acquired these mental habits.

In addition to discussions in the group, Cole and Mill talked together. Cole's work often took him to the records deposited in the Tower of London, and on the way there he would frequently call on Mill at India House. When the Mill household later moved to Kensington in 1 83 1, Cole used to entertain Mill to tea in his new chambers in the Adelphi. They talked of reason and poetry (5 October 183 1), Shakespeare's characters (19 October), the 'difficulty & even impossibility of translations' (2 November), the principles of government (1 2 November), Mill's own character, as he veered between a utilitarian and a mystic, a logician and a sentimentalist (23 November), despotism and revolution (5 March 1832), the scientific and poetical views of life (16 May), and so on.15

Cole's relationship with Mill was not merely a matter of intellectual stimulation. Cole very much liked Mill: he recorded that his 'admiration & esteem for his talents encreases each time that I have the pleasure of seeing him'. 16 Mill must have valued Cole, not as an intellectual sparring partner, of which there were plenty in his group, but as a cheering companion. Mill met Cole, it seems, just as he was emerging from his mental crisis. Cole later wrote that 'our acquaintance began about three years after that period in his life', but the exact date of Mill's crisis is uncertain, and since Mill covered it up as best he could, it seems possible that Cole was on hand at its height. At any rate, as Cole recalled, 'John Mill confessed to me that when his feeling of hopelessness of life was most acute he had seriously contemplated suicide'.17

Cole recorded that Mill told him that the reason for the crisis was 'that he thought his education was a failure'. Mill's interpretation of his crisis, expressed later in his Autobiography, was, in essence, that his education had made him both intellectually advanced and emotionally retarded. Feelings, he concluded, 'were the greatest and surest sources of happiness'. But 'my education . . . had failed to create these feelings in sufficient strength to resist the dissolving influence of analysis, while the whole course of my intellectual cultivation had made precocious and premature analysis the inveterate habit of my mind'. As he recovered from his depression, 'the cultivation of the feelings became one of the cardinal points in my ethical and philosophical creed'.18 Whether Cole fully understood Mill's crisis at the time we cannot be sure;

but later (having had the opportunity to read Mill's Autobiography) he assessed his own upbringing in Mill's terms. 'I myself certainly had no consciousness of having been taught to cultivate "the dissolving power of analysis", although I realised that my parents had subjected me (perhaps without deliberate intention) to a cultivation of the feelings.'19

What Cole was able to offer to Mill is not difficult to understand: the friendship of an uncomplicated contemporary. In Cole, Mill found an ordinary young man, who had played and fought with other boys at school, who had explored adolescence in a large family of male and female siblings and cousins, and had been in love. All this Mill had missed during his cloistered and taxing upbringing; he had not had 'an apprenticeship in human relations'.20 Despite acknowledging in his Autobiography that this is what he needed, Mill did not, in that work, include much about personal relationships, for it was meant as a record of his intellectual development. Many of his friends, it has been noted, "get minimal attention'; and Cole, 'with whom he was intimate early and late . . is ignored in the Autobiography'.21 Nonetheless, Mill spent much spare time with Cole over seven or eight years in the late 18 2os and early 1830s.

Cole often joined in Mill's regular Sunday rambles, long walks into the country around London and back. Usually the company consisted of Mill, and his two closest friends, John Arthur Roebuck (plate 9) and George Graham; with Cole, Horace Grant, Edwin Chadwick (plate I 1), who was Jeremy Bentham's secretary, and J. H. Elliott, who became a deputy in the city. Cole remembered the atmosphere of these sessions as 'a virtuous state of disputation & pleasantry', a mixture of "walking, talking and glee'.22 One Sunday the group started out at seven in the morning and walked to the Greyhound at Croydon, where they had 'a substantial breakfast on rump steaks. After breakfast John Mill read aloud the Banquet of the Ancients from Peregrine Pickle, one of the most humourous scenes in the English Language, and a capital aid to digestion at any meal'. After striding along the edge of the Downs 'discussing and laughing the whole time', they reached Keston Common in the evening and had some bread and cheese and beer. They returned to London by way of Chislehurst, and at Dulwich John Mill remarked, 'I feel as if I had walked between forty and fifty miles'.23

These rambles often had a scientific element, for botany was an interest that most of the Mill circle shared. Cole was able to introduce Mill to Dr William Hooker of Kew Gardens (who was Mrs Palgrave's brother-in-law), and they corresponded and exchanged specimens. Mill and Cole both collected plants, and Cole spent many evenings arranging and rearranging his. Mill, who was busier and far more methodical, kept his collection in neat little parcels that he opened only once a year to add new specimens. Geology was another subject that Cole and Mil explored together. On 3 February 1830, 'our conversation was principally on geology of which his knowlege appeared good. There were also many musical evenings, often involving the family of Cole's singing teacher, Kingston, and some visits to art galleries.24

Mill and Cole also shared longer holiday expeditions. For instance, at the end of July 181, Col t o the Lakt  j Milla o Ga.Te w on a walking holiday and were going to call on Wordsworth at Rydal Mount. Mill ket a journal  the tour in which he calls Grant and Cole'my copaniswihut it, perhaps, some of the feeling that was now being aroused in his breast by his new acquaintance with Harriet Taylor.2 Cole's entries in his diary are more matter of c: 'Patterdale Inn must be remembered or its Ale, Trout and Marmalade', and 'the beauty of the girl waiting at the Ale House' at Dockeray.

As he recovered from his depression, Mill found great solace in the poems of Worwort: 'they see to be the very cultureof the feelings, which I was in uet of.26 He evidently imparted his enthusiasm to Cole, who in autumn 1830 confided to his diary remarks such as these: It would be difficult to employ time better than in the Company of Wordsworth's poems and Metaphysical reflection.' 'Let anyone who has a train of melancholy reflections take up a Volume of Wordsworth [which] . . . will effectually change it to one of extreme & almost boundless delight.7 Before setting off for his holiday with Millin 183 1, Cole re-read Wordsworth assiduously.28

It was hard luck on Cole, then, that when Mill and Grant went to call on Wordsworth they did not take Cole with them, and when they dined with the great man Cole was left behind at the Salutation Inn, Ambleside. No wonder he recorded that he 'passed my even[in]g not so agreeably as usual. The rain prevented my drawing and [a group of students were] smoking and drinking at the Inn. This was the only time since I left Town that the situation felt irksome'.29 Evidently Cole had a somewhat subordinate status in Mill's circle.

But Mill enjoyed his company, and next summer, after the excitement of the general election that followed the passing of the Reform Bill they went on a walking holiday together to the Isle of Wight and the New Forest. When they reached Beaulieu,

the clear transparency of the water, together with its smoothness enticed us into a boat, which in our wisdom, or rather I in my wisdom for J. M. tacitly assented thereto, supposed we could easily navigate. The stream was just beginning to flow down and we proceeded glibly enough - and much farther than I deemed right, but my companion who seemed to have no just notion of the probable difficulty of our return continued expressing his desire to descend lower. . . . We in our boat floated down, nearly within view of Bucklers Hard before we deemed it meet to return and then we essayed our ascent up the stream: Difficult indeed was our task and slow, most slow was our progress. Mill was but a tyro in rowing and in addition to the inequality with which he applied his force he so frequently just but skimmed the surface of the water, whereby the pulling on my part shoved the boat first on one side then on the other of the River.

Luckily the owner of the boat came to look for them. Cole prudently noted that 'it is a matter incumbent on all who wish to estimate the beauties of Beaulieu to get into a boat for that purpose, but it must be recollected that a waterman should form one of the party'.30 The two young men  on a level of equality when it came to boating, at any rate  returned to London loaded with watercolours and pressed flowers.

In so far as Cole adopted the philosophy of utilitarianism, it was Mill's 'soft' version to which he leaned. When he refers to utilitarians, it is as a group to which he does not belong. For instance, on reading a review of Tennyson's poems, he comments that 'the Reviewer is a utilitarian & like the rest of that species knows but little of Poetical criticism or Poetry itself'. And, when recording a meeting of the London Debating Society, where utilitarianism was discussed, Cole notes that Charles Buller was 'the only disciple of that system present'.3' Probably he used the term utilitarian primarily to characterise the older generation: Bentham, James Mill, Francis Place, the radical tailor, David Ricardo, the economist, Joseph Hume, MP, Henry Brougham, Albany Fonblanque, Perronet Thompson, John Austin. It was a younger group which surrounded John Stuart Mill.32 We have already met some of them: Edwin Chadwick, who was to become a great reforming civil servant; Horace Grant, the educationist, and J. H. Elliot. Among them were friends whom Mill esteemed for their intellectual prowess: George Graham, 'a thinker of originality and power on almost all abstract subjects', William Ellis, 'an original thinker in the field of political economy', and William Eyton Tooke, "a young man of singular worth both moral and intellectual' 33 There were also young men out to make a mark in politics, for the utilitarians cherished hopes, in the 183os, of forming a political party. Before the Reform Bill, two from the older generation were Members of Parliament: Henry Warburton, a timber dealer turned politician, who entered Parliament in 1826, and Edward Strutt, from 1830. In December 1832, George Grote (plate 8) was returned to the reformed Parliament. Already 38, he was soon acknowledged, owing to his gravity and ability, as chief of the utilitarians in the Commons, but he lacked a flair for leadership and eventually subsided into a life of scholarship. Others who entered Parliament in 1832 were: John Arthur Roebuck, small but handsome, a firebrand orator and always regarded as something of a demagogue; Sir William Molesworth (plate 10), a slightly cranky Cornish landowner, who had become the eighth baronet at the age of 12 and whose social assurance led him to adopt various extreme views; and his fellow Cornishman, the amiable and amusing Charles Buller. Charles Villiers, a scion of an aristocratic political family, followed in 183 5. These men hoped to apply Bentham's views in the world at large. Bentham was over 7, writing little, seeing very few people, and quite outside politics. The Mills, while promoting his ideas, were also outside politics, as they held official positions with the East India Company. But the utilitarian MPs might make Benthamism a political reality.

It was inevitable that this group would enlarge Cole's political consciousness. This had already been aroused by the debates at the London Debating Society. The subjects discussed included literature, as we have seen, and philosophy ('Whether Logic is more curious than useful', 'Whether Metaphysics are practically useful'); and some lighter topics ('Whether chivalry has been beneficial or pernicious'). Very often, however, they were political issues: 'That the Wellington Administration is entitled to the confidence of the Country', 'The Benefits of Church Establishment', 'The influence of Aristocracy upon Morals and Manners is pernicious', 'That the country is on the eve of some political change and that unless concession be made by the Aristocracy a struggle is to be expected', 'The Ballot'.34 Cole would have been well briefed, through the debates and other contacts in the Mill circle, on the issues which particularly pre-occupied the radicals in the period before the Reform Act of 1832: the need for regular parliamentary elections, the enlargement of the franchise, and the secrt ballot. There is little sign from his diaries that he was a political activist. His spare time was divided between self-improvement (through reading, and attending lectures and debates) and the enjoyment of culture (plays, opera, art galleries), with preference given to the latter. When, however, the House of Lords defeated Lord Grey's Reform Bil in October 183 1, he rushed out indignantly, like everyone else, and worked for a National Political Union.

After the Reform Act of 1832, the utilitarians in Parliament had high hopes of increasing their influence. Though they often supported the Whig ministry they ostentatiously sat with the opposition in the House of Commons. They proved, however, to be too individualistic to make a cohesive group, and they were disappointed in their preferred leader, Lord Durham, known as 'Radical Jack' They did adopt a distinguishing name: 'philosophic radicals' The epithet 'philosophic' was chosen by John Stuart Mill to indicate that the group carefully thought out their policies from fundamental principles, in the manner of philosophers, 'who, when they are discussing means, begin by considering the end, and when they desire to produce effects, al easily be ridiculed. Macaulay described Mills followers as

persons who, having read little or nothing, are delighted to be rescued from the sense of their own inferiority by some teacher who assures them that the studies they have nglected are of no value, puts five or six phrases into their mouths, lends them an odd number of the Westminster Review, and in a month transforms them into philosophers. 36

It myha  hial h Mil ted Cole.

The political aims of the philosophic radicals were based on their equal distaste for both the Whigs and the Tories. They regarded both groups of politicians as 'aristocratic factions', as 'two sets of scramble-mongers, one to grasp and the other to keep the victuals of office'.37 They saw themselves, on the other hand, as champions of that 'greatest number' whose 'greatest happiness' was to be sought, and whom they characterised as 'The People'. The conflict between Aristocracy and People would emerge, the philosophic radicals thought, as the dominating issue in politics. 'The problem will then be reduced to its simplest terms', said J. S. Mill. 'Who is for the aristocracy and who for the people, will be the plain question.'38 There was a moment in 1836 when the philosophic radicals thought they held the balance of power and might achieve a political breakthrough,39 though this prospect soon vanished. Another tempting prospect was that the radicals would absorb the Whigs and defeat the Tories.40 Neither did this come to pass.

Though the political manoeuvrings of the philosophic radicals were probably not of intense interest to Cole, it is not difficult to see how their political attitudes may have stood him in good stead later. When he eventually became a reforming civil servant, his success must have been greatly assisted by an ability to hold to a radical, personal agenda, while distrusting, and keeping at a distance, both the main political parties. Cole always regarded himself as an advocate for the people, though this does not mean that he, any more than his friends, saw himself as supporting those on the very lowest levels of society. Roebuck remarked that the lowest classes were no better than the highest: 'they are both . . . idle, profligate, rapacious . . . . There is . . . very little difference, except in outward manner, garb, and language, between a gambling, horse-racing, profligate rich man, and a thieving, drinking, gambling beggar'.41 It was in the virtuous mechanic that Roebuck placed his trust, and likewise Cole, in his cultural politics, always tried to advance the interests of the artisan. In various ways, then, the politics of the philosophic radicals helped Cole to get a sense of the political landscape through which, in the longer term, he was to cut his path.

The Mills worked in the world of ideas rather than in the political struggle, and their preferred means was the writing of (unsigned) articles in periodicals. 'Journalism is to modern Europe what political oratory was to Athens and Rome', said John Mill.42 This led to their involvement with a monthly journal, the Westminster Revicw, founded in 1824 to serve as the house magazine of the philosophic radicals. 'The need of a Radical organ to make head against the Edinburgh and Quarterly (then in the period of their greatest reputation and influence)'43 had impressed itself so forcibly on Bentham that he provided the finance necessary to set up the review. It was edited by John Bowring, a disciple of Bentham, who was to have an interesting, if erratic, career. Although it caused a sensation at its first appearance, as John Mill conceded,44 it lost momentum, and Mill severed his connection with it in spring 1828. Cole contributed to it  what might appear to be a dry piece of constitutional history, 'The Parliaments of our ancestors' in 1834, turns out to have a mocking radical sting as Cole shows how the current House of Commons was as constricted as the Black Hole of Calcutta. And, very briefly, he edited it in 1 840.

Whatever the influence of the philosophic radicals in shaping Cole's attitudes in the long term, one of the main short-term rewards of moving in their circle was friendship with Charles Buller (plate 7). It was in January 1830, Cole noted, that there took place 'an event which may be said to have affected my career, and greatly influenced all my subsequent public life': his introduction to Charles Buller in John Mill's room at India House.45 Buller, like Mill, was two years older than Cole. He had just finished at Cambridge and was about to become a Member of Parliament for one of the rotten boroughs in Cornwall that his family controlled. As a boy, he had been tutored by Thomas Carlyle who met him again in London at about this time and wrote in a letter to his mother that 'Charles is grown a great tower of a man, six feet three in height, a yard in breadth, shows great talent and natural goodness, which I hope he will by-andby turn to otableacunt In a letter ohi roer Caryle wroe hathe men he saw in London, Buller was his chief favourite; that he was 'really a fine sprightly clear and genial young man; . . . with his gracefullest ease of manners, a very look that bespeaks confidence and respect from you. If his health were good, which unfortunately it is not, I should prophesy of Buller that he would do more good in Parliament than any other man in iCarlyle always enjoyed good conversation, but when Mill and Buller came to see him together he wrote of 'a pleasant forenoon call of five hours and a hal.4 Buller would son come to Cole' rescue in the first big reverse of his life.

The influence of John Mill and the philosophic radicals upon Cole was critical in forming his views and sharpening his criticism of the world. One area of human activity on which they had advanced views was birth control.49 The rapid expansion of the population and the possibility that there would be insufficient food to feed all the hungry mouths exercised many, not least Thomas Malthus. The utilitarians looked for practical expedients, and favoured the limitation of conception. This subject, however, was virtually unmentionable. One writer on the subject, Henry Bickersteth, issued his thoughts under the prim title, Medical Hints for the use of Clergymen (1829). Among the utilitarians, Francis Place was principal spokesman, in his book, Illustrations and Proofs of the Principle of Population (1822), and, more accessibly, through popular broadsheets (addressed to 'The Married', both 'in Genteel Life' and 'of the Working People'), which were distributed free in huge numbers. John Mill became concerned with the problem, and at the age of 18 (when his practical experience of contraception was probably non-existent) was arrested, with a friend, for dropping leaflets into the areas of houses for servant girls to read  an incident which was hushed up.50

The problem of contraception intrigued Cole, especially as his thoughts turned to marriage. He married his cousin Marian Bond on 28 December 1833, and, about a year before, consulted his friend William Peacock about birth control. William, 'astonished' to be asked, suggested that he mixed

3 parts bitter aloes, I part nutmeg or cloves both reduced to a fine powder. Moisten the powder suffciently to admit of its being formed into pieces of this shape & size [drawing of a torpedo-shaped pessary about an inch long]. Aloes & spices together in that situation are of so stimulating a quality that one or two applications will cause the usual apperanc  the catamei & wih them the iharg lsuc juia would be a future foetus.51

Cole was evidentlyatied with this advice, but after he hadmari, Peacck, wi ing from the heat of India, warned him that it might not work:

In a cold climate like that of England people are apt to sleep confoundedly close to their wives . . . we shall see. But how the deuce could you have the face to tell Marian of your intentions on that point? . .. What shall you do with my pills, surely Marian would scratch your eyes out if she had the slightest suspicion of the use they are put to. Take care how you propose such a thing to any woman, for the whole sex are peculiarly toucy n any thig relating to thecov way as Sere call i, & there is o i but you might get a pill crammed down your throat.§2

## Henry persisted, and two months later Peacock wrote:

I cannot tell you how much I laughed when you told me of your sitting up after midnight preparing my anti-conception pills. But I doubt very much whether you would gain Marian's consent to their application, & in the second place I doubt still more whether you could apply them successfully, at least until you had paid particular attention to the anatomy of that part of the body. In this part of the world women are so cheap that you might soon become acquainted with every hole & corner for a mere trifle, but in England I doubt whether you could easily find a woman so entirely shameless as to allow you the necessary degree of examination.3

The contrast between the utilitarians' high-minded concern about population control, and the practical, earthy, indeed waggish, tone of the correspondence of Cole and Peacock hardly needs underlining. Whatever methods Henry and Marian may have used, their first child was not born until June 1837. After that, however, they went on to have eight more children.

Cole's debt to Mill and his circle seems to have encompassed almost all of life, from the most basic human urges to the highest flights of mental activity. Cole must, then, have brought a utilitarian perspective to bear on his own life. The Record Commission was just the sort of organisation that the philosophic radicals most abhorred, for it was run by members of the Tory aristocracy and wasted huge sums of the people's money. Cole was soon to bring utilitarian reformism to bear on it.

# PART TWO: 'A CLEVER AGITATOR'

CHAPTER 3

1830-1838

Wr year, from the te when he joned rave i 18.At frst hi work, aey scholarly and not too demanding, left him opportunity to develop his social life. But crisis.

On the accession of William IV in 1830, a new Record Commission was appointed, consisting of 25 members, headed, as before, by the Archbishop of Canterbury. It included some historians and scholars, but also politicians, peers, a bishop and two judges. Cole thought it too large a group. At least, so he said in retrospect. 'Its members were numerous. The first principle for securing good administration by concentrated responsibility had not then been recognized as necessary to effective government, although Jeremy Bentham had been preaching the doctrine of "single-seated responsibility" for years.'1 Single-seated responsibility - the allocation of administrative authority to identifable, accountable individuals, rather than to fluctating boards and committees  was to become Cole's favourite Benthamite doctrine, which he propounded relentlessly. It was from his experience of the Record Commission that he learned its importance.

The new commission did include some reformers. One was Edward Protheroe, jun., MP, who was to become an ally of Cole. More significant, initially, was the new chairman of the commission, Henry Brougham, the famous reforming lawyer, recently appointed Lord Chancellor. At the first meeting of the board of the new commission, an attempt was made to re-appoint John Caley as secretary, but Brougham pushed through the appointment of his own nominee, Charles Purton Cooper. Cooper was a barrister of about 4o, with a busy practice at the Chancery Bar; an enthusiast for legal reform, and a writer of pamphlets. It was through his writing that he had met Brougham, who had introduced him into the circle at Holland House. Cooper had no professional knowledge of archives, but regarded this as an advantage. He claimed: 'I believe it was the circumstance that I did not possess a knowledge of the ancient records that induced the board to force upon me (for Lord Brougham forced upon me) the office of secretary rather than a record man; I was appointed for the purpose of checking the zeal of those lovers of ancient records.'2 He also wrote that 'the condition on which I accepted the office of Secretary was, that its duties should be made in all respects secondary and subordinate to my professional avocations'.3 Notwithstanding this detached attitude, Cooper began by trying to make reforms. He was scandalized by the amounts of money spent by the commission, and at the first opportunity brought all its publications to a stop, and arranged to sell off old stock. Unwanted volumes were sold by weight at $_ { 4 } d .$ a pound, and the book collector, Sir Thomas Phillips, bought two hackney coaches full.4

Cooper was soon to become Cole's new master. After nine years of 'servitude' working as a clerk to Palgrave, Cole was restless. While Palgrave received at least £1,ooo a year (£5o,ooo at present rates),5 Cole was still being paid £125 a year, though he was working as an editor rather than as a mere transcriber. His horizons had widened, for away from the office he moved easily among aristocratic and radical young Members of Parliament and he had the friendship of John Stuart Mill and Thomas Love Peacock. Palgrave, for all his high income, would not pay him more in his present post.6 On 16 April 1832, Thomas Hardy, who was collating the Parliamentary Rolls for the board, and was by now a friend of Cole's, suggested to Cole that he might like to work with him at the Tower. The next day they called on Cooper (Cole meeting him for the first time), who took him on as a clerk under the Record Commission at a salary of £ 1 5o. Cole wrote that 'Mr Palgrave did not half like my leaving him under such circumstances',7 for Cole was Palgrave's most experienced clerk.8

Thomas Hardy had disliked Palgrave since the early 182os, when, as one of his clerks, he had been in his power. Now they quarrelled seriously over the way Palgrave interfered with Hardy's work. At the end of the week in which Cole gained his promotion, he was present at the Tower during an altercation between Palgrave and Hardy that boiled over into fisticuffs. Palgrave, Cole recorded, 'got a thump of the face for his pains'. Palgrave's position under the commission, as an almost independent editor, was insecure under the new regime, for Cooper was trying to dislodge him. In June a committee was appointed to try to curb Palgrave's excesses, but it was made up of his friends, and had little effect.10 Cole continued to be wary of Sir Francis  who was knighted in 1832 - and gave vent to his antagonism in his diary. 11

Cole's new master, Cooper, found himself 'so vigorously opposed by five or six of the Commissioners that he repeatedly stated his determination either to perform his duty or to resign his situation' 12 In fact, he did not resign. For the commission chiefly consisted (apart from Cooper's few opponents) of busy people who had no time or energy for it, and Cooper took advantage of their lack of interest to do what he thought fit. He did his best to control 'those lovers of ancient records', and became more and more high-handed in his treatment of the commission's staff.

Cole, having been taken on by the commission, began to work at the secretary's chambers on the  February 1833, transcribing a parliamentary roll. Cooper's chambers were in New Boswell Court, just north of Theobald's Road, and he had had them nicely fitted up at the expense of the commission, calling the work 'repairs'.13 Later in February, Cole's cousin Richard Bond left Christ's Hospital, and joined Cole in his work. When the weather became a little warmer in the middle of March, they were able to begin work at the King's Mews, a storehouse that had previously been 'the stables and riding-school of Carlton-house, in which the Princess Charlotte used to delight herself with horse exercise'. Cole described this 'huge barn' 'the inside of this building presents a series of dark alleys  the sides of which are faced with Records ri e thirty  .I a eakehi. Ter hae terribly affected by damp and neglect: 'some were in a state of inseparable adhesion to the stone walls . . . many were in the last stages of putrefaction ... others were so cgulate together that they could not bencoileSir seven perfec ske rat weeWi asway vtanhrrih r gais e uh ,rC set about putting these records in order.15

Cooper had told Cole that in addition to his salary, now £ 5o a year, he would be able to earn another £15o by extra work, writing reports for the agenda of the board. 16 Cole waited a few weeks before asking for a letter from the board formalising his employment. He also suggested to the secretary that as he was doing the work  an editor, he ught to be paid as such. Thismae Cooer xcngly wroth' 18 Although Cole had escaped from Palgrave, he did not find Cooper an easy person to work for, especially since Cooper seemed disinclined to provide Cole with any definite statement of his terms and conditions of work. From now on, Cole was in an increasingly bitter struggle with Cooper. This must have been a formative experience, which caused him to discover and develop the tough side of his character that was to make him such a formidable controversialist and organiser. It is therefore worth following in some detail what might otherwise be dismissed as a tiresome office quarrel.

Cole made an alliance with the reforming commissioner, Edward Protheroe. Soon after his appointment to the Record Commission, Protheroe had a period of illness, but by April 1833, having been elected MP for Halifax, he was back in London and at his parliamentary duties. Throughout May, he and Cole met almost daily. Cole helped Protheroe with reports for the board, and Protheroe advised Cole to tone down the letters of complaint that he was sending to Cooper. Cole discussed his troubles with the Hardy brothers constantly, as well as with Peacock, Grant, Elliott and Chadwick. From Mill in particular he derived 'sympathy and counsel'. 19 In thus involving others in his troubles Cole was beginning to exercise his talents as a networker and agitator.

On 1 May 1833 Cole, while keeping his own office at the King's Mews, went to work in the Augmentation Office, Westminster, which contained the records of the Court of Augmentation, set up by Henry VII at the time of the dissolution of the monasteries to handle his augmented revenue, and amalgamated with the Court of Exchequer in 1554. This store, containing 'extremely important and valuable' records, 20 was in a house near the south-west corner of Westminster Hall, and overlooked New Palace Yard. Next to it was Bellamy's Chop House, which politicians and lawyers treated as a club. The kitchen fires, where the famous steaks, chops and pork pies were cooked, were a constant worry to successive Record Commissions, but nothing was done. Though the storehouse was 'sufficiently airy and spacious', no one slept there and the doors only had 'common locks'.21 Protheroe wrote a report for the board on 22 June 1833:

These rooms had long been in a filthy state; it is possible that when the operation of cleansing the Records commenced, the rooms may have been so encumbered as to render order and neatness scarcely attainable; but it has long been unnecessary to leave the Records on the floor or to permit the workmen to tread them under foot when engaged in picking out those which might merit repair; and it was slovenly to leave heaps of dirt in the chimnies [sic], to be blown over the bound books and cleaned Records every time the doors were opened.22

Despite these dismal conditions, Cooper would not allow Cole to employ a cleaner.

Out of office hours, Cole was writing reports for Cooper that were printed in the agenda of the board, but by June he realised that Cooper was not going to pay him for this extra work. With the help of Protheroe, he wrote to him on 28 June. He reminded him of his promise, and also asked again what were his exact conditions of employment, and whether he was employed by Cooper personally, or by the commission. Cooper replied the next day, with notable insouciance.

You were lately a clerk of Sir Francis Palgrave, at a salary, as well as I recollect, of I 25l. a year. You now fll a similar situation under the Secretary, but your salary has been raised to I 5ol. per annum. What is the precise authority that the Secretary may possess over you, and the other clerks in a similar situation, I will not pretend to determine, having never given the subject a thought, nor do I apprehend circumstances are likely to arise that will render a decision of this question necessary; and if it be of any importance to you, permit me to observe, you should have mentioned it at the time when I engaged you.23

With the help of Mill and Protheroe, Cole wrote several more letters to Cooper, always with no satisfactory result. Supported by his friends and feeling that he was in the right, he began to collect this correspondence and turn it into a pamphlet entitled Record Commission. A letter addressed to the Right Honourable the Speaker of the House of Commons, as chairman of the Commission on Public Records, on the conduct of C. Purton Cooper . . . and the general management of the Commission, which he published after matters came to a head in December 1835.

Meanwhile, he kept Charles Buller up to date with his troubles,24 and had meetings with Thomas Hardy at the Tower to discuss the behaviour of 'that outrageous jobber Cooper'.25 He was asked by the radical MP, Henry Warburton (nicknamed 'Philosopher Warburton'), to tell him of the developing turmoil in the record service, and thereore 'rode into Town in his carriage or the purpose o communicating with him upon the Record Commission'.26

The increasingly tense situation was unexpectedly defused by Cooper, perhaps because he was well aware of Cole's friendships among the young radicals in Parliament. On 16 August, he asked Cole to call on him, and, as Cole later said, 'to my very great surprise, he told me he thought it was for the good of the public that I should make at least 3ool. a year; that although the board had determined I should make only I 5ol., he was satisfied that the interest of the public required that I should make at least 3ool. a year'.27 Although Cooper thus offered Cole more money, he still did not clarify his terms and conditions, and Cole's opinion (recorded in his diary entry for that day) was that Cooper had 'tried to bamboozle me with offers of increased salary'. In another conversation three weeks later, Cooper 'renewed in substance the previous conversation only tincturing it with more examples of selfish lying & vanity'. 28

In early September Cooper went to Bognor, and, with Parliament in recess, the early autumn was a quiet time for Cole. He allowed himself a few jaunts, such as the visit he made with Grant to see the steam boats intended for the Ganges that were beig built at Maudsleys'yard. In the evenings he was transcribing an early thirt-u ol  oe and e tok it wi him vs T e was courting his first cousin Marian Bond. She was governess to three little girls called Maitland, who lived at Loughton Hall, not far from Epping. Though the Maitlands were an established 'county' family, providing magistrates and high sheriff for the locality, Cole was not impressed by the little girls' father, whom he described as 'a hasty bluff good humoured, not very cultivated or informed person. A modern specimen of antient John Bull, hating the French, Up for the King and so forth'.29 Marian brought her charges to see her mother and Henry, and they all 'passed the day very blithesomely & happily. The children enjoyed themselves with glorious boisterousness'.30

On 21 October Protheroe came back from the country, and he and Cole resumed their almost daily meetings. Protheroe had had his own rows with Cooper  notably about a report that he had written but Cooper had changed. It was Protheroe who discovered that Cooper's chambers had been done up at the commission's expense, and that he did not keep proper accounts. He became 'very inveterate against the Sec[retar]y threatening to cane him & calling him a "Beast". 31 Cooper was well aware of Protheroe's antipathy, and wrote to one of the commissioners a couple of years later: 'Mr P's interference has been productive of great mischief and [departments] which had begun to assume a form of order are again involved in the greatest confusion. Some remedy must be found for this or it will be useless to attempt to continue successfully the operations of the Commission.'32

At the end of October Cooper wrote Cole 'an extremely polite letter'33 and, when Cole called on him on 2 November, he 'received me with great politeness and deference'. Four days later he 'strived to be humourous and civil'. It may have been to disarm Cole's opposition that Cooper put him in charge of the Augmentation Office in the middle of the month. The board was anxious not to increase the salaries of the junior staff, and Cooper reassured Protheroe on this point: 'You are quite wrong in supposing that I have increased Mr. Cole's salary, or given him any emolument inconsistent with what passed at the Board. Sir Robert Inglis and the other members of that Board objected only to an increase of salary, but not to any additional emolument arising out of transcribing done out of office-hours.

Although Cole distrusted Cooper, his prospects seemed to have improved. Perhaps that was why, after a 'boisterously merry' and 'very jovial' Christmas with his cousins at Epping, Henry married Marian at St Martin-in-the-Fields on 28 December 1833. Henry's preparations for the wedding, apart from going to hear the banns read, consisted of buying a 'jug for Toast & Water I 6'. The marriage was kept a close secret. Marian's sister Laura chaperoned her to London, but they did not reveal the event even to Lindsay, who came to see them in the evening. Marian went back to the Maitlands, presumably concealing the marriage because she wanted to keep her job for a while.

She eventually came to live with Henry at the end of March, as posts were re-allocated in the record service. On 27 March John Caley, still keeper of the records at the Chapter House, Westminster, died, and Palgrave, whose edition of Parliamentary writs now came to an end, took responsibility for the Chapter House at a salary of £ I,ooo, the amount he had previously received as a sub-commissioner. Cole was now officially appointed as temporary keeper at the Augmentation Office. He told Lindsay that the new post 'probably . . . will lead to more important results and be the means of conducting a better Salary to me. The post happens to be one which is not worth making a job of & thus it is likely I shall have no opponent. I have the keys in my possession & that is much & I do not intend to relinquish them without strugglin

Cole's work was now arranging and cataloguing the collections of records at both the Kings Mews and the Augmentation Office. He was also editing one of the Record Commission's folio volumes entitled Selections from the Miscellaneous Records of the Office of the King's Remembrancer of the Exchequer, but doing it after office hours and being paid as a mere transcriber or clerk, rather than as an editor. Cooper told him not to mention 'editorship' when he promised to pay him £1oo for extra work, 36 while Cole felt justified in calling himself a sub-commissioner. This title was very important to the status and self-esteem of the officers of the Record Commission: to be a clerk was to be on the lowest rung of the career ladder. Both Hardy and Cole had been clerks when they began their work, but after so many years of experience in transcribing and then editing they both considered themselves on a par with Sir Francis. Cole's new authority allowed him to reduce the fee to see a record to 8s. 8d. instead of the £3. 3s. od. that had been charged by Caley, and he allowed a free search of the indexes.37 Caley's library was sold by Hodgsons in July in a nine-day sale. Cole went to watch the indexes of the Augmentation Office sold. They were not bought by the Record Commission, but went to Sir Thomas Phillips for £230.

Cooper continued to be friendly, and encouraged Cole to write a pamphlet calling for reform of the exchequer records. Cole produced and distributed this briskly in May.38 Here he first hit his form as a controversialist. Though the pamphlet had a bafingly ironical title,3 the text was boldly, even foridly, polemical. In ncouraig Cole in pamphleteering, Cooper was helping his young colleague to sharpen a weapon that Cole would soon use against him.

In late July, Henry and Marian made a delayed wedding journey to Cornwall. Cole may have intended to follow in the footsteps of John Mill, whose journal of a walking tour in Cornwall he read before setting out.40 Henry and Marian travelled by coach (outside) to Portsmouth and by steamer to Plymouth. They toured the coast, covering about to miles a day mainly on foot, sometimes getting a lift in a cart. The weather was good and only one day was spoilt by rain. They came back from St Ives by steamer to Bristol, and then by coach to London. They reached home on 1 September, having spent in total £26. 3s. 1d. Apart from this holiday, they visited their relations at Epping, and made Sunday expeditions with members of the philosophic radical circle - Grant, Elliott and Chadwick. Also in this circle were Samuel and William Hickson, with whose parents Henry and Marian spent 12-14 October at their home at Fairseat, near Sevenoaks in Kent. Cole found the family 'particularly remarkable for their great simplicity and their benevolence'.41 They were radicals and shoe manufacturers, and the sons gave up their business to follow political and reforming careers. William, in particular, was a friend of Henry's, and shared his interest in music and education.

L n e o lhT 16 October 1834. Marian and Henry were sitting in their chambers in Adelphi after inner when seve a reat hn vey rs Per Paul the workmen in the record service, came to fetch Cole as the Houses of Parliament were on fire. The House of Lords had caught alight and the fire had taken hold with alarming speed. A south wind was blowing the flames towards Westminster Hall and the Augmentation Office. When Cole arrived, Protheroe was already there. He had happened to be passing very soon after the fire began, and he had broken down the office door and was throwing the records out of the windows. Soldiers and policemen helped carry them over the road to the parish church of St Margaret's. Luckily at about 8 o'clock the wind changed to the west, and the fire, though it destroyed the House of Commons, did not reach the record repositories. Some of the records, however, were inevitably trampled underfoot and soaked with water, which was knee deep in the street. On the morning after the fire, Cole was at the church before eight, putting the records into sacks and arranging for them to be dried before taking them back across the road to the office.

The upheaval caused by the fire gave Cole the chance to re-organise the office. He sorted the records chronologically, and had presses made to hold them. Caley's indexes having been sold, Cole had to make new ones. For the extra work that he did during 1834, Cole sent in a bill for £220, which, with his salary of £ 1 50 brought him up to $\mathscr { L } _ { 3 } 7 0 , \mathscr { L } _ { 7 0 }$ more than Cooper had promised him. However, this sum was paid, and later the accounts were audited without questions being asked. Cole's total income for that year was £424. 13s. 1d. (say, £21,ooo in today's terms), and he was able to invest $\mathscr { L } _ { 5 0 }$ on the Stock Exchange through his cousin Samuel Bilke. The extra income came from fees at the Augmentation Office (at 8s. 8d. a document), and small sums from his articles in magazines and newspapers. He was still writing reviews of plays, exhibitions and operas, many of which appeared in The Examiner. He always praised the music of Marielli, an accomplished composer of drawing-room songs, for she was his friend Mrs Kingston, whose husband had given him singing lessons.

Henry, Marian, Lindsay and Laura, with Mrs Bond, spent Christmas 1834 with the Webbs at Epping. With Charlotte they saw the New Year in at a dance given by the Hicksons. Outside the family circle, Cole was consolidating his friendships with the philosophic radicals, and at this time he also got to know Thomas Carlyle. He and Marian were invited to tea in the evenings at the Carlyles' house in Chelsea. Mrs Carlyle called them 'niceish talkative people',42 and she was grateful to Marian for helping her to find a servant.43

After a busy first half-year in 1835, Cole sent in an enormous bill in August, totalling nearly £ 5oo for the extra work he had done, partly as a result of the fire and partly to provide indexes to the random records bound up by Caley, besides the usual evening work on individual records. This caused relations with Cooper to deteriorate again. Cooper did not deign to answer himself, but had his clerk, George Smith, write to Cole. 'I return you the account, which I have shown to Mr. Cooper, who was very angry with it. He says the amount claimed by you for work during extra hours, is more than double what he intimated you could receive, and that it would make up your emoluments higher than any other in the employ of the board, except Sir F. Palgrave, which, he says is quite out of the question.' Cole replied with a polite letter, meekly speaking of his feelings of delicacy in mentioning his remuneration and continuing, 'I shall be happy to have any conversation with you, and to be corrected in any of my errors, if you will allow me to speak openly on the subject'.44 Cooper tetchily answered: 'Last year your total remuneration was about 3ool., and I never dreamt of its exceeding that sum in the present year. . . . The claim you have made I consider as putting an end to any arrangement that may have been come to as to your spare time, which you will consider your own until the matter can be brought before the board.'45

Cole consulted Protheroe, who reassured him that the board already knew about his extra work and approved of his being paid for it. Cole's resolve to stand up to Cooper was strengthened by the trouble that Thomas Hardy was also having with him. After many years working at the Tower Record Office and as a Record Commission editor, Hardy considered himself a sub-commissioner, and indeed Cooper had called him by this title frequently, both in conversation and in writing.46 Thus encouraged, Hardy, who was editing the Close Rolls, asked to be paid extra for the preface. When this was denied, he declined to hand it over, and Cooper thereupon halted work on all projects, 'every one of them, even those on the eve of completion'.47 Hardy did not take this treatment lying down, and wrote 'a rather taunting letter'.48 Then his younger brother William was rude to Cooper. Finally Cooper ended Thomas's editorial employment on 24 August 1835, saying that he himself 'was the editor appointed by the Board, and I [Hardy} was his mere employee'.49 Hardy thought Cooper had no right to dismiss him without the sanction of the board.

In this worsening situation, Cole again wrote to Cooper on 9 November to ask when a board was to be held which would discuss his fees. Two days later Cooper replied:

If I recollect right, I have more than once informed you that, as the Board in 183 2 had refused to allow you any remuneration beyond 1 ol. a year, all that you have received beyond that has been paid on my individual responsibility, not only without the sanction of the Board, but in contravention of its order. I decline to incur this responsibility any longer, and I make bold to state that your conduct has been such that you cannot be surprised at my determination.50

Cooper was trying to imply here that he had been personally favouring Cole, and had now withdrawn his favours; but he only succeeded in making the damaging statement that as secretary to the commission he had acted against its instructions. o ar which acknowledged the arrangement that Cooper now tried to disown. Protheroe trie to protect Cole and Thomas Hardy.As he wrote to one of the sub-comissione  ths tim ih re to Mrol  m o t wa wi jeal undeserved position'.52

Cole's diaries for 1835 and 1836 are missing, presumed destroyed either by Cole or his family. As the disputes in the record service became more heated, Cole doubtlerecordedlngs that were not thought sitable or iscosure t psy. Although everything in Cole's life conveys an atmosphere of unruffled geniality up to about 1833, he was now fghting with ever increasing determination, and even anger. In early December 183 5, in the office, he was shown the preface to a record publication, written by one of his junior colleagues, Frederick Devon. Here Devon praised

Cooper, and when Cole read this the years of frustration that he had suffered caused him to lose his head and let fly with a most ill-judged outburst. Several other people were in the room and one of them bore tales to Cooper, who summoned Devon to verify the story, and then seized his opportunity to dismiss Cole.53

Cole fought back. After taking counsel's opinion, he wrote to Cooper on7 December, declining to accept dismissal, and disclosing that he had already prepared an attack of his own upon Cooper, in his pamphlet, A letter to the Speaker, which wa r  t.He ro gai ollg y,pg tha had exceeded his powers, to which Cooper replied: 'The principal and immediate cause of my dismissing you was disparaging language used by you in speaking of the Board, and grossly insulting and slanderous words spoken of myself.'55 Cole's answer, on 14 December, while demanding chapter and verse from Cooper, was evasive about his own contribution to the quarrel:

I conceive, though greatly provoked by your unjustifiable conduct towards me, and your general management of the Commission, by which you have seriously affected both public and private interests, I have never used languageunjust or unbecoming: and now, Sir, after that condemnation which you have taken upon yourself to pass upon me, I again request to know the charge upon which you assume the right to condemn me, the precise language attributed to me, the names of the witness or witnesses, and the time and place of the alleged occurrence.56

Finally Cooper wrote to him enclosing a letter that he had persuaded Devon to write, ebleursThir, il isc e grounds upon which I have dismissed you'.58

Cole was not allowed an interview with the board, and when it met on 18 December, it found against him. It was a large meeting, and many of those present did not know what was going on. They concluded that Cole and Hardy were not employed directly by the board and therefore could not be sub-commissioners. A committee was appointed to discuss their claims for fees. The board also 'Ordered that in future the engagement of all persons employed in the service of the Board shall as far as possible be defined in writing specifying the nature of the service and terms of remuneration'.59 Perhaps encouraged by this attempt to regularise Cooper's management of the staff, Protheroe moved to have Cole appointed a sub-commissioner and for him to continue his work at the Augmentation Office, but the board would have none of it. As Protheroe said later, 'The question of Mr Cooper's authority seems alone to have been discussed by the board. At least, the contents of the memorials affecting the characters and livelihoods of the cleverest and most efficient gentlemen in our service did not undergo that attention, consideration and discussion which I thought and contended they deserved'.6" The young clerks who were connected with Cole and Hardy were also dismissed. Cole's cousins Edward and Richard Bond, together with William Hardy, all lost their jobs.

A committee to settle the fees met on 2 1 December 183 5, when Cole and Hardy were present, as were Cooper and his clerk. Cole was given his arrears of salary and a further £127. 13s. od. to make it up to £300o for the period that he had worked under Cooper. He was also given 8ogns for the extra work he had done after the fire at the Houses of Parliament. Hardy's claim was sympathetically received but not settled.

Cole later recorded that he 'received a message from Lord Brougham, through Mr. Protheroe, telling me that the Commissioners would consider my complaint favourably at a future time if I would only be quiet'. 61 But he would not be quiet. As soon as he was dismissed, he distributed his pamphlet and published another, A second letter . . . to the Speaker of the House of Commons . .. on the conduct of C. Purton Cooper . . ., in which he said:

Mr. Cooper well knew how entirely I condemned his mismanagement of the Commission from the day it was taken into his hands . . .. He knew that a parliamentary inquiry was suspended over his proceedings; - he knew the danger of my evidence; - he knew, too well, the humiliation he had undergone in his endeavours to compromise and silence my disapprobation of his proceedings; - he knew that I would not suppress my testimony, and therefore sought to damage its character by a manoeuvre of dismissal.62

Cole's pamphlets were defiant, cocksure productions. He had to payor them himself, and could ill afford it.

He was now out of work, with no resources to tide him over this hard time. He may have been free to speak his mind about the iniquities of the commission but that brought in no salary. His chances of being re-employed were slender, given his reputation and his publications. To help out he took in Edwin Chadwick as a lodger in his house in Notting Hill Square (now Campden Hill Square) and borrowed money from him. Chadwick was now secretary to the Poor Law Commissioners and, an ardent and rigid reformer, was hard at work implementing the Poor Law Act of $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 \ : 3 4$ Cole also borrowed from Horace Grant, from his father and from Lindsay. He earned small sums from articles about the Record Commission in the Law Magazine and the Penny Magazine.

John Mill, lending Cole £roo, spoke of 'the honorable & public spirited course' that he had taken. In the tangleof disputes, Cole was on the whole in the right. But what stands out is his sheer, unappeasable persistence. It might now seem that he had fought his battle to a bitter defeat. But he would not give in. He resolved to escalate the fight to the utmost, taking on the entire Record Commission. What had started as a dispute about personal grievances at work now modulated into a programme to reform a public institution.

## \* \*\*\*

Cole secured the support of Charles Buller, who, on 18 February 1836, moved for a select committee of the House of Commons, to inquire into the Record Commission. This was Buller's first important speech, and he told the House of the excesses of the commission in such a way that the members laughed at almost every sentence. He told them of the waste of money, and of the neglect of the records and of the mismanagement  thef.Oe  the oll  the ission a bee t pu itself and its new members by having leaflets printed in foreign languages and sending them all over Europe. He held up a pamphlet in which the secretary was 'immortalized' as 'Viro illustri, excellantissimo, clarissimo, doctissimo C.P. Coopero equiti Ango This brought Roars of laughter' As Charles Greville put it after Buller's death 12 years later, 'by universal acknowledgement he was an admirable speaker, full of matter, lucid, never dull, and generally very amusing. His greatest speeches were on dry and serious subjects, such as colonisation, emigration, or records, none of which became heavy or uninteresting in his hands'.65 A contemporary wrote in 1837: 'His face is like his speaking in Parliament; almost too lively and gay to correspond with our general notions as to the proper degree of dulness which ought to belong to a modern member of the Commons.'66 Buller's charm and grasp of the facts, almost certainly supplied by Cole, did the trick and the House granted him the committee without a division.

At this period the select committee was the government's device to keep the radicals quiet. A committee could easily be granted; and the process of gathering evidence and preparing a report (which could then be quietly buried) kept the complainants busy. The members of the committee examining the Record Commission were all radicals with two exceptions, these being Sir Robert Inglis, a die-hard Tory and a Member for Oxford University, and Charles Williams Wynn, a moderate conservative who sat for Montgomeryshire. They were both members of the Record Commission. Williams Wynn was a Welshman, a scholar and the first president of the Asiatic Society. Cole said he was 'very sore at the fun which Mr Buller had made of the Commission's proceedings'. And no wonder, as Buller had singled him out to mock his title in Portuguese: 'O muito nobre Carlos Watkins Williams Wynn'.67 The other members of the committee were Benjamin Hawes, Thomas Spring Rice, Charles Villiers, Thomas Wyse, John Jervis, Philip Pusey, Sir Matthew Ridley, Sir Charles Lemon, Sergeant Henry Goulburn, Sir William Molesworth, Sir George Clerk and Dr Bowring.

The chairman, Mr. Buller, showed an amount of patience, ability, and diligence which raised him at once into political importance.' At each sitting of the committee, Cole was at Charles Buller's side.68 Buller relied on him and called him 'the attorney for the prosecution'.69 Cole coached the favourable witnesses and suggested who should be called. From the Record Commission staff, Frederick Devon, who had denounced him to Cooper, was not called at all; nor was W. H. Black, who did not like Cole. Few other supporters of the commission were accorded a chance to give evidence.

The committee met first in March and then intermittently during the summer until July. Often the sessions had to be very short, as members were sitting on other select committees that were in progress at the same time, in particular that on arts and manufactures, and that on the condition, management and affairs of the British Museum. Benjamin Hawes and Sir Robert Hawes were on the British Museum and the records committees and were needed at both. Cole pointed out that this led to members 'fluttering about' between committees, instead of concentrating on one.70 Sir William Molesworth fellill early in the summer and hardly attended at all.

The report was adopted on 6 August. It was largely taken up with a history of the different Record Commissions and their shortcomings. As an afterthought it contained the recommendations that one might expect: a demand for a single building to house all the records, a smaller commission, and one person with a small staff to manage it. Almost the only sensible achievement of the commission had been in 1833, when it proposed the building of a Record Office on the Rolls Estate in Chancery Lane, the site on which a unified depository was eventually built.

Cole's part in the proceedings of the committee and in the drafting of the report was well known. In reviewing the report, The Age, a stoutly Tory Sunday newspaper, published a personal attack on him.

The first person mentioned in the margin of the report is a Mr Cole, who ought to be a gentleman of considerable judgement, ability, and weight of character; for the importance of his evidence is such that he is mentioned no less than thirty-three times. . . . But here is Mr Cole, or, as he modestly writes himself 'Henry Cole Esquire' . . . Mr Cole, the discharged clerk of the Secretary of the Commission, is one of the sons of a respectable coal-dealer; in what is called 'a small way'. We should not mention this diamond's parent, but for his son's impudence, or ignorance. . . . Do you stand behind Buller's chair, and prompt him? Et tu Brute! Thou art indeed a black diamond of the first water. . . . Be assured, Sir, that the vain, expensive, and rag-begetting employ of reducing your superiors to your own poor level willnot serve your purposes.71

The Gentleman's Magazine published a review of the report, taking the side of the commission, and urging readers not to 'depend upon the edition of the Report recently published by Ridgway, as a pamphlet, with notes. Those notes are evident emanation from the same, not spirit, but spite, which has before shown itself in other quarters'.72 This is an attack on Cole, who, pamphleteering ever more energetically, had put out his own 'Report', which looked at first sight just like an official publication, but to which he had added many footnotes to further his attacks on Cooper.73 Cole summed up his campaigning in a fierce article ("written with much personal feeling')74 in Fraser's Magazine in February 1837.75

An interesting side issue, relating to the conduct of select committees, evoked yet another pamphlet from Cole. A witness's evidence before a select committee was taken down by a shorthand writer, and then sent to the witness to be corrected before it was printed. Thus it was possible for a witness to alter his answers to give a better slant to his replies. Cole, with his usual thoroughness, checked the answers that had been given and found that Cooper was the worst offender, rewriting not only his answers but the questions put to him as well. So Cole composed and distributed anonymously Remarks on Certain Evils to which the Printed Evidence taken by the Committees of the House of Commons is at present subject, with illustrations furnished by the late inquiry into the Record Commission (1836). This pamphlet caused a stir, and Sir Robert Peel spoke about it in the House of Commons. The way of taking and printing evidence at select committes was changed as a result.76

Cooper joined in the pamphlet war, with a long compilation of 1285 imaginary questions and answers, printed as if they were part of theofficial papers of the commite. In the preac e state that they 'were ram in conormiy with the uo   Lr Baero Rul  c management of Select Committees & preparation of Reports (1837). These efforts could not, however, redeem his public reputation. The press turned against him and even The Age attacked him in a piece of doggerel verse,8 which must have cheered Cole up because he preserved it in his press cuttings book. The commissioners themselves embarked on pamphleteering. They published anonymously as a pamphlet their Observations on the record report, and this gave Buller the chance to tease Mr

Will speeches on 24 February 1837.

The winter of 18367 was an exceptionally hard one with snow on the ground for most of January. There was a bad epidemic of influenza and many of Cole's friends caught it, though he did not. He was busy drafting a bill to establish a General Record Office, and his consultations with Charles Buller and the others had to take pa t heeo wo wereBulJo Jd Jo Mill cumbed. Cole also consulted Edwin Chadwick and Tom Hardy. The final draft was not settled until after Charles Buller had got the House of Commons's permission to bring in the bill. He introduced it for itsfrst reading in ebruary, with the government's backing.

The plotters often met at the Reform Club, which had just been founded to be a meeting place for radicals and Whigs. The prime movers, Sir William Molesworth and Joseph Hume, succeeded in persuading a good number of Whig Members of Parliament to join. Molesworth had high hopes that the club would help to bring about the hoped-for realignment in British politics. 'The effect will be to break up the Whig party by joining the best of them to the radicals, and the club will be the political centre of Europe, and augment our power immensely.'79 The club opened in May 1836, in an eighteenth-century house in Pall Mall altered by George Basevi, with over 200 MPs and 1,ooo ordinary members. Despite his poverty, Cole was one of the founding members and paid the annual subscription of £5 os. od. The members included almost all the MPs who helped Cole, but curiously Charles Buller did not join, though his brother Arthur did. Protheroe joined in 1842. Cole remained a member until that year, and thus enjoyed, if only briefly, the amenities of the Italianate palace, opened in 1841, which Sir Charles Barry designed as a new clubhouse in Pall Mall.

After the first reading of Buller's bill, the government decided to bring in its own Records Bill. Meanwhile, in March 1837, Lord John Russell, the Home Secretary, persuaded the Master of the Rolls, Lord Langdale, to take temporary responsibility for the records. This was a stroke of good fortune for Cole, because the recently ennobled Lord Langdale was Henry Bickersteth, a philosophic radical, and well known to Bentham, James Mill, Grote, Place and other reformers. Originally trained as a doctor (and son of the Bickersteth who wrote on contraception), he changed to the Bar, and rose to become Master of the Rolls and Lord Langdale in January 1836. James Mill commended Cole to Langdale, and on 3o March Buller reported to Cole that Langdale meant to take him and Thomas Hardy on to the staff of the new record establishment he had in mind. Cole was very anxious to get back into the public record service, but short as he was of money, he would never accept a post if it meant apologising to Cooper.

To keep up the pressure for reform, both of the public records and in other areas, Cole and his friends started a weekly newspaper, The Guide.
