'The aim of the Guide will be to advocate the interests of the Many, and to promote the steady and rapid progress of those Reforms in our Institutions which all but the bigotted [sic] and interested Few are determined on effecting'.80 As editor and one of the registered proprietors, Cole had to make himself an expert on all the 'dismal and frivolous formalities of obtaining sureties against libel, and for payment of stamp duties, [and] with all the intricate mysteries of the rights and privileges of newspapers, divided between the Treasury, the Stamp Office, and the Post Office'.81 The arrangements completed, the first issue appeared on 22 April. There were immediate complaints that it was too small, even though it claimed to be, at 3d., the cheapest weekly paper ever printed. It was distinguished by containing Peacock's 'Paper Money Lyrics', which he had written at the time of the financial panic of 1825, and which he allowed Cole to print for the first time. The paper, despite Cole's working all night to get it out on the streets on time, was a failure, lasting only for nine weeks before being sold off to a paper maker. Cole's need for money was now urgent, and Horace Grant offered to lend him £5o. To add to his worries, Marian was expecting their first child, who was a boy, born just as The Guide was failing. Henry was a delighted father, spending whole evenings nursing the child. 'In the Evening about little else than nursing Baby.'82 He was christened Downer Peacock: Downer after the rich uncle, and Peacock ambiguously after Thomas or William.

Help was at hand, for in July Lord Langdale arranged with the board of the Record Commission for Cole and Thomas Hardy to be given some work. He summoned Cole to see him on I 2 August and told him that he was 'authorized to engage me to arrange the Records of the Exchequer of Pleas', a task that Cole began on I 5 August. The older records are deposited in the only remaining portions of the Old Whitehall Palace, converted into a dwelling House wh: was inhabited by Oliver Cromwell. Went there & looked over the work proposed to me.' The house stood in Whitehall Yard, an open space beside the elm trees of the Privy Garden, with a distant view of St Paul's Cathedral beyond the Thames.

In August, Marian, Henry and the baby spent a week's holiday at Greenhithe, taking Mrs Paul with them as nursemaid and travelling down the Thames by the steamers Vesper and Topaz.83 On their return, Cole, enjoying the security of a permanent post again, was bubbling with ideas for new journalistic projects (further discussed in chapter 5). He tried to start an educational newspaper, the Portfolio of Public Instruction; contemplated publishing a penny paper, compiled from other newspapers and journals; and in January 1838 took over as editor of the British Ladies Newspaper.

The Record Bill, however, was at a standstill. The government had undertaken to bring in its own bill and Buller had stood down. In May, a bill was drafted with the advice of Lord Langdale, but, after the death of William IV on 2o June, when the current Record Commission lapsed, the bill languished too. In October Thomas Hardy was desponding and 'expecting Coopers ultimate triumph & advancement'.84 But in Lord Langdale Cole had a friend and a patron, who not only supported him but also understood him. After an interview on 31 October Cole wrote, I found him very affable and counselling against too rough & direct bearing, & that diplomacy was always necessary to effect objects. He said my character was always to give people pokes & that it was too critical. For his own part he did not mind it, but the world in general disliked it'. This long entry in Cole's diary shows that he took Langdale's advice in good part; but the experiences of the last few years had made him much more abrasive, and that was how he continued.

Through the summer of 1837 Cole was working on his frst book, Henry VIII's horickscont  tesoun thee th I based on the records that Cole knew so well. It was published by Charles Knight, who was an important figure in the dissemination of educational and radical ideas. Cole first mentions Knight in his diary in March 1837, and he put the idea of publishing his Scheme of Bishopricks to him on 29 September of that year. After that, they were on friendly terms until Knight's death in 1863. Knight had first set up as a 'miscellaneous' publisher in 1822 but failed. He was rescued when Henry Brougham's Society for the Advancement of Useful Knowledge appointed him as its publisher in 1827.85 Knight's most successful publication was the Penny Magazine, launched in March 1832. It had a circulation of 200,o0o within nine months, and ran until December 1845. Cole was able to place articles on his chosen subjects in this popular periodical, and in its sister publication the Penny Cyclopaedia: and, in the rapidly developing field of pictorial journalism, where Cole was making a mark, Knight was a very useful contact.

Although Cole's professional life was again secure, his domestic life was not proof against disaster. On the morning of 23 November 1837 he and Marian were awoken at five in the morning by the baby. He was ill, and Henry spent the whole day nursing him. He developed a cough and the doctor was called. A week later it was clear that he was 'very alarmingly ill', and a second doctor 'intimated that he thought there was great danger'. A little food was given to Downer. 'We thought him better, when alas! about 8 Oclock as lying in his Mothers arms, there seemed a catching in his breath, like the hiccup, and with the gentlest sigh he died. Marian tried all means of resuscitation but to no purpose for some hours, and was obliged to believe the sad reality.'86 He was six months old. Two days later, 'Marian after taking leave of Baby, who even in his coffin was beautiful, left the House with her mother & Charlotte. My Father attended the funeral with me, we went in a coach to the [Brompton] Cemetery. Baby's grave was at the Western end . . . the first grave being also for a Child buried the same day'. After this tragedy Marian was unable to stay in their house in Kensington, and Henry tried to sub-let it while they lived in lodgings in Pentonville. Edwin Chadwick disliked being left on his own and suddenly decamped at only one day's notice.

The government's delay over the Records Bill was finally ended in the spring of 1838 when Lord Langdale threatened resignation. As a result, Cooper, who had become Langdale's secretary for record business on the demise of the Record Commission, took Silk and was replaced as secretary by F. S. Thomas, from the State Paper Office. The government's bill was introduced in July, and received the royal assent in August. The various depositories of records were now united to form a Public Record Office, under the Master of the Rolls. Working to him there was now to be a chief keeper of records, confusingly titled 'deputy keeper' (so as not to prejudice Langdale's position as overall head). Thomas Hardy hoped for this post, as he was now senior clerk at the Tower, but after a good deal of to-ing and fro-ing the post went to Sir Francis Palgrave, and Hardy had to serve under his old adversary for over 20 years, until Sir Francis's death in 1861. Sir Francis, who was not paid as much as he wished, had, during negotiations, dismissed as intolerable the suggestion that he might accept a lower post, since this might mean he would be 'compelled to sit at the same desk with Mr. Cole and take orders from Mr. Hardy'.87 In the new dispensation, Cole was re-instated in the record service, under the benign eye of Lord

Langdale, as one of four assistant keepers, first class (£4oo a year). He was in charge of the records of the Common Pleas and Exchequer of Pleas. 88 Cole also managed to get his brother Charles taken on to the staff of the Public Record Office, in the face of Palgrave's opposition. Despite increasing eccentricity, amounting to mental insta-

Henry, secure in his post, might have become a scholar-archivist. He published two academic books, Henry the Eighth's scheme of bishopricks (1838) and Documents illustrative of English history in the thirteenth and fourteenth centuries $\left( \textrm { I } 8 4 4 \right)$ .But he was not cut out to be a desk-bound scholar, and became increasingly involved in administration, putting right some of the inadequacies of the records service, and preparing for the establishment of the new Record Office in Chancery Lane in 1851.

In February 1839, with the cautious backing of Thomas Hardy, he submitted to Langdale a scheme for re-organizing the records, offering it with rather emphatic deference: 'Should it seem, that I am hereby overstepping my proper position, I trust your Lordship will overlook the indiscretion & bear in mind that I am sincerely zealous for the good of the Service.'90 Many of his ideas came through into practice, though his proposed reduction of search fees did not meet with approval from Palgrave.1 Cole's practical streak led him to take an intense interest in the cleaning, binding, labelling and shelving of records, and in Fifty Years he takes pleasure in recalling how he colour-coded the records in his charge, to prevent them from being misplaced, and had labels printed, on a hand press in the office, making clear what was in each item.92

Cole kept up a 'continuous agitation' for an enlarged force of porters and workmen who would look after the physical state of the records. He tried to get them a graduated pay scale, and concerned himself with their welfare to the extent that he was reproved for buying them beer in 1840. When, in 1842, Palgrave tried to favour junior clerks at the expense of workmen, Cole fercely defended the latter, sressing their precarious status, with no insurance or pension.93 In 1845-6 Cole was again in dispute with Palgrave over the workmen, when Palgrave complained that they were taking too much sick leave, and that expenditure on their beer had gone up again. Cole became 'very angry' at these reflections on his administration and threatened to take the matter to Langdale, at which Palgrave backed down.94

From the official fles, then, Cole emerges as a champion of the workers. This is in contrast to the picture he paints, 4o years after the event. Utilitarianism was often concerned more with efficiency than with benevolence and compassion (as witness the New Poor Law), and in Fifty Years Cole paints himself as a hard manager. The workmen, he stresses,

were paid by the hour and hired by the day . . an arrangement which especially commen iel he Treasury, involvinolongengaen a spt. As far as I know, this was the only Government office which had daily servants of this sort. Some years afterwards, there was an extensive strike in the Metropolis between the builders and their workmen upon the question o the length o hours o working in he week, and what interpretation was to be put on the word 'day.' I told my experience of e r strike was ended.The practice is now, I believe,universal in metropolitan trades, and public clerks are paid by the hour.95

Thus Cole writing as a grand ol man with a reputation as a disciplinarian, tie o tone down the subversive activities of his youth.

He himself enjoyed considerable latitude in his terms and conditions of work. From 1838 onwards he was constantly engaged in campaigns which had nothing to do with his offcial work, and for which Langdale gave him time off. It was Langdale's liberal view that 'the salaries of assistant keepers were too low and that they could not be blamed for doing other things'. As to what other activities might be permissible, he again took a very tolerant line.

What endeavours are proper? Is it clear whether extra employments are more or less inconvenient or dangerous to the service in proportion to their being more or less connected with the public or official employments[?] Writing histories, or dissertations or reviews on subjects of history as shewn or illustrated by Official documents in charge of the Officers, will by some be thought more fitting, & by others be thought most likely to seduce the officer from the strictly official line of duty - plausible reasons may be given for very different opinions.

Cultivating - or even teaching  abstract science or painting  drawing - music etc or exhibiting specimens of art - will by some be thought particularly eligible, because the subjects are in general remote from those which are the special object of official consideration.97

This is, in effect, Langdale's charter for Cole's activities between 1838 and 1850. With the select committee report on the records and the subsequent legislation, Cole discovered his abilities as a reformer, and almost at once, having retrieved his principal employment, he turned his attention to major reform in another field: the Post Office.

## CHAPTER 4 The Penny Post 1838-I842

'Of all the events with which my career has been connected, no one I feel surpasses . . . the adoption of the Uniform Penny Postage. All the progress of mankind is helped on by freedom of thought expressed in writing. . .. It will be the glory of England for all time that she was the first country to adopt this ray of light, and the fame of Rowland Hill will be imperishable, as having discovered Uniform Penny Postage.'1 These are exalted words with which Cole describes the next project in which he was involved. But the penny post was a momentous development, the Victorian equivalent of today's internet.

Rowland Hill's famous pamphlet, Post Office reform; its importance and practicability, came out at New Year 1837. A small first edition, privately printed, and circulated to friends and politicians, was quickly followed by a larger edition, published by Charles Knight. Cole had a copy of the first edition (which remained with his books until they were sold by Sotheby's in 1893) so he may well have been a favoured recipient. His diaries are missing for 1835 and 1836, but it is likely that he and Hill (plate I 3) got to know each other during these years. Hill must have known Cole by repute, if not personally, and he would have read the article on the public records that Cole had written for the Penny Magazine. Their first known meeting was at the house of Francis Place in the spring of 1837. Place was also a favoured recipient of the first edition of Hill's pamphlet, but, as he told a friend, 'he had not thought it worth perusal, having supposed that it was only some nonsensical scheme for carrying letters all over England for a penny, and being wearied out with wild-goose proposals for all sorts of impractical measures'.2

Hill's scheme was indeed for carrying letters, weighing half an ounce or less, all over England for a flat prepaid rate of one penny. The existing postal system, the Royal Mail, had been set up as a government department in the late seventeenth century. Letters were carried by post boys on horseback until about 178o, when improved roads made possible the Post Office's dark red and black mail coaches, with coachmen and guards in red and gold uniforms. Postage was charged by distance and paid for on arrival by the recipient. While some towns, including London, had a local penny post, the average cost of postage was 8/d. in the 1830s, a huge amount for poor working people, who often refused to accept letters. Hill well knew this problem, fo when he was a child in a srugglng fmily, his mother, epg a let  t (more valuable than coloured ones), which raised three shillings and set her mind at rest. Another way of escaping the charges was to send news by a coded message that formed part of the address on the outside of a letter. A glance at the address would transmit the news and the letter could be declined. Hill himself had once, when on holiday in Scotland, conveyed his whereabouts to his family by messages on the wrappers of old newspapers, sent through the post. Newspapers, which bore a stamp duty, were carried free of charge by the Post Office. A curious regulation was that a let a e e heplyowavy. I  w any enclosure, double postage was charged. There were various ways of evading the high charges. Businessmen, wanting to write to several addresses in the same town, would write the letters on one very large sheet, which was cut up on arrival and the pieces distributed by hand. Letters were often entrusted to travellers going to the appropriate destination: once, when Cole and Marian went to visit their cousins in Essex, they found that every passenger in the coach was carrying a letter for somebody else.3

The government, and by extension members of the Lords and Commons, were exempt from paying postage. 'They could', as Cole mischievously remarked, 'receive an unlimited number of letters free of postage, of any weight, even a pianoforte, a saddle, a haunch of venison.' Their signature at the bottom left-hand corner of the outside of the letter meant that the post office would stamp it 'Free'. Politicians would spend hours signing their names on blank sheets of paper in order to have a supply of 'franks' to give away, or even sell, to friends and supporters. They were supposed to send out 10 a day and receive 15, but in the 1830s over 7,00o,ooo franks a year were sent or received. Cole would often get franks from Charles Buller or Edward Protheroe. The government regarded the postal service as a revenue-raising tax, so attempts to reform it had to ensure that changes did not reduce revenue. Although this requirement inhibited change, it is obvious that the obstructive system was ripe for reform.

Rowland Hill came from a radical, reforming background. The Hill family's school, Hazelwood, near Birmingham, became famous throughout the world for its liberal methods of teaching, and was visited by such figures as Jeremy Bentham, Henry Brougham, Thomas Malthus, Joseph Hume and Robert Owen. The school was so successful that Hill and his brother Matthew started a branch school in 1827, at Bruce Castle, in Tottenham, north of London. At the school there was no beating discipline being maintained by committees of the boys  and no fighting or bullying. Science was taught as a core subject, which was unheard of at the time. By 1833 Rowland and Matthew had handed this school over to their other brothers. Matthew then read for the Bar and entered Parliament, and Rowland devoted himself to inventing and to radical reforms. With his brother Edwin he developed and patented a rotary machine for printing newspapers, which would have been successful but for the fact that the excise tax had to be impressed on each sheet of a newspaper, and the Treasury would not allow it to be done on a continuous roll. Hill was also concerned with the development of South Australia.

After publishing his pamphlet, Hill had an opportunity to explain his ideas on the penny post to a government Commission of Inquiry into the Twopenny (local) Post on 13 and 23 February 1837. Here he dilated on his idea that the fact that payment had been made should be signified by 'a bit of paper just large enough to bear the stamp', a sticky-backed 'label'.4 Hill's scheme was so novel that in early discussions envelopes had to be described as 'little bags called envelopes' and stamps as 'small stamped detached labels - say about an inch square  which, if prepared with a glutinous wash on the back, may be attached without a wafer'.

After the publication of the second edition of Post Office reform at the end of February, Hill's ideas gained the support of the press. He sent Cole papers for him to use in The Guide. Petitions were presented to both Houses of Parliament. In November 1837 Robert Wallace succeeded in obtaining a select committee of the House of Commons to look into the matter. Wallace, MP for Greenock and a founding member of the Reform Club, had been agitating in Parliament for the improvement of the postal service since 1833, even before Hill took up the cause. He had given Hill a 'half hundredweight of raw material' to help him write his pamphlet.6 It was as the select committee got under way that Cole became involved.

At this juncture, Cole was on the point of taking a decision that would have changed the whole path of his career. For in February 18 3 8 he was asked by Charles Buller to accompany him, as his secretary, to Canada, where Buller was bound on official business with Lord Durham. Durham was the white hope of many radicals, as the man who would become their prime minister if they ever achieved power. A strange mixture, he was a fabulously rich coal-mine owner, possessor of a Gothic castle and a peerage bestowed by the Tories, and had a romantic Byronic air, intensified by his poor health. He was, nonetheless, genuinely accessible to the humbler classes. His radical credentials were established by the part that he played as lord privy seal in Lord Grey's cabinet, in the preparation of the Reform Bill. The fact that he got quite out of step with his colleagues and eventually resigned, only enhanced his reputation as radical leader-in-waiting. After some time on the fringe of political life, he was sent out as governor-general of Canada, to settle ominously rising tensions between French and English colonists, with Buller as his right-hand man. Cole would have liked to go with them. He said that 'the temptations to do so were very great, but the tears of my dearest and best adviser, and my recent return to public service with the Records, prevented my acceptance'.7 Cole did well not to join this mission, for Durham tried to take a strong line, miscalculated, to the embarrassment of the government at home, and soon resigned in an atmosphere of muddle and recrimination, from which his reputation never recovered. At the time when Marian shed tears before Henry, she was expecting another child, and there was relief when, on the night of 3 1 May/1 June, a girl was born, 'fat and healthy', after Marian had been in labour for only two hours. They named her Laetitia after Henry's mother.

When Buller's offer arose, Cole had just started to work for Hill. Hill's postal reforms were being promoted by a pressure group of city merchants  the 'Mercantile Committee' - led by George Moffatt, MP for Herefordshire, a wholesale tea importer. Hill, meeting Cole by chance in Piccadilly, on 9 February 1838, asked him to 'assist him with Post Office Agitation', and a few days later Cole became secretary of the Mercantile Committe. As soon as Cole had received Lord Langdale's permission to take on the extra work - 'on the understanding that my work on Public Records was not prejudiced'  he went to the city to confer with Moffat and William Ashurst, a solicitor and parliamentary agent who acted as legal adviser to the committee. Moffatt and Ashurst, like Cole and several other supporters of Hill, were members of the Reform Club. It was agreed that Cole would brief Wallace, the c a i Offofcials who were o appear beore the comittee.

Cole did this, but his real bent was for public relations, or what he called 'the busines of communicating with the public He quickly drafted a 'plan for circulating petitions, addresses and communications on the uniform penny postage at the cheapest rate'. Drawing on his experience in running The Guide, he planned a broadsheet that would contain just enough news to pass as a newspaper, though it wouldprarily be an organ of propaganda. Charles Whiting 'agreed to take the risk of a paper if the Mercantile Committee would take 500 copies at £6. 6'. The paper was titled The Post Circular. Or Advocate for a Cheap Swift and Sure Postage. Since (as we have seen) newspapers were carried free by the Post Office, this paper duly went free, and Cole thus forced the Post Office to carry propaganda against itself free of charge all over the country. The Post Office, Cole crowed, thus became 'the chief instrument for reforming itself'. The first number of the Circular appeared on 14 March, published by Henry Hooper, the well-known bookseller of Pall Mall East, who supported the campaign. The Circular was written by Cole, and it ran during the parliamentary session. About 1,7 5o copies were published of each number, and they were widely distributed to all who seemed to Cole to be influential: 'three thousand clergymen . .. every provincial newspaper ... most of the permanent and subscription libraries throughout the United Kingdom . . . Town Clerks . . . Chambers of Commerce . . . the commercial rooms of most respectable inns', and many individuals. A few were sold for 2/d. Sometimes a whole issue, apart from a few snippety news items, consisted of a specimen of a petition, with instructions for how to collect signatures and send them to a member of the House of Commons or the House of Lords, post free. The 1oth number contained a poster ready to be put up. Cole used his experience and colour sense to have the poster printed in a striking light red and green.

The select committee began sitting in March and did not finish taking evidence until July. On 17 March, it agreed on the establishment of a uniform rate of postage throughout the country. Thus Hill's vital principle was established. But the committee would not accept the penny rate, and only after much argument, and forceful chairmanship from Wallace, did it settle for a two-penny rate. Henry Warburton, Cole's radical friend, was recruited to prepare the report, and after some weeks had gone by, Hill asked Cole to help Warburton by preparing abstracts of the evidence. Nonetheless, there were more delays and Hill became anxious, as did Cole. In October, Hill wrote to tell Cole that Warburton had promised the report would be ready for the printer in the following week, but added: 'Warburton is not easily led'. He suggested that the Mercantile Committee might be able to put pressure on him, and remarked: 'I am much amused by your perseverance. If we could bring together 20 such men as yourself & Ashurst we should quickly carry the question'.° The report finally appeared in March 1839, when it was welcomed by the press. The Spectator devoted a special issue to it and printed it in full.

The reformers kept up the pressure, urging the government to implement the select committee's suggestions through legislation, and, indeed, to go farther and introduce the penny rate, as at first envisaged. The Mercantile Committee revived the Circular to this end, and Cole clearly relished the opportunity again to exercise all the arts of persuasion. In reply to the charge that the penny post would greatly increase the amount of mail carried in the mail coaches, to the extent that the transport costs would increase twelvefold, Cole resorted to graphic communication. He drew a caricature in the number for 30 April 1839, showing a mail coach, laden with parcels of different sizes, demonstrating how a small bag of letters paid the cost of transporting a large bag of newspapers and another of franked letters that were taken free. He took the statistics on which the caricature was based from those published by the Post Office itself. Doubtless pleased with his work, he had an extra 3,ooo copies of this issue printed and sent them to all members of the Lords and Commons.

He even tried drama as a means of persuasion. The previous autumn he had written a scene about postal reform to go into a Christmas pantomime, Fair Rosamund. It was accepted by the eminent actor-manager William Macready, and handed on for performance at the Drury Lane theatre, but there was not enough rehearsal time to put it on. Now Cole followed up this dramatic experiment by writing a skit on postal reform, which put the arguments for and against the penny post into the mouths of the Queen, Lord Melbourne, Lord Lichfield (the postmaster general) and Rowland Hill. The Queen, speaking on behalf of reform, has much the best of the argument, and concludes by suggesting that Lord Lichfield and the secretary of the Post Office should step down. 10 Some 2,ooo copies of this were printed and sent to the Lords and Commons, and another 2,ooo were handed out. A cheap edition of 10o,ooo copies was published and was either sold or given away. Then in March 1839 Cole arranged with the publishers, Chapman and Hall, for a further 40,ooo to be bound into one of the monthly parts of Dickens's Nicholas Nickleby, then being serialised.

Cole did not restrict his efforts to literaryl1 and pictorial persuasion. On 2 May a large deputation of MPs and others went to see the prime minister, led by Henry Warburton, because Robert Wallace was away in Scotland. Cole organised this. Lord Melbourne received them, supported by Francis Baring and Thomas Spring Rice. Warburton made a long, incisive speech, and Daniel O'Connell stood on a table at the back to plead on behalf of the Irish poor, whose family affections were frustrated because they could not afford to write to each other. Moffatt said that his consortium of merchants was ready to take over the postal service from the government, if it shrank from reform. Lord Melbourne told them that the government would give the whole matter 'prompt and earnest attention'1 At the end of the meeting Warburton was loudly cheered. That evening, Cole was taken by Hill to the dinner of the Political Economy Club, which had been founded by William Tooke, Malthus, Ricardo, James Mill and others in1821. In his after-inner speech, John Ramsay McCulloch, the controller of the Stationery Office, attacked Hill's plan 'in toto'. Hill, Chadwick and S. J. Loyd were able to refute his reasoning, and at the end Cole, forsaking obscurity as a back-room organiser, intervened with a sharp question: if McCulloch was against postal reform, why, not long before, had he signed a petition in favour of the penny post? This, Cole noted, 'raised a laugh?

Gal f laughter were soon rais by nother mishevoussratage, ai at demonstrating the stupidity of the existing postal system, in which a leter of ne seet, however large went at  wer ratethan eter  toheets, owerall. A single-sheet letter weighing fractionally less than the limit of one ounce was prep  hee  y

Cross Post Office.

He produced first one of the largest letters. The clerk looked at it suspiciously. He held i help his jument. Allthis caused a delay, and a crowd began to collect at the winow, who watched the process with interest. At last the clerk marked it with the single rate, and the spectators laughed. Then the smallest leter was produced and the Post Office offial turned crimson, became furious, and cursed a little, but he could not help marking it a double postage. Roars of laughter came from the crowd. Then fifty more of each letter were produced ....

Despite such mettlesome campaigning, the prospects of legislation still seemed remote. Fears were not allayed when, on 25 May, Lord John Russell, then Home Secretary, assured Warburton privately that the government hoped to introduce the penny post. Moffatt, with Cole's help, immediately arranged a public meeting at the Mansion House to collect more petitions. After a few days, when nothing more had been said in Parliament, he ostentatiously cancelled the meeting and announced that it was postponed until the government did more than 'hope' to bring in the measure. Cole thought that it was Moffatt's 'sagacity and courage' in doing this that forced Lord John to announce that there would be a resolution to introduce the penny post. This encouraged but did not satisfy the reformers, for they felt that a resolution could easily be rescinded and what was needed was an act of Parliament.

Petitions in favour of the penny post continued to come to both Houses of Parliament. Cole exploited this device of persuasion to the full, estimating that by 20 July 1839, 2,007 petitions with 262,809 signatures, had been gathered. On 5 July Spring Rice, the Chancellor of the Exchequer, presented his budget and promised a bill, provided that the House would pledge itself to make good any resulting reduction in the revenue. Possible loss of revenue was still the great impediment to change, and enabled the Chancellor of the Exchequer to keep the whip hand in matters of postal reform.

A few days later the postponed meeting at the Mansion House was held and 'passed off brilliantly'. A petition with 12,500 signatures, that had been collected that day, was presented to the House of Commons, and despite further obstruction the Postage Duties Bill was brought to the House on 18 July. On 24 July it went through the committee stage unopposed in three quarters of an hour. There was still the possibility that the Lords would throw it out, and Cole occupied himself, during much of this 'critical period' before the bill came on, in calling on peers to get them to attend the debate and vote for it. He found that considerable support could be expected in the Upper House, provided that the bill was well explained. Accordingly he prepared some notes for Hill to take to Lord Melbourne.

When the bill reached the Lords on 5 August, Cole and Hill sat on the steps of the throne. They heard Lord Melbourne introduce the bill 'in a good humoured and jocular manner'. 13 He was answered by the duke of Wellington, who was against the change 'on the score of depression in the finances, but fully recognized the evils of high postage rates'. 14 Cole wrote: 'Rowland Hill and myself felt alarmed, and my heart beat quickly, but we were relieved when he said, "what was called Mr Rowland

Hill's plan was, if it was adopted exactly as proposed" (this said with emphasis) "of all plans most likely to be successful." He ended by saying, "I throw the responsibility on the ministers, and I shall, although with great reluctance, vote for the bill, but I earnestly recommend your lordships to do likewise"". The bill was read without a division, 'committed in about two minutes' on 8 August, passed on 9 August and received the royal assent on 17 August.

Richard Cobden, with whom Cole had stayed in Manchester when campaigning for postal reform, exclaimed, 'There go the Corn Laws!' He felt 'that new flood-gates of knowledge were . . . opened' by the new postal legislation, and that prospects were thereby improved for reform in the field with which he himself was most concerned, the Corn Laws. Cole was very nearly caught up in Cobden's movement. The Corn Laws had been introduced after the Napoleonic Wars, to protect Tory landowners, who grew most of the wheat in the country. Their effect was to keep the price of bread artificially high, which was a great hardship to the poor, particularly in the new industrial towns like Manchester, where Anti Corn-Law associations had been established. Manchester's association was much more active than that in London, and in 1839 Cobden sent John Smith, chairman of the Manchester Chamber of Commerce, and some others to London to try to revive the London association. The Manchester party called on Henry Warburton, who sent them to see Francis Place, and he gave them a letter of introduction to Cole. Consequently they tried to persuade Cole to become secretary of the Anti-Corn Law League. He was too busy with postal reform, and declined this interesting offer.

He did what he could to help Cobden, however, and persuaded his new friend William Makepeace Thackeray to do some drawings that pointed out the price difference between bread in England and on the Continent, where it cost less by a third.15 In a letter to Cobden, Cole recommended Thackeray as 'a genius, both with his pencil and his pen. His vocation is literary. He is full of humour and feeling'. 16 He and Cole had first met two years before when Thackeray was editing The Constitutional. They had much in common, as they were both struggling to support their families, and they were both grieving over the death of a child (Thackeray's second baby daughter having died that spring). Thackeray's sketches were published in The Anti-Corn Law Circular, which had been founded to campaign in the same way as Cole's Post Circular had done for the penny post. Cole also helped Henry Warburton collect material for a speech on the Corn Laws in the House of Commons. It was another seven years before the potato famine in Ireland precipitated the repeal of the Corn Laws in June 1846. Cobden would later pay tribute to Cole as 'a clever agitator, and a public spirited fellow'.17

Cole's decision to stay with postal reform meant that he was involved not only in and Francis Baring, the new Chancellor of the Exchequer (who was appointed on 17 August, the same day as the act received royal assent) offered to employ Rowland Hill on this work for two years at the Treasury. After his initial salary offer of £500 had been raised to £ 1, 5oo, Hill accepted. Baring assured Hill that he would support the pe st Heart nSu He ogave ionr Col tohe Hil. Oaolea to t hs hi' s    wrk, and  ll on Ld Langdale to tell him of the Treasury offer which he agreed to my accepting if it did not interfere with the Records.' Cole's diary entry continues: 'Whitehall No Recr  ratheppt csio  acsidnHe  wrot tya    u view he became more conscientious about his records work, writing down how many pages of transcription he completed each day.

On 27 September Cole joined the whole Hill family at dinner, to celebrate Rowland's 1 2th wedding anniversary. A big silver candelabrum presented to Hill by the people of Wolverhampton was on the table for the first time. Its inscription read: 'To Rowland Hil, Esq., . . . Founder and able Advocate of the Plan of Universal Penny Postage, A.D. 1839'. Cole and Hill now turned to making the planned penny post a reality. One of the matters in which Cole was to become most involved was methods of pre-payment. Even before Cole joined Hill at the Treasury, the lords of the Treasury had, on 23 August, invited 'artists, men of science, and the public in general' to send in ideas of the best way to arrange for this. Four desiderata were laid down: convenience for the public; prevention of forgery; simplicity of checking by the Post Office; and control of the costs of production and distribution. A first prize of £200 was offered for proposals, and two second prizes of £100. Cole himself was a competitor, among over 2, 5o0 others. Many of the proposals were sent in by printers or inventors, who had patented ways of water-marking the paper to be used for stamps, or who knew how to engrave so intricately that forgery would be almost impossible. Others had perfected an indelible ink for the cancelling stamp so that the label or cover could not be used more than once. Several people known to Cole competed: Dr John Bowring, one of the founders and the former editor of The Westminster Review sent in three entries. Alexander Bain, the pioneer of electricity, competed, as did the physician and inventor Neil Arnott, and Isaac Pitman, who had just published his book Stenographic Sound-Hand. John Mill's hanger-on, John Robertson, joined in. Charles Whiting submitted several examples of machined printing and helped Cole with his proposal, on which he worked in the evenings during September and early October. He employed William Kingston, his singing teacher, to act as his clerk to fair copy it, and paid him £4 5s. od. for doing so.

Two days before Cole had finished his proposal, he began work informally with Hill, and on 8 October he began his official duties. He described his work as being 'of a most miscellaneous character; consulting the Post Office Offcials, . . . inquiring into modes of conveying letters by public carriages, discussing daily with Rowland Hill points of diffculty'. His chief task came to be 'the superintendence of the production of the three forms of stamps which it had been resolved to adopt; namely, an adhesive stamp to be attached to any letters; envelopes; and a stamp to be embossed up paper o ny kind sent to the Stam OffeThis ast idea was shelved or eral years, though the other two went ahead.

When Cole began his work at the Treasury, Rowland Hill was away in Paris, sent by Francis Baring to study the French postal system. The day after Hill's return a week later, Cole 'walked home with R Hill who advised my not urging him onwards too much because he should overwork himself'.21 Poor Hill was frequently ill from overwork, and he drove himself to the limit without any encouragement from an energetic young man such as Cole. Cole himself was overworking: he went to his office in Whitehall Court in the morning to do his work as a record keeper, had his dinner brought to him there, and then in the mid-afternoon joined Hill at the Treasury, where he would often stay until after seven o'clock. He was still writing The Post Circular and arranging its distribution. In the midst of all this, Marian had another baby on 1 October, born in 45 minutes, before Dr Merriman could arrive. She was named Henrietta.

In early November Cole had a boil come up on his leg, which was so bad that he consulted Dr Merriman, 'who ordered linseed poultices, poppy & camomile fomentations'. After a few days 'Dr Merriman called, & took out the core of my wound. Still desired I should keep quite quiet, & forbade even my riding out'. While Cole was housebound, he spent most of his time analysing the Post Office Returns. His brother Charles ran errands for him, and he received a constant stream of callers about the penny post. He could not return to work for two weeks, and, when he did get back to the Treasury on 23 November, he was faced with a difficult meeting with officials from the Post Office.

At the Post Office, both the staff and the politicians were against any change at all. Lord Lichfield, the Postmaster General, had said of the penny post that 'of all the wild and visionary schemes which he had ever heard or read of, it was the most extraordinary'.22 But he eventually came round to acquiesce in the measure, because he chose to believe that the revenue to the Post Office would go up. Colonel Maberly, the joint secretary of the Post Office, was a more serious adversary, as he worked from within the institution itself. He was socially smart, having served in a fashionable regiment and married the sister of an Irish peer. He looked down on Hill, calling him 'that man from Birmingham', and resisted every change until he was finally moved to the Board of Audit in 1855. His entry in the Dictionary of National Biography says that 'for more than seven years Maberly continued in authority, and improvement of every kind was delayed and some millions of public money were wasted'. This is less bland than is usual for an entry in the DNB. It was written by George Boase, the Cornish scholar and bibliophile, who came from Penzance and fully appreciated the need for an efficient post to outlying parts of the country. Hill had already encountered Maberly in the course of his earlier agitation, and when he met him formally for the first time in September he wrote of the meeting: 'I was received with great apparent civility, but little things were said & done, I firmly believe with the intention of annoying me. The less I have to do with the gallant Colonel the better.'23

Cole found Colonel Maberly's deputies in the Inland Office and the Twopenny Post Department, William Bokenham and Robert Smith, 'very civil', at first. They accepted that, since the penny post had passed into law, 'they should now do their best to make it succeed'4 But at the end of the month, Bokenham's manner revealed to Cole that they still had reservations. 'It was clear', Cole noted, 'that he still dislike the P.P. He spoke of "Mr Hill as our Ruler". He thought P.P. wd be a total failure'.2 However, a few days after temporary dual rates had been brought in, Bokenham had the good grace to tell Hill that 'a greater saving than he expected results from uniformity of rate', and that owing to flexible administration the new system 'has required no increase of strength' in the workforce.26 There were still attacks in the papers on Hill's scheme. An article in the Quarterly Review in the autumn said that 'prepayment by means of a stamp or stamped cover is universally itt   uit hevern  habithe pe Cole called the article a martish & shallow production', but Hill took it susly, and had his brother Mathew write a reply in the Edinburgh Reviw.

Cole helped Hill assess the pre-payment proposals entered in the competition, and to write the report on them. The result of the competition was announced in December: Cole, Charles Whiting, Bogardus and Coffin, and Benjamin Cheverton shared the £40 equally, despite the original terms of the competition. Bogardus and Coffin joined together to send in examples of complicated engraving for the stamps; and Benjamin Cheverton suggested printing the stamps on a mile-long coil of paper anhathe hev  houl owhe heMery Ihary that Cole did well: he was working from the inside and had a deeper knowledge of the subject than anyone else. Not only had he worked closely with Rowland Hill for two years but also he was writing The Post Circular, and composed over 65 articles, draft petitions, pamphlets and analyses about the penny post. All this in addition to his experience as an agitator and journalist. His essay incorporated many of Hill's ideas, and in advocating these he was able to quote with precision from the Minutes of Evidence of the Postage Committee and from government papers. He did not agree with Hills idea that postage might be paid by having the stamp printed or embossed on the sender's own writing paper. He pointed out that this would be very inconvenient, involving at least two visits to a post office to deliver and later to collect the sheets after printing. There was also the difficulty of guarding against forgery. Cole recommended adhesive labels and stamped covers  that is to say envelopes - printed on watermarked paper. Envelopes were rarely used up to this time, as their use involved the extra expense of double postage. Once charging by weight came in, envelopes became common, though it was another ten years before a machine for making them was invented.

To promote the new system, Cole compiled a Post Office Almanac. But this was stopped when Kelly's, the big publisher of directories and almanacs generally titled 'Post Office Directories', took out an injunction against it. In granting the injunction, the Vice Chancellor said that 'nearly all which gives value to the Almanac has been pirated verbatim et literatim from the Directory, or from proof sheets thereof surreptitiously obtained'.29 Cole (who kept a press cutting of the judgement) thought this very harsh. In his opinion, no copyright had been established by Kelly's. Within a few days he issued a new edition of his Almanac, with a defiant statement inside the cover pointing out that 'the Injunction obtained by the Proprietor of "The Post Office London Directory" applies chiefly to the old Post Office Regulations', which had now lapsed. Cole's Almanac, on the other hand, contained the new regulations, 'just issued'.30

Another by-product of Cole's work was a letter balance. He foresaw the need for accurate small weighing machines to be used at home to ensure that letters were under half an ounce in weight, and accordingly designed one. It had a coin-shaped counter balance and could weigh up to 4 ounces. It was patented in August 1 839 and was made in various versions by Silvesters of the Strand, who were engravers. It was published by Henry Hooper and it quickly sold out.31 Cole made £3o out of it, paid him by Henry Hooper, which may have been a commission or a payment for the copyright. Hill, with his brother Edwin's help, had meanwhile arranged for scales with weights to be designed and made for use in Post Offices. After stamps went on sale, other weighing machines and spring balances were quickly available to save the public visits to the Post Office. Edwin Hill joined the team to supervise the machinery to print the stamps.

The penny rate for London was brought in on 5 December 1839, with a uniform rate of fourpence for the rest of the country. On 10 January 1840 the penny rate for the whole country began, even though no stamps were ready and the public had to take their letters to a post office to pay their Id. Hill and Cole tactfully stayed away from the main Post Office at St Martin's le Grand on that first day, but they heard that when the windows closed 'the mob, delighted at the energy displayed by the officers, gave one cheer for the Post Office and another for Rowland Hill'. Two days later, Cole visited the main Post Office 'to see operations', and found it 'very noisy & crowded at the Pay Windows'. 32

The great hall was nearly filled with spectators, marshalled in a line by the police, to watch the crowds pressing, scuffling, and fighting to get first to the window. ... Formerly one window sufficed to receive letters. On this evening six windows, with two receivers at each, were bombarded by applicants. As the last quarter of an hour approached, and the crowd still thickened, a seventh window was opened, and that none might be turned away, Mr Bokenham made some other opening, and took in letters and money himself.33

The famous 'penny black' stamp was introduced on 6 May. There had been dire warnings in the press that stamps would be forged or used repeatedly. To combat this many complicated designs had been submitted in the prize proposals, but in the end an engraved portrait of the Queen was chosen as the motif on the stamp, because she was unmistakeable.34 A medal designed by William Wyon, engraver to the Royal Mint was used as the source of the Queen's image. Copied by Henry Corbould, and engraved by the Heaths, it had an engine-turned background, and was printed in sheets of 240.

The Treasury decided to publish a decorated cover to celebrate the new cheap postal system. While Cole was still laid up with his bad leg, he made a drawing and discussed it with his friend, the engraver John Thompson. Several well-known painters were asked to submit ideas, but Francis Baring did not like any of them and on his advice, Cole approached William Mulready, one of the most successful painters of the time, 63 years old, and a Royal Academician of over 2o years' standing. When Cole called on him on 13 December, he found him 'very intelligent and affable'. 'He appeared readily to enter into the idea & agreed to make a trial.' Within two days Mulready made 'a highly poetic design', which Cole showed to Hill, who liked it. Over the next week there were several meetings at Cole's house with Mulready, Hill and Thompson to discuss the design and its engraving. Cole admired the extremely careful finish of Mulready's work: what Mulready himself once called "an ostentatious mastery of execution'.35

Hispces plat )showeBriti wavig her rs ver gure mbolicof Britain's far-flung empire.Angels fly out, apparently at her command, as winged messengers (symbols, perhaps, of a world-wide postal service). To either side are groups representing Asia (with elephants) and the New World (with Pilgrim Fathers and American Indians), while two smaller, more domestic, scenes below show figures eagerly reading letters. Behind can be seen some old-fashioned ships, pul in which to write the address, but no room on the front of the cover to put on a stamp, because the cost of the cover, . included postage up to hal anu.

The design was approved by the Royal Academy and the cabinet, and all thought well of it. William Wyon took the trouble to write to Cole about it: 'Although I expected much from him [Mulready] it exceeded my expectations & I should really regret it if so admirable a design should be lost to the public'.36 John Thompson engraved it on brass, William Clowes printed it, and it went on sale on 1 May. On 2 May the first of the rude comments about it came out in The Times. 'We must say that we never beheld anything more ludicrous than the figures or allegorical device by which it is marked with its official character . . .. Cruikshank could scarcely produce anything so laughable'37 Comments like this were published over the next few weeks, and there were some cruel caricatures. Many poked fun at Mulready's Irish origins. All observed what even the Queen, who was sent a proof - and also Mulready, Thompson, Hill, Cole, Baring, Wyon and the Royal Academicians - had failed to notice: that one of the angels lacked a leg. Hill wrote in his diary on 12 May: 'I fear we shall be obliged to substitute some other stamps for that designed by Mulready, which is abused on all sides. In departing widely from the established "lion and unicorn" nonsense, I fear we have run counter to settled opinions somewhat rashly. I now think it would have been wiser to have followed established custom in all the details of the measure [the introduction of the penny post] where practicable. '38 In his autobiography, Cole wrote: 'The postage cover was for dry commercial use, in which sentiment has no part . . . Had an allegorical fresco for any public building been required to symbolize the introduction of the universal penny postage, nothing could have been better than Mulready's design.' The adverse publicity was a disaster for Mulready, but it did bring him to the notice of Francis Baring's younger brother who became one of his patrons. And for Cole the episode brought lifelong friendships with both Mulready and the whole of the Thompson family.

Cole went on working at the Treasury until 1842, trying to bring in a parcel post and to improve the sending of letters abroad. Characteristically, he preserved all his work relating to the penny post in albums, which are now in the Victoria and Albert Museum. Then the paths of Cole and Hill parted, Hill becoming a director, and later chairman, of the Brighton Railway. He returned to the Post Office in 1846, where Maberly was still in charge and still opposed to every change. It was not until 1854 that Hill was appointed sole secretary, with his brother Edwin as his assistant. Honours and honorary degrees were heaped on him, and in 1864 the Society of Arts gave him its Albert Gold Medal. He was the first recipient of this honour, instituted in memory of the Prince Consort.

Cole, in his work for the penny post, learned much about printing and illustration, which influenced his future interests. But what stands out in his postal work is his zest and exuberance as an agitator. When campaigning for the reform of the Record Commission, he had been fighting for his livelihood, a serious business. During the postal reforms, however, personal survival was no longer a problem, and he could throw himself into campaigning with enjoyment, and exult in his talents for mischief and irreverence, earning Rowland Hill's praise for 'those amusing devices with which my friend Mr. Henry Cole knew so well how to strike the public eye'.39

## CHAPTER 5 Journalism and Railways 1838-1849

By the beginning of the $\tau 8 4 0 \mathrm { s } .$ Cole had gained a reputation as a campaigning reformer and polemicist. If public relations consultants or lobbyists had then existed, he would have been able to earn his livelihood that way. During the $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 4 0 s ,$ however, while he was busy with a wide variety of issues, his interests increasingly focused on artistic matters, and, as things fell out, it was in the arts that he ultimately made his mark. His turn to art during the $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 4 \bigcirc \mathrm { s }$ is dealt with in the next section, while this chapter follows out his activities in the $1 8 4 0 s$ as journalist, publicist and campaigner.

During this period, Cole had the security of his job at the Record Office, where he was now an established and appreciated member of staff. Lord Langdale, Cole noted, 'considered me one of the least irritable of the Record folks'. Both Langdale and Palgrave had a high opinion of him as a manager, and Langdale was eventually to say 'I had been quite a Magician' in the offce.1 Cole did not lack energy at home. His family was enlarged by the births of Mary Charlotte on 12 October $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 _ { 4 } \mathrm { ~ I ~ }$ , Henry Hardy on 10 March $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 4 3$ , and Alan Summerly on 18 August $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 4 6$ , while Isabella Langdale came along on 12 August $1 8 5 0 ,$ after Marian had suffered miscarriages in December $8 4 7$ and July $\texttt { T } 8 _ { 4 9 }$ Within the Coles' extended family, there were some problems with Henry's brothers. Charles was at hand to be roped into many a scheme, and Richard was out of the way in India. But Lindsay was constantly in financial trouble, needing Cole to bail him out. On 6 January ${ \mathrm { ~ I ~ } } 8 { \mathrm { ~ 5 ~ O ~ } } ,$ Cole made an unusually lengthy entry in his diary after Lindsay had visited: 'Very feeble & tottering in mind I thought. Sang ostentatiously & broke down in voice, time and tone. He is almost the wreck of a goodnatured, weak, kindly selfish man no strong principles of any kind.' Lindsay's son Wentworth (born in $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 \ 3 \ G )$ did not attract Cole at first. 'Wentworth excepting his looks, had scarcely anything to commend him', he noted on 15 July $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 4 2$ ,selfish, rude, sly: untruthful.' But he warmed to him later, describing him on 7 September $~ 1 8 4 6$ as 'a well behaved boy, & with good mode of expression & feelings'. Cole's brother William also often needed financial support. Cole tried to get him work, but at te no the ead ewas stil n toubler ettgMaran' miy wee reliable; her sisters Charlotte and Laura (the latter married on 12 October $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 _ { 4 4 } )$ were often around, and shared in the Coles'cultural interests. Their mother (Granny') also supported the family, though she had a patch of bad health in $1 8 4 4$ and her life was l lham m m hi decade, when his career began to take a settled direction.

As a journalist, Cole had won his spurs in $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 3 \mathrm { ~ I - } 4$ writing occasional reviews, of maa chi nTheE.s hebenvo w , he wrote or  wier rane picls, ut ail whatev d to be pushing. Records reform and the penny post were his principal topics. Although he had thus effectively relinquished the possibility of becoming a professional journalist, he did continue to hanker after the role of editor. As we have already seen, he had a first, brief stab at this in spring 1837 with The Guide. Unabashed by failure here, Cole was soon projecting 'a weekly journal to promote National Education in this country ... to be called the Portfolio of Public Instruction'. This was to be concerned with education in its practical rather then philosophical aspects, and its propectus4included an interesting diagram f the rent educational system, which would be keenly scrutinised in its columns. It was to be published by Henry Hooper, who had published The Guide and The Post Circular. Nothing came of this venture, but in the following year, when Cole heard that the printer Charles Reynell (who had printed The Guide) had bought the British Ladies' Newspaper, he contrived to secure the editorship of this. A 16-page, foliosize weekly, it seems to have lasted only for nine issues, from 6 January to 3 March 1838.6 Cole preserved some advertising posters for it, including a large one on yellow paper which reads like a parody by Dickens: 'TO THE FAIR sEX!! / THE BRITISH LADIES' NEWSPAPER / Addresses you Weekly / on all subjects in which the / Most Lovely of Creation / are deeply interested.'

When Cole compiled a sort of curriculum vitae in 186o, he described himself as both 'Civil Administrator & Author' and made brief reference to his earlier journalistic work: 'He contributed to the Westminster, & British & Foreign Reviews.'7 These were the most prestigious periodicals for which he wrote, and he no doubt recorded them in preference to less esteemed journals. He failed to mention, however, that he briefly served as editor of the Westminster.

Founded in 1824, the Westminster amalgamated with John Mill's London Review in 183 5. But the resulting London and Westminster Review, financially backed by Sir William Molesworth, did not prosper. Molesworth, who was losing money, withdrew in 1837, and Mill, after trying to manage as well as edit the journal, wanted to step down by 1840. After the political demise of Lord Durham and the weakening of the radical party in parliament, he felt that he had done all he could for the cause of reform. It is also possible that he was tired of dealing with the (nominal) editor of the Review, John Robertson, whom Carlyle had once described as 'a burly Aberdeen Scotchman of seven and twenty, full of laughter, vanity, pepticity and hope',8 but later dismissed as a 'a flathoofed jackass'.' Furthermore Mill was 'drained so dry' financially by the journal and other calls on his purse 'that I cannot incur the smallest extra expense on account of the next number of the review, and, all things considered, I would not recommend your doing so'. 10 Mill's recommendation to Cole to steer clear of the journal indicates that Cole was already angling to take over. He and William Hickson pushed hard and achieved their aim, since there were no other obvious or eager candidates to replace Mill. In these rather unpropitious circumstances, Henry Cole thus got a chance to fulfil a great ambition: to edit a prestigious journal. He was to be unpaid editor, while Hickson was to provide the money. Mill hoped that they would keep Robertson on as editor, as he was poor and needed his salary, but he had to go home to Scotland. Mill insisted that the name of the journal be changed back to Westminster Review, so that the readers would know that he was no longer involved with it.

Cole bustled round, calling on contributors at home or catching them at the Reform Club. Romilly, Buller and Thackeray all promised him articles. But Carlyle, recognising that Hickson and Cole lacked the prestige of John Mill, declined to allow them to reprint his series of lectures on heroes and hero-worship. He wrote disparagingly to John Sterling: 'The Westminster Review has come into the hands of Cole and Hickson; to be conducted on new principles! What words are adequate? No words'.11 In writing to his mother he called Cole a 'Dud' as editor.12 He seems to have been right, for Cole reported that the sales of the first number were 'very languid'.

Cole soon lost heart. His 'enthusiasm waned with surprising speed', comments one r   o While Cole was clearly a talented controversialist, and a skilled manipulator of information, it may well be that his forcefulness and partisan intensity deprived him of the breadth of sympathy (and the range of contacts) that a successful editor needed. By the end of July 184o, after only a few months, he left the Review to Hickson, who remained owner and editor for over 1o years. Hickson's wife, whom Cole liked, was cool to him for many months afterwards for having abandoned the loss-making Review to her husband. It was to be nearly nine years before the Hicksons again came to dinner with Cole and his family in 1849.

Though Cole had forsaken the Westminster, there were still openings for him elsewhere in journalism. One of them was the weekly paper, the Athenaeum, which was primarily a literary review, but covered all cultural matters to some extent. Cole first wrote for it in 1837, but a lifelong connection developed when he became friendly with Charles Wentworth Dilke, the son of its proprietor (plate 29). He was two years Cole's junior, and a sort of pig-in-the-middle in his family, caught between a famous father and a notorious son, who both shared all three of his names. 'Old' Charles Wentworth Dilke is best remembered today as a friend of the poets Keats and Shelley, but later in life, alongside a post in the navy pay office, he became proprietor and editor of the Athenaeum. Founded in 1829, this was faltering when Dilke bought it in 1830. He transformed its fortunes by reducing its price from a shilling to fourpence and increasing its size, and it so far succeeded that in 1845 it was said that it 'is now to literary journals what the "Times" is to newspapers'. I4 Cole already knew Dilke senior, for the Athenaeum supported the penny post and Cole had met him during the campaign. When Cole was asked to dinner on 1 October 1842 to meet the whole family, he wrote that 'there is a homeliness ab[ou]t them which puts you at your ease and in old Dilke especially good common sense with decision of character'. The middle Charles Wentworth Dilke, usually known as Wentworth, married Mary Chatfield in 1843, and they had the third Charles Wentworth Dilke, later to become an MP, whose promising political career was blighted by his involvement in a divorce scandal. The three generations of Dilkes lived together in a house in Sloane Street. Old Dilke was able to retire from the editorship of the Athenaeum in 1847, and, as propor, lin   r  C'v ur prominent member of the Horticultural Society, and founded a periodical, The Gardeners Chronicle. Cole began to write articles for this, as well as for the Athenaeum, to which he often contributed 'gossip', 'pars' (i.e. paragraphs) and 'miscellaneous notices'.16

In the early 1840s Cole was very busy with literary work, producing guidebooks, children's books and some artistic publications, which will be discussed in the next chapter. In the midst of this, he found time for yet another attempt at being an editor: of a newspaper dealing with births, deaths and marriages. Cole started proposing this to publishers on 6 June 1844. It sounds like the sort of journal that might appeal primarily, perhaps only, to people with a highly developed interest in public records, like Cole himself. Indeed, he described it in his promotional literature as 'a kind of personal Doomsday book - highly interesting and useful to the present time, and indispensable to the future' 17 The principal point that Cole advanced in its favour was that the compulsory legal registration of births, marriages and deaths had been in force only since 1836, and that these hitherto unpublished facts should be made available in published form.18 One of the publishers whom he lobbied conceded that births, deaths and marriages 'were the most interesting of human events & most read in the N[ews] Paper',19 and Cole made this point with characteristic emphasis in his publicity. In England and Wales, he claimed, 'a birth happens every minute, a death every minute and a half, and were marriages spread over the whole day, every fourth minute would witness the celebration of one. The hopes, fears, and sympathies of twenty millions are stirred by these events'.20

Longmans, with whom Cole was already working, agreed to take on the project on 27 August, even though the senior partner, Thomas Longman, 'could never get over the idea' (as he confessed on 2 1 October) that births, deaths and marriages 'were not sufficiently interesting' to the general public. Cole urged his projected paper, which was to be titled the Historical Register, upon all his contacts. Macaulay, on 9 November, had doubts; the scientist Richard Owen, on 2o November, thought it 'one of the Happiest Ideas' of the time; on 27 November 'Old Dilke thought the preparations for Hist: Register as good as possible & if we failed it wd be for want of a public'. Cole was so busy rallying contributors that, he noted on 2o December, 'diary neglected: but every minute at Register'. The Register started publication in the first week of the New Year, and there is nothing in Cole's diary for the first I 1 days of January, so hard was he working.

The Historical Register described its subject matter in its masthead as 'Family Events, National and Private Biography, Life and Health', and announced that it was produced 'with the Sanction and Assistance of various Government Departments'. Presumably the latter supplied the details of 'B.D. & M.', as Cole referred to the prime subject-matter of the 16-page, folio-size weekly journal. All Victorian newspapers looked grey and grim, with tightly packed columns of small type, and the Historical Register was as grey and grim as any. The core of each number consisted of several pages in minuscule type, simply listing births, marriages and deaths, together with a statistical analysis of mortality in London. After this were a few articles, usually biographies or obituaries. The lightest of these was a piece on 'The Christmas Pudding' in number 1; the darkest, a piece on 'Prussic Acid' (as a poison) in number 5. In the pages preceding the B.D.& M. came news, mostly copied out of other newspapers. On the front page was a section headed 'News of the Week. Summary'. In number 4, this began: 'The news of the week is in general unimportant'. Number 5 started with: 'Her Majesty, the Prince, and Royal Family continue in the enjoyment of excellent health'. The level of excitement rose a little in number 6:

'Her Majesty opened Parliament in person on Tuesday'. But number 7 was back to 'Her Majesty, Prince Albert, and the Royal Family continue to enjoy good health', and number 8 recorded that 'Her Majesty, Prince Albert, and the Royal children, returned to town from Brighton on the 2oth, in the enjoyment of excellent health'. Although this is hardly a fair sample of the paper's contents, it does indicate that the Historical Register did not have a very keen edge.

Alarm bells began to ring as soon as I4 January, when Longman said that the Register 'did not look very promising'. He brought in a Mr Maunder on 18 January, in the hope that he could 'work . . . the paper into Success'. On 4 February, Longman agreed to enlarge the paper to 24 pages, and 'to placard and try for 4 weeks more'. By the end of February, a shutdown seemed inevitable. Cole tried to get Bradbury and Evans to take over the paper but they declined on 27 February. The final number appeared on 8 March.

Cole's diary suggests that at this point he may have experienced a degree of agonising re-appraisal. He rearranged his room on 1o March ('taking down shelves &c.'). On Sunday 16 March and through the following week he did his accounts for the last three years, hitherto neglected. And he was slightly ill, with 'unhealthy urine'. His doctor and friend Seymour Haden warned that this portended gout, but his prescription was that 'I need only take some Soda in the Ev[enin]g'.21

Cole continued to be very busy with other projects, including journalism. He now found an outlet in collaborating with Wentworth Dilke in writing about railways, a highly topical issue. The 184os were the days of 'Railway Mania', as the network of new railways spread out across the country. In 1844 Dilke started the Railway Chronicle, to cater to the rising public excitement over this transport revolution. There were already many such journals in the market,22 the principal one being the Railway Times, which had been started in the 183os and which claimed for itself a weekly circulation of 27,000 by 1845. In 1845 there were over 700 railway companies, already working or being floated, many of them fraudulent. Cole wrote a spoof of a prospectus for the Railway Chronicle: 'The Great National Direct Independent Lands' End and John O'Groats Atmospheric Railway, with Steam Ferries to the Scilly and Orkney Isles, and Coasting Docks at both Termini. Provisionally registered Capital, £9,500,00o; in 95,000 shares of £100 each'. Such was the hysteria at the time that some people took it seriously.23

Cole wrote not only about the politics and economics of rail travel, but about the interesting new experience that it offered to sightseers. Guides for railway outings had been published since the late 183os, and 'Railway Rambles' to places near London had already appeared in the Penny Magazine in 1841. Cole came to this genre through writing a series of 'Day's Excursions out of London' in the Athenaeum, in autumn 1842 and 1843. At first, he envisaged that his readers might ramble by foot, carriage o steamer as much as by rail, but gradually rail became the preferred method, and Cole offered thoroughly practical advice: 'The seats at the end receive the most pitiless currents of wind and rain  air at alltimes  seasoned with the cinders from the engine.'24 The purpose of the expeditions was antiquarian, to se old churches and historic buildings in such places as Esher, St Albans, Rochester or Winchester. Some of Cole's articles were re-issued in book form in 1843 as Felix Summerly's day's excursions out of London.

Cole proceeded to a further series of articles in the Railway Chronicle,25 which later appeared in book form as Felix Summerly's pleasure excursions.26 His personal railway mania then led him to produce 'travelling charts'. His innovation here was to make the railway journey itself the central feature. The Railway Travelling Charts: or iron road books, for perusal on the journey included details not only of 'towns, villages, churches, mansions, parks', but also of 'stations, bridges, viaducts, tunnels, cuttings, gradients'. These features were all marked on a sort of map, which adopted a traditional format for road maps, in which the road meandered down the middle of a page, with details of places and landmarks on each side. In Cole's charts the railway line went vertically down the centre, flanked by text, and by illustrations in the form of charming little wood engravings after such artists as Richard Cox, jun., Charles C. Pyne, and Fraser Redgrave, the half-brother of the painter Richard Redgrave. The charts were individually printed on long strips of paper, which folded up into handy pamphlets, and were published from the Railway Chronicle office. They described the lines from London to Brighton (fig.3), Cambridge, Oxford, Southampton, Tunbridge Wells, Guildford and Birmingham, among others.

Cole also wrote general articles, and paragraphs of news called 'gossip, accident and progress of works' for the Railway Chronicle, and on at least one occasion, in March 1846, acted as sole editor. His work brought him into contact with Wentworth Dilke's associate, John Scott Russell, a Scot whom Cole first met over dinner at Rowland Hill's. Russell had arrived in London with a huge reputation as a naval architect, an engineer and an experimental scientist. He had made discoveries about the behaviour of waves and the displacement of ships' hulls. In 1837 he was given the Gold Medal of the Royal Society of Edinburgh for his paper $\mathfrak { s } _ { \mathrm { O n } }$ the Laws of Resistance of Floating Bodies' (reprinted in the Athenaeum), and he was elected a fellow and made a member of the council. He also made a name at the British Association. He was at the cutting edge of experiments with steam propulsion, both on land and at sea. He would have liked to be a university academic, but had to earn his living as manager of a shipbuilding firm on the Clyde, where he designed and supervised the building of some of the first ocean-going steam ships for the Royal Mail Line. He realized that if he stayed there he would never be anything more than an employee, so he moved to London with his wife, two children and hardly any money. They lived in a two-roomed flat in Westminster, which was so full of books that they could hardly move. He and Cole became close friends and they were in many ways very similar: within a few weeks of the same age, clever and energetic, and in their different ways ambitious. Russell was determined to reach the top in his chosen career, while Cole's ambition was tempered by a public-spirited streak that he had learned as a youth from John Stuart Mill. Russell was un-pompous (though pomposity was an occasional vice of Cole's), but not unaggressive. Cole, never physically violent, was amazed to see Russell knock a man down for pulling at his arm at Croydon Station one day (5 October 1845) when he and Cole had been out together for a long Sunday walk. Russell was in charge of the money at the Chronicle and at first Cole was paid £2 2s. od. a week and then $\mathcal { L } _ { 3 }$ , with extra for 'compilation' or editing.

Inevitably the Railway Chronicle was involved with railway politics. The most immediate threat to the efficiency and the future of the railway network was the difference in the gauges. The Great Western Railway, which by 1845 extended from

One of the earliest railways was that for fullers earth, between Wadworth nd Merstham, bot it whs never wurked prottably: it haa now been purebased and closed by the Brighton Railway: In 1805 an experiment was tried to see how much one horse could draw on it, und 36 tons were con. veyed six miles in an hour and 40 miuutes. gay their projectors more thun 10 per cent, and that at lent 100,000,000. sterling have been embarked in them.There is a trudition that the road of the old Filgrims to Thomas à Hecket's shrine t Canterbury, whose progrtes Blake and Stothard is pietores, ad Chaucer in imortal ee have made famous, passed at the foot of the Chalk Hills in the parish of Merstham.
