Figure Part of Cole's Railuay Travelling Chart, London  Brighton, showing the line between Croydon and Merstham.

London to Exeter by way of Bristol, was the most effcient and successful company at that time. Its track was brilliantly engineered by Brunel, and ran on a gauge of seven feet and half an inch. Brunel had invented this width of rail for his first railway to Bristol, and he believed passionately that this was the best and only width to use, clinging to his invention through thick and thin. However, the first railway in Liverpool in 1829 had been built to a gauge of 5 ft 8% ins  the 'narrow gauge' which became the almost universal width for the rest of the country. By 1845 there were 2,00o miles of narrow gauge to only 300 miles of Brunel's 'broad gauge'. Early in that year the Great Western applied to the government for permission - a private act was needed  to extend its network to Oxford, Worcester and Wolverhampton and to join it with the South Wales Railway, of which Brunel was also the engineer. This brought opposition to the broad gauge into the House of Commons, and on 25 June Cobden asked for a royal commission to consider bringing in an act to make all railways, existing and to be built, conform to the narrow gauge. The report of the commission was published early in 1846 and favoured the narrow gauge. Brunel, who 1o years earlier had written 'It's a proud thing to monopolise all the West as I He directors and investors were influential people. He wrote a long pamphlet, Observations on the Report of the Gauge Commissioners and distributed it to all Members of Parliament. The result of this lobbying was that a clause was inserted in the Gauge Bil, which allowed him to continue using the wide gauge and to build the railways that he was planning to his seven foot width.

After the bill became law in August 1846, Cole was inspired to intervene with a pamphlet of his own, addressed to George Carr Glyn, the chairman of the London and Birmingham Railway. This trumpeted his frustration at the way that the supporters of the narrow gauge had stood by doing nothing while Brunel was allowed to have his way. 'Keeping aloof' from controversy, said Cole, was 'an egregious mistake'. 'Public questions are not carried in this country upon their intrinsic merits. It is by the energies of those whose private interests are touched by them.' In the battle of the gauges, the London and Birmingham directors had shown 'feeble supineness', and now ´uniformity of gauge is temporarily sacrificed, and worse still for us, a ruinous competitive battle will have eventually to be fought between us and the Great Western'.28 The London and North Western Railway was the company most threatened by the incursions of the broad gauge, and, roused by Cole's pamphlet, 'they engaged my services to create a public opinion to support Uniformity of Gauge as best for national interests'. Once more Cole was in business as a lobbyist.

By this time the L.N.W.R.'s lines stretched from London to Edinburgh, but anyone wanting to travel from the South or West of England to Scotland had to change gauges at Gloucester. Those in favour of the narrow gauge foresaw that if the broad gauge continued its expansion there would be more junctions where passengers would need to change trains, as Cole had pointed out in his pamphlet. Cole got permission from Dilke and Russell to spend £ 1 5o on leaflets and maps to be put out by the Railway Chronicle. His principal allies in the agitation were the two Dilkes, John Scott Russell and John Chorley, who wrote for the Railway Chronicle and reviewed for the Athenaeum. Cole himself wrote at least five pamphlets, besides his week-toweek work for the Chronicle. His most successful pamphlet, which ran into three editions, was entitled A railway traveller's reasons for adopting uniformity of gauge addressed to I. K. Brunel, Esq. Cole suggested that Brunel should put himself in the place of a commercial traveller who had to change trains at Gloucester at night to go to Cheltenham,

with all your proper equipments, of samples, and packages, and great coats, and feet wrapper, and neck wrapper, and night cap and umbrella, and carpet bag, and dressing case. Make an honest actual experiment of it, and though you yourself are the inventor of the Broad Gauge, I will wager any stakes, that you pronounce the break of Gauge an unsufferable nuisance, and that you return to London a wiser man, bent on annihilating the monster you have created, and were contemplating to spread throughout the length and breadth of England.30

Although most of Cole's railway pamphlets were printed in ordinary, everyday type, this one was printed, perhaps as a courteous gesture to a great man, in Cole's favourite Old Caslon typeface, its anachronistic look being enhanced by the use of long S's. The old-fashioned appearance of the pamphlet was in comic contrast to its subject — the latest technology of the day.

As in his earlier lobbying, Cole was not content with words alone. He arranged for Henry Townsend to make drawings of travellers and porters struggling with goods, luggage, children, babies, horses and dogs to change platforms, which were reproduced in the Illustrated London News in 1846 (plate 15). Cole and Thackeray visited Gloucester together, which resulted in Thackeray writing two sketches published in Punch in May and June 1 846. In the previous year, Thackeray had written a series, 'Jeames Diary', about Jeames Plush, a footman, who makes £3o,ooo out of railway speculation, leaves his place and sets himself up as James de le Pluche (of Norman descent). Then he loses all his money, and has to revert to being Jeames Plush the footman again. In $\mathtt { r 8 4 6 }$ , Thackeray had a new series in Punch, 'The Snobs of England', but he interrupted this to write two more Jeames skits, to help Cole in his campaign.

These efforts were of no avail, and the broad gauge continued its advance until Brunel's death in 1859 caused his campaign to lose its impetus. The broad gauge was supplanted, though it lingered on the Great Western line to Penzance until the 1890s. Somehow Cole remained on friendly terms with Brunel, though his opposition was so outspoken. They knew each other personally, meeting sometimes at the house of Cole's friend, the artist John Horsley, whose elder sister was Brunel's wife. Brunel's sister was married to Benjamin Hawes, whom Cole knew from the select committee on the records. A further link existed in Scott Russell, with whom Brunel collaborated, towards the end of his life, in the building of the Great Eastern steamship.

The campaign for the single gauge may not have been a success, but as a result of his involvement in railways Cole obtained another commission as a lobbyist, when he was made the public relations man for a newly projected dock at Great Grimsby. Railways and docks were often constructed at the same time, as Cole realised when he was writing his spoof prospectus. In the past, docks had been built as farinland as possible on tidal rivers, in order to cut down the cost of transporting goods over land. With the advent  railways and larger ships, it became more practicable tobuild large docks on the coast and move goods to and from them by rail. Grimsy, in

Lincolnshire, was well placed at the mouth of the Humber, and had been a prosperous port in the middle ages. But by the late 183os it had declined so much that little ports along the East coast like Boston, Wisbech, Gainsborough and Spalding had more trade than it had. Lord Yarborough was the principal landowner in the town and in the country around, and he and other worthies took over some small railway companies to form the Manchester, Sheffeld and Lincolnshire Railways Company, so as to bring the line to Grimsby. There was also a new gas company, and a company to build a pier. The earl of Yarborough was chairman of all three companies. In the early $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 4 \mathrm { { O S } }$ , they expanded their ideas into a project for a deep water dock, capable of holding over a thousand ships, with connecting railways to carry goods to and from London, Sheffield and Manchester. When the news of the passing of the necessary act of Parliament came through in $\tau 8 4 5$ , the good citizens of Grimsby rang the bells of their beautiful but crumbling medieval parish churches.

In the autumn of $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 4 7$ Cole stayed two nights at Brocklesby Park, Lord Yarborough's house about 1o miles from Grimsby. Also present were John Fowler, engineer for the railway, John Jobson Smith, a stove manufacturer from Sheffield and deputy chairman of the railway company, and Mowbray Morris, manager of The Times. They discussed the project and travelled about to Louth, Grimsby and New Holland to inspect the works. His lordship amused them in other ways. He took them to see the family mausoleum, and after dinner he led them by a covered way to the stables to watch his 6o or so horses being tucked up for the night in their white nightcaps.31

Cole set to work with his usual vigour to get publicity for the cause, and pictures and paragraphs of news soon began to appear in The Times, the Illustrated London News and the Railway Chronicle. A feature in the Illustrated London News of 15 August $\textrm { r } 8 4 8$ praised the new link between the railway and the Humber ferry. An illustration shows passengers tottering aboard the ferry from the old landing stage, and the text emphasises that wet feet will be a thing of the past when passengers can alight from their trains and walk down a covered staircase to the boats. On 7 June Lord Yarborough asked Cole to find out whether Prince Albert would lay the foundation stone of the new docks. Cole called on the prince's equerry, Colonel Phipps, on 19 June, taking with him a pamphlet written by his friend Samuel Sidney on the importance of the new railway lines to agriculture in Lincolnshire. This seemed to do the trick, and Phipps sent a formal letter of acceptance.

For a salaried civil servant to be moonlighting as a public relations consultant might have been deemed odd. Cole's accounts for $\textrm { I } 8 4 8$ show that his official salary at the records amounted to $\pounds _ { 4 3 } 6 \ \mathrm { ~ r ~ } 6 s . \ 8 d .$ , which was exceeded by his fee of £500 from the Manchester, Sheffield and Lincolnshire Railway Company (and he received a further £100 in $_ { \textrm { I } 8 4 9 } )$ . What might be deemed even odder was the involvement of the Prince Consort in this affair. Indeed, Cole did get into hot water. On 22 June, Palgrave told Cole that 'his attention had been called to my visit to Pr: Albert', and that he had reported it to Lord Langdale. The latter summoned Cole for an 'unofficial' interview, where he stressed that 'literary labour was admitted but not other without leave'. Presumably he gave Cole leave to carry on working for the Grimsby project, since to ban him from it after he had involved Prince Albert would have been too embarrassing. But discretion was called for.

When the time came for the laying of the foundation stone, Cole was put in charge of the ceremony, planned for 18 April 1849. The prince was to travel up in the royal train, and, anxious that the royal mind should not be unoccupied during the journey, Cole assembled a collection of books on the archaeology and agriculture of Lincolnshire, together with a portfolio of prints and watercolours. He also borrowed some paintings from Richard Redgrave, Thomas Creswick and Charles Cope, but the authorities would not let him hang them in the prince's carriage. At the London station on 17 April, Cole was pleased to be acknowledged by a bow from the prince, and he travelled in the same train, arriving at Grimsby at 1 o'clock. The prince received a loyal address from the mayor before going to Brocklesby Hall to stay with Lord Yarborough. On the way he was accompanied by a cavalcade of 8oo horsemen. He was then taken out in an open carriage to admire the farming of Lincolnshire. The weather was very cold, with unseasonal snow.

It snowed again early on the morning of 18 April, but nonetheless huge crowds came into the town. There was fluttering bunting everywhere, and a squadron of ships lay off-shore decorated with flags borrowed from the dockyard at Woolwich. On the offices of the Dock Company 'were the Royal Colours, purple, quite new, and inscribed as follows:- Long Live Queen Victoria. Welcome to the Prince. Prosperity to the M. S. & L. and Grimsby Docks. Long may the noble Earl of Yarborough's efforts be crowned with success'32 The prince and his party arrived by train, and at the entrance to the docks the carriages were unhitched from the locomotive, and 'were drawn along by a whole host of navvies dressed in clean white frocks, and showing as they pulled "with a will" the great brawny arms by the strength of which they earn their livelihood'.33

'During this part of the ceremony a formidable snowstorm came on, and the scene in the amphitheatre [where the ceremony was to take place] was ridiculous in the extreme. The wind blew in the faces of the spectators, and the snow and hail fell so copiously that at length every back was turned from the platform, and for five minutes nothing could be seen of the company but an indistinct mass apparently consisting of white umbrellas, nether garments and hoar frost.'34 This was a pity for the mayor and aldermen of the town, who had ordered new robes from London. However, the sky cleared, and the royal party came down the steps to a platform well below the low tide mark, where there were seats for five thousand spectators. The ships in the Haven fired a royal salute, and six military bands played nearby. Here the prince laid the foundation stone (plate 16), attended by a small procession which included John Fowler bearing a glass vase to contain coins under the stone, and Cole carrying in a pur s hichhehaslyta o e Royal Mint.Aterfire, and a blessing from the bishop of Lincoln, lunch was taken by 6oo guests, with the prince, in a tent put up by Mr B. Edgington of Duke Street, Tooley Street, London, partaking of food provided by the London Tavern. All were transported back to Grimsy byhe ilwy.Colewas theving ital antravelle back tLnn in the prince's train. This was the first time that Cole choreographed a royal ceremoa s ha ew a It h sion he programmed himself into the proceedings as ceremonial coin-carrier.

A severance, Cole persuaded The Times to publish an announcement in English,

French and German advertising the docks. The German text appeared in German Gothic print (fraktur), the first time that such an announcement had been printed in this way in an English newspaper. Thackeray noticed it when he was in Hamburg, having dinner with Thomas Baring. Seeing the unusual type in The Times, Thackeray exclaimed, 'That's Cole's doing!' Cole claimed that the effect of the advertisement on the trade of the docks was immediate.35

This brought to an end Cole's work for the railways. He had not influenced the battle of the gauges very much, and interest in it had tailed off. The Railway Chronicle ceased publication in 1849, when Dilke, Scott Russell and Cole took on the publicity for the Great Exhibition of 185 1. This great project came as the culmination of Cole's interest in matters of art and design, which had increasinglyocupied him through the 1840s, and to which we now turn.

# PART THREE: ART AND INDUSTRY

# CHAPTER 6

# Felix Summerly

Henry Cole, after he had become a fgure of authority in the art word, would quite often be attacked as an untrained ignoramus in artistic matters. Indeed, even one of the most zealous defenders of his posthumous reputation, Sir Henry Trueman Wood, said that 'he had no aesthetic judgment and no artistic power'' Did Cole really know nothing about art? How did he become involved in it?

At least he began with some capacity for the co-ordination of hand and eye, as is witnessed by his calligraphic abilities at school. We have seen, too, that he took drawing lessons from David Cox and Charles Wild in the 182os, and was encouraged by Elizabeth Palgrave. We shall see that in due course he took up etching. He believed that his creative capacities included an artistic element, 'some instinct which, although not sufficiently cultivated to make me an artist, I have always felt I possessed'.2

As to his capacity to appreciate art, we know that from early adulthood he visited art galleries.³ In the winter of 1827-8 he attended lectures on anatomy, perspective, painting and sculpture at the Royal Academy, and would have continued with this course the following year, but found that the lecturers just repeated themselves annually.4 By this time he had evidently developed architectural and antiquarian interests, because he tells us that the Dean of Westminster gave him permission to draw in the abbey 'as early as 1827'.5 There is also some evidence of artistic sensibility in his early travel journals. He made a tour in Scotland in 1833, sending back to his fiancée, Marian, conscientious descriptions of the terrain, assessing it as it presented itself to 'the discerning eye of the Artist', and dropping in references to Richard Payne Knight, high priest of the cult of the picturesque. Townscape as well as landscape attracted his criticism, as when in Edinburgh he asserted that 'throughout the New Town . . . instances of bad taste are perpetually forcing themselves on the attention'.6 The following year he and Marian, now married, travelled to Cornwall. 'In respect of the Picturesqueness of the County . . ', commented Cole, 'I should not give it a very strong character . .. . The grandeur or quaintness or boldness of every scene pleases but they do not furnish sufficiency for the pencil to work upon.' Nonetheless, he and Marian did make a number of sketches.7 In Yorkshire in 1835, Cole was assiduous in architecture-spotting, assigning columns and tracery to their correct periods. He also continued to lay down the law on taste. Of Beverley Minster he said: 'This relique of the catholic Church is preserved in good repair . . . and excepting the abominable whitewash, which has been daubed throughout the interior, the reparations and attentions of modern times are not altogether in bad taste.' After admiring a little medieval church at Hemingborough, he disparaged modern churches: 'They are erected upon a lathe & Plaster sentiment of hypocrisy - wanting firmness, consistency & feeling - and the physical materials are of corresponding flimsiness and rottenness.'\*8 From his journals, then, one can see that Cole had some knowledge of the history of art and architecture, and cultivated aesthetic responses, albeit at a fairly commonplace level.

As a tyro journalist, Cole wrote as a critic of music and drama, but not, it seems, of art, until early in 1838 he embarked on a substantial article for the London and Westminster Review on contemporary wood engraving - a well-researched survey that is still informative to present-day scholars. Wood engraving, a more subtly expressive technique than traditional woodcut because it used finer tools on harder wood, was pioneered by Thomas Bewick $\left( \mathbb { I } 7 5 3 \mathrm { - } \mathbb { I } 8 2 8 \right)$ While Bewick worked for most of his life in his native Northumberland, the new technique of wood engraving necessarily moved to London, the centre of publishing. Here Robert Branston $\left( \mathrm { I } 7 7 8 \mathrm { - } \mathrm { I } 8 2 7 \right)$ stood at the head of a 'London school' of wood engravers. It was his disciples whom Cole studied, still quite a small group of craftsmen (and some women). In May 1838 and through the summer, Cole went to see engravers such as John Jackson, Orrin Smith and John Thompson $\left( \mathrm { I } 7 8 \varsigma \mathrm { - } \mathrm { I } 8 6 6 \right)$ , the final name being generally regarded as the leading exponent of wood engraving in the 1830s.

By the autumn, Cole had become firm friends with Thompson, and with his family. Thompson was 53 in 1838, and had two sons somewhat younger than Cole: Charles Thurston, born in 1816 and Richard Anthony, born in 1819. Thurston was to marry Marian Cole's sister Charlotte in 1857, and, moving over from wood engraving, in which he had been trained, into photography, joined Cole's South Kensington Museum as its principal photographer. Richard also joined the museum when it opened in South Kensington in 1857, and ended his working life there as an assistant director. John Thompson lived in Kensington, partly because it was convenient for Chiswick, where the printer with whom he mostly dealt, Charles Whittingham, had his press. Whittingham also was soon to become one of Cole's important collaborators. As the Coles lived in Kensington at various addresses, the Thompsons were near enough to be neighbours, and the families constantly visited each other.

While friendship with Thompson brought Cole to the fringes of the artistic world, his professional contacts with artists seem to have begun in the following year, when, implementing the penny post under Rowland Hill, he had to commission designs for stamps and envelopes. Naturally he went to the top first, consulting the president of the Royal Academy, but eventually arrived at William Mulready. 'On the 13th December, 1839, I made my first visit to Mr. Mulready, and began an acquaintance which lasted til is deathn8.Mulready, who was f Irih birth though he had lived in London from the age of six, was a near contemporary of John Thompson, having been born in $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 7 8 6$ He had a wild streak in his nature, which was exercised, perhaps relatively harmlessly, in his enthusiasm for prize-fighting, but which had, more damagingly, led him into an injudiciously young marriage and an acrimonious breakup.Hermai reonsible or hi children as a lone part, and hismiy relations were not without violence. He was a superb draughtsman, and a rather slow painter. His paintings, mostly scenes of ordinary life, had (surprisingly for such a rough man) an exquisitely elegant balance and finish, and vibrant colouring. He lived in LindenGrove,Kensington GravelPits (now Linden Gardens, Notting Hill Gate),

Another local contac inartistic circs wasSrAugusts Callcott (7798, who was a very successful landscape painter and Royal Academician, although inevitably somewhat overshadowed by Turner. Cole got to know him through his physician, Dr Merriman, in February 1841. Callcott also lived in Kensington Gravel Pits, and it was largely hrough the influnce of the Callco that a group oartiss settled in this suburb of London'10 The gravel pits that gave the area its name had been worked, it seems, since the Middle Ages. They were found to the north and south of the present Bayswater Road, around Notting Hill Gate. The area was thout tobe 'prculary healthy ct t ry l n rang 'a village of houses, embowered in trees, had casually grown up around the pits.

Callcott introduced Cole to his wife Maria, who became an important influence during the short time Cole knew her before her death. A travel writer and art historian, she had been bedridden for some years, but presided over an artistic salon, in a room in which she had created an exotic setting for herself. 'Vines had been trained as natural blinds, and living arabesques were made among the shoots and branches by the mice and birds, as they came half-tamed, to take the meals that Lady Callcott daily placed for them.' In the room 'prints, choice and rare as works of art, or associated with loved objects, covered the walls, unless otherwise occupied by paintings or sculptures, memorials of Wilkie and Chantrey and others'.12 Cole took to her immediately. 'She is a sharp witted woman, fitful as it seemed, rather trying after effect & talking blue & off hand.'13 Another time he said of her: 'I like the fine hearty sense of truth in her, & the absence of all humbug: she said no taste was bad taste but an exclusive taste.'14 Maria Callcott loved children, and always welcomed Laetitia Cole when her father brought her to visit, once giving her a book and a doll.15 She wrote stories for children, the most famous being Little Arthur's History of England (183 5). Cole helped her by doing the legwork involved in putting her last children's book, The Little Brackenburners and Little Mary's Four Saturdays, through the press.

Augustus Callcott 'had close links with many continental artists. His interests, which included German and Italian primitives as well as the German Nazarenes, proved a formative influence on younger friends, including William Dyce'.16 Through the Callcott circle, Cole enlarged his acquaintance with artists. His contacts with Dyce, who was two years older, were not close, though the schools of design were an arena in which both men worked. Dyce was soon to take a leading part in the 'fresco revival', the project keenly advocated by Prince Albert, to decorate the principal rooms of the new Houses of Parliament with frescos depicting scenes of British history. Other collaborators in this scheme became known to Cole. One of these was John Callcott Horsley (18 17-1903), a nephew of Augustus Callcott, who also lived in the Gravel Pits village. Another was J. R. Herbert ( 18 10-90), a friend of Pugin and a colleague of Dyce at the School of Design. He lived in Gloucester Road, a little way south of Kensington. H. J. Townsend (18 10-90), also involved in the fresco revival and a colleague of Dyce at the School of Design, lived in Old Brompton, not far away. Yet another fresco painter was Charles West Cope (181 1-90), who lived in Hyde Park Gate, just south of Kensington Gore. In an adjoining house lived his close friend

Richard Redgrave (18o4-88). Redgrave was a painter, who provided a cartoon for a Westminster fresco but did not execute it; he turned to art administration and became Cole's closest, lifelong associate. Cope stood as godfather to Cole's son Alan. Other Kensington painters, outside the fresco group, were Callcott's nephew Thomas Creswick (181 1-69), a landscape painter, and the genre painter Thomas Webster (18oo-86). Both lived in the Gravel Pits village. Not far away, in Victoria Road (running south from Kensington High Street), lived the sculptor John Bell $\left( \vec { : } \mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 \mathrm { ~ I ~ I ~ } { - } 9 5 \right)$ 17 Cole got to know all these artists in the early 1840s, and socialised with them frequently. Webster took him to a meeting of the Graphic Society, which Cole called 'a sort of artists conversazione', reputedly the best of such groups.18 It was here (for he attended quite frequently) that Cole was introduced to Creswick and Redgrave. It will be noted that, while Callcott and Mulready were of an older generation - and so was John Linnell (1792-1882), resident in Porchester Terrace, Bayswater, to whom Mulready introduced Cole - the others were almost all of an age with Cole. Quite suddenly, he found himself part of an artistic peer group, and a group that looked to Prince Albert as its leader.

Cole's first formal engagement with art was in a literary mode: he wrote guidebooks to historic and artistic sights in or near London. The first was to Hampton Court, published in 1841 (fig.4). The Queen had allowed the public to visit Hampton Court since 18 3 8 and Cole already knew it well, having studied the building during holidays spent nearby at Shepperton and Weybridge. He decided that the pictures on display there had already been well described by other authors, so he concentrated on the building, and the restorations being done at that time by Thomas Willement, who was the best restoration architect of the day, famous as an expert on heraldry, and the first to design stained-glass windows in a believable medieval style. Cole became friendly with him, and was invited to Willement's house in London where he admired his collection of medieval artefacts, or, as Cole was still heedless enough to call them, 'knick knackeries'. Later on he visited Davington Priory, Willement's house in Kent, soon after he bought it.19 Although the two men did not become close friends, acquaintance with Willement provided Cole not only with a link to architectural history but also, as will be seen, with useful contacts in other directions.

Cole's Handbook was declared to be 'By Felix Summerly Esq. With embellishments engraved on wood by ladies'. The nom de plume was adopted because convention precluded a public servant from publishing under his own name. No one knows why he chose 'Felix Summerly', but it suggests happiness in the summertime and is typical of Cole and his ebullient and cheerful personality. The 'ladies' who made the illustrations were not just females, but ladies of rank: Lady Callcott and Lady Palgrave. Cole also called on other female friends and relations to help out with illustrations, and he drew some himself, as did David Cox junior.

Humpton Court (which had been published by H. Cunningham, with whom Cole fell out)" was followed in the next year by Westminster Abbey (published by George Bell, who went on to publish the rest of the series). The abbey was another building that Cole knew exceptionally well, having worked in the Chapter House, and made drawings of the tombs in the 1820s. He claimed to be the first to notice the artistic mr the mue say i is autirahy that e buildi  a p museum of illustrations of the Technical Arts, from the days of Edward the Confessor to the present time, of sculptures, brasses, mural paintings, glass, mosaics bronzes, &c'.21 Again Lady Callcott and Lady Palgrave drew some of the illustrations, but David Cox junior did a couple as well, as did Cole himself. The engraving was done by Marian's sisters Laura and Charlotte Bond, and by John Thompson's sister Eliza and his daughters Augusta and Isabel.

The first two guidebooks were very successful. Westminster Abbey, for instance, was hailed as 'a well arranged and correct description, neither too popular nor too pedantic'.22 Both books were translated into French, and ran into many editions. Cole then wrote a series of handbooks, to the National Gallery, the Soane Museum, the Society of Arts, and other galleries that were open to the public free of charge. In those dealing with paintings he introduced little outline wood engravings, hardly

Figure 4   
A wood-engraved   
design for a cover or frontispiece for the guidebook to   
Hampton Court,   
1843.

![](images/1b21d4e6a6a781d43901d5b30d7a34925668f42af0e307746561d753416c440e.jpg)

br tha  postagestamp, many the picture. Thee coul ny ncin aides-mémoire, or 'reminiscences' as he called them,23 but the provision of images after Old Master paintings in a guidebook was most unusual at the time, and is another sign of Cole's enterprising originality.24

Among Cole's guidebooks was a slim pamphlet on the Temple Church (fig.5), where restorations by Thomas Willement had just been completed. For the floors of the church, Willement had bought tiles from Herbert Minton, the most innovative and successful china manufacturer in the world, based at Stoke-on-Trent. These tiles, a re-creation of a medieval art form, 'were made after the models of the ancient and most interesting pavement in the Chapter House of Westminster Abbey'.25 Willement introduced Cole to Minton (plate 2 1), and their collaboration and friendship persisted throughout Cole's career. Their first joint project was to bring out an educational toy, consisting of small coloured tiles, made by Minton, using a process recently invented by Richard Prosser for which Minton owned half the patent.26 Cole called it the 'Tesselated Pastime', and he wrote a booklet to go with it, in which a juvenile Felix Summerly instructs two little girls in the 'principles', as he significantly calls them, of flat pattern making. The toy came out in time for Christmas 1843. Lord Langdale told Cole that his daughter, who was about six years old, "was delighted' with the toy and the booklet, 'which he had to read 6 times a day'.27 This pleasingly responsive girl was later to marry the Count Teleki, and to be a benefactress of the South Kensington Museum.

The guidebook to Hampton Court appeared in a second and expanded edition in 1843. Here Cole upgraded the historical information, making the publication more scholarly. It received a somewhat critical review in the Athenaeum, which deprecated the increased amount of 'antiquarian information', preferring the earlier, chattier tone. 'Popular Guide Books should be brief. We do not object to Felix Summerly's gossiping and suggestive style; indeed we think it a great merit; it helps people to a pleasant idea, and gives its humanizing moral to inanimate things.'28 For once in his career as an art publicist, Cole was criticised for being academic rather than populist. In a publicity flier for a later handbook, he claimed that what he wanted to provide for his readers was 'the guidance of a good natured and intelligent friend'. The friend would be willing to match his reader's pace, loitering or hurrying as occasion demanded, but he would also be authoritative, supplying 'judicious guidance' that would help the reader to a 'correct' view.29

Cole's guides were sufficiently successful to bring him trouble with imitators. Over Hamptn Court he had to takeut an nuncon t prevet Ear Jee depuy head of Woods and Forests, from selling his version of the guide. Over the National Gallery he clashed with no less a person than the famous radical Member of Parliament, Joseph Hume. When a piracy of Cole's book appeared under Hume's name, selling for one penny, Cole was encouraged to get an injunction by Edward Protheroe and Mulready. But after calling on Hume and finding him 'as garrulous auhi sCle v n h waso

Col not nly wroe e tex  h books ancbute  el u alsodratingowiiters ththeiboo prducn, decorative covers andtte pages.3 The books led him to acquire some pracical experience of tourism administration. For instance, selling the guides at the sites required organisation and tactful negotiation.32 Ensuring that pictures were numbered to correspond with information in the guides was a task he had to do himself both at Hampton Court, and at Cobham Hall.33 After seeing two thousand people turned away from Hampton Court on a Friday, its closed day, Cole compiled a Summerly guide with accurate opening times for all London attractions, the Handbook for holidays spent in and near London (1842). Hampton Court remained a matter of concern. In December 18 59 Cole published a pamphlet advocating that the palace's interiors should be 'restored as much as possible to their original state of decoration', and that its works of art should be conserved.34 On the face of it, a pioneering conservationist tract, this had a hidden agenda, underlying its proposal that works of fne art at Hampton Court should be 'brought to the Metropolis' By 1859 Cole had already carried off many objects from the palace for his new museum at South Kensington, and in due course he secured the Raphael cartoons.35

Having achieved some success with the guidebooks, and perhaps having formed a taste for small-format publications, Cole now turned to children's books. By 1843 his two little girls were aged five and three, and the first boy, Henry Hardy, was born in March of that year. Cole was looking for books for the children, and he found that tvilabrorh narha eThe w didactic or too vulgar, and for the illustrations any daubs were thought good enough. Nursery rhymes and fairy tales were almost forgotten. 'Little Red Riding Hood, and other fairy tales hallowed to children's use, are now turned into ribaldry as satires for men; as for the creation of a new fairy tale or touching ballad, such a thing is unheard of.236

![](images/c796324b94acb0e2d8c0aff16cfbfe7de5bdb52aa98f2f68442db38d4d007702.jpg)  
FigurePages, printe in red and black, rom A glance at the Temple Church, 1844

Most children's books of the time, claimed Elizabeth Rigby - a periodical essayist of some weight, who was to marry Sir Charles Eastlake - contained 'an insipid tale [that] goes feebly wriggling through an unmerciful load of moral, religious, and scientific preaching'. Such books, she said regretfully, 'have been the most abundant and characteristic product of the present day, not intended, we are assured, for school use, but, we speak equally from experience and observation, with no charm for any other'. 37 Cole's friend Thackeray was equally severe on the books that he had had as a child:

Abominable attempts were made in those days to make useful books for children, and cram science down their throats as calomel used to be administered under the pretence of a spoonful of currant-jelly. Such picture-books as we had were illustrated with the most shameful, hideous, old wood-cuts which had lasted through a century . . . Some painted pictures there were in our time likewise, but almost all of the very worst kind; the hideous distortions of Rowlandson, who peopled the picture-books with bloated parsons in periwigs, tipsy aldermen and leering salacious nymphs, horrid to look at.38

Cole set out to produce books that, in both text and illustrations, would appeal to the imagination of children. He wanted both to amuse his own children and to reform children's literature.

His publishers, up to 1843, had been Charles Knight, George Bell and Wood, Hugh Cunningham and, for pamphlets, Henry Hooper. Of these, Bell and Wood, who were publishing the guidebooks, seemed the most likely to be interested in a series of children's books. But when he approached George Bell he found no support, because Bell's partner, Wood, had absconded, leaving him short of working capital. Bell did, however, continue to publish the guidebooks. In March 1843, Cole had the good fortune to meet a new publisher: the 25-year-old, red-haired Joseph Cundall,39 who had just taken over a shop in New Bond Street, known as The Juvenile Library. He had already written some children's books and published others. With him Cole embarked on a long series of children's books that he called 'The Home Treasury'.40

Cole paid great attention to the appearance of his books, both outside and inside. They were small for small hands to handle. The bindings were decorative in themselves, with designs related to the contents of the book.He took the trouble to go to the British Museum to look at covers and bindings that he could use as models. Though he wrote some of the stories himself, he roped in others, and Marian, Charles Cole and Harriett Mill all wrote for him. Marian, in the thin disguise of 'Mrs Felix Summerly', was acknowledged as the author of The Mother's Primer (plate 17). Charles Whittingham the Younger of Chiswick, one of the best printers in the country and a friend of John Thompson, printed the books. John Mill encouraged Cole by saying that 'Felix Summerly would be as notorious as the Brothers Grimm'.41

The old tales retold in 'The Home Treasury' included Little Red Riding Hood, Beauty and the Beast, Jack and the Bean Stalk, Cinderella, Jack the Giant Killer, Sleeping Beauty and Dick Whittington, plus the less well known Sir Hornbook, Reynard the Fox, and Chevy Chase. There was also a volume of nursery rhymes. The longest text was Cole's version of Berthold Niebuhr's Heroic Tales of Ancient Greece.

Cole announced that all 'The Home Treasury' books 'will be illustrated, but not after the usual fashion of children's books, in which it seems to be assumed that the lowest kind of art is good enough to give first impressions to a child. In the present series, though the statement may perhaps excite a smile, the illustrations will be selected from the works of Raffaelle, Titian, Hans Holbein, and other old masters. Some of the best modern artists have kindly promised their aid in creating a taste for beauty in little children'.42 Cole's triumph was to persuade the fashionable painters who were now his friends to do the illustrations. So Mulready, Redgrave, Horsley, Webster, Townsend, Linnell and his sons, and Cope all worked for him, together with Frederick Tayler (1802-89), who specialised in hunting scenes, and Henry Corbould (1787-1844). The pictures were lithographed and hand-coloured. Cole himself coloured the illustrations in the set of these books that he sent to the Queen for her children, not trusting anyone else. The series also included several books whose illustrations were drawn from the work of the Old Masters. Holbein's Bible Events, Raffaelle's Bible Events and Albert Durer's Bible Events had illustrations based on work by these three artists. An Alphabet of Quadrupeds included illustrations after Paul Potter, Teniers and Rembrandt, among others. 'Pictures by the Old Masters' illustrated A Century of Fables. In The Little Painter's Portfolio were to be found illustrations after Giotto, 'S. Del Piombo' and Holbein.43

Cole's series of books was an immediate success. Already in November 1843 a reviewer in the Athenaeum could greet the latest volumes with enthusiasm:

Another bouquet of flowers, immortals' as the florists would call them for they are not subject to change, and will be fresh and fragrant at Christmas, or on New Year's Day, or at Easter, or at any other and all other holiday seasons - for which our young friends are indebted to Felix Summerly. We earnestly hope that these little volumes will be welcomed in every family: the paths of learning may thus be made paths of pleasure, and children's libraries become lustrous with beauty, pleasurable as well as profitable. The illustrations are greatly superior to what is usually met with, even in the best works of the class.44

It would be interesting to know who wrote this cordial review. Since Cole himself was writing regularly for the journal at the time, it is quite possible that he was the reviewer of his own work. However, there were other reviews, no less ardent. Thornton Leigh Hunt, for example, hailed the books as reaching a new level of good taste.

This is a novelty in infant literature; a novelty as important as it is daring. It is due to Felix Summerly, a name synonymous with taste. All the 'common sense' of the kingdom will be ready to declare that art is thrown away on children 'who cannot understand it'. Does 'common sense' itself understand it? No. Why not? Because it has not taste. Because in infancy its taste was never cultivated; because children's books were filled with glaring daubs of red and green, which were considered 'good enough' ... In bestowing our hearty approbation . . . let us not forget to thank the eminent artists for their good nature in thus lending their great talents to a task hitherto performed by the humblest draughtsman. Think of Mulready, Cope, Horsley, F. Tayler, Webster, Redgrave, and the rest illustrating children's books! In a country like England where 'appearances' are so highly prized, we can understand the sneers of ambitious mediocrity when hearing of such 'degradation'. But the above artists are superior to any fantastic pride. They thought  and justly  that their talents were not ignobly employed, in endeavouring to introduce a new element in early education.

In 'The Home Treasury' we see Cole at his most engaging, directing his overflowing vitality into a project that was both creatively satisfying to himself and sympathetic to the developing pleasures of his children.

Having embarked on publishing reproductions of classic art in his children's books, Cole was not slow to seize an opportunity to do it for adults also. On 3 November 1843, through the good offices of John Linnell, he met Henry Josi, keeper of prints and drawings at the British Museum, to look at the original woodblocks of Dürer's Small Passion. Josi had bought 35 (out of 37) of these for the museum in 1839. Josi 'said he thought I might have casts if I chose to apply'. Stereotypes were indeed made for Cole, from which he printed an edition of Dürer's designs. For The Passion of Our Lord Jesus Christ, pourtrayed by Albert Durer Cole wrote a 16-page preface, a serious excursion into art history, with a characteristic aside on the virtues of the (relatively) new technology of stereotyping.46 He published the book not under the name 'Felix Summerly' but under his own name - 'Henry Cole, An Assistant Keeper of the Public Records' - because, having laboured long over it, he regarded it as a work of scholarship. The book had an impressive imprint, being issued in 1844 by a consortium of superior publishers: Cundall, Pickering and Bell in London, with Parker of Oxford and Deighton of Cambridge.

Another publication for adults that Cole arranged was So much of the Diary of Lady Willoughby as relates to her Domestic History, and to the Eventful Period of the Reign of Charles the First. This, while purporting to be a seventeenth-century diary, was in fact a new work by Hannah Rathbone, a member of the well-known Liverpool Quaker family. Richard Rathbone, her husband, asked Cole to arrange its publication, anonymously, by Longmans. Whittingham designed a special pseudoseventeenth-century typeface for it, and the book appeared in an archaic-style binding in the autumn of 1844, though it could also be bought bound more cheaply in half leather or paper. It caused a great stir and several authors, including John Murray, were thought to be the inventors of it. It ran into several editions, and 'Some Further Portions'o the 'Diary' wee published in 1848, when it was amitted that a 'lady' had written it.

In varus ways, Cole move towars criial writing on art. His frst venture as an attempt to explain some basic principles to children. In 1838, his utilitarian friend Horace Grant had written a manual, Drawing for Young Children, with which Cole had helped before it was sent off to Charles Knight for publication. It claimed to 'furnish . . . the parent with a pleasant means of turning to account the wanton scrawls made by an infant on slate or letter cover' Cole followed it up with First Exercises in Light, Shade and Colour, with numerous illustrations, being a supplement to 'Drawing for Young Children'. The text of the book took the form of questions addressed to the young reader. This was a variant on a common device in educational children's books, described by Elizabeth Rigby: 'dialogues, where a profound mother and a docile child play alternately into each other's hands, and where a question is set up, like a ninepin, only to be knocked down by the next answer'48 Cole was not quite o prescriptive, for he provided questions but not answers, which were left to the child to supply from its own experience. The book also contained diagrams, some black and white and some hand-coloured, to demonstrate the relationship between different colours and shades. Cole's well-meaning intention was to encourage children to be observant and to enjoy their surroundings. In his preface he set out his aims.

The Art of seeing opens a long and never-tiring voyage of discovery where freshness and innocent excitement are never exhausted. Everyone may possess himself of a picture gallry o his own, as far superior in beauty to all Louves as Nature is to Ar,  where nothing is paid for entrance or maintenance  where all is gain and nothing loss. The desire to aid increasing numbers to enrich themselves with the powers of seeing and appreciating the beauties nature displays to the eye, rather than of teaching the rules of practice of art, is the object of this little work.49

The book s a creitable tept to t intothe mino a hiland i isnte that Cole says he attempted it because other such books 'have always seemed to the writer to take for granted the possession of a certain amount of observation and experience in those to whom they were addressed'. Cole might have been speaking for himself. He started with little knowledge of art, and one way to teach himself was to teach his children.

Let it not be thought, however, that Cole only engaged with art history and criticism at a childish level. As he prepared his handbooks he caught up with the latest literature, such as Anna Jameson's Handbook to the public galleries of art in and near London (1842), Franz Kugler's Hand-book to the History of painting (of which Part I, The Italian schools, appeared in 1842, edited by C. L. Eastlake), J. L. Petit's Remarks on church architecture (1841) - 'very interesting book' - and Joseph Gwilt's Encyclopaedia of architecture (1842).50 In his characteristically efficient way, Cole knew how to look things up, and was a member of the new London Library51 and a reader at the British Museum Library.\$2

As a critic, even in these early days, he seldom failed in confidence, as may be seen in three papers on house decoration which he wrote for the Athenaeum in 1843. Was he again drawing on personal experience, this time the experience of setting up his home with Marian in Notting Hill Square? At any rate, an interest in colour and the theory of colour was very much in the air. David Hay of Edinburgh, the principal interior decorator of the day, had written the standard work in the late 182os. Titled The Laws of Harmonious Colouring adapted to House Painting, it developed a system by which colours were analogous with harmony in music. The book ran into many editions and was still selling well in the 1 84os. Cole read it before he reviewed Charles Eastlake's translation of Goethe's Theory of Colours for the London and Westminster Review in 184o. It was still in his mind when he expounded his own theories in the Athenaeum articles. He pointed out that fashions in decoration change every seven years or . 'Sometimes the rage isfor warm shade of colouring, at othe for cold.' But it might be possible to find styles of decoration that would remain valid for ever.

It appears to us, that certain principles of decoration may be laid down, which, if recognized and applied, would make our dwellings much more cheerful and comfortable . . . not only without any additional cost, but would make the keep of them more economical, by rendering them, to a great degree, independent of the caprices of fashion. It is the absence of correct principles which causes decoration and furniture to be out of fashion  tiresome — palling to the eye, and subject to constant change,  whereas, what is really beautiful, being based on everlasting principles, is subject to no change.53

When Cole here again advocates 'principles' of design, we begin to recognise his emergence as a design reformer. As we have seen from his holiday journals, he had all the bumptiousness of youth in telling Marian what was in bad taste. Now, in the Athenaeum articles, he seemed ready to tell the world. His diary for I 1 January 1842 notes that he read Pugin's True principles of pointed or Christian architecture, published the previous year. He makes no comment on it, but in view of his ready deployment of the concept of 'true principlesin later debates about taste, it is tempting to believe that he was fired by the urgent, incisive prescriptions and interdicts of Pugin.

Anyone in the London art world in the 184os must have known about the project to decorate the new Houses of Parliament with frescoes.54 Cole moved in a circle of artists who were deeply involved in this project, so he naturally paid it some attention. Prompted by the first report of the Fine Arts Commission (which, under Prince Albert's chairmanship, was in charge of commissioning the frescoes), he persuaded William Hickson on 11 May 1842 to accept an article in the Westminster Review. This reveals him in the role of politician rather than art critic. After an initial review of the state of the arts in England, he took issue with the often expressed view that public money spent on artistic works would be wasted. This gave him the opportunity, since he was writing in the Westminster, to make a statement, from a utilitarian standpoint, in support of state subsidy of the arts.

It is the fashion to ascribe motives of economy, in respect of the arts, to the Radical party especially. We do not think that the Radical party are more enlightened on the subjet than other political parties, but it is only justice to them to say that they have generally regarded a grant for the encouragement of the fine arts as an outlay to promote public education, and have been more liberally disposed than the Whigs and not less so than the Tories, who assent to such grants for the good of the arts themselves, rather than for any moral effect they may have . . .55

After this, Cole went on to air another opinion derived from his utilitarian backgrdAlthug his arti as ben uc s npresin aith i r s aais mateursh poit ws reudiatin mtration yc The commission, Cole thought, was far too big. 'It is impossible to accomplish any thing great by a numerous agency. Who would think of fighting a battle with a Council of War, consisting of twenty-three commanding officers, and what single instance can be shown where a fine work of art has been produced by a committee or cai? Heogt e o okey the individual responsibility of Prince Albert, or Sir Robert Peel, who was one of the commissioners, or Charles Eastlake, who was secretary to the commission.

An exhibition of designs, or 'cartoons', for frescoes was opened in Westminster Hall in summer 1843. Cope, Horsley and Townsend were among the prizewinners. Even before the private view on 1 July, Cole had been setting up, on 26, 29 and 30 June, a 'speculation' with Longmans, to publish reproductions of the winning designs, to be made as lithographs by John Linnell and his sons. A portfolio of 1 1 illustrations for £5 was advertised in summer $\cdot 8 4 3 , ^ { 5 7 }$ and these lithographs eventually (a longer delay has taken place in . . . publication . . . than was anticipated')58 appeared. The preparatory process of commissioning designs for the frescoes was long drawn out (with three further competitions in 1844, 1845 and $\mathbf { I } 8 4 7 )$ , and when work started on the site (Dyce led off with his The Baptism of King Ethelbert in 1845) progress became even slower. Cole seems to have lost interest, for he liked quick results. On the other hand, he had a habit of retrieving good ideas that had not been achieved. The Westminster fresco project may have stayed in his memory as an inspiration for the future, and in that connection, it is worth pondering T. S. R. Boase's remark that 'the real successor to Westminster in scale and variety was the decoration of the South Kensington Museum'.59

The interior fittings and finishes of the new Palace of Westminster were eventually left to Barry and Pugin, but in the early stages the commissioners took an interest in these too. Craftsmen were invited to submit specimens of woodcarving, metalwork, stained glass and floor coverings,60 which were exhibited in spring 1844 in the former St James's Bazaar, King Street, St James's. Cole visited this exhibition on 22 April, and that same evening started work on an article about it. It is not known where (or if) his article appeared, but there was a detailed, two-part review of the exhibition in the Athenaeum on 27 April (pp.3878) and 4 May (pp.409-10). If Cole did not write this, he must have read it. He would have approved of the way it advocated sound 'principles', such as that 'the first object of decoration is its perfect and subordinate suitableness to its specific purpose'. The article remarks, prophetically: 'We cordially welcome this exhibition . . . as a beginning, by forcibly directing public attention to the subject: it is a thing, once begun, we shall hope to see followed up year by year, and year after year, we doubt not, to be able to record a progress.' This is almost a prescription for Cole's future life.

Cole's attempt to start the Historical Register in 1845 seems to have put paid to Felix Summerly's efforts as author and publisher, though Summerly was to be resurrected in the later 184os. From Summerly's earlier period, the product best remembered now was the first Christmas card, produced in 1843.61 The pictorial design on the card (plate 18), by J. C. Horsley, shows a happy family enjoying Christmas festivity, flanked by illustrations of charitable good works. For this first commercial Christmas card, Cole put an advertisement in the Athenaeum: 'Just published. A Christmas Congratulation Card: or picture emblematical of Old English Festivity to Perpetuate kind recollections between Dear Friends.' Horsley's illustration was printed lithographically in outline, and could be coloured in by hand. The price was 2d. plain, or 13 for 2s. Coloured 6d. each or 13 for 6s.62 It was thought that about I,ooo copies of the card were sold.63

In the early 1 84os, the rediscovery, or re-invention, of the Old English Christmas by the Victorians was just gaining momentum, and it was typical of Cole to sense this. Through the 1 84os the Illustrated London News and Punch would do much to popularise Christmas imagery and sentiments, but as yet they had done little. Washington Irving had laid the foundation in 182o when in his Sketch Book he described Christmas at Bracebridge Hall. In ${ \bf I } 8 3 6$ Charles Dickens, with the help of his illustrator 'Phiz', presented Christmas at Dingley Dell in Pickwick Papers. The year 183 5 saw the appearance of the illustrated Book of Christmas by the journalist T. K. Hervey (a sub-editor on the Athenaeum), while the children's book Peter Parley's Tales about Christmas appeared in 1838 with illustrations by the Cruikshanks. Daniel Maclise's celebrated painting Merry Christmas in the Baron's Hall was shown at the Royal Academy in 1838, and in the same year R. W. Buss's less celebrated (and indeed now lost) painting Christmas in the Olden Time was shown at the Society of British Artists. Christmas was obviously in the air, and in $1 8 4 3$ not only did Cole invent the Christmas card, but also Dickens published A Christmas Carol, the first of his 'Christmas Books', which did more than anything to put emotion into the Victorian Christmas. So far as one can see, Cole and Dickens could not have known what each was planning, unless they told each other. But they were hardly acquainted at this point, though they had both been present at a meeting on copyright on 17 May 1843. It seems likely that the Spirit of Christmas inspired them both independently.

## \* \* \* \*

Alongside his publishing experiments, Cole's railway work brought him a friendship with John Scott Russell. The two men must have felt some inclination to work in harness together, for on 5 December $\tau 8 4 5$ Russell tried to persuade Cole to join him in the railway department of the Daily News. Cole, however, was more interested in another of Russell's activities. After drinking tea on 5 December, they passed the evening preparing subjects for Prizes for the Soc of Arts' Russell, appointed secretary to the Society of Arts in autumn 184 5, found himself responsible for the continuation of a project begun by his predecessor, Francis Whishaw: a Grand Annual Exhibition of Manufactures. The first such exhibition, in $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 4 5$ , had not been successful. Now it fell to Russell to take on the project and do it better. Obviously he drew Cole into it, seeking his advice on how to run the next annual exhibition. Cole decided to enter the competition himself.

Russell put up some of his own money as a prize fund, and he and Cole decided to make it a rule that the pieces put in for the competition should have the names of the makers on them. At that time, retailers hardly bothered to draw attention to the makers of the things they sold. The manufacturers Wedgwood and Spode had begun to stamp their wares with their names, but Herbert Minton was certain that he would be ruined if he dared to put his name, as opposed to a coded mark, on his china. Cole's friendship with Minton had ripened. He stayed with Minton at his house at Hartshill near Stoke-on-Trent, on 10-1 1 September 1844. Minton showed him over his enormous china works and he experimented with throwing a teacup.

Two years later, in April 1846, Cole again went to stay with Minton, and, while th tu h     T submission in the Society of Arts competition. It was made in a red unglazed clay, and was intended to be cheap to produce. To equip himself to design the tea set, Cole, British Museum. He accompanied his entry with a note in which reverence for art history combines with practical sense in problem solving.

The aim in thee models has been to obtain as much beauty and onament as is commensurate with cheapness. A higher standard in the ornamental parts would have led to much greater cost.

The forms in principle are new combinations of those of the best Etruscan Pottery, with ornaments at the handles, &c. superadded and designed so as not to interfere wih the simplicity of the outlines.

TheCup be deeratr than wier leat sope or he n  he nd will keep the tea warm.

Te Mi os re    ge to be poured at both angles, right and left, which requires only a motion of the wrist, rim, because much size in that part is needless. The red clay being unglazed is calculated to exhibit the forms of the Tea Pot, Sugar Basin, and Milk ot to most advantage . .

Cole persuaded Minton to enter two jugs for the competition. When entries were judged in April 1846, prizes were awarded to Minton for his jugs and to Cole for his tea set (plate 19). Prince Albert admired the tea set, though he must have been shown a grander version than the one in the exhibition, because he said that he thought the gilding was not as good as that done by the French. He ordered one to use as a breakfast set at Balmoral. Some 4o years later Cole could write that the cups were still being used at the Society of Arts and that hundreds of thousands had been exported all over the world. 'I presented a set to the South Kensington Museum, which I hope may be kept and always exhibited there, as a link in the circumstances leading to that great Exhibition, which sowed the seed for the beginnings of the South Kensington Museum itself.'67

Cole viewed this second Society of Arts exhibition (which was more successful than the first) as a turning point. The acclaim bestowed on his tea set (and Minton's jugs) 'strengthened my conviction that an alliance between fine art and manufactures would promote public taste, and conduce to the interest of all concerned in the production of art manufactures.' Consequently he set up 'an organization of artists, manufacturers, and designers', and, resurrecting Felix Summerly, called it 'Summerly's Art-Manufactures'.68

This new project, as often with Cole's ventures, was not an entirely unprecedented idea. Indeed, in the little manifesto (fig.6) with which he announced his scheme, and which was reprinted in subsequent catalogues, he made a point of emphasising that his purpose was to 'revive the good old practice of connecting the best art with familiar objects in daily use'. In recent times, the 'good old practice' had fallen into disuse and it was very rare for fine artists to design anything to be manufactured, though the sculptor, John Flaxman, had designed for Wedgwood, and for Rundell, Bridge and Rundell, the royal silversmiths.69 None of Flaxman's designs, however, could be said to be for daily use, and Cole wanted to bring art down to earth again.

A precedent might have been found in the Art-Union Society, which had been started in 1837. This operated a kind of genteel lottery (overt lotteries being illegal), whereby subscribers might win an annual prize of an oil painting by a well-known artist, or a lesser prize of a reduction of a sculpture, or at least an engraving. In 1846 the Art-Union journal could claim that art-unions 'extend and encourage a love and appreciation of Art among the people; they actually supply annually little less than 80,ooo persons with a work of Art; and they afford profitable occupation to a large number of artists' The aims of the union were very near Cole's own, for, like him, it wanted to improve public taste and 'spread to all classes of the community that great and important feeling, a love of the fine arts'.1

Cole had already flirted with the idea of an 'Art Distribution Society', though he did not follow this up,72 and had involved himself in various schemes of graphic reproduction, as we have seen. The sculptures that were given as prizes in the Art-union

Fourth Edition, Dec, 1847.

might have given him some ideas when he turned to three-dimensional reproduction. These miniature sculptures were based on famous fullsize works, and were taken down to the right size to sit on a chimney shelf. They had to be re-modelled by another sculptor until 1844, in which year Benjamin Cheverton patented his pantograph, which could scale them down automatically. This is the same Benjamin Cheverton who won a prize for his proposals for stamps for Hill's penny post. The miniature sculptures were usually made in Parian, which was a hard white unglazed china body that looked like marble. It had been developed, mainly by Copeland and Garrett, and by Mintons, snce the 18os.Cole was to use it, and in ne f his catalogues he describe it as bi 'chiefly compounded of the finest clays, reduced to a liquid of exceeding fineness, with siliceous matter acting as a flux to vitrify the whole. In its hardness and durability and freedom from permanent stains or discolour it is like glass; like glass it is highly vitrified and intensely fred in the oven. Like glass, too, it ought not to be subjected to reat heat from boiling water, &c. instantaneously, or it is liable to crack'.73 Sculptures by Francis Chantrey, Richard Westmacott, Thomas Thorneycroft and Cole's friend John Bell were all turned into ornaments for the art-union. These were all works of art, albeit reduced, and were undoubtedly mass-produced.

Whether or not Cole took inspiration from the art-unions, his little business had a considerably wider range, for 'the Art-Manufactures will be of all kinds, and executed in pottery, wood, glass, and other materials'.74 By the spring of 1847 the business was in full swing.75 Cole made agreements with various manufacturers that he would choose the designs, while they would pay the designers. The final price would be agreed between him and the manufacturers. The latter paid all the expenses of making and transport, while Cole undertook the publicity and marketing and was paid a commission on each sale.76 The terms were tough and provoked disgruntled letters in the Art-Union journal.77 Cole later denied that he made any profit out of the business, claiming that he had incurred 'a good many hundred pounds outlay'.78 He began to issue catalogues, which were as elegant as the goods themselves. They were printed by Whittingham with headpieces and initial letters designed by Richard Redgrave. On the front page of the first one he described how medieval and Renaissance artists designed decorative articles. Invoking Raphael, Leonardo, Fra Angelico, Holbein, Dürer, Poussin and others, he declared: 'Beauty of form, and colour, and poetic invention were associated with everything. So it ought still to be, and we will say, shall be again.'

It must be admitted that, at the start, the only modern artist to contribute to Cole's scheme was a less impressive figure than these renowned masters - namely, Cole himself, with his tea set. But he quickly recruited bigger names, largely from the band of Kensington artists who had worked with him on Felix Summerly's books. He announced in his first catalogue that he had secured 'John Bell, Sculptor, C. W. Cope, A.R.A., T. Creswick, A.R.A., J. R. Herbert, R.A., J. C. Horsley, a Master of the School of Design, S. Joseph, Sculptor, D. Maclise, R.A., W. Mulready, R.A., R. Redgrave, A.R.A., H. J. Townsend, a Master of the School of Design'.79 He was soon able to add the name of William Dyce; and Richard Westmacott allowed one of his sculptures to be reproduced. Cole enlisted the best manufacturers: Minton and Copeland for china, Jennens and Bettridge for papier maché, W. Rogers for silver and silver plate, Taylor, Williams and Jordans for carved wood. Many of the objects were decorative rather than useful, as Cole implicitly acknowledged when he advertised h

The first to come out was the 'Cupid Inkstand', registered in March $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 4 7 .$ It was designed by John Bell (who was paid 25 guineas for it), made by Minton, and blatantly aimed at the marriage market. Indeed it was later retitled the 'Bride's Inkstand'. It consisted of a flat porcelain dish, coloured in Minton's famous Sèvres blue celeste, with a Parian figure of a cupid attached to the rim, holding the inkwell, a tube for the taper and a quiver for the pen. On either side were recumbent lizards that added an exotic note, though they could be omitted. The prince admired it and pointed out that a quill upright in the quiver of Cupid was essential to the design. It cost £1 13s. od. If made in gilded bronze, the inkstand was thought suitable for a bridegroom, but was then priced at £6 6s. od.

The most successful china ornament, which quickly sold out and had to be reordered, was 'Dorothea', a Parian figure reduced from a sculpture of $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 4 4$ , again by John Bell, and made by Mintons. Dorothea was a sexy, lightly clothed nymph bathing her feet in a stream. Bell also modelled, as a pair for her, 'Clorinda' (the original having been carved in 1839); and he produced two figures of kneeling children - 'The Lord's Prayer' and 'The Belief', sometimes known as Morning and Evening Prayer. In the 184os Bell was a popular sculptor, whose sentimental figures were in tune with the times. His works were reproduced as minor prizes by the art-union and he was given a puff in the journal: 'His mind is deeply imbued with poetic feeling; he is one of the few artists who attempt higher efforts than mere busts; and as his success has been great, he may take a very prominent station in the most elevated department of the arts'81 He did so much work for Felix Summerly's Art-Manufactures that he set up a special studio for the purpose.82 As well as belonging to Cole's Kensington group, he was a member of the Etching Club, the only sculptor among the members, many of whom were friends of Cole.83 Bell's sculptures for Felix Summerly were usable, of course, only as ornaments, but he also designed a pair of salad-servers, 'The Endive', in ivory and wood, and an elaborately carved cellaret or wine cooler.

Much of the stock advertised in Cole's catalogues was 'in preparation' and so only available if ordered. This allowed him to see how things went before committing himself to more items. He was able to expand the list, until, a year from the start, his catalogue contained some three dozen items, with a further two dozen nearly ready. The Art-Manufactures were sold through many retailers, with Joseph Cundall as the main outlet. Cole sent nearly 10o examples of his wares to the Society of Arts annual exhibition of British manufactures in the spring of 1848, where they were criticisd for hogging the limelight. 'It was Felix Summerly on every corner, on every table, against every wal, and always in the central situations.'84

Cole was proud of re-introducing carved breadboards to the public. John Bell e wi br wa,, ba n yrA keep down the price, Cole found that Taylor, Williams and Jordan could carve it by macine and he ws able to el it at  .. In h ubgraphy e wr i revived the use of wooden bread platters or trenchers. . . . From the year $\tau 8 4 8$ to the present time, the Summerly platter has been sold  besides innumerable other versions produced at the lowest possible prices; in fact a new branch of industry was throughout the kingdom'.85

Henry Townsend, who had made the drawings to illustrate the difficulties of the change of gauge on the railways, designed a brown earthenware jug, 'The Two Drivers', on which the raised decoration depicted the old coachman and his horses on one side and Brunel's broad gauge railway engine on the other. Townsend also designed another jug, 'The Hop Story', for which Mintons were awarded a gold medal at the Society of Arts exhibition in 1847. Cole was gratified by an approving notice in the Spectator: 'It is solid and stable; the housewife's rule that the neck should be wide enough to admit the hand for cleaning is observed. The design is full o thout nntion.Oh rti  ticus cludealcela spill case, a match box (in the form of a crusader's tomb), a mustard pot, a tea caddy, decanters and water jugs, glasses, cutlery, butter and cheese dishes, and a hall stand.

Felix Summerly's attempt to 'introduce High Art to our wardrobes and our elli sy  h, y e zi Punch, which looked forward to

a mustard-pot after Etty . . . a sugar-basin from Designs by Pickersgill. . . Landseer is to be intrusted with a commission for a set of pudding-basins . . . Maclise has been offered his own terms to illustrate a serious of blacking-brushes; and Stanfield is looking out for an appropriate subject to adorn a coal scuttle, which he has been requested to illuminate with the fire of his genius. Redgrave has got a shirt in active preparation with a domestic incident on each cuff, a scene of home affections on the bosom, and a bit of charming landscape on the collar.87

A few weeks later, Punch had another go, with an illustration (plate 2). It now asked orSy'ac  plber

a series of implements for the fire-side. . . . The stiffness of the poker will afford an ample opportunity for the introduction of that starched military erectness that is so effective in our iron-work; while the tongs, by their graceful pliability, are at once suggestive of the easy hornpipe with which the other service is identified. The shovel by its undulating curve, and the broom, by its elegant sweep, may be easily made subservient to the purposes of Art; and as to the scuttle, it offers a scope, not to say scoop, to the most refined handling.88

Cole's art-manufactures were also made fun of in the 1849 edition of the annual Comic Almanack, which offered some 'Valuable Advice . .. To persons about to Marry.  Don't buy your furniture at Felix Summerley's [sic] Cheap Art-Manufacture Mart.' It imagined 'a fond but imprudent couple' shopping there. 'They are tossed about on the beautiful carpets and lovely counterpanes, quite dazzled with the glittering warming-pans, inflamed with the glowing coal-scuttles of every possible age and period'. They purchase an 'Egyptian clothes-horse. The favourite design of Edwin Landseer'; 'six Gothic Swan-of-Avon Egyptian chairs'; 'one Stonehenge dressing-table'; and 'a beautiful historical Louis Quatorze French bedstead'.89 It is interesting that this comment mocks Felix Summerly's objects as examples of historical stylistic revivalism.

Cole tried to offset these denigrating jokes in the fourth edition of his catalogue in December 1847, by quoting praises from other journals. The Illustrated London

News said that Summerly's Art-Manufactures had 'a national object in view  the enabling Great Britain to assume an Artistic Station proportionate to her rank in manufacturing skill'. The Art-Union hailed them as 'a project pregnant with immensely beneficial results to British Manufacturing Art'. The Spectator praised Summerly's sound principles: 'He has induced those whom he has set to work to make the design spring from the purpose and use of the Article designed. Takig a long view, however, it is hard to disagree with a modern critic who considers that the Summerly products 'were hardly epoch-making' and had 'no lasting impact'. 'To a very large extent, they were true products of their time with their craze for ingenuity, their urge to tell a story.'91

Cole went on arguing vigorously. In March 1848 he published his catalogue in a new format, as the Art-Manufactures Circular, arranging for it to count as a newspaper so as to travel post-free. It was now to feature not only his own products, but also 'brief notices of events connected with the subject of Art-Manufactures'.92 For instance, he advertised the Society of Arts annual exhibition of British manufactures and promoted a new idea broached by the society for a big national exhibition of manufactures. 'Our readers we are sure will have great pleasure in learning that this great object, in which France and Belgium, and even the small German towns, have been allowed to outstrip England, seems at last in a far way to be accomplished.'93 The attempted transformation of the catalogue into a periodical shows that Cole's itch to be a journalist was not yet assuaged, and that he wanted to reform taste by argument as well as by example.

The productions of Felix Summerly, first in two dimensions and then in three, must have given Cole great enjoyment, and an outlet for artistic creativity at a very busy period. During the 184os, in his fourth decade, he was full of energy and fertile in projects, but had not yet found a true vocation in life. Perhaps 'Art-Manufactures' offered an appropriate mission. On 30 January 1848, Mulready advised Cole 'to determine whether to go thoroughly into Art Man or to stand still'. He did choose to devote himself to 'Art Man' Not, however, as a small-scale entrepreneur: despite his admiration for businessmen, he did not choose to become one. By exercising his talents as a publicist, lobbyist and organiser, he contrived to become a pacesetter in the art and design politics of his time.

## CHAPTER 7 Design Reform 1846-1852

Cole's transformation from a small-time arts entrepreneur and occasional critic into an influential design pundit began with his entry in 1846 into the Society of Arts. This institution, founded by some go-ahead men nearly 100 years earlier, in 1754, was properly titled the Society for the Encouragement of Arts, Manufactures and Commerce. It became the Royal Society of Arts in 1908. As its original name suggests, it had a wide interest in human ingenuity in all its forms. In its early days the society involved itself in, among other things, agriculture, forestry and land drainage; in medicinal drugs and soap; and in the manufacturing techniques, especially in the field of textiles, of the industrial revolution in Britain. It was also interested in drawing, painting and sculpture, and in 'Fancy, Design and Taste'.1 Through its support for art exhibitions, it paved the way for the foundation of the Royal Academy in 1768, and, while it kept clear of art exhibitions thereafter, it continued to make awards to artists and art students. Its chief method of proceeding in other fields was by offering prizes for new inventions, and it contributed tirelessly to the gadgetry of nations. Founded in a coffee house, and meeting at first in a circulating library, in 1774 the society established its headquarters in the purpose-built premises it still occupies, in a building that formed part of the Adam brothers' redevelopment of the Adelphi area, south of the Strand (plate 28).

In the early decades of the nineteenth century, after many successful years, the society was running down, and by the early 1840s it was almost bankrupt. It had only 320 members² and was about to sell its investments to pay off its debts. A rescue committee under a young patent lawyer, Thomas Webster, was appointed in 1841, and was mastering the problems by 1843. At this point, the president, the duke of Sussex, died, and was succeeded by Prince Albert. The assumption of this office marked an important stage in the prince's creation of an independent sphere of influence for himself. In 1845, a permanent management committee, called the council, was created, and the society began to recover.
