Its new secretary, during 1843-5, was Francis Whishaw, an acquaintance of Cole through Rowland Hill. 'He was very energetic and successful in personally canvassing for members, inducing one hundred and forty new members to join within a few months.' Furthermore he changed the format of meetings, which were now devoted to papers and discussions and 'soon became the acknowledged reunions of all the leading professors of science and art'.3 Whishaw was succeeded in $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 _ { 4 } \mathrm { ~ 5 ~ }$ by a closer crony of Cole, John Scott Russell, who, as a vice-president of the sister society in Scotland, had been welcomed with open arms by the society in London, when he joined in $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 4 4$ Through Russell, Cole began to get involved. After joining in $\textsc { i } 8 4 6 .$

he went quickly on to the council. From the moment Cole arrived in the society, 'his enterprise, energy, and perseverance stimulated its proceedings and supplied much of the driving force that gave the Society a new importance in the land'.4 The society, with its wide interests - embracing art, science, technology and trade  provided an ideal arena in which someone like Cole, with his all-purpose ingenuity, could ride any number of hobbyhorses.

The revived society, at the instigation of Whishaw, also embarked on exhibitions of contemporary manufactures. As we have seen, Cole won a prize with his tea set in the 1846 exhibition. He then seems to have seized control of the exhibitions as a means of promoting his efforts in design reform. It was announced early in 1847 that Prince Albert, as president of the society, had agreed 'that the department of the Society which would be most "likely to prove immediately beneficial to the public was that which encouraged most efficiently the application of the Fine Arts to the various manufactures of the country". 5 Thus Cole's personal mission had now been adopted, with royal support, as the mission of the society. A new exhibition followed soon after, devoted to 'British Manufactures and Decorative Art', which eclipsed previous exhibitions and was presented as the first of a new annual series. Appropriately, perhaps, for an introductory exhibition, it not only showed modern manufactures, but also had an opening historical section showing works of decorative art from the Elizabethan period, through the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, to Wedgwood and Flaxman.6 Running from I I March until 3o April, this exhibition attracted some 20,000 visitors.7

Another exhibition followed in 1848, running from 15 March to 29 April, and attracting some 7o,ooo visitors. This exhibition, hailed as in every respect, superior' to the previous year's show,8 was honoured with an exhaustive 12-column review in the Art-Union. 'Already', commented the reviewer, 'the spread of one species of artistic propriety is real and palpable; we mean that propriety which regulates the due proportion between any object and the material of its composition.' 'Lightness and freedom' in metalwork were commended, and in glassware 'simple and pure outline', with no cutting. The only dubious feature of this exhibition was (as we already know) the preponderance of Felix Summerly material. The display of the exhibits, in which presumably 'Summerly' had a big hand, was praised as 'in excellent taste'. 10

In the summer of 1848, the Society of Arts moved even further in the direction of pure art, with a retrospective exhibition of the paintings and drawings of Mulready.11 This was the first of a series of such retrospectives of contemporary British artists intended to build up towards the foundation of a National Gallery of British Art, which had been proposed to the society by Cole in a paper given on 27 January 1847.12 Then, he had suggested Landseer as the first artist who should be so honoured, but somehow his old friend Mulready emerged as preferred candidate. Cole spared no pains to promote his friend's reputation, himself writing a notice that this was really a piece of cheerleading, designed to convince readers that this was an exhibition they should not miss. Cole described the animation of the private view, as 'artists and intelligent cognoscenti asked each other 'where was the old Venetian whose colour was finer? where the Dutchman that was more closely or more truly imitative of nature? and, above all, where was the old master of any school or any period, whose power of drawing' could compare with Mulready's. Not only were the cognoscenti enraptured by Mulready's 'academic power'; their hearts were touched.

A connoisseur in European galleries avowed that no painter had ever dignified and eleva lowy subject s Mulrdy had; 'he was as great a poethe id, 'with hiel as Wordsworth with his pen.'An earnest man observed to us that this marshalling of the deeds  a ong careerws sething quit solenAndone f strongeli  bu n,y Hv, I  y ort makes a lump come in my throat!'

(We know from Cole's diary that it was Maclise who had a lump in his throat. 'Cope thought it solemn. Eastlake said the academy figures were the finest drawings in the world.')14 Cole's review ended with the assertion that Mulready's paintings were 'essentially popular pictures, to be read, understood, and enjoyed by everybody' 15 Next year William Etty was honoured, but in a less successful exhibition. After that Cole had other things to occupy him, and the retrospective exhibitions lapsed; but in due course he did create, with Richard Redgrave, a National Gallery of British Art in his museum at South Kensington.

All these art exhibitions were not to the taste of some members of the Society of Arts, who were more used to inspecting such things as Hick's elliptograph, Cameron's improved method of bleaching palm-oil, Captain Dickinson's diving-bell, Osborn's window-sash suspender, Augustus Bosanquet's improved champagne cork or Dr Warnsborough's leaden nipple-shield (all these being on the programme in 1842-3). Signs of discontent emerged at a meeting on 22 November 1848 when the council faced complaints that, for a couple of years, 'Fine Arts' had been unduly privileged over 'the Mechanical Arts and Manufactures'. It acknowledged that 'this may perhaps be in part true'. The excuse was that 'in reforming the operations of the Society it was necessary to do one thing at a time. The Council have postponed the mechanics — not lost sight of them', and promised to pay more attention to them. 16

In 1849, however, the exhibition of British manufactures (7 March to 28 April) reached new splendours. The society's rooms, it was said, 'present an appearance of excessive brilliancy, if not of magnificence'.17 Bright lights were reflected from 'metal work, of a very superb and costly description' (including the still celebrated  or perhaps notorious — silver centrepiece, girt with dogs, designed by Prince Albert), while 'the specimens of glass likewise contributed greatly to the brilliancy of the scene'. Silks, carpets, lace and shawls appeared for the first time, adding further richness.18 Again, some 7o,ooo visitors attended and, unlike in previous years, the exhibition made a small profit. If such success continued, commented the Art-Journal, the society "will have to congratulate themselves on having effected one of the most startling triumphs upon record relative to the advancement of Decorative Art'. It discoursed at some length on the fact that the society was now looking ahead to a much more ambitious exhibition in 1851.19

Cole's irresistible progress was, however, briefly interrupted in 18 50, when there was a power struggle at the society. Already on 9 January Cole's diary faces the possibility that it might be necessary to 'fight for the exhibitions in the Socy of Arts'. Thomas Webster 'attempted to carry a motion in the Council to the effect, that all future exhibitions of manufactures and pictures should be stopped', but fost. He then tried to change the bylaws of the society, so as to diminish the influence of Cole and his allies, and, on 4 March, won. Cole regarded Webster as an opponent not only over the issue of exhibitions, but also in relation to patent law. Reform of the patent law was another of Cole's causes at this time, but Webster, as a patent lawyer, had a vested interest in preserving the system Cole was attacking. So Cole saw two parties contesting for supremacy, Webster's representing 'Patents or Retrogression', and his own, representing 'Exhibitions or Progress'. In the face of Webster's manoeuvrings, 'the Exhibition party took no trouble at all, being too content to leave their successful efforts to revive the Society to speak for themselves - and were defeated'. When Webster achieved his change in the bylaws, the progressive party - Cole, Russell, Fuller, the two Redgraves and Dilke  resigned from the council in disgust.21 Disgust turned to anger when they saw the slate of candidates assembled by Webster to replace them: just a gang of patent lawyers, in Cole's view. They decided to fight back, with support from the very top ("both Col Grey and Col Phipps' - Prince Albert's right-hand men  'thought there ought to be a fight at the Socy of Arts')22 and put up their own slate of progressive candidates. Cole's diary shows that over Easter weekend 8 5o he was constantly busy with the 'Society of Arts election', presumably canvassing vigorously. The vote was taken on 3 April, and the progressive party re-took control of the council.

Even while all this was going on, the society was mounting another art exhibition of an even more extraordinary kind: an exhibition of 'Antient [sic] and Mediaeval Art', held from 18 March to 29 June. This had not been originated by Cole, but arose from a suggestion from the Archaeological Institute. An organising committee, chaired by Prince Albert, assembled (besides the usual lords, politicians and clerics) distinguished collectors such as Hollingworth Magniac, Octavius Morgan and A. J. Beresford-Hope, antiquaries such as Henry Shaw, C. Roach Smith, W. J. Thoms and J. R. Planché, dealers such as Henry Farrer and John Webb, and a contingent from the society, including Peter Le Neve Foster, Digby Wyatt and Cole himself. The secretary of the committee was the young Augustus Wollaston Franks, soon to begin his distinguished career as founder of the medieval collections at the British Museum. The exhibition was laid out by Henry Shaw and Henry Farrer in the Great Room of the society. Although James Barry's vast wall paintings showing the arts of civilisation formed an incongruous background to this exhibition (as to almost all others shown in this room), the spectacle was nonetheless 'gorgeous in the extreme'.

The whole getting together looks more like a vision from the Arabian Nights, or some realized dream of a Sir Epicure Mammon, than a sober reality. All that Walpole or Beckford brought together was a mere nut-shell of good things compared to this general harvest from al the corners of the three kingdoms.23

Ther was plae lent by the Que, te y cpan and the colleg  Oxfor and Cambridge, Limoges enamels from the earl of Shrewsbury and John Webb, glass from the duke of Buccleuch, Felix Slade and Henry Farrer, Gothic carvings in wood and ivory from Augustus Pugin, Renaissance woodcarvings from W. G. Rogers, Palissy ware from A. J. BeresfordHope: 898 exhibits in all.

The exhibition, intended as a counterpart to the forthcoming International x    l which every cultivated mind must feel in the contemplation of all that is rare and i rAt h tol wold hav aturally pth more w first object,25 but the second was to become increasingly important for him. This exhibition provided him, in effect, with an introductory course in connoisseurship and collecting, for it gave him the opportunity to study historic objects and to meet collectors and dealers. The exhibition was important not only for him  a neophyte gaining entry to a privileged circle - but also for the collectors and connoisseurs themselves. Beresford-Hope commented that it

was an era in the art history of the country. Many persons of the highest education and the widest acquirements were then for the first time made cognizant of the existence of grat school  rt  vey uropen cunty durg tho lo cnturee activity which it was once the fashion to designate as the 'dark ages'.26

While Cole never became a real connoisseur, he was to be responsible for running a museum of medieval art, and it was to his advantage, as director of the South Kensington Museum, that he had been present among the pioneering cohort in England that rediscovered medieval decorative art.

Towards the end of 185o, Cole became chairman of the council of the Society of Arts: the new bylaws required the council to elect its own chairman annually.27 One of the new bylaws compelled the chairman to deliver a policy statement at the beginning of his term. This Cole did on 15 January 185 1. Most of his measured speech would not be inappropriate on the lips of a modern managing director propounding a mission statement. But it also included this resonant Victorian sentiment.

It is with societies as with individuals, they do not, cannot prosper by following mere formulas. An individual who has no purpose in the world but to vegetate may as well not exist; and whether he be rich or poor, the world soon finds out that he is little less than an incumbrance, and treats him with indifference. To prosper, indeed, in these times, the man must be at work. We find the same analogies existing with societies. Unless they prove their ability to work, and work to some useful purpose, they become virtually extinct. A society cannot exist merely upon its name. Not only must it be alive to perform the functions it affects to do, but it must perform them in accordance with the advancing knowledge and increasing demands of the time.28

The Society of Arts continued to work with and for Cole through the rest of his life. In the immediate future, it and he would be chiefly preoccupied with the Great Exhibition.

## \*  \* \* \*

Cole's activities as Felix Summerly and his work with the Society of Arts led to his involvement with the government schools of design.29 He had been well aware for years of the London School of Design and its provincial satellites. William Dyce, headmaster of the schools from 1838 to 1843, and the most powerful influence on their philosophy, was an acquaintance, perhaps a friend; and Cole was certainly on terms of close friendship with Richard Redgrave, John Horsley, H. J. Townsend and J. R. Herbert, who were all employed at the School in the 1840s.

The London School of Design had been founded in $\overline { { 1 8 } } 3 6$ as the government's swift, indeed pre-emptive, response to a select committee of Parliament on arts and manufactures, which sat in $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 3 5$ and $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 \boldsymbol { 3 } \in$ This was prompted by lobbying from various directions. The painter Benjamin Robert Haydon was pursuing a personal vendetta against the Royal Academy, which called in question the work of the Academy schools. Many members of Parliament were concerned, in the post-Napoleonic-war period, with the economic damage that the old enemy, France, might be doing to Britain. Such people as Dr John Bowring, whom Cole had known as editor of the Westminster Review, were arguing that French consumer goods were selling better than British because they were better designed, and that the best way for Britain to catch up was by improving design education. The radical MP William Ewart accordingly moved for a select committee and, as its chairman, steered it towards its strong recommendations in favour of a 'Normal School' of design, and 'the formation of . . . Public Galleries or Museums of Art . . . open gratuitously to the people'.30

The new school opened on 1 June $\mathbf { I } 8 3 7$ in rooms in Somerset House previously occupied by the Royal Academy, rooms which were now conveniently vacant on the Academy's move to share the new National Gallery building in Trafalgar Square. The first headmaster was the architect J. B. Papworth, probably only a makeshift appointment until someone better qualified could be found. William Dyce soon appeared to fill this role. He had been running a school of design in Edinburgh, along with Charles Heath Wilson. Dyce laboured to establish the government school, writing a textbook on design and founding branch schools in the provinces. His was not an easy task, however, because he was answerable to many masters. The school was ostensibly run by a council of artists and businessmen, but this was too big, and its members either failed to come to meetings or interfered too much. Real power over the school was exercised by the Board of Trade, which provided the money, but in matters of policy and educational philosophy the board was not much help. In $\tau 8 4 3$ Dyce, tired, disillusioned and under pressure from the council, stepped down to become inspector of provincial schools, and Charles Heath Wilson took over. Wilson fell out with both staff and students, and from $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 4 5$ the London school was embroiled in acrimony, in what was called the 'rebellion of forty-five'. Wilson's chief adversary on the staff, J. R. Herbert, was got rid of, but conditions did not improve and in $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 4 6$ his three senior colleagues publicly voiced their complaints. These colleagues were the architect C. J. Richardson, and the painters H. J. Townsend and Richard Redgrave (the latter standing in for J. C. Horsley who was on long leave abroad). Their letters of complaint (Redgrave's addressed venturesomely to the prime minister) had an effect. The council of the School of Design set up an internal inquiry over Christmas $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 4 6 { - 7 }$ , followed by another in summer 1 $8 4 7$ As a result, Wilson was dismissed but not replaced, a new smaller committee of management was formed, and in the school itself authority was left in the hands of a triumvirate of masters, which by $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 4 8$ (after some shifting around) comprised Redgrave, Townsend and Herbert. It was obvious that a triumvirate was an unstable arrangement, and the Board of Trade knew it had a problem on its hands. Cole was called in as a troubleshooter.

His own story of his life marks as a crucial moment the occasion when he 'became acquainted with' John Shaw-Lefevre 'on board a Chelsea steamer, th August, $\tau 8 4 7 \cdot ^ { 3 1 }$ Shaw-Lefevre was permanent secretary at the Board of Trade and served on the committee of management of the London School of Design  and so wielded as much power over the schools as anyone. Though Cole's diary entry implies that he had not previously conversed with Shaw-Lefevre, they must have known each other by sight and reputation for many years. Shaw-Lefevre was a founder member of both the Athenaeum and the Reform Clubs, and he was working with the Board of Trade when Cole was helping Rowland Hill with the penny post. After talking to Cole he asked him to call at his office, which Cole did a few days later.

Through Shaw-Lefevre, Cole was introduced to the two Board of Trade ministers in Lord John Russell's Liberal government. The president of the Board of Trade was Henry Labouchere, a conscientious politician just turning 5o, with a solid reputation as an administrator. He had good Whig connections, though they were city rather than aristocratic ones, as both his mother and his wife belonged to the banking family of Baring. He was later to become secretary for Ireland and colonial secretary, and eventually Viscount Taunton, taking the name of the constituency that he represented throughout his life. His vice-president, a figure to be of central importance in Cole's life, was Lord Granville, a rising star in Liberal politics, in his early thirties $\scriptstyle ( p l a t e 2 4 )$ Known to his friends as Pussy and to his immediate family as Gink, Granville was born with a golden spoon in his mouth, though his rather squashed features hardly had the stamp of nobility. His family name was Leveson-Gower, the same as his cousins Lord Gower and the duke of Sutherland. Through his mother he was closely related to the dukes of Devonshire. Another cousin was the political diarist Charles Greville (a grandson of the duke of Portland), who shared his house in Bruton Street from 1849. His father was ambassador in Paris, and after education at Eton and Christ Church, Oxford, Granville spent a short time as an attaché in his father's embassy. As Lord Leveson, and through the influence of Lord Grey and Lord Carlisle (a cousin), he was given a safe seat in Parliament in $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 \boldsymbol { 3 6 }$ With genuine ability he soon made his mark as a Whig politician, so that at the age of 2 5 he was already an under-secretary in the Foreign Office. He went out of office with Lord Melbourne in $\mathtt { I 8 4 I }$ , and came back in $\tau 8 4 8$ (having meanwhile, in $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 4 6$ ,become 2nd Earl Granville and entered the Lords) to be vice-president of the Board of Trade. Despite his commitment to free trade, his appointment provoked howls of protest from the radicals, notably from John Bright of Manchester, as an example of Whig nepotism, for it was discovered that through his grandmother he was related to Lord John Russell, the prime minister.32 However, he weathered this storm without trouble, being a smooth, conciliatory and reliable operator. Much later he was to recall that, when he first met Cole, I did not like him at all'.33 Indeed, he was so wary that, when Charles Buller, at Cole's request, interceded for him with Granville, the latter (as Cole recorded) 'asked "If I shd get him into a scrape". To which Buller loyally replied 'that I had never done so with him'. 34 Granville and Cole became political allies for the whole of Cole's career and into his retirement, and, as far as the social gulf between them would allow, the two men were friends.

Cole's relationship with the Board of Trade took some time to ripen. After his summer meeting with Shaw-Lefevre, his diary records no further contact until early $\mathrm { ~ \tt ~ { ~ I ~ } ~ } 8 4 8$ On 29 January, Lefevre asked Cole to lecture at the School of Design (which Cole did not in the event do), and sought his advice on appointments. In the summer, contacts grew gradually closer. On 17 June Cole went to dine with Shaw-Lefevre at his home in Wimbledon, and on the 24 June Shaw-Lefevre trailed the possibility of appointing Cole to the council of the School of Design, and said that the Board of Trade would commission from Cole a report on patent law reform. Shaw-Lefevre did not follow this up, and Cole records in his diary on 9 August, doubtless with chagrin, that 'Mr J Lefevre was to have called but did not'. So Cole called on him on 14 August and jogged his memory. With typical persistence, Cole then, on 22 August, asked Buller to lobby Shaw-Lefevre, and was no doubt pleased to discover that the latter had written to Lord Granville. Meetings quickly followed with Granville on 24 and 26 August, and Cole clearly felt that he had now got a foot in the door, for he spent the evening of Sunday 27 August 'writing out course of Action for the School of Design'. On 29 August he dined at Granville's.

Cole got his official commission from the Board of Trade on 14 September. His task was to follow up a suggestion he had made to the Board for re-invigorating the schools by employing them 'in the production of designs for the Government departments'. Cole had urged that the schools should be exploited in this way partly because it would do them good, and partly also because of 'his belief that the schools might be made to a certain extent, if not altogether, self-supporting'.35 Here is an early statement of what was to be a leitmotiv in Cole's campaigning, later to be constantly repeated. From now on through the autumn, Cole was occupied with the school, spending a lot of time there, and no doubt getting under the feet of W. R. Deverell, the secretary to the new committee of management, and of Stafford Northcote, the Board of Trade official who dealt with most of the school's business. Northcote had started his career, after a first in classics at Oxford, by becoming secretary to W. E. Gladstone, who (still a Tory) served as vice-president and president of the Board of Trade from 1841 to 1845. When Gladstone resigned, Northcote stayed on at the Board of Trade and became legal adviser. Cole 'thought it my duty . .. to consult very freely with Mr. Northcote' in compiling his reports,36 and Northcote greeted the first as just the thing wanted'3 But it is perhaps hardly surprising that, under the continued impact of Cole, Northcote became guarded, if not hostile. Cole and his sympathisers eventually saw Northcote as 'Jesuitical', as he tried to keep his balance in the disputes.38

Cole produced three brief reports.3 In the first, dated 26 October 1848, he argued that designs for decorative art objects required by government departments should be 'systematically originated in the school by some of the more advanced pupils, and made to aid its present course of instruction as the culminating point of it'. Throughout its short history the school had been bedevilled by the question as to whether it should be a drawing school (a 'mere drawing school', as critics would say scornfully) or a school that gave some sort of practical insight into crafts and technical processes. Most people were agreed that it ought not to try to train technicians, but to produce designers who knew enough about techniques to make designs that could be successfully executed. 'Mr. Redgrave appears to me to draw the line exactly' wrote Cole, when he said, "We do not want to teach the art or process of weaving, but the art of designing for weavers".' Cole was all for practicality  at this point, anyway, though he was to change his views later  and he was able to quote from many earlier policy statements of the school and from recent testimony from teachers such as Dyce, Richardson and Townsend to back his view that this was the true function of the school. He felt that if students'designs were carried through into production, this would furnish an acid test of whether the school was succeeding in its educational purposes. 'If it be made a condition, as my proposal supposes, that the design must be useful, and its use is guaranteed, the course of instruction will become self-acting as it were, for it cannot halt at any stage before realising successful design. There is no escape from doing the right thing. Here speaks a Benthamite, a believer in self-regulating systems.

Cole's first report, making the theoretical case for using the schools productively, was politely received by the Board of Trade, so he proceeded to his second, dated 16 November, in which he considered how to put into practice his idea. He revealed that he had investigated how decorative art objects were currently commissioned by the Board of Works, the Admiralty, the Ordnance department and the royal palaces. So far as the last named were concerned, he had engaged the interest and support of Prince Albert. He now suggested that the Board of Trade should collect information about what departments wanted, should commission designs from the schools, and should get them prototyped by manufacturers. These designs, if successful, could then be put into production, and made available, not only for government use but also on the commercial market, in order to raise money for the government.

After presenting this report, Cole 'could not conceal from myself that . .. my suggestions were not very acceptable . . .; in fact that was what a member of the committee, Mr. Poynter, said expressly to me'. Faced with opposition, Cole 'saw that any such plan as I proposed would not touch what, in fact, I never concealed from Mr. Lefevre or Lord Granville appeared to me to be the inherent defect of the school, which was, as I thought, its constitution and management'.40 So Cole set to and wrote a third report, dated 29 December, which denounced the administration of the schools. 'An ungrateful task', he rather sanctimoniously remarked, but it would be 'a dereliction of duty' not to do it. He advocated a 'complete change of system'. He reviewed at length the lamentable history of the administration of the schools, citing as the principal defect the fact that  and here the Benthamite speaks  too many people without direct and accountable responsibility were supposed to run the school, and could not. The corollary of this arrangement, 'as in all cases where the responsibility is divided among numbers', was 'that almost the whole machinery is guided by the judgment of some one individual who is not responsible'. It is obvious whom he had in his sights. 'It is the secretary, rather than the committee, who originates proceedings, and gives the key-note to decisions . . . the secretary becomes the actual manager, irresponsible and unrecognised, far less cautious and prudent than he would be if he were fully recognised as manager.' Cole stopped at denunciation, not supplying any suggestions as to how things should be improved, except for pointing to the reform of the public records as an illustration of how to do it. Concluding that 'the defect being in the system, it is the system which must be altered', Cole resigned his official commission from the Board of Trade, refusing to accept the promised fee of £ 1o0.41

His blood was up, however, and he did not cease to agitate. He now formed an alliance with Thomas Milner Gibson, MP. Gibson (plate 22) was a close supporter of Cobden and a free-trader. His exertions in the Anti-Corn Law League brought him a parliamentary seat in Manchester. When Lord John Russell formed his government in 1 846, after Peel had introduced free trade, Gibson accepted office as vice-president of the Board of Trade, but, finding a government position too uncomfortable for one of his convictions, he rejoined his Cobdenite colleagues on the back-benches in 1848, Granville succeeding him. Gibson was an accomplished parliamentarian: 'smart and pithy', he was able to deploy a 'suave earnestness', and to say 'pointed, hard-hitting things' in a 'pleasant and gentlemanly style'. 42 As a Member of Parliament, he was in a position to launch Cole's ultimate weapon, a select committee. He agreed to do this, and (as Cole recorded on 10 February 1849) 'wd allow me to pull the "wires".

So Cole got busy. He did not neglect to try to secure his position with Granville, whom e viusly un elpul n idnot wish t lenate. In Feruary e to Granville:

Your Lordship will easily imagine that my head has been running a good deal on this subject of Design . . . I may be quixotic, but I think I am bound to try and make my truth in this matter prevail and to do so without giving personal offence if possible. I would not have it thought that I am not prepared to tell openly to those who have a right to ask it what I am about. I feel your Lordship is one of them and if you were minded to listen, I would unreservedly and candidly tell your Lordship all that I am doing & what I intend sooner or later to accomplish. But you may see an impropriety in such communication with me and indeed I do not ask it. I wish only to prove that I do not forget the confidence with which you have always treated me. I believe the time will come when Government & the public will thank me for what I am doing ..3

Gibson duly moved for a committee, which was set up on 15 March. Chaired by Gibson, it included Labouchere, as the responsible minister, and Peel, among others. With Cole busily co-ordinating things behind the scenes, it took evidence on 13 sessions in April and May, Cole appearing on 10 and 1 1 May (having first rehearsed his li wi Gisnon the oig  1May. He teti hiely as a exper  - cient administration, not only denouncing the School of Design's confusion, as he had done before, but now also tabling a plan for reform. He also spoke as an authority on taste, taking a stronger line than he had hitherto dared. 'Taste has its principles as well as morals, which people understand and know.' 'There are certain principles in taste which all eminent artists are agreed upon in all parts of the world.' When challenged over the 'fallibility of artists in matters of taste', he insisted that 'I do not admit the fallibility in such matters of taste. I think taste has certain broad rules, like all other mundane subjects. People who attend to the matter can understand those rules, and arrive at conclusions which are generally the same.'44 He here assumes an oracular tone, which he was to adopt ever more readily.

The committee's draft report, ostensibly written by Gibson, was in fact composed by Cole, who produced a severely critical first version, expressing his own views. However, he misjudged the mood of the committee, who would not accept his draft, even when he toned it down. Finally, after long disputes, the committee backed a much tamer draft provided by another member.45 The final report was, in Cole's words, 'a thing of compromises'.46 This was widely acknowledged in the press, where the report was described as 'a severe disappointment',47 as 'wishy-washy' and 'a very "yea-nay" sort of document', which 'might as well have remained unwritten',4 and as 'milk-and-water', an example of how 'Parliamentary etiquette and the unwillingness to give personal offence to "powers that be" have thrown the veil of polite ambiguity over the ugly yet honest faces of Truth and Candour'.49

After Cole's attempts at reform had been thus stifled, there was not much more that he could do. He did try to pass the baton to Lord Granville, when he gave the prizes at the Spitalfields branch of the schools in the autumn. 'We are glad to see that Lord Granville . .  presided', wrote Cole in the Journal of design. 'We hope we may view this as a token that his Lordship is going to distinguish himself as a reformer of the Schools . . . . That Lord Granville can do so if he will, and that he may make the country and our manufacturers indebted to him for rendering our Schools of Design rli, wehave litte oubBut, temmet, this ws ust whisting  e his spirits up.

From the school itself came a groan of exhaustion. 'So I hear . . .', wrote Richard Redgrave, 'that your long fight has been made a sort of drawn game after all . . . in truth I thought vested interests were too strong for you.' Speaking on behalf of his teacher colleagues, he said: 'I suppose we labouring men will soon know what is on the cards for us and the boys', i.e. the students. 1 What was on the cards proved to be a plea from the Board of Trade to struggle on, appealing, as Redgrave wrote, 'to our willingness to continue, if we were not interfered with - to which of course we could have no objection'.52

## \* \*\*

As we have seen, while battling for power at the Society of Arts, Cole identified two parties: 'Patents or Retrogression' vs. 'Exhibitions or Progress'. He belonged to the latter party, but his enthusiasm for exhibitions did not exclude a powerful desire to do something about patents also. When initiating his 'Felix Summerly' art-manufactures, he had taken care to register the designs he commissioned, and this had made him well aware of the current state of the British patent laws. The result was that he was eager to apply 'Progress' to 'Patents'.53

One of his first moves to forward patent law reform was linked to his developing relationship with the Board of Trade in late 1848. On 6 September 1848, he wrote to the joint secretary of the board, Sir Denis Le Marchant, offering his services as a consultant to report on both the Schools of Design and the patent laws. Interestingly, his letter was mostly about patents, with only a brief note at the end about the Schools of Design.54 Cole was at that time aiming at 'effecting a concentrated system', embracing education, promotion and protection in design and invention. He drew attention to many problems over intellectual property law: uncertainties over the extent of various kinds of rights, obstacles and delays in obtaining patents, the complexity of finding out what patents had already been granted, the difficulty of getting redress for infringement in 'what has been called a lottery of justice', and the imperfections of the system of specification.

In respect of decorative art objects, he pointed out that protection was currently afforded under the Copyright Act of 18 1 5, and the Registration Act of 1843. Under the latter act, protection was for three years, a fee was paid, and redress was relatively effective. Under the former, no fee was paid and the protection lasted 18 years, but redress was uncertain. Literary copyright lasted for 28 years. Cole believed that the Registration Act could be regarded as a first step in the reform of the patent laws, but it had its snags. For instance, 'it does not make clear whether an article registered in one class is protected from piracy in another; say, if registered in earthenware, is it protected also in iron or plastic materials generally?'

Above all, patents were expensive, 'seldom less than 25ol., and oftentimes more', and this discouraged inventors from seeking them. It thus also reduced the possible income to the state from patents. Cole did not think patents should make money, but he did claim, using one of his favourite words, that 'the whole system of promoting useful design might be made self-supporting to a very considerable extent'.

When, in response to this letter, the Board of Trade gave Cole his commission, it did not encourage him to pursue the question of patents, so he dropped it and concentrated on reporting on the Schools of Design. He then took up patents at the Society of Arts. He had already flown a kite at the society when on 9 March he 'carried resolution against "Tax on Invention". His resolution alluded to the cost of obtaining patents, the 'difficulties and anomalies' encountered in this bureaucratic process, and the inadequate protection afforded.5 Later in the year, on 4 September, the society set up a committee on the rights of inventors, which included, among its 29 members, Cole himself, Milner Gibson, Owen Jones, Herbert Minton and Charles Dickens — who, as an often pirated best-selling author, was already known as a campaigner for improvements in copyright. It was Cole who instigated the committee, and he was the driving force behind it.6 He offered to compile a report, which was in proof by the end of September, and printed on 2 December.57

Cole's treatise has been described as 'a remarkable document for its time, containing powerful rhetoric against the Chancery system'.58 Cole focussed first on the fact that 'a British subject has no rights of property whatever in that intellectual labour which produces invention or scientific discovery, excepting such as he can obtain by petition from the Crown'. In a strange survival of the feudal system, a Briton could only obtain legal protection over an invention by begging that it be bestowed as a privilege by the monarch, in the form of 'letters patent'. Though Britain, as Cole thundered, was 'the greatest manufacturing nation in the world, possessing boundless capital and most active industrial energy, combined with a vast amount of inventive ability, to which the genius of the people gives the most practical development', it was far behind other countries in its intellectual property laws.

Not only was the system in principle obsolete, but also in practice it was ludicrously complicated. Cole set out 3 5 official stages through which an applicant for a patent had to work, visiting and repeatedly paying fees to a master in Chancery, the HomOfe tet-l, e at Offe te ivSelOffe, te Office, and the Lord Chancellor. The Queen had to give her signature twice, and the process petered out in visits to the Lord Chancellor's purse-bearer, the clerk of the h  e riht to protec an ivention, nd had to gon to rovie a eaton  wh e invention actually was.

In early autumn, Cole, whose family were on holiday at Herne Bay, left proofs of his treatie with Dickens whowas staying at earb Rasgate, and Dickes ppt ly composed an article for the weekly magazine Household Words, which he edited. Apom'a  pt  he  r  the 9

October.60 Ostensibly written by a 56-year-old Birmingham engineer, who travelled to London to patent an invention, it focuses on the 35-stage application process. Deploying his arts of fiction, Dickens first sympathetically sketched the characters of his narrator, John, and of his friend William Butcher; and then John and William were followed through the exhausting application process. John's final comment was: 'In William Butcher's delivering "that the whole gang of Hanapers and Chaffwaxes must be done away with, and that England has been chaffed and waxed sufficient," I agree.' As a publicity agent, Cole was able to recruit astonishingly distinguished copywriters: Thackeray on railways, and now Dickens on patents.

The Society of Arts committee sent Cole's treatise out to cabinet ministers and in early 185o prepared a draft bill. Interest in the patent laws was stimulated by the approaching Great Exhibition, for prospective exhibitors feared that, if they put their innovations on show, others would copy them without permission. Lord Granville moved successfully for a House of Lords select committee on the patent laws, before which Cole appeared on 26 May. Most of his testimony was a civilised conversation between him and Granville on the principles of protection, set against the benefits of freedom of information. After the select committee, a series of bills came before Parliament, as the various interested parties struggled to agree on a new system. As we shall see, Cole was diverted on to further work on patents in autumn $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 \varsigma \circ { , }$ just as the preparations for the exhibition were beginning to crowd in. Finally, in $\mathrm { ~ T ~ } 8 \varsigma _ { 2 }$ , the Patent Law Amendment Act was passed. This got rid of the chaff-waxes, replacing them with a central Patent Office, under the commissioners of patents, where the necessary paperwork for applications could be carried out in one place, and at considerably less expense than under the old system. At the very end of Cole's life, in the run-up to the Patent Act of 1884, he was again involved in the subject.

## \*   \* \*

By the end of the 1840s Cole had made himself an influential figure in design reform, advising the Board of Trade, and running powerful campaigns through the Society of Arts. It was only to be expected that his old love of journalism would reassert itself. In $\mathbf { 1 8 4 6 - 7 }$ , he had toyed with the idea of an 'Arts Newspaper', perhaps to be titled The Manufacturer.61 In March 1849 he started up a monthly magazine to campaign for design reform: the Journal of design and manufactures (plate 23, fig.7). This ran for 36 numbers, until February 1852, and might, perhaps, have kept going indefinitely. But by then, Cole had accepted an official post under the government, which meant that he had to cease campaigning.

Cole's previous attempts in journalism  The Guide, for instance, or the Historical Register  had been directed at a popular market. He had aimed to succeed in masscirculation journalism (though the 'mass' then was much smaller than now), and had failed  obviously because, despite his talents, he did not have the popular touch. By contrast with these earlier efforts, the Journal of design was a 'niche' publication, aimed at a particular interest group. It turned out that the group was large enough, and prepared to pay enough, to sustain the publication. Cole was negotiating about the new journal with Edward Chapman, of the publishers Chapman and Hall, on 6 December 1848, and found him well disposed towards the project. They came to an agreement, on 5 January following, that Chapman and Hall would pay the expenses of publication and divide the profits. The journal was in octavo format and cost a shilling for each issue, which was usually of 32 pages. It was printed in small dense type, but its appearance was enlivened by wood-engraved illustrations of recent products, as well as scraps of fabric and wallpaper, pasted on to the pages. Cole the hoe,   uctory 'Adr hat the ees ill the pattern book of all decorative manufacturers'. It is true that the specimens (213 of them) and the illustrations (over 7oo) made a fine show. But these were hardly enough to provide coverage of all' decorative manufactures, as Cole hoped.

The written content of the Journal of design closely matched Cole's own interests. As proof that the new journal was timely, he cited several recent developments: 'the recognition of property in Ornamental Design by the Copyright Acts; the support of Schools of Design by public money; the Exhibitions of Ornamental Manufactures at the Society of Arts in the metropolis, and by other institutions in the country; and the diffusion of Art-Manufactures'. In effect: 'What Henry Did'. He laid out his policies othesss, anduly rtenfrther oreatber the.

![](images/4002c0810967c4ddbf03d6e75a2a4f1c16b2abc066e662cf215f3619f6792d3d.jpg)  
Figure 7 Pages from the Journal of Design, December 1850, showing illustrations of new manufactures, and a review of Cole's first design exhibition at the Society of Arts.

The select committee on the Schools of Design, patent agitation at the Society of Arts and in Parliament, the Paris exhibition of 1849, and preparations for the London 185 1 exhibition were all covered. Indeed, the journal proceeded steadily on throughout the tumult of the Great Exhibition, providing a 'Monthly Report of Progress' that tells us much about what Cole was up to there.

Each number of the Journal of design had the same structure. As well as the specimens and illustrations of new manufactures, there were sections devoted to 'Original Papers', book reviews, 'Institutions' (under which Cole could campaign about the Society of Arts and the Schools of Design, among many other things), 'Table Talk' (soon to become 'Miscellaneous'), and correspondence. Although the journal includes a mass of interesting information, Cole did not intend that it should be merely a newspaper. It was also intended to teach, by including a 'doctrinal part', which would feature 'treatises developing sound principles of ornamental art'.63 The first three numbers, for instance, reprinted lectures on ornament by William Dyce, which set out many of the principles that came to constitute the Cole creed. (For example: 'Designs geometrically constructed and capable of repetition are absolutely necessary for the decoration of floors and all spaces where the notion of flatness and solidity must be preserved.')64 Later, in June-August 1849, came lectures by Redgrave on botany as a source of ornament, and in January and February 185 2 a lecture by Lyon Playfair on industrial education. There were further contributions from Dyce, and from John Bell, J. C. Horsley, Owen Jones and Matthew Digby Wyatt.

The most pointed piece on 'doctrinal' lines came in the number for August 1851, and was entitled 'Universal Infidelity in Principles of Design'. It quoted scathing articles about the Great Exhibition from The Times and the Morning Chronicle, and made play with the slogan "'art has its dogmas and its orthodoxy'. At this time, as the impact of the Great Exhibition sank in, many in the art world came to the conclusion that it had exposed an enormous amount of bad taste in British goods. This was the burden of the quoted newspaper articles. The Times derided the over-elaborate ornament that characterised the manufactured goods, and found itself able to admire only the machinery (where beauty arose from 'the stringent application of mechanical science to the material world', carried through with 'truthfulness, perseverance, and severity') and the building, of which it declared: 'All is plain, simple, and mathematically severe; yet who can enter that vast interior and not feel his heart swell within him at the solemn and majestic impression which it creates.' As for the Morning Chronicle, it was appalled by the variety of styles that had been imitated by ornamentists. 'The Exhibition shows that we are most skilful mimics . . . . But what do we create?'. In its editorial comment introducing these excerpts, the Journal declared: 'The very best things in the Exhibition are the least ornamented.'65 This theme was taken up again in the penultimate number in January 1852, where a piece reflecting on design reform over the last 15 years, asked 'Which direction is ornamental art likely to take in this country, toward elaboration or simplicity?'. The answer was the latter.

It is this kind of writing that has earned for the Journal the modest reputation it enjoys as a publication of significance in design history. In the 193os, as the Modern Movement in architecture and design began to be appreciated by advanced Englishspeaking critics, attempts were made to find precedents for its aesthetic in English art criticism of the nineteenth century. Opinions such as those just quoted were music to the Modernists' ears. Richard Redgrave, Matthew Digby Wyatt and Owen Jones were first presented as 'Prophets of the Modern Movement' in 1937.66 Cole and the Journal of design were brought forward to join them in 1948,67 and thereafter were regularly acclaimed, especially in the works of Nikolaus Pevsner.68 If such critics were inclined to attribute rather too distinct an outline to the doctrines of Cole and his allies, in their zeal to see them as proto-Modernists, other critics have looked more searchingly at the journal.69 One of them stresses that at first 'much confusion reigned in the editorial office' over what might be true principles of design; and among the new manufactures illustrated were 'all kinds of designs, later to be the targets of scorn and anathematisation'. Through the journal can be traced the development of the ideas of Cole and his group (from naturalism to conventionalism), and by the end a 'clear and consistent set of principles' was formulated.70

We can assume that Cole wrote most of the unsigned articles and paragraphs in the Journal of design. Often enough, his own voice seems to come through, as when he dismisses the retrogressive party at the Society of Arts as 'twaddling with incipient patents, nursing jealousies as to the origination of this lever or that crank'.71 And, although he had to hand over the editorship to his brother Charles for a time when the demands of the Great Exhibition became all-consuming, we can be sure that he was deeply involved as an editor, and that the journal stands as his intellectual construct. It is here, if anywhere, that we meet Cole as a thinker, rather than an administrative force.

Tantalisingly, the Journal of design contains a trace of what must have been his first encounter with Ruskin. The number for October 1849 contains a brief review by Cole of Ruskin's Seven Lamps of Architecture - 'this thoughtful, eloquent book'. While acknowledging that 'its beauties will sink deep in the minds of all who feel that true art is the earnest expression of realities and human sentiments', Cole does criticise Ruskin for shunning the modern world.

Insboldlynising the tenencthee whicavitable,pr the wide distribution of certain kinds of art, and the positive good that is in this instead of considering the means of improving these tendencies and results, he either puts up his back against their further development, or would attempt to bring back the world of art to what its course of action was four centuries ago! - as easy a thing as to put the world itself back. Our course in this nineteenth century may be hateful if you by all the light that God gives them, to direct it rightly.72

Cole sent the review to Ruskin, with an invitation to cntribute to the Joural design. Ruskin replied politely, declining. The paths of the two men crossed from time to time in the future, and they maintained a civil acquaintance. Each, however, was aware of the other as representing a diametrically opposed tendency in art education, and indeed in philosophy of life. Robert Hewison sees them as standing for two traditions in the nineteenth century, the utilitarian and the romantic anti-apitais, the ratialist and the maginatiCole,probably, i not thik about h very much. He wasalways up and doing. And as the ecade of the 18 begn,v while, through the journal, he was thinking as hard as he was ever to do about his mission as a design reformer, he was busy in a practical, handy, effcient way with the Great Exhibition.

## CHAPTER 8 The Great Exhibition 1849-1851

The Great Exhibition opened and closed with the singing of Handel's 'Hallelujah Chorus' Sometimes it has been interpreted as if it were itself a sort of Hallelujah Chorus  a grand, harmonious outburst of praise, celebrating the stable values of Victorian Britain. Recent accounts, however, have stressed that the exhibition, like Victorian Britain, was 'a site of contention, a source of conflict and debate', ' and have criticised previous accounts as having 'little appreciation of the complexity of the question'.2 Complexity and contention would have been recognised all too easily by those who were actually involved in setting up the exhibition. Cole, for one, was not loath to explain all the difficulties he had gone through. He left two accounts. One was in his introduction to the general Catalogue of the exhibition. In supplying this official account he was instructed by Prince Albert that 'accuracy is . . . the first essential' and that 'there should be total absence of anything like "puffing".3 So, with due sobriety, he explained the problems that had to be surmounted. First, from small beginnings in the Society of Arts, the project had to be built up into an international event, under the patronage of Prince Albert. Then the government had to be won round, money had to be raised, and support mobilised throughout the country. Third, a site had to be secured and a building erected at high speed. Finally, the exhibits had to be assembled and put on show. In the 1oo pages that Cole devoted to the exhibition in Fifty Years, his second account of it, he took pains to emphasise that at each of these important stages he played a decisive part. It would be wise to bear in mind a reviewer's judgement that Cole's account 'is not in all respects accurate, and he, unconsciously no doubt, has somewhat overlooked the services of his coadjutors' (the review was in the Athenaeum, so was probably well-informed), but on the whole the evidence bears him out.4

This chapter does not give another conventional account of the exhibition, but a Cole's-eye-view. Lest this should give the impression that preparing for the exhibition was nothing more than one long squabble, it is necessary to remember that the enterprise was an inspiring one, especially for people of the time, who believed so strongly in the value of making and selling things, and even more especially for Cole, for whom the exhibition of art-manufactures had now become a life's mission. Cole manoeuvred tirelessly to bring the scheme to fruition, and to win for himself a dominating position in an enterprise in which it seemed that many more important people outweighed him.

The Society of Arts' exhibitions of British manufactures in 1847, 1848 and 1849 seemed to Cole to have opened a prospect of something more ambitious, so he persuaded the society to send a petition to Parliament. In this he tried to show  for it was he who wrote it  'the progressive success of the annual exhibitions of the Society of Arts, and that they had now become a source of income to the Society, after paying their expenses'. He proposed a similarly self-supporting 'National Exhibition' to the government.

After flying this kite, the society's group of exhibition enthusiasts went to test the wind in Paris, where the latest of the French exhibitions of manufactures was held in the summer of 1849. Leaving for Paris on 29 May, Cole joined Matthew Digby Wyatt, from whom the society had commissioned a report. They stayed in a cheap hotel and Cole remembered that 'we were awakened at midnight by the stifling odour of emptying the cesspools, altogether a novel sensation, which years have not obliterated from my mind'. 6 On 1 June Cole went with Herbert Minton (who was also in Paris) to see Louis Buffet, the French minister of agriculture and commerce. For long Buffet had thought that the French should hold an international exhibition, but had failed to find supporters for such a scheme. In Fifty Years, Cole quotes Buffet's proposals at length, which suggests that he regarded them at the time as a significant contribution to the climate of opinion he was trying to create. Buffet gave the British party tickets to visit the exhibition before it opened.

It was held in a specially built 'Vast parallelogram' of a palace (675ft × 328ft; 206m x 1oom), painted outside to look like stone, while inside 'all the pilasters are papered & painted in a species of graining to imitate chestnut wood, and even the ceiling is covered with the same work. . . . The architecture of the whole is mesquin, although the gigantic scale of the building necessarily elevates the general effect into something of impressiveness'.7 In the course of the next week, the English visitors, meeting at the exhibition or dining together, discussed the possibility of a British international exhibition, and Cole asked everyone he met what they thought of the idea. He came back to London on 7 June, leaving Wyatt to complete his report.

The first public disclosure of the Society of Arts's hopes for a major exhibition of British manufactures in London in 185 1 came on 14 June 1949, when Prince Albert, as president, gave away the prizes at the society. With his permission, Scott Russell, as secretary, mentioned the project in his report. A few days later the prince's equerry, Col. Phipps (plate 3 I), wrote to Cole on the prince's behalf to arrange for further discussions on 3o June. The day before, Cole, seizing the initiative, called at Buckingham Palace and succeded in holding an informal talk with the Prince in Phipps's room. 'I asked the Prince if he had considered if the Exhibition should be a National or an International Exhibition . . .. The Prince reflected for a minute, and then said, 'It must embrace foreign productions,' to use his words, and added ephatically, International, certainlyThe prince went on tsuggest that aR Commission would be the best way to get it started. He and Cole discussed where the exhibition might be held and Cole suggested Hyde Park. The prince agreed, and askd him to find an area that was not used to drill soldiers.The park had the advantage that, since it was royal rather than privately or publicly owned, it would be simpler to get permission to use it than to commandeer a London square, as was also suggested.

Cle descbe theng inFiy ears. It was viusy pant  his

At the meeting on 3o June, next day, attended by Cole, Francis Fuller, Thomas Cubitt and John Scott Russell, these ideas were formally adopted, as recorded in the minutes taken by Russell.9 For the Royal Commission Cole offered to prepare a draft, 'grounded on precedents of other Royal Commissions'. The membership of the commission would include cabinet ministers, and people representing 'the various interests of the community'. Also, significantly, 'for the execution of its details, some of the parties present and who are also Members or are officers of the Society of Arts, and who have been most active in originating and preparing for the execution of this plan should be suggested as members'. As to finance, the prince brought up the difficulties of raising loans or subscriptions, and suggested that the Society of Arts could collect and hold a fund. He thought that £1oo,ooo might be raised. He wanted 'to carry forward this business without delay'.

The following morning, a Sunday, Cole called early on Edwin Chadwick, who had wide experience of Royal Commissions, and by Tuesday he had sent his draft to the palace. As would often happen, others did not meet his promptitude with equal speed. The commission was not set up until January 185o, and at that point a later draft by Cole, based on the Record Commission of 183 1, was used. Cole was already, even at this early stage in 1849, seeing the exhibition as one of a series, as is revealed in the phraseology of his offer to go to Osborne and see the prince, to 'answer the end of promoting the large periodic exhibition'.10 It was about now, in the summer of 1849, that the term 'Great Exhibition' began to be used.

In early July, Cole was busy with the select committee on the Schools of Design, but the exhibition soon displaced other concerns. On Wednesday I 1 July he was asked to call on Henry Labouchere, president of the Board of Trade, to brief him about the Paris exhibition. The following Friday evening he set off with Fuller and Scott Russell for a meeting next day with the prince at the royal retreat, Osborne House, on the Isle of Wight. The prince revealed his provisional list of commission members, with Russell and Fuller included as commissioners and Cole as secretary. Since it was essential in pushing the project forward to secure the support of the government, the prince had also summoned Labouchere, who now joined the meeting. It was unlikely that the government would take action before the imminent end of the parliamentary session, so Labouchere asked the exhibition lobby to set to work to strengthen their case. Cole suggested that during the parliamentary recess the Society of Arts might collect 'more evidence as to the readiness of the great manufacturing and commercial interests to subscribe to and support the undertaking'.11 They could then brief the government when it met again in the autumn. They had lunch with the prince and the equerries, and talked for so long that they missed the boat, but the prince summoned the Fairy to take them to Southampton to catch the train back to London.

Although Cole must have felt that he was on the brink of a great adventure, he was not without worries. On 26 July he was fetched from his office because Marian was ill. She was pregnant and - although she had been well enough a couple of weeks before to launch the Manchester, John Scott Russell's new ship, at Millwall  she was now haemorrhaging. Seymour Haden called in Dr Robert Lee, the well-known obstetrician of St George's Hospital. 'She was delivered in the night, & did not suffer very much'. The next day she was 'quiet & not in pain' and continued to recover from her miscarriage. Nonetheless, it took its toll, and on holiday at Herne Bay in the midle of September she was still easily overtired.12 In midsummer, as she lay at home convalescing, Cole was out and about, campaigning.

In working up support, money was of the essence. The Society of Arts decided that it must find a builder who would put up a building at his own risk, so as to have something settled to encourage businessmen to support the exhibition. Fuller took on the task of finding a contractor, and within a few days (with the help of his fatherin-law George Drew) he persuaded James Munday to come forward. Munday did so 'mainly with a view of assisting his nephew Mr George Munday, as he conceived that this would be a first-rate opportunity of introducing him to the world as a Contractor on a large scale'.13 The building was likely to cost $\scriptstyle \pm _ { 7 5 } , \supset \infty ,$ and in addition the Mundays promised a prize fund of £2o,ooo. They thought that the building would revert to their possession at the close of the exhibition. The contract was made on 23 August, with a clause, which Cole suggested, that if a Royal Commission wanted to do things differently, then the contract could be cancelled, with compensation, before i February 185o. This gamble on the part of the Mundays made a great difference to the way the exhibition could be presented to the public. As Col. Phipps told George Drew in December, the prince had 'no hesitation in acknowledging that it was owing to [their] liberality and public spirit . . . that it became possible for him to bring the scheme of the Exhibition of the Industry of All Nations before the Government and the public, in a shape ensuring the practicability of its execution'.14

To negotiate the contract and plan the exhibition, the society now set up a special executive committee, consisting of Cole, Fuller and Drew, with Digby Wyatt as secretary. Russell could not be a member, as he was the society's paid secretary. At the end of July, the prince, as president of the Society of Arts, officially approached the home secretary, Sir George Grey, for government approval for a Royal Commission, and was assured that the request would receive 'careful and deliberate attention'.15

Various official strings had thus been pulled. Such an ambitious project as a 'Great Exhibition', however, needed wider support than that of officials in London. This is where Cole was able to make a difference, for it was decided that he and Fuller would embark on a nationwide lecture tour, arousing the interest of manufacturers and communicating this to the government. On 1 August, Cole and Fuller saw the prince at Osborne for a third meeting. Here they were equipped with a 'diploma' signed by Col. Phipps, 'by Command', which stated that the holder was authorised by the prince, as president of the Society of Arts, 'to collect opinions and evidence with reference to . . . a great Exhibition . . . to be held in London in the year 1851, in order that His Royal Highness might bring the results of such inquiry before Her Majesty's Government' 16 Prince Albert then departed on holiday. Cole and Fuller, with Scott Russell who arrived too late for the meeting, watched the royal yacht Victoria and Albert steam away down the Solent carrying the Queen and her family on a visit to Ireland, after which they resorted to their holiday home at Balmoral, arriving on 1 5 August. While the Prince relaxed in Scotland, and while Cole's family went to the seaside at Herne Bay, Cole campaigned.

alists of the North of England and to collect promises of subscriptions. Subscribers' nme were tbe rollen pecalotooksb inilblock e l:

earrFuli  Vinber Mu.er pagandists met businessmen at Stoke-on-Trent, Manchester, Sheffield, Leeds and Clitheroe. They had to be careful how they conveyed their message. Cole had been advised by Labouchere that 'the Prince ought not to be subjected to individual responsibility',1 i.e., that the prince's part in launching the exhibition should not be publicised. Inevitably, however, the royal involvement became known. Labouchere wrote toCol. Phipps, ater visiting Birmingham and the Potteries, saying that he was sure that the prince's project 'will meet with a general candid support', but that I perceive that Mr Cole has given more publicity to the plan in all its parts than I was led by him to expect that he wd do'.19 On this occasion, the only specific indiscretion he complained about was Cole's claim that there would be a first prize of £5,ooo. But greater disquiet was later to be aroused by Cole's propagandising.

When their tour reached Glasgow, the trio made a diversion to report to the prince at Balmoral, and 'went through all points'20 Cole wrote to tell John Scott Russell, then in Germany, that the prince 'was much pleased with the Success . . . . We intend to get 2ooo names of the best promoters in the next six weeks and the Prince is going to appoint additional missionaries'.21 Balmoral at that time was not the grey forbidding palace that is now so familiar, but its predecessor, a modest whitewashed granite country house that the Queen had begun to rent the year before. The royal family 'live there without any state whatever', recorded Charles Greville, 'like very small gentlefolks, small house, small rooms, small establishment. There are no Soldiers, and the whole guard of the Sovereign and the whole Royal Family is a single Policeman'.22 Cole, no less than Greville, was surprised at the informality of Balmoral, especially when he heard the prince tell a valet to escort my wife' out.23

From Balmoral the 'missionaries' went on to Perth, Dundee, Stirling and back to Glasgow, where a public meeting was 'very unanimous'.24 Then they proceeded to Greenock, where they saw the Provost, and from there crossed to Belfast, travelling south to Dublin. There was a lot of goodwill in Ireland following the royal visit, and the lord mayor of Dublin organised a large meeting at the town hall. Cole made a speech in which he said that 'the various and conflicting interests of parties had been debated by the Prince with great ability. . . . The Prince was not moved to these inquiries by any man or body of men. It was his own spontaneous act, irrespective of external influences'. Fuller followed this, saying 'that among all the men Mr. Cole and himself had met in their inquiries at the great manufacturing towns of England and Scotland, in which they had heard the opinions of the most eminent men in trade and manufacture, "the best informed man they came into contact with on all the points of their inquiry was the Prince himself".25 Cole thought the meeting was 'most successful'. The two men then went happily towards home, spending an afternoon at Bangor in North Wales to visit Robert Stephenson's wrought-iron Britannia Bridge across the Menai Straits, where they 'went into the Tubes', and toured the workshops. Cole rejoined his family at Herne Bay, where he found Marian 'much improved'.26

Unfortunately, unknown to Cole, reporters were in the audience in Dublin, and what was said about Prince Albert was published in the Dublin papers and copied by the English ones. Col. Phipps reprimanded Cole for over-exposing the prince, and Cole apologised, saying that he had not known that reporters were present, and hoping that 'his Royal Highness will look leniently on this somewhat premature declaration and ascribe it to the very exuberant desire which all seemed to manifest'.27 The prince was 'somewhat annoyed' to find himself represented as the sole inventor of the scheme, and told Phipps

to remind Mr. Cole that the strictest privacy was originally observed, and to caution him not to be drawn away by degrees from the original position. Praising me at meetings looks as if I were to be advertised and used as a means of drawing a full house, &c. &c. Mr. Cole excused himself about Dublin . . . . In London additional caution will be required.28

It was, however, inevitable that once the press had shown an interest 'our visitations necessarily have assumed a somewhat less private character', as Cole put it when he reported to the prince in October.29

Early in October, Phipps had to reproach Cole again. 'I see by the accounts in the newspapers, that at some of the country meetings, Mr Fuller has again entered into details as to the prizes, and has also held out the expectation of the medals being presented by the Queen. I was in hopes that I had already been sufficiently explicit upon this head.'30 Cole replied that he had done all he could to warn Fuller to be circumspect. He added:

There seems a fatality that you must be mis-reported. I have felt this ever since the Dublin meeting, and so strongly at Canterbury and Dover, that I spoke at both meetings as if tongue-tied and I felt quite beaten on seeing the reports. Notwithstanding all my pains, the reporters put things in a way I never did. . . . I assure you that I am very nervous and uncomfortable.31

Privately, however, he must have reflected that he could hardly be expected to drum up support for a great public enterprise by maintaining tight-lipped secrecy.

Touring the country making speeches was an expensive business. Cole had to borrow £ roo from Fuller, which he repaid the following April.32 Although he was on the stump, Cole was still employed by the Record Commission, and when in London worked in his office at the Carlton Ride every day. In mid-September he discussed his extra-mural work with Lord Langdale, who, fortunately, took a lenient view of 'moonlighting'. 'He hoped I was going to be paid: wished the occupation had more reference tte O:but thout we wee quie jusi in doing h thing as ur salary was t ow.Thought this usess just s rht s eviwi writig History It w still necessary, however, to keep up appearances: a few days later Francis Palgrave told Cole that he hoped his "attendance at [the] Office wd be "ostentatious"4

## \*   \*

The campaign for the exhibition reached a higher level at what was in effect its official launch in October. The good news of public support, which Cole and the 'missionaries' brought,3 prompted the prince to ask the lord mayor of London to arrange a meeting at the Mansion House for 17 October. The City aldermen and councilmen, bankers, council members of the Society of Arts, and the press were all to be asked. Despite the importance of this event, Cole heard, on the preceding Saturday, that no tickets had been sent out. He immediately set two of his clerks to work and on Monday four clerks were busy at the Mansion House, with Dilke,

On 17 October the Egyptian Hall was packed. As the prince was still avoiding the limlight, it fell to Cole to make the mai pech. It was, Cole reorde, 'my first ulic spech', and he posted old Dilke at the back of the audience ready to signal to him in case he could not be heard. His speech was repeatedly interrupted with cries of 'Hear Hear!' and cheering. He began by following the prince's instructions to make it quite clear that such preparations as had already been made were not final. Then he tried to paint for his audience a picture of what the exhibition might be like.

We shall have spices from the East; the hops of Kent and Sussex, the raisins of Malaga, and the olives of the Pyrenees. . . . Of Mineral productions, we shall have gold from California and from the East Indies; silver from Mexico, Russia, and Cornwall; iron ore from Wales, Wolverhampton, and Tunbridge Wells. . . . Then we shall have clays from Bideford, Truro, and perhaps from Putney . .. . In machinery we shall have the steam-engine in all its endless applications, both to land and water purposes. We shall have marine engines in all their varieties; and we shall probably have such machines as Messrs Whitworth of Manchester have recently constructed, measuring to the fifty-thousandth part of an inch. We may have the looms of the Dacca muslin weaver, and the last new power-looms made by Messrs. Roberts. ... I should not wonder if Messrs. Applegarth were to exhibit a printing-machine like that now used by 'The Times' which pours forth 1o,ooo copies of that newspaper per hour.

Some of his remarks revealed his concerns as a design reformer. He suggested that Britons would learn from the section on plastic arts and sculpture 'how much the French are in advance of us in the manufacture of bronzes', though he hoped that Britain could catch up. And he trailed before his audience the prospect of seeing Indian textiles 'which are now almost unknown to us', and which did in the event make a great impression on him.

After stating that the exhibition would be held in Hyde Park, and would be funded by private subscription rather than from taxes, he reached his peroration. 'In short, London will act the part of host to all the world at an intellectual festival of peaceful industry, suggested by the Consort of our beloved Queen and seconded by yourselves — a festival, such as the world has never seen before.' The Times recorded that 'Mr Cole resumed his seat amidst much cheering from all parts of the Hall'. 36

Formal resolutions were passed in favour of the exhibition, for the setting up of a city committee, and for agreeing that the funding would be by voluntary subscriptions only. In the evening Cole made a tour of newspaper offices to make sure that he had been reported correctly, reaching home well after midnight. In the morning he received a note from Col. Phipps which read, 'Your meeting in the City appears to have been most successful.'37 Cole reprinted his speech in its entirety in Fifty Years, as well he might, since it was obviously one of his most immediately effective interventions in public life.

It was clear by now that a Royal Commission would be appointed, as the prince had requested, and Cole recorded that the 'preparation of it was confided me'.38 During the next week he went twice to Windsor Castle to see the prince and discuss how the Society of Arts would work with the Royal Commission. The prince was worried that the House of Commons might yet reject the whole plan. He was in a despondent mood, partly because of the sudden death of George Anson, his private secretary. 'He was my only intimate friend. We went through everything together since I came here. He was almost like a brother to me.'39 Anson's health had been poor for several years and he suffered from bad headaches. All the letters that Cole received from the prince had been written by Col. Phipps, who was an equerry, rather than the secretary proper. After Anson's death, Phipps continued to write letters for the prince, but was promoted to be privy purse and treasurer to the prince and cofferer to the Prince of Wales. As secretary, Col. Charles Grey was appointed. He belonged to one of the great Whig families who were close friends of the Queen, and had been sent to Saxe-Coburg to fetch the prince to England for his marriage. He became for Cole a most friendly, tactful and useful ally at court, first as the prince's secretary, and later as the Queen's. He told Cole to call on him at any time.

Early in November Wentworth Dilke joined the executive committee. Robert Stephenson was elected on to the council of the Society of Arts, and at Russell's suggestion also came on to the executive committee. By now, the committee was set up in an office at No. 1 Old Palace Yard in the Palace of Westminster, with smart headed writing paper. The government agreed to a Royal Commission, and it became urgent to decide who would be its members. On I 1 December Labouchere was surprised at the delay and Lord Granville said he would write to Col. Phipps. There were difficulties in finding statesmen willing to lend their support to such a risky project. Free traders were in favour, but there was no enthusiasm from high Tories, such as the duke of Richmond. Prompted by a 'confidential letter'40 from Phipps, Cole and Fuller invited Richmond to serve, but, an arch-protectionist, he declined.

There were other, more personal, reasons that held some politicians back: 'The Ministers could not of course commit themselves by publicly avowing their disapprobation of an undertaking commenced under such high auspices. It might, however, readily have been foreseen that they would be averse to such a scheme, because whilst it was sure to give them a great deal of trouble, it would afford them no compensation in the shape of patronage.' This was written by the disgruntled mathematician and inventor, Charles Babbage, who for many years had been struggling to get offcial help for his calculating machine. He went on: Those, however, who usually reflect and retail the opinions of the Government, were by no means silent; at i  U heh  eal i "pooh-poohed".41

While there was opposition to the exhibition, antagonism was also mounting against Cole himself.
