Russell told him \*that someone had said I was a great jobber & wondered why he had connected me with the Scheme!. Cole was angry and noted that Russell 'rather stumbled out an explanation on perceiving my indignation'. Edwin Chadwick, who was a true friend of Cole's, called on him expressly to tell him that "znd rate people were jealous of me, & wondered that the Prince shd set me to o the thin r Frans alrvecalle  ol' e n wasao 'vy j However, Cole had his supporters. Richard Cobden told him that 'he had full confidence in any arrangements to which I was a party'. And, as Thomas Cubitt reassured him, 'The Prince was fully sensible of the share I had in the Business & appreciated it.42

Cole and his fellow lobbyists from the Society of Arts were worried about what positions they might ultimately hold in the organisation of the exhibition. By now they accepted that they would not be on the Royal Commission itself, but hoped that the society's executive committee might in some way be set apart from other committees serving under the commission. Russell further suggested at dinner one evening with Cole and Fuller that 'we three shd be distinct & superior to other members of Ex: Comm'.43 They were also worried about whether they would be paid. Cole's salary at the Record Office was £450, to which he could add something for his journalism and literary work. Among the enthusiasts for the exhibition there was an optimistic feeling that the Mundays would make huge profits, and that some of that money would find its way to them. Cole had no such hopes, and with his ever-increasing family he needed to be paid while he worked and could not afford to wait for the jackpot that might or might not be emptied into their laps at the close of the exhibition. He hoped for £ 1,200 a year and told the prince so, while Fuller expected £ 1,000. Drew, Russell and Cole had dinner at Fuller's house one evening in November and Drew agreed to ask the Mundays to pay £ 1,20o a year to Dilke and Cole, and £1,000 to Fuller. Russell said he 'would make no obstacle to this arrangement'.4 But later he changed his mind and 'said the public would regard £ I 2oo a year as a job, that he shd be content with 7oof for his services'. Cole agreed with him, but wrote, 'Fuller upbraided me with Upsetting the nicest pail of milk worth to me £ 1o,ooo at least. . . . Altogether very quarelling [sic] & personal'.45 Fuller later said that Cole had got 'a letter from Messrs. Munday agreeing to give him £ 1,2oo a year for his services; and left the remainder of the Executive Committee to make such arrangements with the Contractor as they might be able. . . . I made application to Messrs. Munday for a similar letter, which they declined to give'.46 Such bickering over terms, however unbecoming, is inevitable when a large enterprise is launched. It was particularly vexatious for Cole, since his financial position was less secure than that of the others.

Cole's position was further complicated when he was officially seconded from the Record Office to work full-time for the executive committee. When he called on Lord Langdale at his house in Roehampton on 28 December  the day that he officially transferred to the executive committee - Langdale 's[ai]d he grieved that I sh[oul]d leave the Record Office: thought I ought to be amply indemnified: to have fixed payment & no uncertainty: w[oul]d help me as much as he c[oul]d & w[oul]d see Trevelyan'. When Cole himself consulted Sir Charles Trevelyan at the Treasury, he thought my proposal reasonable & honourable'. Cobden and Milner Gibson both understood Cole's need to be properly paid. The prince, however, insisted that 'there must be perfect harmony and no jobbery', and that any remuneration should be left to the commission. Cole was in the position of poor relation, among distinguished people who could afford to work for nothing. To be paid might well mean that he lost face. Later, at the first meeting of the commission's finance committee held at the Board of Trade on 29 January, he had to stand up for himself and tell the committee 'I would not take an office or Salary which implied an inferior position to anyone else'.47

Behind this statement, and some of the disagreements just described, was not only Cole's discomfort at being a player among gentlemen, but also a rivalry that had grown up between him and John Scott Russell.48 It had already surfaced publicly. On 13 December, when Cole stayed at home 'physicking', Dilke came to tell him that there was evidently a feeling in Ex Comm: that He and I were working against Fuller & Russell'.49 Russell was a successful and still-rising marine engineer, while Cole was a minor government official, even if he was well known as a reformer and troublemaker. Russell felt that Cole was his protégé and, as Cole recorded a day or two before, 'He said that my office was essentially that of Secy, said we wd be friends on this understanding. Wanted me to agree to £8oo a year for the whole time, he having £7 5o for a part only'. 50 The commission was eventually to give Cole £8oo a year with an extra £25o to cover the work that he had done at the early planning stage. While Cole thus struggled to protect his own position, there was little official progress on the commission. A preliminary public announcement that a Royal Commission would be appointed was put out on 2o December, but discussions as to who should be on it still dragged on.

Christmas intervened. Cole had a children's party on Christmas Eve with charades and a Christmas tree. (The Christmas tree was an innovation in the British Christmas; Prince Albert introduced it from Germany, and after the royal family's tree had been featured in the Illustrated London News in 1848, the public hastened to copy it.) The guests at Cole's party included Thackeray's two little girls, the Dilkes, and Fuller's daughters. On Boxing Day Cole called on Labouchere at the Board of Trade, but there was no news about the commission. Cole spent 27 December walking with his cousin John Bond. They visited Cole's father at Kew, and then walked to Richmond and Roehampton, returning to Kensington over Hammersmith Bridge. On 28 December Cole noted in his diary, with irritation, 'Names in Commission not at all settled'. On New Year's Day Cole again saw Labouchere, who thought that the secretaries ought not to be named alongside the commissioners. Cole pleaded for Russell, who was away in Berlin, to be a secretary. However, later in the day at a meeting at Windsor, the prince 'thought that Russell's absence was significant [and] that he wd not make Commission his primary business. Proposed not to name him as Secy, but as a Commissioner'. Then they discussed whether Digby Wyatt or Samuel Redgrave (Richard's brother) might be a secretary. Cole suggested himself, saying that 'I wd see that matters went right if Secy'. To which the prince emolliently replied: 'I'm sure you would', 1 but gave Cole no further encouragement to aim at this post.

The Royal Commission was gazetted two days later. None of the people who had so far done the work of promoting the exhibition were on it. Prince Albert was the president, and Lord Granville the vice-president. From the political world came Lord John Russell, the prime minister, and Lord Stanley, the leader of the opposition, with MPs Robert Peel, Thomas Baring the banker, William Gladstone, Henry Labouchere, the radical Richard Cobden, Philip Pusey and William Thompson. The duke of Buccleuch, the earl of Ellesmere and the earl of Rosse represented the aristocracy. The latter was president of the Royal Society (Britain's premier scientific association) and was therefore balanced by Charles Lyell, president of the Geological Society and Charles Eastlake, president of the Royal Academy. The artistic world was also represented by Charles Barry, architect, and Sir Richard Westmacott, sculptor. Since the exhibition was to be concerned with manufactures, it was obviously necessary to include industrialists and businessmen: Thomas Bazley, chairman of the Manchester Chamber of Commerce; Archibald Galloway, chairman of the East India Company; Thomas Field Gibson, a Spitalfields silk merchant; John Gott, a Leeds wool manufacturer. Finance was represented by the MPs Baring and Thompson already mentioned, together with banker Samuel Jones Loyd. William Cubitt, builder and president of the Institute of Civil Engineers, might be presumed to have a special capacity to help with an exhibition building. This group had in common a sympathy for the liberal side of politics.

Cole was disappointed in his ambition to be secretary, but at least Russell was not put over his head as a commissioner but was appointed a joint secretary. The other joint secretary was Stafford Northcote, Cole's adversary from the Schools of Design committee. An Etonian with a first from Balliol, he may well have been chosen as some kind of counterpoise to the uncouth men from the Society of Arts. To restore good relations, Cole wrote him a placating letter, which he read over to the prince beforehand. When he had his first discussion with Northcote, he found him 'very cooperative & pleasant'.2 From then on the two men worked harmoniously together until Northcote retired six months later because of his father's illness, his place being taken by Edgar Bowring. The members of the Society of Arts's executive committee were appointed as the executive committee of the Royal Commission, with Robert Stephenson as chairman and Digby Wyatt as secretary. Cole emerged, therefore with no office. He was only one member among others of a subordinate committee. He undoubtedly felt that he had been pushed down. Later, in Fifty Years, he recalled that from this moment 'my relations with the Prince were of necessity altogether changed'. There was now an official distance between them, whereas 'up to that time I had had the privilege of being consulted by him on all occasions, for several months'.53

## \* \*\*\*

Now that the commission was in place, it might be expected that work on the exhibition would speed ahead. So far as Cole was concerned, however, the next few months brought only frustration and jockeying for position. The prince called the first meeting of the commission for 1 1 January 185o. Cole and Fuller attended the meeting and heard the decision taken to ask the Society of Arts to cancel the contract with the Mundays. It was clear that, without the £75,ooo that the Mundays were prepared to put up, the commission would have to find the money for its own building. So the task was no longer a matter of collecting assurances of goodwill towards the exhibition, but of finding substantial cash to set up the organisation and run the exhibition.

The commission met again on the following Friday. Stephenson, Cole and the rest of the executive committee hung about outside the meeting and were never called in. They heard afterwards that their reports had not been adopted, even though the Prince had approved them. No instructions reached them. It was becoming apparent that the Royal Commissioners were ready to attend meetings, but averse to decisive action. They were peers, politicians and notabilities, who had been appointed to lend weight rather than dynamism to the commission. It did not help the committee that the sympathetic vice-president of the commission, Lord Granville, was away in Paris for the first three weeks of January. He later told Cole that the executive committee members 'were objects of suspicion' to the commissioners.

The prince must have known that the grandees on the commission would not want to be told what to do by the middle-class members of the Society of Arts's executive committee. As a journalist pointed out with hindsight,

The Royal Commission consists of noblemen and gentlemen of the nicest honour, entitled to the respect and confidence alike of the high and the humble; but it is no reproach to them to say they were entirely ignorant concerning the work they undertook .... This evil might have been remedied if a few practical men had been added to the Commission; but whether it was considered infra dig to mix up such men with the aristocracy, we cannot say: at all events, if we sift the Commission, we shall scarcely find one to compensate for the absence of experience, and other advantages in the Commission generally, and in their Secretaries.55

Cole and the other Society of Arts lobbyists wanted to be present at the meetings of the commission. But the prince urged them to be patient, anticipating that the commission 'shd find the want of us. He must beg us to give submissiveness in this point'.56 It was not in Cole's character to be submissive. He felt that he was prevented from doing the work at every turn. He could see that an organisation needed to be set up and funds collected. Government departments could give him no help. When he needed to write to towns and cities throughout the country to ask them to set up local committees he found that the Board of Trade had no list of chambers of commerce, nor had the Home Office a list of corporations. This inefficiency amused Cobden. Cole discussed his position with Mulready, who like most of his friends told him to keep calm. 'Advised patience & then instant action when resolved upon.' Rumours and gossip were flying around: Charles Cole told Henry on 19 January that he had 'heard that Wyatt had a contingent interest of £2ooo, & that the Ex:[ecutive] Com:[mittee] was at a "fix" with Comm[issio]n'. The week before, Milner Gibson had shown Cole an article in the Manchester Examiner 'imputing disingenuousness to me'.57

On 22 January John Scott Russell came back from Germany, where he had been building gunboats for the Prussian government, and the difficulties between the executive committee and the commission were compounded by the prickly rivalry between Cole and Russell.8 At Windsor on 28 January the Prince was quite blunt about the position of the executive committee. He would not admit it to the commission's discussions, telling Cole, Northcote and Russell that he 'objected to attendance of the Ex: Com: on the ground that it would make unnecessary the attendance of the Members of Commn. He thought the Ex Com might remain as it was'.59

Jones Loyd talked to Northcote about the problem of the restive executive committee. Northcote in turn saw Labouchere, Lord Granville and Gladstone. Milner Gibson talked to Cobden. The problem as it presented itself to all these eminent perons wasciybetomaroe Co:

It is a I be ecli cey acount of the knowledge and ability they bring to bear upon it. It would be unfair and q will require a strong hand over them, or they may bring His Royal Highness and the Commission into serious embarrassment. It may be unjust, but His Royal Highness and the Commisson ougt to be inorm f the act that ther is reat distrust omee fidence in those who are working it.

No a chair

w v  a   v , and wit cnirabl on the moden whic  oul caryut hneral views of the Commission. Under such a Chairman work might easily be found for all the present members of the Executive, who might take different departments of T      - stitution untouched.

'Who should be the man?' asked Northcote, and revealed that the preferred candi-f for instance Colonel Reid, the late Governor of Barbadoes'. He thought that Reid might be appointed 'quite easily and quietly, and without any material disturbance of the existing constitution'.60 Sadly, Northcote was over-optimistic, and the change was not effected quietly.

On 8 February, Cole and Dilke went to tell Col. Grey that they had decided to resign. Grey had a note handed to the prince during a meeting of the commission, imparting this news, and Cole and Dilke were sent for at once. They were told that Col. Reid was to be their chairman, and their link with the commission. The next day Robert Stephenson returned from a visit to Liverpool earlier than expected. Cole and Dilke told him the news and whipped him up into such a rage at the idea of being displaced that he too resigned. He told Cole that 'he would never consider any one his friend who did not resent the indignity put upon him'.61 So Stephenson, Dilke and Cole all signed official letters of resignation and Dilke informed the finance committee. Immediately they were called in. Labouchere and Lord Granville calmed Stephenson down; he was invited to be a commissioner  in effect, a promotion — and withdrew his resignation. Lord Granville said to Cole that, if he resigned, he 'shd not stand well with the public'. Jones Loyd told him that 'my demand to be present [at meetings] was against all precedent at the Bank: Poor Law Board: Customs: . . .' Cole thought that if I were weak enough to consent [to the new arrangement] the Commissioners wd despise me. All repudiated such an idea. They wd be nearer doing so, if I declined.' He said he 'wd give an answer tomorrow' 62 Cole was in an awkward position, as he had positively resigned and did not know how to back down without losing face. That evening Northcote wrote a private letter to Col. Grey: 'We have been in the hottest of water since yesterday, but I think it is beginning to cool a little.' Stephenson, he reported, had been pacified. He thought that Cole would come round:

I have had a great deal of conversation with him and think he is not so much annoyed at the appointment of Col Reid, as at the retention of the Executive Committee in a shape which gives him no other position than that of Mr Fuller and Mr Drew who nevertheless do not intend to, and cannot render the services that he can and will. Still I think he will not like to give up his connection with the Exhibition, and as the Finance Committee took great pains to assure him how much importance they attached to his continuing to serve, and expressed the same feeling on behalf of all the Commissioners I hope he will think better of his resolution.63

Cole still found it difficult to rescind his resignation, asking Col. Grey, 'pray tell me if you thin I can with proprty ter your letter yestery takeanothercur? Col. Grey tactfully suggested that refusal to serve under Col. Reid would look like jealousy, 'which considering his Character and position as a Public Man who has filled high situations would not be an intelligible reason to the Public'. 65 As Cole recorded, John Shaw-Lefevre also 'urged me not to cut off my parental connexion with "the greatest scheme in the world": that I was sure in any position to be of some use. great use, that it would be injured if I retired, that I should lose all the incidental advantages, My family would suffer. He wd not hesitate to serve under Col: Reid: &c: '66 So Cole, after consulting Labouchere and Lord Granville, withdrew his resignation, as did Dilke.

The man who was expected to save the day, Lieut.-Col. William Reid, Royal Engineers, was nearly 6o years old, a Scot and a distinguished soldier (plate 3o). He had served under Wellington, and, during the various campaigns in which he fought, he had been wounded four times and had three horses killed under him. He spent many years in the West Indies, where he became governor of the Windward Islands and where he did all he could to get some education for the black population, who were very poor since the abolition of slavery. Back in England in 1848, he was elected a vice-president of the Royal Society for the research he had done into the law of storms. He had recently taken command of the Royal Engineers at Woolwich. After his death in 1 8 58, the Royal Society obituarist wrote of him: 'It has been said that his singular simplicity of manner and total absence of pretension caused the distinguished men, with whom he was associated . . . to wonder at first what had led to his selection for the office.'67 Over 2o years later, Cole called the appointment 'prudent and successful',68 but at the time, in the privacy of his diary, he gave vent to hisfrustration. His laments at enforced idleness continued. 'Nothing to do but look at proof of Col: Reids prospectus'; 'waited doing nothing till 5'; 'Nothing whatever to do'; Nothing to be done'; 'meeting of Commission. Nothing to do: waited till 7. heard nothing: Col Reid did not come over'6 Col. Reid's years in the army led him to be content to wait for orders from the commission, while Cole chafed to be up and doing. Reid remarked to Cole at this time that he 'seemed apt with suggestions'70 It is hard not to read a note of irony into this remark. Cole was certainly all too ready tell the colonel what needed to be done.

With amont, it began tolook  i Col.Reid wasnot the man r the jo all. Lord Granville wrote an extremely frank letter to Col Grey, which he aske him not to show to the prince.

SRPeel call me ut f the H.f Lor yetray evenig. He told me that he was seriously alarmed about the manner of conducting the business of the Exhibition, that t Cosn a     rk h  R From the enquiries we made of the Colonial Office, the Treasury, and o Offiers of the Es   n I h  R even of genius, whose only fault might be a liability to take offence. He turns out to be a man of very conciliatory manners, of ability, and good judgment (more I think than appears) but without any facility of writing, and without a sufficient quantity of what is called Devil.

## Granville went on to criticise Scott Russell.

Mr Scott Russell is a bad secretary, his manner is disagreeable, he is stated to have been insolent in some of the Committees, and Colonel Reid seems to have reason to complain o i svralrpectsitmigh edeable iwhen you are har itions from the Prince, you should communicate more with Northcote and Col: Reid than with Scott Russell. Indeed I think that it would be advantageous if he were sent . . to report on the Leipsic Exhibition, or on any other mission which would take a little time.71

Russell was preoccupied with his shipbuilding career, and was neglecting his work as a secretary to the Royal Commission. He failed to send out notices of meetings in good time, his reports were sometimes unfinished, and he tried to cover himself by evasions. He was nearly late for one meeting at Windsor Castle, and had to hire a railway engine to get him there. As we have already seen, the prince had doubts about him, and thought of making him a commissioner rather than secretary. Furthermore, the prince felt he could not 'rely on him for drafting, he did not write good English. Flowery style, too fulsome for him, like a Russian despatch. Manner affected'.72 Furthermore, Russell had very untidy, almost illegible, handwriting. In the spring, Northcote confided to Cole that 'some of the Comnrs had found out that Russell did not speak the truth'.73 In April, Lord Granville had the task of smoothing down Philip Pusey, to whom Russell had been so insolent at a meeting of the agricultural committee that he would not sit in the same room with him. There was friction also between Russell and Reid. They had several acrimonious discussions after Russell refused to show Reid reports discussed in committee. Cole recorded that Reid described the tension between the commission and the executive committee as: 'two different things done on two sides of the way', and that he characterised Russell as 'the wrong point of the needle'. Cole also recorded that Russell 'was the only man that had told Reid he was slow and feared responsibility'.74 Nonetheless, in spite of all his faults, Russell remained a secretary to the commission until after the exhibition.

While both Reid and Russell were earning bad marks in London, the provinces were feeling neglected and resentful. Although Cole, Wyatt and Fuller had toured the country in the summer of 1849, successfully arousing support and setting up local committees, nothing had happened in the provinces for a year since then, and local papers were attacking the Society of Arts and the commission, calling the exhibition a 'job'. This damage had to be repaired, so in June Sir Robert Peel had Lyon Playfair appointed a special commissioner to act as a liaison officer between the commission and the local committees. Playfair was a scientist, and professor of chemistry at the London School of Mines. He had been a student under Baron von Liebig at Giessen in Germany, and on returning to London in 1844 he made friends with Peel, who presented him to Prince Albert. He advised on the dangerous state of the sewers at Buckingham Palace, and discussed with the prince his invention for ventilating a drain. His connection with the Great Exhibition began in February 185o, when he entered the discussions about the classification of exhibits,75 and brought Prince Albert, who had firm notions about the philosophical basis of classification, to agree on a system similar to that used by botanists to classify plants. Cole and Playfair had already met to discuss the subject and had got on well, Playfair being among those who dissuaded Cole from his resignation, urging that 'he was the real pilot of the vessel'.76 In his post as go-between, Playfair was a great success, as he had a lot of charm and an ability to get on with people. He would say encouraging things to Cole, which Cole noted in his diary along with the snipes of Col. Reid. He was 'one of those men . .. whose bright spirits create around them an atmosphere of good will and confidence which is recognised by all with whom they are destined to be brought into contact'77 Like Cole, Playfair was a short energetic man, and like Cole he always wore spectacles.

Playfair, with his fellow special commissioner Colonel J. A. Lloyd, set out to reanimate the provinces through meetings in the principal towns. A team of lecturers was sent out to whip up enthusiasm. But the lecturers, too, failed to give satisfaction to those in charge. Lord Granville again wrote to Col. Grey: 'Col. Reid has been somewhat uneasy as to the class of persons who had been sent out, and is distrustful of Cole's recommendations on this point. He does not intend to assign any fresh duties to these lecturers, without communication with me, when I shall take care to get His Royal Highness' commands'.78 Nonetheless, the work of Playfair and Lloyd in sustaining countrywide support for the exhibition was of great importance.

To support the provincial campaign, it was decided to hold another big publicitygenerating dinner on 2 1 March at the Mansion House, to which over I 50 mayors of towns all over the country would be invited. This was to be an important occasion because the prince would at last step forward in public as patron of the exhibition. The prince held a levee in the state drawing room, and the i 5o mayors were presented. Cole had heard that many of them had bought new clothes for the occasion. The dinner was held in the Egyptian Hall, which was decorated with trophies and with the arms of the towns, fixed to the walls and illuminated by gas. During dinner 'an efficient choir, under the superintendence of Mr. Hobbs, sang several glees . . . [and] contributed to the pleasure of the evening'.79

When the prince rose to speak there was 'enthusiastic cheering'. His speech, brief but intense, was a hymn to progress. Advances in science and industry, and improvements in communication, now made it possible, he said, for all mankind to combine together. 'Man is approaching a more complete fulfilment of that great and sacred mission which he has to perform in this world . . . to conquer nature to his use.' The prince regarded the 18i exhibition as a true test and a living picture of the point o development at which the whole of mankind has arrived . . . and a new starting point f wc ll  wil   c  Te - bined international co-operation, peace, commerce and industry with religious rhet-si by the Archbishop of Canterbury, the French ambassador, Lord Lansdowne, Lord John Russell, Lord Stanley, Lord Palmerston, Sir Robert Peel, Lord Carlisle, Sir George Grey, the lord mayors of York and Dublin and the provost of Edinburgh. Cole thought the speeches capital' and the whole evening as good as it cd be'.81

Shortly afterwards, with Lord Granville's approval, Cole put out a leaflet about the exhibition to consolidate support. Although it was essentially a publicity release  he referred to it as a 'Popular Statement of the Nature of the Exhibition'2 it was not freely distributed but old for.each, wit reductions for quantity. It was printed in very small type, such as a modern publicist would recoil from, but was well articulated with bold punchy headlines to mark each section. Its prose style was colourful, evenlurid It is expected, cried Cole confiently,

that princes will be exhibitors: it is known that the most scientific agriculturists, the most skilful manufacturers, and artists of the greatest genius, will be exhibitors: at the same time every cultivator, or producer, or discoverer in the world, be he ever so humble, will have the prtunity  cntrbuting t thi exhibiton fhe brig forwr an object of a superior character. The cottager who cultivates any seed of remarkable excellence, the cottager's son who invents any implement of increased usefulness, or, working at the loom, weaves a choice and novel specimen of skill; the cottager's daughter who works more elegant pillow-lace than common, or plaits straw in a new and more beautiful way, all may be exhibitors. The exhibition will be one enormous unrivalled storehouse for the useful results of all human industry.

He wrote rhapsodically of what would be on show, including exotic products such as 'Animal substances used for food, such as Preserved Meat, Portable Soup, Consolidated Milk, the Nepang or Sea Slug, and Nest of the Java Swallow, consumed in China'.

Cole went on to describe the building that would hold these marvels, even though no design for it yet existed. 'Eight hundred thousand feet will be under an umbelliferous roof of glass, one more expansive than the world has ever before witnessed,' he foretold. He pointed out the advantages of using Hyde Park for the exhibition, in an attempt to quieten the mounting opposition to its use. The site, he claimed, was well placed for access by road and rail. 'Myriads of working men,' he cried, '. . . will come in "monster trains" from the manufacturing districts to witness their own noble share in this work.' The final headlines come in a mounting crescendo. 'Festival of the Working Man and Woman', 'Dignity of Labour asserted', 'Promotion of Universal Peace'. And Cole ends with an apocalyptic flourish. 'The Exhibition of 185 1 will fulfil the prophecy of the Sacred Volume, and hasten the period "when men shall beat their swords into plough-shares, and their spears into pruning-hooks." It is a stage forward in that millenium [sic] which announces "peace and goodwill towards men".'83

Despite the success of the Mansion House meeting, and the verve of Cole's propaganda, many people were still against the exhibition, and opposition was not limited to the protectionists, though they, like Col. Sibthorp, MP for Lincoln, were vociferous in Parliament. The prince himself attracted criticism, as Charles Babbage noted after his Mansion House speech. 'The best speech which has yet been made on the subject, containing the far-sighted views of a statesman, was ridiculed as full of German notions, by cox-combs whose intellect was as defective as their foresight, and whose selfishness was more remarkable than either.'84 Apart from those influenced by mere chauvinism, there were also many who were concerned lest the government might have to use taxpayers' money to rescue the enterprise. Even the lobbyists suffered doubts. Cole himself now thought the exhibition could not wholly succeed; his view was that 'things would muddle through & that there w[oul]d be a loss at last'.85 He never expected subscriptions to be more than $\scriptstyle \mathcal { E } _ { 4 } \circ , \supset \bigcirc \bigcirc$ . In spite of public meetings and promotional dinners, a gloom settled on all the exhibition lobbyists. The prince was sleeping badly, and Cole complained several times in his diary of his own exhaustion. In the spring he developed a stye in one eye (a sure sign of overtiredness), which Seymour Haden lanced for him. The early months of 18 5o were a low point for Cole. Although in his 'Introduction' to the exhibition catalogue, he dutifully states that the 'fundamental principles' behind the exhibition were 'formally announced in the month of February 18 5o, so that there was a full year for preparations', in his account in Fifty Years he ruefully acknowledges that the executive committee 'received from the commission few orders for any work'.86 As the date for the opening, 1 May 1851, had been decided, time was getting very short.

Feeling that the Society of Arts group had been sat upon by the commissioners, Cole must have felt doubly betrayed when criticism came from the society itself. A clique led by Thomas Webster was gathering, to attack the way so much of the society's energy and money were being used on exhibitions, and especially the Great Exhibition. Not surprisingly, members were worried that the society might be held liable (or even that members of the executive committee might be personally liable) for any losses made by the Mundays as a result of the contract being cancelled. At one council meeting, 'All agreed that Socy cloul]d be cut adrift in the Contract'. At another 'Webster accused the Ex Com: of betraying the interest of the Society by assenting to the determination of the Contract'. Then 'Webster attacked Dilke for corrupt motives. Indignant repudiation'.87

The row between the pro- and contra-exhibition factions developed, ending, as we have already seen, with the triumph of the 'progressive' group, and the possibility that Cole might become chairman of the council. Dilke, speaking frankly to Cole (as his good friends were not afraid to do), said he ought not to become chairman 'because I was so unpopular'.88 A more tactful secretary was appointed to the society after Russel's resignation. On his recommendation the post passed to a young civil engineer called George Grove, who had worked under Robert Stephenson on the Britannia Bridge. He had other, more artistic, talents that would flower later. After the Great Exhibition he worked at the Crystal Palace in Sydenham and organised the weekly concerts there that became so popular. In the 187os he compiled his Dictionary of Music and Musicians, which is still in print today. He was a delightful man: a friend said that 'cheerfulness entered the room with him and stayed even after he had left, so infectious were his spirits'.89 No one could have been better suited temperamentally to cope with the rows that were building up within the society.

In early summer Cole was still chafing. As reports came in from Playfair and Lloyd in the country, he wanted to ginger up potential exhibitors, whose contributions would, after all, make or break the exhibtion. On 1 1 May he was 'rafting Circular to local Committees'. Reid, however, 'objected to too much popular agitation,' since it would compromise the prince and already had done so. He tried to inhibit Cole, but sympathetically, by urging his own experience: 'he had been as great a radical as myself, Cole dutifully noted on 24 May. Cole gained some ground with an analysis o e ace ee  hiors whih was pve byi ert n 3 My.

A month later this was still going round the committees though Playfair tactfully praised it as 'the greatest discovery'.9° Reid remained fearful and petulant about publicity. On 1 June he insisted that 'we must not force the local committees'. If they did not take the initiative in approaching the executive committee themselves, they must be left alone. Evidently Reid was very apprehensive that the prince's involvement in the exhibition would put him at the head of a democratic movement' On 15 June, he told Cole that he 'wd sooner there shd be £5o,ooo debt than that there shd be a great agitation of the working Classes, & Ld Granville he said agreed with him'. (On returning from a foreign holiday on 5 October, Granville reported that 'the Upper Classes abroad thought we shd have a revolution in 's 1'.) In the face of this negativity, Cole was again threatening to resign by 25 June, but Reid soothed him, for a time. However, when, only a fortnight later, on 5 July, Reid returned to his refrain that 'we must not be the focus of any agitation', Cole again wanted to walk out. Dilke counselled against it, as did Redgrave and Northcote on 1 1 July. Northcote pointed out that 'the Commission sympathize with Col: Reids laissez faire system [rather] than yours' He brought Reid into the discussion, and yet again Reid pleaded with his junior to stay put.

## \*  \*  \* \*

Fortunately an opportunity for an intervention by Cole was just arising. At this stage, the main matter of concern in the preparations for the exhibition was the building. The commission had reason to thank Cole for writing into the contract with the Mundays the escape clause that allowed the commission, as almost its first act, to withdraw from a partnership with a commercial firm. This appeared inappropriate or even corrupt, now that the government and a Royal Commission were sponsoring the exhibition. However, Cole and his allies were expected to keep their noses out of whatever alternative arrangements the commission might choose to make. The Royal Commission appointed a building committee in February. The members were two peers, the duke of Buccleuch, who was an engineer, and Lord Ellesmere, who was well known as a patron of the arts; three architects, Charles Barry, C. R. Cockerell and Thomas Donaldson; and three engineers, William Cubitt (chairman), Robert Stephenson, and I. K. Brunel. Like the commissioners, they did not want the members of the executive committee at their meetings.92

An international competition was held, and 245 designs were sent in - a few of them, as it happened, for iron buildings. None of them was selected, and the committee prepared its own official design. This was a huge, solid brick building, with a slate roof and a 'striking feature to exemplify the present state of the science of construction of this country',93 namely a squat dome 200 feet across, designed by Brunel and covered with sheet iron. When the design was published in the Builder on 22 June, there was uproar. Protests about the aestheticinadequacy of the building brought renewed objections to the use of a site in Hyde Park. The Times of  July suggested that the exhibition be pushed out to Battersea Park or even the Isle of Dogs.

Such protests came to a head in a debate in the House of Commons, held on 4 July. Cole organised local committees to send in petitions in favour of the exhibition, and on the morning of the debate briefed Labouchere  for at very short notice

Labouchere had to take Sir Robert Peel's place in leading the defence of both the exhibition and the site. Peel had been thrown from his horse in Constitution Hill on 29 June, and died on 2 July. The proponents of the exhibition were demoralised by this tragedy, but perhaps the shock of it abashed the opponents too, for they failed to carry the day. Cole went to the debate where he sat next to Charles Barry. They discussed the building and Barry told him, 'I have had nothing to do with the design, and repudiate it'.95 Cole called the debate 'flat: began adverse and then improved'. The implacable Col. Sibthorp tried to get a select committee appointed, and forced a division, which resulted in 162 to 47 votes in favour of Hyde Park. Cole went straight to see the prince, and was asked to go up to his private room. 'He was very nervous: Said it had never entered his head that any one cd object. If the site had not been affirmed he was prepared to give up the Exhibition: It was like asking yr friends to yr flower garden & putting them among the Cab[b]ages . . . Personally he showed no thanks for my calling and did not thank me.'96 The prince had come very near to suffering a direct democratic defeat at the hands of his wife's subjects, a situation that he was extremely anxious to avoid. No wonder that, when faced with Cole exulting in triumph, he withdrew into royal formality.

Although the vote was won, it was clear that the building committee's proposed building would not do. A near-miraculous solution arrived from the blue, and Cole was well placed to help it along. On 7 June, a month before the Commons debate, Cole had written in his diary that 'Mr Paxton called [at the offices of the executive committee] to propose his flat roof of glass'.97 Joseph Paxton was generally described as the duke of Devonshire's gardener, but he had become far more than that. It is true that he came of a gardening family and learned the trade. He was only 23 when the duke of Devonshire took him on as head gardener at Chatsworth. There, after designing the waterworks and the greenhouses, he soon became the duke's personal assistant in running the estate. He was made a director of the Midland Railway, and collaborated with Wentworth Dilke in starting The Gardener's Chronicle in 1841.

It occurred to this ingenious man that it would be possible to devise for the Great Exhibition an iron and glass building, along the lines of a garden green-house. He had been talking over the delay in finding a design for the exhibition building with a Member of Parliament, who urged him to put forward his 'notion', even though the last day for entering designs had been in April. On 7 June, Paxton promised Cole and his colleagues that 'in nine days time [I] will bring you [my] plans complete'. As he said in a lecture in the autumn, 'no doubt the executive thought [me] a conceited fellow, and that what [I] said was nearer akin to romance than to common sense.' In the midst of his other business, he reflected on his idea, and it was during a meeting in the boardroom of the Midland Railway at Derby, that his mind wandered and 'whilst the business proceded, [] sketchedmy design on a large sheet o blotting-paper'9 This famous sketch is on long loan from the Paxton family to the Victoria and Albert Museum.

for designs for the building, especially as the commission had by now called for tenders from contractors for the erection of their own design. There was a loophole, however, in the fact that they had invited tenderers to suggest improvements' to their .  ul back of a tender for the committee's design, it might scrape in. This is where Cole came in useful. He had already been sent off to Liverpool, Manchester and Birmingham to try to persuade builders to tender for the commissioners' project. At Smethwick, near Birmingham, he called on the contractors Fox Henderson & Co., construction engineers with a wide experience of building in cast iron. He found that they were ready to tender not only for the commissioners' building but also for Paxton's. Cole stayed up talking to them until half past midnight.

With the help of Fox Henderson & Co., and of Peter Barlow, the mathematician and civil engineer, Paxton was able to turn his idea into a set of drawings in quick time. On his way to deliver them in London, he met Robert Stephenson at Derby Station, and in the train Stephenson 'minutely examined the plans and became thoroughly engrossed with them, until at length he exclaimed that the design was just the thing'.99 He showed the plans to the building committee, and meanwhile Scott Russell showed them to Sir Robert Peel. Paxton told Cole his building 'would cost £80,ooo for the hire'. 100 By this he meant that the contractor would have the building back at the end of the exhibition to dismantle and perhaps put up again elsewhere. He hoped that it would not be sold to the USA. The building committee was hesitant and deferred a decision, but by publishing an artist's impression of his project in the Illustrated London News on 6 July Paxton forced the committee's hand. On 15 July they agreed to 'reject the abortion of a child of their own, and unanimously recommended [my] bantling'. 101 The Royal Commission accepted their decision the next day.

Once Paxton's scheme and their estimate were accepted, Fox Henderson had a frenzied rush to prepare the working drawings. A visiting engineer remembered 'that small band, stripped to their shirts on a hot night of August, planning, drawing, and ccuati sreh, an sres, an cost, a ie This was the  ntribution made by Fox Henderson. It was they, calling on their experience of building railway stations and dock buildings, who were able to turn Paxton's 'notion' of a huge greenhouse into one of the wonders of the nineteenth century. Thus, thanks at least partly to Cole's efforts as a behind-the-scenes negotiator, the great problem of the building was solved.

The finances, however, remained shaky. Though nearly £78,ooo had been collected in small donations, this was not nearly enough. The death of Sir Robert Peel was a tragic blow. Everyone from the prince downwards was in despair. Col. Reid told Cole that 'he did not approve of the Ex: he thought the Prince in a false position . . . wd be glad when '5 2 came'. Lord Overstone (lately Samuel Jones Loyd) called at the office 'to deplore the risks which a Contractor or guarantor wd run: Queens or Princes death. Foreign War', not to mention cholera. Mulready said that 'the great object in working the Exhibition appeared to be not to do but to prevent somebody else doing'. Much later Lord Granville told Cole that he also had despaired 'when subscriptions were low & when nobody liked his fellow' 103 To make matters worse the weather was very hot and muggy.

Once more, however, Cole was able to seize the initiative. On Friday I 2 July, he happened to meet Samuel Morton Peto outside his office in Westminster. Peto, who was an MP and a hugely successful building and railway contractor, was one of the five treasurers to the Royal Commission and on the finance committee. Back in

March he had already offered £o,ooo to the fund, but now he was unwell and about to go on holiday. He stopped Cole and asked,

'How is the guarantee getting on?"

'Not at all; everybody is afraid to begin.

'Recollect, I am quite ready to act when the time arrives.

The time has arrived, and you will do good service by beginning it.'

'I am going down to Lowestoft to-night, and shall be back in a fortnight.'

That will be a fortnight lost, and time is most precious.'

'Do you feel confident that if I offer to do anything, it will be acceptable?'

Quite confident; what you intend to do, do directly.'

Then come with me to the Reform Club, and I will write a letter to Colonel Grey.'

Peto then wrote his letter, pledging that 'I am willing, on behalf of myself and friends, to guarantee the sum of £5o,ooo or, if necessary, to advance the same for the purposes of the Exhibition'. Cole recorded this little scene in full in Fifty Years, 104 and obviously treasured it as a prime example of his capacity to strike when the iron was hot. It seems to have worked.

A fund of £35o,ooo was soon raised, which backed a loan from the Bank of England. Cole promised £5oo, Fuller, to everyone's amazement, put himself down for £20,000, the prince promised £10,000, Fox and Henderson £2,000 each, the duke of Devonshire £500, Herbert Minton £1,000, Robert Stephenson £10,000, Wentworth Dilke £1,o0o, Lord Overstone £10,0oo, Lord Granville £5,o0o, and many more. The guarantors were never asked for their money, as the exhibition was a success. The prince ceremoniously tore up the loan document in October 185 1.

In the face of Cole's discontent, Northcote had assured him on I 1 July that 'Lord Granville will find you something to do'. Shortly afterwards, Cole was sent to Dublin to see the exhibition there. It is difficult not to see this as a manoeuvre to keep him quiet. He set off on 25 July  good timing, since he was able to pause to watch the near-completion of the Britannia Bridge. 'Off to Bangor to see floating of the 4th & last tube. grand solemn & silent & perfectly done.'105 In Dublin, Cole had several meetings about the Great Exhibition, and on his way back to London called on the mayor of Birmingham, who promised £ 5o,ooo for the guarantee fund.

Before he went to Dublin, and on the day that the building committee met, Cole called on Col. Grey. The prince came in and sat on the edge of the table, as he often did. He was delighted about the beginnings of the guarantee fund and said that 'it would stir up others. He said now was the time for work. It was not plans that were wanted'. He was anxious to find someone who could take charge of the arrangement of the exhibits inside the building. 'He did not know where the man was who was to bring the thing together.' Cole with unwonted restraint  for the prince had forgotten that he had already circulated a paper on this subject  waited until the prince and gone, and then had a further talk with Col. Grey in which he 'asked to be charged with the arrangement. He said he wd promote it' 106 Cole's cool reception from the prince 1o days earlier may have taught him caution. Now, with the help of Grey's inside influence, he contrived at last to secure for himself a key function in the exhibition, entirely suited to his talents. While Dilke controlled the paperwork of the exhibition, and Digby Wyatt acted as superintending architect (although officially secretary of the executive committee), Cole the hustler had the task of pushing around the exhibits and the exhibitors.

Col. Reid's reaction to Cole's appointment was adverse. He refused to have the appointment officially minuted by the commission, and 'got quite angry . . . He said he wd make a stand against it'. Furthermore, he declined to act on any of Cole's suggestions to make the organisation better, refusing to allot special duties among the members of the committee, saying that they had power already. The administration of such a huge enterprise seemed to be quite outside his experience. Cole wrote of him that 'altogether he seems quite paralyzed at the prospect'. While he prevented Cole and the others from getting on with the work, Reid would not even make good John Scott Russell's omissions, saying that 'He might go wrong & neglect, but he Rei would o e'  wees  owve e wa  ps more proactive impression, writing on 8 September to Col. Phipps:

From the moment I became connected with the Exhibition I felt impressed with the opinion that . . . success can only be insured by adopting the simplest organization for carrying it out, and making the organization complete before the time comes when it shall be called into action. I have therefore been, for some time back, considering this subject and in fact arranging it . . . to assign to Every one his part, and then to employ myself in ordering and supporting him in it. In charge of Each Section must be men of ability and zeal.108

He then specified who was to be in executive charge of the various sections (for which committees of advice already existed). Cole was put in charge of manufactures, Dr. Royle of raw materials, William Cubitt of machinery, Owen Jones of fine arts. Dilke would be responsible for the general police of the building, Playfair would supervise the juries for prizes, and receipt of money would be entrusted to Mr Carpenter. 'These gentlemen,' wrote Reid, 'will enlist others to take charge of subdivisions of their respective sections.' Furthermore, Reid, turning military experience to good account, arranged for Sappers and Miners (soldiers from the Royal Engineers) to be quartered in the nearby cavalry barracks. 'They are to be employed, in the first instance, in assisting Mr Cole in making a plan and model of the Exhibition . . . so that the Allotment of space for Every individual exhibition will be marked on the plan', and subsequently on the floor of the building, so that the place of every thing will be arranged before hand'. Thus Cole emerged as overall co-ordinator. Reid was not alone in having reservations about Cole's appointment. Even Lord Granville, eventually to be a staunch supporter of Cole, acknowledged that 'I am always a little afraid of Cole's unpopularity with the Manufacturers, but his knowledge of manufactures, his activity, and powers of arrangement make it necessary to employ him'.109

On 27 and 28 August, Cole noted that he was busy with correspondence, 'whipping up exhibitors'. Was it coincidence that the next day 'Reid suggested I should draw up a bill for Patent Law'? Patent law was a subject dear to Cole's heart, as we have seen, and legislation was going through Parliament. He probably could not resist the temptation to become involved. From Reid's point of view, this task would keep Cole out of mischief. Thus commissioned, Cole spent most of his family holiday at Ramsgate on patent reform, breaking off occasionally for walks with the children and Marian, who was rather unwell after giving birth to Isabella Langdale Cole on 12 August. When Cole returned to London, fired with enthusiasm for patent reform, he found Reid, predictably, a wet blanket. 'Reid said he was glad to see me privately to say Ld Granville wished the Executive not to appear to agitate on patents, lest the Cabinet shd not take up the Question . . . Reid said he felt I was in a delicate position.' When Granville returned from holiday, Cole eagerly reported on what he had been doing, but Granville 'looked rather blank when I talked to him ab[out] Patents'. However, he soon pulled himself together and wrote to congratulate Cole on his 'able report'.110

It looks as if Reid made a last desperate throw when, on I 1 November, he told Cole that 'he wished a man of my energy would arouse the people to a perception of the defenceless state of England: The French c[oul]d land 5o,ooo and march up to London'. Cole declined to be diverted on to this topic. By now there was so much to do - 'the work so thickened', in Reid's own words'll \_ that Cole was indubitably needed. The building was going up and practical problems were arising.

The construction of the building had begun tentatively on Sunday 16 June, when Cole went with Harry and Hennie to see the first ground broken. Paxton's design was adopted in mid-July. On 7 September, Cole and Tishy watched the arrival of the first cast-iron columns for Paxton's 'bantling'. The main nave was three storeys high, with identical castings for each storey fitted together like telescopes. The columns were cast at Smethwick and brought to London by train. They were set in cement foundations, using gravel from the site for the mix. The foundations are said to be still there under the grass.

Because so much of the manufacturing was done off-site, the work of erection seemed at first to go on in silence. 'The loudest sound that reaches the ear is the occasional chink of a hammer "closing rivets up". Over so large a space the noise of labour is lost and the building rises almost as silently as did Solomon's Temple.'112 The building was immense: 1,848 feet long and covering 19 acres. This was over twice the length of the riverfront of the new Houses of Parliament, which were nearing completion at the time and are 872 feet long, covering 8 acres. Under Digby Wyatt as upeteding arhit, Chare Fox idmost  the real work,oten ying on site from seven in the morning until ten at night. Cole frequently found him there on Sunday afternoons. Cole visited the building almost every day. Granville also visited, and was deeply impressed. I believe few people have any idea of what the Building will be. When I came out of the enclosure I found myself wondering at the narrowness of the High Road . . .. We may now congratulate His Royal Highness on the great obstacles being all surmounted.'113

Cole was happy. Despite the way Col. Reid had tried to put him down, he now had work to do, and set about it with gusto. Practical problems seemed to find their way to him. On 13 October, he was settling the provision of refreshment facilities, and tiles.On 9 Ocober, he was workingout how togane the inxs. On 24 November he became involved with the catalogue of the exhibition, settling me i e uhe ior Robert El wh em  he , duction, 'without payment'.

On the building site, the columns were up by mid-November, and 8oo carpenters were at work. The ribs of the great transept that covered the tall elm trees were of wood, as were the beams (which in the illustrations of the building can be taken for iron) and the floors. All the glazing bars that supported the glass in the walls and roof were of wood, cut on a steam-driven machine that Paxton had developed, and for which he had received a medal from the Society of Arts in 1841. The magical quiet of the site was now gone, and The Times reported that 'listened from without it seems as if some merry and incongruous village bells were in agitation for the celebration of a marriage'.114 Some 2,o00 workmen were taken on each day, and 20 horses.

As the building arose, stretching from near what is now the top of Exhibition Road to a point east of the Knightsbridge Barracks, it became known as 'Paxton's Palace' and then 'The Crystal Palace'. This name is often said to have been used for the first time in Punch on 2 November 1850.115 Punch, however, was alluding to Paxton's speech at Derby, reported in the Illustrated London News of 19 October 1850, where the term 'Crystal Palace' had previously appeared in print.116

In spite of a strike, the glazing of the nave was done by the third week in November. In the first week of December the wooden transept ribs were hoisted into position. Then scaffolding was attached to special hooks and 5oo painters painted the roof, moving in teams from one end to the other. The roof did not prove to be watertight. In January 185 1 there was a bad storm when 72 feet on the south side of the transept was lifted up. In March there was another bad storm and the roof was damaged in the same place. Cole threatened Fox, two weeks before the opening, 'that if the roof was not proved to be watertight before the end of the week: we shd publish it to be failure'.117 All turned out well.

With so much wood in the building, and with such an enormous space to be filled with inflammable materials, Cole was very concerned about the danger from fire. He collaborated with Mr Braidwood, the superintendent of the London Fire Brigade, and submitted a paper that amazed the commissioners and caused them to order 9- inch fire hydrants all round the building, and to have the Sappers trained to fight a fire. As already mentioned, Col. Reid brought in the Royal Engineers, or 'Sappers', to help with the organisation. The officers worked on the details of planning and the ordinary ranks acted as porters when the exhibits began to arrive, as well as serving as firemen. In command was Capt. Henry Cunliffe Owen, who worked with Cole on the allocation of space to the exhibitors.

The Royal Commission was able to hold its first meeting inside the Crystal Palace on 5 December 1850. Afterwards, the prince gave 250 gallons of beer to the men. This piece of public relations was reported and illustrated in the weekly newspapers. One reported that, as the brewer's dray arrived, 'Hurrahs were vigourously [sic] given, to the manifest pleasure of the Prince, who repeatedly acknowledged them'1 Notwithstanding the benefits such publicity could bring, the royal commissioners were wary of the press. Lord Granville was the only one who wanted them to be allowed into the building. It was not until February 185 1 that an artist from the Illustrated London News was allowed in officially. In The Times account of the building work given above, it is pointedly mentioned that the report is written from the outside of the building. When it was proposed that the press should not be admitted free, but only allowed in if they paid like everyone else, Cole 'entreated Commissioners not to make so fatal and suicidal a rule'. He had to explain that it was in the interests of the exhibition 'to attract the Press to come and report on everything that was going on as much as possible'. He carried his point and Dilke wonderingly commented: 'You get your way when you are in a minority of one'.119 Cole, with so many campaigns behind him, fully appreciated the usefulness of the press and the damage that it would do if thwarted.

It fell to Cole to decide how much space each exhibitor should have, and where the exhibits should be placed in the building. The prince's broad classification scheme had already been considerably revised by Playfair. Now Cole's dispositions were a further departure from the prince's ideas. Lord Granville agreed to Cole's plan on 3 December, but "said the Prince would suspect him of conspiring against him'. The prince made a last stand for his ideas at a meeting on 16 December, but ruefully accepted Cole's plan. Half the building was reserved for Britain and its colonies, and here exhibits were grouped according to Playfair's 3o classes. The other half was allotted to foreign goods. It was impossible to intermingle all these contributions so as to observe the 3o-part classification, so each country was allowed to organise its own display. Space was allotted to countries in proportion to their trade with Britain. So France was given most space, though it was very late in applying for it. It must have been Cole's sheer joy in practical problem solving that caused him to angle for the very testing task of allocating space. Despite his inexhaustible ingenuity, it must have been frustrating sometimes. In his introduction to the official catalogue he ventured to let off a little steam about the impracticality of others, lamenting that the terms 'superficial' and 'square' feet were often misunderstood, and 'the expressions "horizontal" and "vertical" space disregarded or confounded.' One exhibitor mistakenly requested '27,ooo superficial feet  to be arranged in such a way as would occupy half the length of the whole Exhibition!' However, Cole sorted out all these problems, and gracefully acknowledged that 'whilst almost every exhibitor desired some kind of special arrangement, convenient to himself, but inconvenient to everybody else, almost every one submitted to a curtailment of space, and a constraint on his wishes, with a patience that greatly lightened the labours of the Executive Committee.'120

No doubt the commissioners were glad to leave such brain-teasing problems to Cole. We can imagine him, when the exhibits began to arrive, bouncing around in his spectacles and top hat (plate 32), and literally putting the exhibitors in their places. The difficult ones he would pass on to Col. Reid, who would turn his long, earnest countenance upon them and soothe them with his slow calm. It was said of Reid: 'It was curious to see the enraged and frantic exhibitor (the foreigner particularly) swearing at the injustice and favouritism which had consigned his article to some obscure corner or some bad light . . . pass into the presence of the Chairman of the Executive Commiee, and presently merge all cheerfulness and contentent'

On 1 Feruary thexhibi began toarriv. Col siseon nyetrc theil y whg c hepeyh ber of police needed. At first Richard Mayne, the commissioner of the Metropolitan Poic wan tploy ableo uar thebuilin, ute December he was persuaded to reduce the number to 4.

The royal family visited the building frequently. On 18 February 1851, the Queen took her five children to see it, and acknowledged that it 'really now is one of the wonders of the world . .. The sun shining in through the Transept gave a fairy-like appearance. The building is so light and graceful, in spite of its immense size. . . . We were again cheered loudly by the 2,ooo workmen, as we came away. It made me feel proud and happy.'122 Fox, however, was to complain that the royal family 'excited the workmen and exhibitors so much that each visit cost him £2o in loss of time'. 123

Owen Jones designed a colour scheme, 'parti-colouring', which would make clear the articulation of the building, and provide a receptive background for exhibits. He used the primary colours of 'blue, red, and yellow in such relative proportions as to neutralize or destroy each other. Thus no one colour will be dominant or fatigue the eye.' To avoid colours clashing, or tainting each other, he 'interpose[d] a line of white between them, which will soften them & give them their true value' 124 At the end of February, and with eight weeks to go to the opening and goods pouring in by the cartload, it seemed unlikely that this time-consuming scheme would be completed by the 1 May. Fox Henderson put in more scaffolding and extra painters, but in mid-April Cole had to threaten Fox that he would order the Sappers to take away the scaffolding. It was taken down in the nick of time. Cole and Fox were able to remain on friendly terms, despite Cole's bullying, as is witnessed by the fact that Fox took Cole in his carriage on their way to a dinner with Lord Carlisle on 12 April.

As the exhibition was due to open on i May, it was high time to make arrangements for an official ceremony. On 1 5 April, Col. Phipps told Cole that the Queen would grace the exhibition with a state opening, but that this must take place in private. She had been attacked twice about a year before, and her fear of crowds persisted. The next morning, Cole took his introduction to the catalogue to show the prince (who thought it too long), and tried to persuade him to let the public in at the opening. The next day, he was at the building before seven, and found Richard Cobden, William Cubitt and Thomas Bazley all voicing disapproval of the proposed private ceremony. On 19 April, Easter Saturday, there was nonetheless a meeting in the building to make arrangements for a private opening. Under pressure from Cole and his allies, Lord Granville went straight from the meeting to Windsor, and with difficulty persuaded the prince to agree to a public ceremony. Granville gave the news to Cole when they met at the exhibition on Sunday. On the Monday the prince came to the building early with his programme for the opening ceremony, which he read aloud to Col. Reid and Cole. Practical arrangements fell to Cole. It was decided to admit season-ticket holders to the opening, with the result, naturally, that the sale of season-tickets soared.

On 3o April, Cole arrived at the building by 6.oo a.m., and discussed details with Col. Reid and Lord Granville. The Queen, with the Prussian royal family, came for a last private visit and spoke to Cole. She wrote in her journal that 'my poor Albert is terribly fagged'. Problems and difficulties were constantly referred to him, she noticed, 'all of which my beloved takes with the greatest quiet and good temper'. 125 No doubt Cole, Reid, Dilke, Playfair and their staff were equally fagged. At two o'clock the building was cleared of workmen and exhibitors, so that the final arrangements could be made for the great occasion on the following day.

The opening ceremony did not go as smoothly as Cole had planned. The police proved to have been badly instructed, and did not know the nave from the gallery.

Special places had not been reserved for the commissioners. Crowds pressed towards the throne where the centre of the ceremony would be, and had to be held back by Captain Owen and his Sappers. 'Excepting this everything went off remarkably well,' wrote Cole, and next day he received 'universal Congratulations on the success of the opening'. He was constantly in the building, early and late, during the next few weeks, perhaps irresistibly drawn to a blaze of glory that in many ways was a personal triumph. The royal family paid repeated visits during May and June (Cole took pains to record them in Fifty Years), which no doubt enabled him to put himself forward in a good light.

$$
z _ { 1 } ^ { + } = \frac { 3 ^ { r } - 2 \sqrt { 3 } } { r ^ { 2 } } , \sqrt { 3 } = 3 .
$$

If Cole now paused to contemplate his achievement, he would have reflected that first and foremost, the Great Exhibition was a great spectacle. The building  vast but strangely insubstantial, sparkling among the greenery of Hyde Park - was a spectacle in itself. 'A blazing arch of lucid glass / Leaps like a fountain from the grass / to meet the sun!' versified his friend, Thackeray. 126 Inside, against Owen Jones's subtly variegated colour scheme, crimson drapes provided a glowing background for unimaginably diverse exhibits. Many were household goods: in the days before department stores no-one was accustomed to see so much of so much, all together. Other exhibits were huge. Apart from the machinery section, where cogs and cranks clattered away under the impulse of steam power, the main aisles of the building were crammed with giant objects ranging from a colossal bronze lion from Bavaria, through prodigious porphyry vases, fountains, lighthouses and telescopes, to trophies of Canadian timber and American rubber, intermingled with statuary. Thackeray again, writing as a comic Irishman: 'There's statues bright / Of marble white / Of silver and of copper; / And some in zinc, / And some, I think, / That isn't over proper.' The bishops wanted the improper statues to be covered up, but thy remained on view, unashamed.128 Visitors could see manufactured articles from advanced western countries, which, though astoundingly profuse, were fundamentally familiar; they could also, perhaps for the first time, wonder at the exotic, even bizarre, artefacts from eastern and 'primitive' cultures. The exhibition was a window on the world, as well as a temple of consumerism. The credit for designing the architectural setting of the exhibition must go to Paxton and Owen Jones, with Digby Wyatt in the background. It was Cole who was responsible for the disposition of exhibits, so he too must take some credit for the brilliance of the spectacle.

The exhibition was also a community festival on a hitherto unparalleled scale. It was the stated intention of the organisers that all classes should mingle in the Crystal Palace. Although the Royal Commission's working classes committe, on which Cole served alongside Dickens, Thackeray, the bishop of Oxford and three Char h to the working classes to attend, especially in the shape of graduated admission charges. Top people were encouraged to take season-tickets, which secured them privileged admission at all times. From the opening day for about a month the admission charges were high enough to ensure that most of the visitors were from the comfortable classes. But from 26 May onwards there was cheap entry at one shilling, on Mondays to Thursdays, and working people came in crowds. There were fears that they would misbehave, but good humour and propriety prevailed. Many 'common people' came on works' outings sponsored by their employers, or on specially subsidised trains, but some at their own cost and under their own propulsion. An 84-year-old woman walked all the way from Penzance. The organisers conscientiously tried to supply the services necessary for thousands of visitors. There were three restaurants (carefully collected statistics reveal that 934,691 bath-buns were consumed),130 public lavatories were provided, fire precautions were taken, lost property was carefully collected. 'One mother did lose her child, but the first policeman she applied to set her mind at rest, assuring her that the little fellow would be certainly taken in charge by the police, and be deposited, along with the lost handthe organisers, Cole was clearly the most eager to give all visitors a good time.

Cole was delighted with the success of the public lavatories, for the use of which a charge of a penny or a halfpenny was made, with the result that they covered their costs. Why not, he thought, extend this new facility throughout London? So he took 'the W.C. question'132 to the Society of Arts, which promptly, on 14 May, formed a committee, consisting of Cole, Dilke, Lord Granville, Lord Carlisle and S. M. Peto, to establish 'some model Water Closets on the self-supporting principle'. Next day Cole drafted a scheme, and a few 'public conveniences' were set up in London, only to prove a 'complete failure'. 133

A third important feature of the Great Exhibition was its political significance. It was the first truly international festival. 'All Nations' seems to have become a catchword. When two foreign visitors came to blows, urchins gathered round shouting 'Go it, All Nations!'134 Apart from such little incidents, the nations met in amity, and world peace was fostered. The nations did not meet, however, in equality. What made a nation estimable in the exhibition's terms was its success as an advanced capitalist, industrial society. So nations such as Britain and France, with their profusion of consumer goods and industrial products, were the leaders. Nations such as Spain or Canada, which could yield up abundant raw materials but were not yet far advanced in making things out of them, cut a lesser figure, as did places like Switzerland or the Scandinavian countries, which exhibited peasant handicrafts as prominently as industrial products. Modern commentators have often felt uncomfortable over the position of 'indigenous populations of the southern hemisphere'. 135 Their displays were at any rate prominent, since they were placed around the edge of the central transept, presumably because of their exotic appeal. But it is argued that they were presented as ripe for exploitation by greedy colonialists. This is to some extent true. The general catalogue's introductory comment on the section devoted to 'British Possessions in Australasia', for instance, points out that 'many of the objects are of great importance to the merchant seeking a new source of known materials'. But it also calls attention to 'interesting collections of native produce'. The comment on British Guiana becomes quite eloquent about such indigenous objects, praising 'that neat but rude and simple industry which, with little or no elaboration of the raw material, produces implements and ornaments from the most convenient substances yielded by nature'. The argument over whether the Great Exhibition was an imperialist racket will continue. Cole's view on the matter was that the coverage of the colonies was 'imperfect'; consequently, 'the idea suggested itself to me that we wd have a Colonial Exhibition in a few years. Dilke agreed . . . & Reid said "the idea was worthy of you" 36

Cole had an opportunity to expound a political interpretation of the Great Exhibition in a lecture 'On the International Results of the Exhibition of 18 5 I', given at the Society of Arts on 1 December 1852, as the last of a series on the exhibition, suggested by Prince Albert. This lecture was written at speed in the closing days of November 18 5 2. It has been pointed out that it draws heavily on Bentham's 'Plan for Uniealneual eplIal but this indebtedness does not make the lecture any the less interesting as Cole's most sustained effort at a political credo, along radical, Cobdenite lines. He urges that culture can be a more successful internationally unifying force than conventional governmental diplomacy. 'The men of Art, Science, and Commerce, have hitherto had but a very subordinate voice in the regulation of their own interests, which had been too much left to the professional superintendence of their brethren of Politics, Law, and War.' He believes that such events as the exhibition can 'keep nations from going to blows as hastily and foolishly as they have been accustomed to do' 138 And he goes on to advocate a raft of beneficent measures that might be furthered through international co-operation. Already, he points out, first steps have been taken towards standard international postage rates and patent laws, harmonisation of quarantine regulations, and subsidised cultural exchanges. The time is now ripe for harmonising international commercial law; weights, measures and coinage; customs administration and passports; and copyright law. And at the conclusion he veers off into a vision of international bibliographical control for libraries, urging the British Museum Library to lead the way in compiling a 'Universal Catalogue' to which each nation would contribute bibliographical records of its own publications, thus avoiding much duplication of work. This idea was to re-surface in his project for a universal catalogue of art books in the 1860s.

This lecture shows that Cole was able to see the exhibition in the widest of contexts. But his most intense reaction to it came in the narrower context of design reform. It came home to him and others that the exhibition showed that, while British goods were best so far as quality and price were concerned, they were certainly not the most beautiful in the world. His already existing conviction that British design must be improved was confirmed by the exhibition, and the repercussions of the exhibition were to shape his future life.

Although, in the summer of 185 1, his way ahead was not yet clear, he must have felt that he had successfully climbed the ladder of success. The strained relations between the grand commissioners and the middle-class activists were now moothed over, and Cole had arrived as a person of repute.

# PART FOUR: THE LEVERS OF POWER

CHAPTER 9

If the Great Exhibition can be seen as symbolising a new sense of confidence in Britain in 185 1 - for Britain had successfully adjusted to the French Revolution, the Napoleonic Wars, the industrial revolution and the emergence of a democratic electorate  it must be acknowledged, as a modern scholar observes, that 'the sense of confidence was recent, and was celebrated with a degree of relieved surprise'1 Not for the only time, Cole's personal mood probably matched that of the nation. His work on the Great Exhibition had brought him to a position of national influence and prominence, but success had been achieved at some risk, and suddenly. His instinctive confidence must have been tempered by a sense of relieved surprise.

He must also have wondered what would come next. To start with, he might have reflected that the project on which he had worked so hard still had considerable impetus. By the summer of 185 1, the Great Exhibition team had had time to get their breath back. Having achieved a triumph, they began to make plans to perpetuate something of their achievement, rather than see it all disappear when the exhibition closed, as it was pledged to do, on October I 1. Cole was first off the mark. On 5 July he persuaded the commissioners to form a small collection of exhibits and documentation from the Exhibition, which could be the basis of a Trade Museum' or 'Museum of Manufactures'. Cole himself selected and organised this collection, which survived until about 1858.2 Soon, more ambitious ideas were abroad.

Prince Albert intervened on Wednesday 13 August, when he told his inner circle of trusted aides, including Cole, at a meeting at Osborne House, that he had devised a plan to invest the profits from the exhibition  for it was now clear that it would be a financial as well as a cultural success - in a new educational institution.
