By means of this institute, or college, or university (all sorts of names were tried out), the aims of the Great Exhibition could continue to be promoted: 'by the application of Science and Art to Industrial pursuits, the Industry of all nations may be raised in the scale of human employment'.3 The prince had launched his idea to several people in his circle a few days earlier, apparently urging them, as Cole later learned from Dilke, 'not to tell me'.4 But Cole probably got wind of it anyway, since even before Dilke spoke to him, he had spent the afternoon of Sunday Io August 'writing out plan of International University'. At any rate, Cole participated vigorously in the discussions on the prince's project that followed through the month of August. By January 1852, it was clear that whatever form the educational establishment might take, it would be located on land to be purchased by the exhibition commissioners near to the site of the exhibition. The site came to be called 'South Kensington', and, as it turned out, it was here that Cole's activities would be centred for the rest of his life.

In summer 185 1, however, his future was by no means clear. He spent some of Sunday 3 1 August developing a bright idea: a proposal to purchase a collection of objects from the Great Exhibition to serve as examples for students at the School of Design. The school's troubles had been thrown into the shade by the exhibition, but it obviously made sense that the school should derive some advantage from the great event. Through the Board of Trade, Cole obtained a grant of $\mathcal { E } _ { 5 }$ go to finance his project. Extracting such a sum from the Treasury was no mean feat: Cole's biographers detect here the influence of the Prince Consort, while Cole himself gave the credit to Lord Granville, vice-president of the Board of Trade.6 There is little doubt who gave the initial push. Cole wrote quite pressingly to Granville: 'If I could induce you to form a strong will to have the thing done it would be easily done, though I dare say there are no ostensible funds for the purpose, and when done I believe Parliament and every one would be grateful.'7 On getting the money, Cole put together a committee consisting of himself, Redgrave, Owen Jones and A. W. N. Pugin, and, during the final week of the exhibition on 7, 8, 9 and 1 1 October, they met to choose their objects. Their discussions must have been a significant crucible for the concoction of design doctrine in the Victorian period, but no record was kept. Almost all we know is that Pugin, whose 'enthusiasm' for the project was noted,8 and who had 'received the kindest attentions' from Cole, became very upset over the selection of the 'Italian poets' shield designed by Antoine Vechte, because it was not in the Gothic style.

In mid-October came news of rewards and honours for those concerned with the exhibition. Lord Granville told Cole on 15 October that knighthoods were to be given to Paxton, Fox, Cubitt and Dilke, while Northcote, Playfair and Cole himself would be made Companions of the Bath. At this time, the range of honours that the sovereign might bestow was limited, and strict precedent governed their bestowal. Peerges and bronecs were only given t thoe posses  sufficient pey means to support the dignity with becoming splendour'. 10 For ordinary people such as Cole, the only alternatives were knighthoods (which tended to be given to civic worthies), or the Companion of the Bath (the standard honour for people in the public service).

Dilke declined his knighthood, though he was later to get a baronetcy. Paxton, Fox and Cubitt were invested at Windsor at a Privy Council meeting on 23 October.Cole does not record any occasion on which he was invested with his honour, 12 though his accounts for November 185 1 reveal that he had to pay a fee of $\mathcal { E } _ { \mathrm { I } }$ for it. Some of his friends thought he ought to have got a knighthood, as is witnessed by a mock Valentine card sent to him by Thackeray in 1852: this has a sketch of the Queen knighting Cole, with verses (in Thackeray's comic version of demotic speech), 'The Quean I sea / That Rawyal Sole / "Rise hup (seshhee) / Sir Ennary Cole!" /And now at Vin / -ser Castle dine; / And be my Ansum Walentine.' It was not until 1875 that Cole became Sir Henry.

On 15 October 1851, Granville also disclosed that financial awards would be made: $\mathcal { L } _ { 3 }$

of the executive committee. Though Reid and Dilke declined the honorarium, Cole was not able to make such a grand gesture.

Mr. C  f was suspended, though he was moderately paid for his services by the Royal Commission, accepts remuneration, which no one who has witnessed his exertions will grudge him. Indeed, he deserves to be considered the real acting spirit . .of the undertaking, and, therefore, deserves to be amply paid.13

For his exhibition work Cole was paid £3,692 in all.14

This task now being completed, Cole must have looked round eagerly for new opportunities. He did not have to wait long for a door to open. On Friday 31 October, Lord Granville offered him the secretaryship of the School of Design. This was an existing post but not a vacant one, still being occupied by Walter Ruding Deverell, who had been secretary since 1843. But Granville evidently intended that Deverell should be moved, perhaps into the post in the Public Records that was still being kept warm for Cole. 15

With this prospect in view, Cole took a late autumn break, travelling to Vienna with Herbert Minton to deliver a dessert service as a gift from the Queen to the emperor of Austria. Leaving on 19 November, they travelled via Bruges, Cologne, Düsseldorf, Dresden and Prague, arriving in Vienna on 4 December; on the way back they took in Berlin. It was a snowy journey, sometimes by sledge when trains and carriages could not get through. Travel had its ups and downs. Among the downs were hot, tobacco-filled rooms: 'the variety & intensity of the heated stinks is something most impressive,' Cole noted at Magdeburg. Among the ups was food. They passed an hour in the station restaurant at Düsseldorf dining for three shillings and sixpence on 'pease soup . . . beefsteak fried in fresh butter, with pickled gherkins . . . boiled codfish and veal cutlets with potatoes & greens;— then some eels, some tête de veau & a sweet omelette: then some partridges - & lastly a dessert of Pears, Apples, & three sorts of sweet biscuits . . . with a bottle of Rhein wine'.16

Since Cole was about to begin a career as one of the most successful art museum curators of all time, it is interesting to notice some of his comments on museums and monuments. Guided by his Murray's handbook, he sought out a 'very fine' collection of Raphael drawings in Düsseldorf. After some trouble, he 'penetrated where an old professor sat in an unfurnished room' and was shown a box of drawings 'professing to be by Perugino, Raffaelle &c.' He found them 'very indifferent', and, acting the connoisseur, remarked that 'it was a weary half hour to affect to examine them'.

He met the late Gothic and baroque art of Prague for the first time, and his design reformer's dogmatism comes through in his condemnation of 'violent Specimens of Naturalistic Ornament in an exaggerated French 1 7th century style' and 'vile statues on the parapets' of the Charles Bridge. In the Brunswick museum he saw maiolica but was not impressed: 'we had much finer specimens in the Society of Arts Mediaeval Exhibition and they are often to be seen in John Webb's window in Bond Street'. The Green Vaults in Dresden, one of the greatest European treasure chambers, he dismissed as 'mere articles of no use to which years & months of work had been given'. He was happier in the Historical Museum at Dresden, where 'the Art in Metalwork . . . is quite wonderful. Specimens of all kinds of Tools'.17

He often noted down things that he thought should be copied, in the form of plaster casts or drawings, as models for the School of Design, and he purchased some ceramics in Dresden, and in Berlin two fans 'on the chance of them being useful for the Female School of Design some day'. The habit of organising exhibitions stayed with him. Looking round the Continental museums, he wondered: 'Might not some of the things be borrowed by the Government? Might not a system be established of an international lending of those portions of a collection that would not be likely to suffer in travelling? This might easily be done by Railways now?

Back home, Cole took up the question of the purchases for the School of Design, meeting with a setback when the secretary of the Board of Trade, G. R. Porter, 'said the whole thing was most irregular, that the purchases ought never to have been made as there was no . . . room', declaring that he would take no responsibility for the project.19 However, Cole was unstoppable as the coming man at the School of Design, and on 14 January 1852 Henry Labouchere officially asked him 'to undertake the management' of the school. The intention now was to relaunch the school, and to have it run by an artist and a layman, working in harness. Cole was to be the layman, while the artist would be his old friend Richard Redgrave (plate 33), who was already the principal teacher in the school. Redgrave was discreetly asked by Labouchere if he could work with Cole, since the latter 'had the reputation of not being an easy man to get on with'.20 Redgrave agreed to try. Labouchere thereupon proposed to the Treasury that the Board of Trade should set up a sub-department called the Department of Practical Art. Cole would be the full-time head of it, paid £1,ooo a year; 'his tenure of office should depend altogether upon the satisfactory manner in which he discharges the duties which belong to it'. Redgrave, being 'an artist of high professional character', could not be 'expected to give up all his time to the business of the Department' and would work part time for £3oo a year. The department was created by Treasury approval on 3 1 January.

On the day when Cole's department was created, Minton (who was I5 years his senior) wrote to him: 'I am glad to hear that you have taken office in the management of the Sch. of Design. You may be very useful if you will only be more mild & gentle than you usually are  not so obstinate & dictatorial  and believe that you may sometimes be wrong & others right. I hope this plain speaking may not prove unpalatable.1 Perhaps Cole bore it in mind during his rather diffcult early days when he had to impose his authority upon the existing staff in the School of Design.

It had been decided that, while Cole and Redgrave would be in charge of the new department, Deverell would stay on as secretary of the school. Labouchere thought him 'not the best applointment] but he cloul]d not get rid of him'. Cole soon learned that 'Deverell was annoyed' at the arrival of a new chief.2 Cole and Deverell had it out on 2o February. Cole had discovered that at Somerset House 'students come and go as they like', and there were no records of 'the period when any student entered i   a dent is now studying in, or what his present or proposed occupation may be, or any similar information'.23 Cole laid this negligence at Deverells door, and pressed him to 'admit that he had been responsible for the management' of the school and had 'failed'. Deverell hit back by accusing Cole of misrepresenting the School in his enquiries in 1849 so as to create an opening for himself: 'said I had made a hole large enough for myself to creep in by the S[elect] Committee of 1849'. Deverell, 'peevish & fretful', tried to obstruct or bypass Cole and complained whenever he could. Cole warned him that 'one or other of us w[oul]d have to go', and Labouchere made him promise to work amicably with Cole.4 Deverell took to coming in late or staying off sick. Cole tried to win him round by appointing his son Wykeham to a clerkship in the department.25 In July, Cole was lobbying Prince Albert, Lord Derby, the Lord Chancellor and J. Shaw-Lefevre to move Deverell into the Patent Office, but with no luck.2 He next tried to get Deverell transferred to a post as inspector of the Drawing Schools but this was not approved by his minister. The problem was solved only when Deverell, who had been 'run over & lamed', died in June 1853.27

Other members of staff were also unhappy. J. R Herbert resigned on I 1 March; R. N. Wornum, the librarian, had to be brought to heel; and H. J. Townsend wanted to resign.28 Cole also had to secure his position with his superiors. To begin with he had to get a foot in the door at the Board of Trade. A friendly clerk, Thomas Poole Ward, offered him a seat in his office, but eventually Cole was allotted a desk in the boardroom, where he installed a screen and a wash-hand-stand, to the annoyance of G. R. Porter.29 Within his first month in the job, however, Cole was planning to move the School of Design into better premises - to the royal mansion Marlborough House in the Mall, which was already partly in use as overflow exhibition rooms for the National Gallery, and had also been used in spring 185 1 to accommodate an exhibition of the work of students of the school. 30 Through Prince Albert, Cole gained the Queen's permission to use the upper floors. He recalled that on obtaining the keys, 'I moved in with all speed, and when the rooms were all arranged, it was found out that the usual official formalities had not been followed . . .. I was upbraided'.31 Cole knew perfectly well that he was cutting corners. The department moved into Marlborough House in Easter week, and Cole agreed with Porter that he would have his office there. No doubt Porter was glad to see the back of the wash-hand-stand.32

Cole's primary task was to put into good order the art teaching system of the School of Design in London and its 19 satellite schools in provincial towns. When offering his suggestions to save the schools to the select committee in 1849, he had ridden one of his hobbyhorses  the faults of administration by committee ("a subject which, for the last 1 5 years, I have paid some attention to'), and had recommended 'practical individual responsibility'.33 Now that he was in practical and individual control of the school, the main part of this problem was solved. But he wanted to extend the principle down the line. Some classes were still to continue at Somerset House, and though Redgrave had moved up to be jointly in charge of the department, and Herbert had resigned, Cole did not want any trace of the old system in which 'the chief authority is divided between masters of equal rank'. Accordingly, on 30 March 1852, he proposed to his superiors a reorganisation that would place 'the whole duties of discipline, management, and instruction at Somerset House under one master, who . . . shall be charged with the sole responsibility of this school'.34 The man he chose for this role was Richard Burchett, who had been one of the most talented students at the school in the 184os. Despite this  or perhaps because of it he had been a leader in the student revolt against the feeble teaching. However, 'he distinguished himself so considerably by industry, intelligence, and especially by his attainments in geometry, that he became a sort of unofficial master before he took office at all', and subsequently 'his capacity appeared so strongly that his rapid promotion was assured'.35 Cole installed him on 1 1 May, and told him he 'might recommend his assistants'. 36 Burchett remained as headmaster of the London school, in its various metamorphoses, until his death at the age of 57 in 1875.

Cole prided himself on the clear lines of responsibility in his department, and on its administrative transparency and accountability to the public.37 He also repeatedly asserted that it should be 'self-supporting'. This was the opening point that he made in a memorandum prepared to accompany his first estimates, that is, the budget for his department that he had to submit for parliamentary approval. He worked on this at the start of the year, and tried it out on Lord Granville, who polished the style and wrote at the end 'hurrah'. He read it on 29 February to Prince Albert, who hardly changed it. And he submitted it to Stafford Northcote, who made some trenchant criticisms, undermining Cole's statistics and counselling him not to devolve power to the local committees that ran the art schools. While Cole did not pay much heed to these points, Northcote was successful in persuading him to omit the madly reckless claim that 'the test of success' for the new department 'would consist in a progressive diminution of the Government grant'.38 The final version of the memorandum became a letter of 10 March 1852 to J. W. Henley, who became president of the Board of Trade in the new Conservative government, which took office on 27 February 1852. Here Cole contents himself with the ambition to make 'the Department as far as practicable self-supporting'.39 No doubt it was a shrewd move to assure his political master that the department would not cost anything, since he found that Henley regarded the Schools of Design as 'miserable failures' and did not want to 'extend the nuisance'.40 However, provincial schools, which had hitherto received block grants, were severely discomfited by the new requirement that they should pay their way.41

Cole held to his position, but with prudent qualifications. In a lecture in June, he repeated that 'the highest point of ambition' for the department 'should be, to become able to decline any pecuniary assistance from the Government', but fell back on a more cautious phrase: it would be 'as far as possible self-supporting'.42 In a lecture in November, while declaring that 'the principle of self-support should never be forgotten', he conceded that for the time being the department would observe the 'mixed principle' of partial subsidy.43

While extracting as much money as he could from the government with one hand, Cole continued to hold out his other hand to the public. He encouraged the Schools oDig to hol classs not nly for artisans, butin themongs when the artn could not attend, to welcome 'the upper and middle classes', who could be expected Henley, Cole floated the idea that regional art schools might be supported from local taxation, just as museums and public libraries could be, in consequence of William Ewart's Museums and Libraries Acts of 1845 and 1850. Nothing came of this, but the department insisted that new schools must be begun and administered by local committees of management, with the department in a supporting role. Eventually, the department devised a system of paying teachers on the basis of the results their pupils achieved in the department's examinations. Payment by results has been much criticised as educationally stultifying. The department viewed it both as an incentive to teachers and as a reassurance to the public that standards were being maintained. Cole always had a powerful drive to expand his empire to the utmost, but he had to trim to the orthodox view that then prevailed (and which, indeed, he largely shared) in favour of 'small' government and minimal public expenditure.

Wrestling with the budget was only one of the many issues that Cole and Redgrave dealt with. Equally, if not more, important was educational policy. The first essential in art education was the teaching of drawing. Testifying before the select committee in 1849, Cole had declared: 'I should wish to see it carried ten times beyond what it is now, and even to the extent of making the learning of drawing a part of the instruction in national schools'.45 When the select committee failed to give him the influence he hoped for, he turned towards the promotion of elementary drawing by other means: through a drawing committee of the Society of Arts, formed in 1850, in which he was the principal activist. The committee achieved only a little  after all, Cole was very busy at this time  and faded away in 1852 when Cole's department started. As has been pointed out, 'it seems more than coincidence that the Society's interest in elementary drawing schools should begin in the year in which Henry Cole gained control of the Council, and end when he, realizing the Society's inadequacy, had found a seemingly more efficient machine to carry out his project'.46

The teaching of elementary drawing was the first task to which Cole committed the new department. In March, he argued to Henley that 'drawing is in fact an indispensable branch of good general education'.47 One of the earliest acts of the department was to open an elementary drawing school in the Mechanics Institute, Great Smith Street, Westminster: at its opening ceremony, on 2 June, Cole and Redgrave took the opportunity to deliver speeches extolling the value of drawing as a second language.48 In his November lecture, Cole felt confident enough to say that 'the Government . . . has broadly affirmed the principle that elementary knowledge of form and colour shall become part of the national education: and this department is charged to carry this important principle into effect'.49 The department would now not only provide elementary drawing instruction in its own schools, but would also support drawing classes in any elementary schools that were prepared to co-operate with it.

Once the basic drawing skills had been learned, they could be developed in various directions. This was to be the task of the department's network of provincial schools, which henceforth would be able to devote themselves less to elementary and more to advanced teaching. They were to follow a 22-stage syllabus in drawing, painting, modelling and composition, devised by Richard Redgrave; the department set and marked exams in all the stages of the syllabus. Cole was determined that the teachers he employed should be adequate to their task. Their employment was now dependent on their possessing appropriate certificates from the department, which were obtained by passing exams in the various stages of the syllabus. Six certificates were available, based on groupings of the 22 stages in the syllabus; each certificate obtained brought a teacher a £ 1o annual bonus to his wages.50 In order to provide a supply of teachers for the expanding elementary drawing classes, training courses of six months duration were provided by a 'Teachers' Training Master', appointed in October 18 52. This brought to London the young J. C. Robinson (plate 34), a bright, up-and-coming master from the department's school at Hanley, Staffordshire: he was soon to play a decisive part in the development of the department's museum. Teacher training continued to be necessary, and in due course the London school came to concentrate entirely on producing teachers to service the rest of the department's network.

In his testimony to the select committee of 1849, Cole's main complaint, educationally, had been that the schools of design were 'not practical'. He had argued that the teaching of practical skills, necessary for various crafts and trades, was the prime purpose of the school, and in 1852 he returned to this theme. In his November lecture, he went to some trouble to demonstrate by appropriate quotations from official sources that technical instruction had always been at the heart of the schools' work.51 Nonetheless, he was not sure how to develop it. 'Caution will necessarily have to be exercised,' he had remarked in March, as he drew up a list of 'classes of manufacture in which ornamental design forms an element, and is governed by the process', and a questionnaire to manufacturers, intended to elicit just what kind of demand for technical classes existed in British industry.52 Meanwhile, a small group of classes was set up at Marlborough House. 'Architectural details and practical construction' was taught by C. J. Richardson; Octavius Hudson taught 'Textile fabrics and surface decoration'; the German émigré Gottfried Semper led a class on the principles and practice of ornamental art applied to metals and furniture; painting on porcelain was taught to men and women by J. Simpson; and wood-engraving was taught to women only by John Thompson, Cole's old friend. Classes on lithography for women, on plaster casting and moulding conducted by Signor Brucciani, and on technical drawing appeared and disappeared. These specialised classes were obviously much more difficult to sustain than the drawing classes, and lasted only a few years.53

Hardly had they begun, in September 1852, when Semper's metalwork class and Hudson's fabrics class were called upon to make a very public demonstration of their skills. The duke of Wellington died on 14 September and a state funeral, with procession, was decreed. The funeral carriage proposed by the royal undertaker did not meet with Prince Albert's favour, and the School of Design was commissioned to produce something better, 'unready as the students may have been to respond to so early a call upon thei slightly trained abilities'5 It was a race against time, sincy three weeks remained before the funeral on 18 November. 'So great was the pressure at last, that for eighty hours preceding the starting of the car, the workmen of two bronze foundries had not had their clothes off their backs; and up to the very dawn of the day some fifty female students of the school were at work on the embroideries.' Cole conceded that a couple of missing bronze details had to be faked in papier mâché, and that the whole thing was perhaps too elaborate, but it was nonetheless a triumph for the school to have produced such an object.55

Public reaction was not very favourable. Charles Greville thought the carriage tawry, cubrous, andvulgarAnother learne criic while conding therivation of the carriage from 'the well known woodcut designs of Maximilian's triumps, lmented that the execution had been rushed (hardly a single thoough piece of work throughout the whole affair') and saw this as of a piece with the 'restless vitality'of Cole's methods of administration. He remarked that 'the constant fresh bulletins and edicts from the central school to the provincial localities, one order almost overtaking the other on the road, have, with reason, frequently put the Art-commites in the country seriously out f tmper Nonetheless, when the new academic year opened in September 185 2, the Schools of Design had been thoroughly overhauled by the new management.

In pursuing his mission, not just to administer a network of design schools, but to transform the taste of the whole nation, Cole attached great importance to the influence of a museum. Examples of works of art had been collected by the old School of Design in London for the benefit of students, and had been circulated to some extent to provincial schools.58 In March, in his letter to Henley, Cole recommended that such study of examples should continue.59 It could not have been long, however, before he came to see the exhibition of examples as more than just a service for students: in his November lecture he stressed that the public museum that had now been established was the means of 'educating all classes' in order to 'improve the national taste'.60

The creation of a museum of international repute from almost nothing was one of Cole's greatest achievements. It seems likely that he looked around him in 185 2 and perceived a gap to be filled. Certainly, when he looked back in 1866 after 14 years of museum development, he found little to praise in the cultural amenities of early nineteenth-century London. Then, the only museum was the British Museum, jeered at in Parliament as a 'terra incognita' and visited by 'about a hundred visitors a day . . . in parties of five persons only'.

The state of things outside the British Museum was analogous. Westminster Abbey was closed, except for divine service . . . . Admittance to the public monuments in St. Paul's and other churches, was irksome to obtain, and costly: even the Tower of London could not be seen for less than six shillings. The private picture galleries were most difficult of access, and, for those not belonging to the upper ten thousand, it might be a work of years to get a sight of the Grosvenor or Stafford Collections. No National Gallery existed, and Lord Liverpool's Government refused to accept the pictures offered by Sir Francis Bourgeois, now at Dulwich, even on the condition of merely housing them . .. . Kew Gardens were shabby and neglected, and possessed no Museum. Hampton Court Palace was shown, by a fee to the housekeeper, one day in the week . .. . The Royal Academy had its annual exhibition of modern art on the first and second floors of Somerset House . ... It was only at the British Institution, or at Christie's auction rooms, that a youthful artist like Mulready could chance to see the work of an old master, as he has often told us . . . . But an immense change has taken place within a recent time. No topics excite such warm and animated debates in Parliament as the purchase and preservation of pictures and sites of museums, and the public give manifestations of their wishes throughout the country, which are apparently in advance of the temper of Parliament.61

No wonder that in 18 5 2 Cole seized the opportunity to create a popular museum of art. And no wonder that later on he believed that it had had a tonic effect.

Cole's museum opened in Marlborough House on 17 May, only a month after his department had moved in. The Queen and the Prince Consort attended the opening, and 'seemed very pleased'. The first exhibition was limited to designs by the students of the school (who were accustomed to mounting annual displays of their work) and the examples of good modern design purchased from the Great Exhibition. This exhibition was open until the end of June, when the museum closed for the summer vacation. In August, Cole obtained some respite from administration, and with the help of the dealer John Webb started to seek out examples (for loan or purchase) of historic art for the museum. His diary reveals that they went prospecting in the royal collections: to Buckingham Palace for Sèvres porcelain on 13 August, to Hampton Court on 20 August for pictures. Tapestries were also requisitioned from Hampton Court. Cole treated with dealers: Webb himself loaned 'China &c.', and Henry Farrer of Bond Street agreed to exhibit Dutch pottery 'subject to our buying some'.62 When the museum reopened on 6 September, its holdings of historic art had been considerably boosted.

The refreshed museum was featured in The Times, in an article inspired, perhaps even written, by Cole. Henley greeted him on 6 September with the words, 'I see your Puff in the Times. I use a homely word, but I don't mean to imply that it is undesirable to have it.' His words hint, all the same, at a fastidious distaste for publicity. The Times article made the most of the fact that the Queen had lent very expensive pieces of Sèvres porcelain, in which 'there will be found united the highest triumphs of chymistry, the most exquisite skill of the modeller, and the finest achievements of the painter'. It expatiated on the new plaster casts on view, stressing that they showed the architecture and ornament of the Renaissance: Cole was never an enthusiast for the Gothic Revival. And it introduced a new section, on which the writer bestowed a catchy nickname. 'The ante-room is fitted up as a sort of "chamber of horrors," with a collection of all kinds of so-called ornamental manufacture, which are considered to exhibit false principles of decoration.'63

Only Henry Cole would have ventured to devise this display of bad taste, thrust at visitors as the first thing they saw. It contained 87 items (listed in a printed catalogue, which, according to Cole's diary, he was writing on 12 August).64 These objects offended against the principles on which Cole and his 'Purchases Committee' had relied when selecting, from the 185 1 exhibition, the examples of good taste that now formed the core of the museum. Richard Redgrave had developed the principles in a special report he prepared in 1851,65 and Cole's catalogue quotes from Redgrave, cially criticised by Redgrave.

Or-molu stems and leaves bear porcelain flowers painted to imitate nature, and candles are made to rise out of tulips and China asters, while gas jets gush forth from opal Arums. Stems, bearing flowers for various uses, arise from groups of metal leaves, standing tiptoe on their points . . .and every constructive truth, and just adaptation to use, is disregarded for a senseless imitative naturalism. In the same way . .. enormous wreaths of flowers, fish, game, fruits, &c., imitated à merveille, dangle around sideboards, beds, and picture-frames. Glass is tortured out of its true quality to make it into thecup  a lily o an anemone  In fabrics where flatness would sem most essential, this imitative treatment is often carried to the greatest excess . . .; and carpets are ornamented with water-lilies floating on their natural bed, with fruits and flowers pouredforthinverwhelming abundance in all the gloryof their shades and hues or we are startled by a lion at our hearth, or a leopard on our rug . . . while palm-trees and landscapes are used as the ornaments of muslin curtains.66

Objects exemplifying these and other supposed solecisms in design were held up to ment, in giving its reasons for condemning the individual examples here shown, afford the public an opportunity of testing the accuracy of the canons it enforces in its instructions to the students' But surely Cole was motivated not just by an earnest desire to stimulate disinterested consideration of the principles of design, but also by an urge to grab publicity by making mischief. He succeeded  at the cost, perhaps, of rubbing up his public the wrong way. An article that appeared in Dickens's weekly magazine Household Words on 4 December 1852 (from which Cole quotes in Fifty Years) amusingly portrays the resentment of a visitor on discovering that his taste is bad. And the makers of the displayedobjects 'raised an outcry' at their publichumiliation.67 Before too long, the display was tidied away.

Cole reached his most extreme position as a design reformer in the early 1850s. His belief in a playful seemliness, which inspired the Summerly Art-Manufactures, had gradually given way to a dogmatic definition of artistic propriety. This was expounded negatively, by malediction, in the 'chamber of horrors'. Positively, it was preached through a set of rules that Cole and Redgrave caused to be drawn up in 1853. In describing their efforts, they consciously used religious language. In the previous paragraph Cole is quoted on enforcing 'canons'. Redgrave, too, spoke of canons, and of dogmas and catechisms. Their rules, drawn up with the help of Gottfried Semper, were issued in departmental publications, under the title 'General Principles of Decorative Art', and were hung up in large format in the Art School, where they remained to guide students for many years.68 It is not surprising that they provoked some derision. The article about the chamber of horrors in Dickens's Household Words was not written by Dickens but by Henry Morley; it was not long, however, before Dickens himself had a go at Cole. The latter appears, in the guise of an aggressively pedantic government official laying down the law on taste to schoolchildren, in chapter 2 of the novel Hard Times, which began to appear in instalments in April 1854.69 Dickens and Cole corresponded good-humouredly about Cole's immortalisation in fiction, Dickens tactfully conveying his view of Cole: 'that there is reason and good intention in much that he does . . . but that he over-does it'.70

During the year 18 5 2 Cole's life continued at a hectic pace. His working day often extended into evening meetings. Even if at home in the evenings, he frequently saw colleagues for discussions, or dealt with paperwork. He made a point of dining with his children on Sundays, but even that day often included a sizeable slice of work. In early summer, from 7 to 19 June, he made an official trip to Ireland, to visit the Cork International Exhibition and to institute a school of design at Limerick and a drawing school at Waterford. At the end of the year, from 4 to 17 December, he made a tour of the Schools of Design in the north Midlands, Marian and Mary accompanying him. The family spent their holidays at Broadstairs in September, and Cole stayed the last fortnight of the month with them, though still polishing off offcial work. On Wednesday 29 September, for instance, he 'played at Marbles with [the] Children' and at whist in the evening, but also occupied himself 'revising administration of the Department'. The main family events in the year were the birth of Cole's eighth child (and fifth daughter) Rose Owen on 2 August, and the death of his brother Lindsay, of acute dropsy at the age of 42, on I 5 July, while Cole was in Ireland.

On Good Friday, 9 April 18 52, Cole commissioned the painter George Smith to make 'pictures of [his] Children', a project which developed into a 'Family Picture', for which Cole himself sat on 2 1 October. The painting, entitled Evenings at Home (plate 2 5 ), is an interesting variation on an established iconographic theme. In a conventional family portrait, the father would be accorded the place of honour, the wife would gracefully defer to him, and offspring would be disposed hierarchically around. In Cole's picture, Marian modestly dominates, and the children command more or less equal notice, but Cole himself is almost invisible, reduced to a shadowed head, apparently dozing, in the background. A family tradition suggests that he was always too busy to turn up for sittings, so had to be squeezed in inconspicuously. However, a 'first sketch' for the picture71 shows a similar disposition of figures, so perhaps Cole deliberately subverted the accustomed iconography.

## \* \* \*\*

The year 18 5 3 was no less busy than 1 8 5 2. Cole continued to grapple with the problems of administering the schools; the London male school finally vacated Somerset House in this year.72 Cole also tried to develop the museum, which, as an innovation, was on trial and aroused tantalisingly different responses. He noted in his diary on 22 February that distinguished visitors to the museum had given him within hours of each other the contradictory advice that 'you sh[oul]d not teach the public' and 'you should first teach the Public'. Cole continued to pursue acquisitions and loans. Purchases were made from dealers, notably Webb and William Chaffers, and from the silversmiths Garrards, and Hunt and Roskell, who dealt in historic metalwork. Cole was directly involved in seeking loans from Lord Willoughby d'Eresby (11 March), Lord Spencer (23 April), and Mr C. Mills (25 May). The year's major acquisition was the ceramic collection of the Foreign Office official, James Bandinel, who died in 1849. This was purchased for £250 on 21 April 18 53.73 A few days before, on 18 April, Cole was invited by Ralph Bernal to see his collection; and on 23 May his old friend John Sheepshanks spoke to him 'about leaving his pictures to the Department of Art'. Both occasions were portents for the future of the museum.

The rooms in Marlborough House were already sufficiently full to make it impossible to accommodate there another annual exhibition of students' work. So the department borrowed Gore House, Kensington, now owned by the Royal Commission for the Exhibition of 1851, who were purchasing property in South Kensington for Prince Albert's grand scheme. Cole went to Windsor on 7 February to negotiate the use of Gore House with Prince Albert. The students' work was to be augmented by a loan exhibition of English cabinetwork, to which the prince agreed to lend. This was to comprise 'the finest Specimens of all periods and styles from the h h  tu  r u  ublc and included cabinets, coffers, commodes, buffets, chairs, tables, clocks, drawers, presses, couches, flower-stands, fire-screens, and even pairs of bellows'. It was noted that 'the rooms are not b nough to hold them;o that the visitors arecwed; and . .. the general effect, notwithstanding occasional gorgeousness, is heavy, and even somewhat gloomy'.75

The exhibition also included a display of studies by William Mulready from nude models. The Queen and Princess Royal paid a visit to the exhibition on 3o May. The department's minister, Edward Cardwell, taking a look round in advance with Redgrave, was shocked by the nude studies, and, as Redgrave recalled, 'particularly told me not to take the Queen into that room on any account. However, the Queen iI, yCo wh skey onne.76

In the exhibition of cabinetwork, the museum turned away somewhat from the doctrinaire towards the historical, but Cole remained a convinced design reformer, and in this capacity (and presumably also in his capacity as a Kensington resident) delivered an address 'On public taste in Kensington' on 5 April 1853. 'Meeting full & sympathetic,' he recorded in his diary, 'but many thought the lecture very illnatured.' Indeed, so it was, as we can tell from the script (hastily written, much corrected, and ending in furious scribble), which Cole preserved in his Miscellanies.77 Just after the speech in Miscellanies volume 11 is another document, which deals with the setting up of drawing classes in Kensington. This, judicious in tone and neatly written, is perhaps what Cole originally intended to say. But the Kensington churchwardens had repulsed his attempts to hire the Vestry Hall as a drawing school. Much of his speech, therefore, is devoted to inciting his audience to complain to the vicar - and not only about the churchwardens' rebuff, but also about the low standard of music at St Mary Abbots church.

At the start, Cole attempts to convince his audience, by rhetorical ingenuity, that good taste must be imposed. He invokes a smelly 'Irish Hodman', living in squalor in Jennings Buildings, Kensington. In distinctly ill-natured tones, he argues that this wretch must be coerced into socially acceptable behaviour. 'We will call the police to see that only a limited number of persons shall sleep in his miserable, low, paper windowed garret. We will have its smoky walls whitewashed. He shall have better drainage whether he will or no & shall not have his accustomed stenches. It is not our taste to let him breed typhus fever whatever his may be.' Similarly, the hodman must be forced towards good taste in matters of design. But Cole's argument is too punitive to be convincing.

church as a patron of the arts.

Before the Reformation the Church in this country as in all countries and at all times was the director of the Arts. Each religious foundation partook more or less of the character of a School of Art where architecture, building, painting, sculpture, embroidery, glass painting, missal painting, music, physical science, agriculture, in fact all arts, were taught and practised and the results of the teaching illustrated. This agency spread over the whole land.

Can it be doubted that Cole felt that this divine mission had now devolved upon his own department?

While Cole may have been buoyed up by a sense of almost religious vocation, he had to contend with considerable difficulties in the political sphere. First he had to get used to new ministers, when in December 1852 Lord Derby's short-lived Conservative government was replaced by a coalition led by Lord Aberdeen. Edward

Cardwell took over from Henley as president of the Board of Trade, with Lord Stanley of Alderley as vice-president. Meeting Cole for the first time on 8 January, Cardwell modestly remarked that he 'wished me to consider him quite ignorant of the Department' but followed this up with a rather depreciatory comment when he 'asked if it were not altogether a novelty arising out of the Exhibition'. Cole does not record his own reply. Cardwell, slightly younger than Cole, was a Peelite Tory now on his way over to the Liberals, but not yet sufficiently accepted to earn a seat in the cabinet. This may have increased his natural caution: it was said that while Peel's 'caution was only exceeded by his ability, Mr. Cardwell's caution is exceeded by nothing at all, and it is sufficient to enable him to steer successfully through the nicest refinements on the greatest of questions without so much as a scrape'.78 Cole often felt impatient with Cardwel, to the point of considering resignation.79 But it was an advantage for him to be protected by such a circumspect minister.

A crisis arose soon enough, when the Department of Practical Art was re-created, over Cole's head, as the Department of Science and Art. On 21 January, Cardwell came to Marlborough House with Cole's old crony from the Great Exhibition, Lyon Playfair (plate 35). They had been to see Prince Albert the previous day (Playfair assured Cole that it had been intended that he should go too) and now came to tell Cole that his department was to be expanded to cover scientific as well as artistic education, and that Playfair was to come in as head of the science side, with rank and salary equal to Cole's. The initiative seems to have come from Prince Albert, as part of his endeavour to apply science and art to industry. Playfair attached importance also to his own interventions in support of scientific education, notably a lecture on Technical instruction on the Continent, which he delivered in autumn 1852 in his capacity as a professor at the Government School of Mines.80 We may regret that Cole did not confide his feelings to his diary when presented with this fait accompli. He could not have been pleased, but he bit his lip, and we have to reconstruct the situation from the comments of others. Playfair recalled that 'it was not without many a struggle and many a feud that the Science and Art Department at last took shape'.81 And in 185 5 Redgrave wrote to Cole, after a difficult discussion about science and art with their minister, Lord Stanley: 'I reminded him that it was not our wish the relation began - and that Science was at present an incubus to Art.'82 Cole and Playfair were friends, but there was certainly jockeying for position, with Cardwell trying to keep the peace. For instance, Cole recorded in his diary on 8 March that 'Mr Cardwell proposed my name sh[oulld be first as Secretary', above Playfair's, 'but that Science should stand before Art in the title' of the department. With such compromises were the new arrangements cemented. The department now took over several existing scientific institutions - notably the Geological Survey, the Government School of Mines, and the Museum of Practical Geology, all of which were commodiously rehoused in 185 1 in a new building in Jermyn Street, and the Museum of Irish Industry and the Royal Dublin Society. To be placed under new management was not to the taste of many staf in these institutions, especially the veteran Sir Henry de la Beche in Jermyn Street,83 so further personnel problems emerged.

Another complication came on 4 April when 'Northcote & Trevelyan began inquiry into our Department'. Northcote was Cole's friend Sir Stafford Northcote. Trevlyan washarTreveyan, whoarte h reern ndia with theas

India Company and eventually returned there as governor of Madras, but who was assistant secretary to the Treasury from 1840 to 1858. Cole usually dealt with him ovfial mars, ndig hi 'y cv  ridly rrg ht e to  retnteehe et houtheu t In tandem, Northcote and Trevelyan have their place in history as the authors of a report advocating reform of the organisation of the civil service, which they were commissioned to make in 1853 after serving together on a commission to re-organise the Board f Trade.Their report was instrumental in getting riof the old ligent and nepotistic staffing customs in the service and replacing them with disinterested, professional practices.

Cole was on their side. When they had written their report, it was circulated for comment to prominent officials, and Cole was 'one of the very few to approve the method of open competitive examinations', most being content with the system as it was.86 Characteristically, Cole had his comments printed for distribution. Denouncing the 'mischiefs of patronage' in the civil service, he alleged that, 'in respect of punctuality, promptitude of action, simplicity, accuracy in the accounts, style in the forms of registration, and cost of management', public offices could not compare with "any large merchant's counting house'. Pointing out that in the public service 'there is virtually little penalty paid for mistakes', he advocated that public servants should be employed on contract, that 'all candidates for the Civil Service without exception should be subjected to examination before appointment', that those appointed should undergo what we would now call induction training, and that civil service departments should be accountable to Parliament through annual reports. He took pleasure in averring that 'the advantages of preliminary examination before appointment have been signally proved by the great success' of his own appointment system in the Department of Practical Art.87

When he and Playfair frst encountered Northcote and Trevelyan on Monday 4 April, they were eager to enlist the support of the inquisitors in respect of their own pressing staffing problems. Cole secured Northcote's agreement about Deverell's 'incompetency', while Playfair gave 'strong testimony' as to the 'obstructiveness' of Sir Henry de la Beche at the School of Mines. After this good start, Cole was perhaps disconcerted when, on Thursday, Northcote and Trevelyan put to him their conclusion that the whole department 'depended on me', and that this ought not to be the case. The department should be 'so organized that it [would] go on without me'. Cole agreed that his staff should be augmented by a deputy and an 'Inspector'.

There were further discussions with Northcote and Trevelyan through the summer.88 The inquiry seems to have provoked anxiety and insecurity, as such inquiries usually do. By July, Cardwell was 'irritated with the idea' that Playfair and Cole were in a conspiracy to supersede his authority, Cole was threatening resignation because Cardwell seemed to be blocking Northcote and Trevelyan's report, and Cardwell was deploying the counter-threat that it was always open to him to 'cancel the proceedings of the department'.89 But on 23 July, Cole and Cardwell 'had a pretty full & frank explanation. Told him I felt I had not his confidence & that I w[oul]d prefer to retire. He promised every thing to make the Department a success sh[oul]d be done.' These days were made worse for Cole by agonising gall-bladder trouble: on 3o July he at last passed a small stone and had some relief.

Deverell's death in June solved one of the department's staffing problems. On 10 October a deputy arrived for Cole, in the form of Norman MacLeod (plate 37). Four years younger than Cole, he was the chief of the MacLeod clan; while a conscientious landlord of his Scottish estates, he was 'compelled by the disastrous famine of 1847, which ruined owners and tenants alike, to seek other means of livelihood'.90 At first confessing to being 'quite puzzled with the details' of his work, MacLeod was soon on top of it, and was unafraid of Cole, telling him on one occasion that he 'was too candid, to a fault'.91 Cole and MacLeod were soon on friendly terms. On 18 December 1854, Cole stayed overnight with the MacLeods in Ealing. He recorded that MacLeod had 'a good deal of Scotch Clanship' about him, noting also that his wife was 'gentle and pretty, so also his daughter', and that his sons were 'frank'. The MacLeod of MacLeod, as he was formally known, remained as the kingpin of the department for 30 years, until his retirement in 1882.

Another important staff appointment was the transfer of J. C. Robinson from the post of teacher training master to 'have temporary charge' of the museum from 12 September 1853, his position as curator being confirmed on 8 July 1854. Robinson had been trained in an architect's office, and had subsequently studied painting in Paris. On the strength of this experience, he had obtained employment as a drawing master at the schools of design first in his hometown of Nottingham and then in Hanley. The formative experience of his life, however, was his encounter in France with the new fashion for collecting decorative art objects of the Middle Ages and Renaissance: this, reinforced by an exhilarating visit to Italy, became his lifetime passion.92 It is clear that he advanced his career in the Department of Practical Art by deliberately drawing himself to Cole's notice, as a letter of 24 September 1852 illustrates.3 Whether, once in London, he was able to inveigle himself into the curatorship or whether the appointment was a prodigious stroke of luck, we cannot tell. We know that Cole had his eye on A. W. Franks of the British Museum as a possible curator,94 and Robinson was as yet an unknown quantity as scholar and connoisseur. So luck may have played a part. Robinson's appointment was of inestimable benefit to the development of the museum, and in the short term it must have relieved Cole of part of his workload and enabled him to move on to other matters. Robinson was set to work on re-arranging the museum during the summer vacation.95

In the later part of the year Cole escaped from his office quite frequently. After settling the family for their holiday at Farmingham in August, and spending some time with them in September, he made a tour of art schools in Scotland (7-15 September), and then proceeded to Belfast and Dublin, returning on the 28 September. In October (2o-26) he made a tour with Redgrave in the Midlands, which included a visit to Paxton at Chatsworth. In November (17-22) he toured to Birmingham, Worcester and Cheltenham. He had a more relaxed mode of life at home, for his diaries frequently record him playing at chess, draughts, dominoes and whist with his children in the evenings. And he found more time for serious reading, studying Newman ('something very novel to be able to read a book quiety rug Ruskin  Jea Paul Rich ucatin. Hower, the raised its head from time to time, especially in regard to two matters: Prince Albert's burgeoning South Kensington project, and the forthcoming international o attention in $\tt { I 8 5 4 }$

\* \*

Most of the land needed in South Kensington had been purchased by the 185 1 commissioners by the start of 1853. In August 1853 the prince sought ideas for the overall planning of the site from several architects: T. L. Donaldson, James Pennethorne, and C. R.Cockerell. Cole intervened with a pamphlet in which he argued that the 'promotion of Institutions of Science and Art at Kensington' should be undertaken 'rather by the public themselves than by Government'. This was his most focussed statement of faith in 'private enterprise' as against the civil service, but it does not seem to have had any effect on events.9 In the early months of 1854, Cole and Redgrave were once more drawn into discussions with Prince Albert and Edgar Bowring, secretary to the 185 1 commissioners, on how to lay out the site, and they produced a plan of their own. After this, however, the matter went off the boil, until it was brought up again in 1855.100

The museum continued to expand. No doubt Robinson took care of smaller acquisitions (which, he reported enthusiastically, were 'literally of daily occurrence'),101 but Cole had to lead when large collections were in question  and two became available in 18 54. One was the Gherardini Collection of models in wax and terracotta said to be by Michelangelo, Donatello and other Italian Renaissance sculptors. Partly through the agency of William Dyce (who lobbied Cole on 27 March, and proved to be 'jealous ab[ou]t the Department'),102 these had been offered to the nation by Madame Gherardini, who was in London striving for the best price.103 Gladstone, as Chancellor of the Exchequer, was involved in discussions about the sale,104 and presumably took the final decision to provide the money, but in principle the decision to acquire was taken by a procedure that well exemplifies Cole's challengingly unorthodox methods. Right at the start, in a minute dated 2 November 1852, he had laid down that proposed acquisitions should be exhibited in the museum before purchase, so that the public could express a view both as to their 'desirableness' and 'the reasonableness of the prices'.105 Accordingly the Gherardini models were put on show for a month from March 14, to elicit 'from the public and the artists of this country' (as Robinson carefully put it in an introduction to the accompanying catalogue) 'such an expression of opinion, as to their value and authenticity, as will justify the purchase or the rejection of the collection'6 In later times, the authenticity of most of the pieces has come to be doubted, but the collection was acquired.

Later in the year, Ralph Bernal's collection came on the market, after his death on 26 August. Cole knew the collection from his visit on 18 April the previous year, but everyone knew it: it was the only English private collection of decorative arts that could compare with those of France, and it was said to be of superb quality. Cole, Redgrave, Robinson and their adviser, the dealer John Webb, examined the collection on 2o November, and Cole noted that 'the greater part if not all ought to be secured for the nation'. He sought Prince Albert's support and on I 5 December was 'summoned to Windsor' to discuss the matter.107 He rallied the Society of Arts to the cause, and they sent a petition to Parliament on 23 February $\mathrm { ~ \tt ~ I ~ } 8 5 5$ .Meanwhile, however, A. W. Franks of the British Museum was also in pursuit of the collection, and gained the support of his trustees, who also applied to the government for money. In the event, the government did not purchase the entire collection, which had been offered by Bernal's heirs at $\scriptstyle \mathcal { L } _ { 5 0 , \textnormal { O O O } }$ , but let it go to sale at Christie's and gave grants to both national museums: £12,ooo to the museum at Marlborough House and $\scriptstyle { \mathcal { L } } _ { 4 , 0 0 0 }$ to the British Museum. Gladstone also ordered that Marlborough House should not bid for any item more than the price put on it in the marked catalogue, a very disabling restriction, keenly felt by Robinson who took charge of the matter while Cole was in France. Robinson wrote to him:

With all my endeavours, and I have left no stone unturned, we shall not be able to spend more than from seven to eight thousand pounds. The British Museum has done better being unfettered - they have spent every farthing. Mr Franks and I have all throughout worked together with utmost harmony - because, following implicitly your directions, I have always ceded to him immediately. Our losses in that direction have been rather trying.

Nonetheless, both the museums were greatly enriched from the Bernal sale.

These successes for the museum may have compensated somewhat for unsatisfactory conditions in the department, where Cole found the partnership with Playfair increasingly uncomfortable. So indeed did Cardwell, who described himself as 'Phaeton having to drive two Spirited Steeds'. 108 Cole's strategy seems to have been to ensure that he kept a finger in every pie in the department. He did not want to be confined to art matters while Playfair built up a rival scientific empire. He was, then, gratified when Playfair agreed on 14 March that the department 'sh[oul]d be worked as a unity'. There was, nonetheless, a public perception that "Mr Cole & Dr Playfair" represented Art & Science'. 109 Cole seems to have lobbied Northcote and Trevelyan 'to have two Secretaries abolished for one', and was no doubt pleased when Sir Charles Barry, discussing plans for South Kensington, undertook to suggest that Science & Art be disunited permanently' 110 The joint management had to go on, however. Cole submitted a letter of resignation to Cardwell on 5 July $[ 8 5 4$ ,but was talked out of it. Perhaps one factor that made the situation more bearable was that Playfair's responsibilities seem to have sat rather lightly on him. Redgrave told Cole that on one occasion Playfair proposed to go on holiday since 'there is nothing to do so we may as well be away'. To which Redgrave added resentfully, 'I know I find plenty'.111

Cole' dissatisfaction with 'two Secretarie & divided responsibility'boileover in December $8 5 4$ and he submitted another letter of resignation. Cardwell again tried to soothe him, and wished me to write to him my grievances fully & he w[oul]d see how far they could be redressed & what better working of the Depet  o  te f I have felt in the present working of the Division of $\mathrm { A r t } ^ { \prime }$ on 30 December. The departmental structure, he protested, was 'contrary to those principles of administration which I have always advocated. The absence of a clear line of command confused everybody. 'Elements of insubordination are created, and it is due not to the system, but to the singular forbearance of the several officers towards each other, and their mutual confidence and resolution to do the work somehow, that the Department has not become an arena of contest and confusion' He came to a thunderous conclusion:

The system of Administration in the Department which allows scarcely anything to be done, without the signatures of Accountant, Financial Clerk, Registrar, Art Superintendnt, Secretary, and President, leads inevitably to . .  torpidity, and to aconstant timidity o action andistrust o the powers on the part of te Secetari, which deprives the publicof the beneft of talents which they may possess: individual energy and responsibility are paralyzed. It is uffcent if the machine simply goes . . . . The end will be disgrace and failure; torpidity will be embodied in the Department, the whole spirit of which should be active progression, limited only by the annual votes of Parliament, and constantly alert in responding to public wants for extended and improved art instruction.113

Redgrave began the new year of 1855 with apprehensions that Cole was about to accept an offer from Sir Joseph Paxton to become manager of the Crystal Palace in Sydenham. 114 But Cardwell obviously decided that something must be done, and on 26 January 185 5 the department was reorganised. After Playfair's arrival, Redgrave's position, originally joint head of the department with Cole, had been redefined (on 27 April 1853) as inspector for art, and he was effectively in charge of day-to-day art business. Now Sir Henry de la Beche, as 'Director of School of Science', was to assume a similar responsibility in respect of day-to-day scientific business. Playfair was to become sole secretary of the department, and MacLeod, now called registrar, was to attend boards on his behalf in his absence. Cole, retaining supervision of the museum and now adding to that supervision of the Art Library (for R. N. Wornum had resigned) was to be 'Chief Inspector' or, as he came to be called 'Inspector-General'. 11s This arrangement was no less of a muddle than what had prevailed before. Cole later recalled that, nonetheless, 'understanding one another, we have gone on, according to our own instincts as it were. Playfair, although called Secretary, has been professional Adviser, whilst I, called Inspector-General, without duties of administration, have chiefly administered'.116 In the end, it did not matter greatly, because for much of $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 \varsigma _ { 4 }$ and all of 185 5 Cole was engaged in administering the British contribution to the Paris International Exhibition, and the department had to take care of itself.

The French ambassador had announced the forthcoming exhibition in April 1853 to the Foreign Office, which asked the Board of Trade to give it the 'widest publicity'. 117 Cole duly circulated all the 185 1 exhibitors on 1 June. But before this, following his usual strategy, he raised the matter at the Society of Arts, on 25 May, and a committee was formed. Further announcements emanated from France, notably the news that this exhibition was to have a fine arts section, and these were transmitted by the department. By the end of 1853, though, Cole could only note in his diary, on I December: 'No progress with arrangements for Exhibition'.

Things began to move in 1854. On 14 January Cole, who must have been working at practicalities, told Cardwell that the cost of organising a British section in the exhibition would be £7o,ooo. Cardwell was 'alarmed'. He confirmed on I 1 February that the Department of Science and Art should organise the British section, but three days later proposed a budget of £2o,ooo. After some weeks of inactivity, Cole complained, on 13 April, to his ally Lord Granville, president of the council, that Cardwell was 'wavering', and Granville promised that he would "see to it, but did not want his name introduced'. Cole also tried to stir up the 18 5 1 commissioners, though in the event they did not need to become involved. 118 Cole and Redgrave visited Paris on 23 to 27 April, to find out from the British ambassador what was going on. Cardwell began to come round. On 5 May he still 'had no confidence at all that the Paris Exhibition w[oul]d succeed', but thought 'we ought to do our best and spend even 5o,ooo£'. At last, on 19 May, he announced that he had secured government approval for the project. That very day Cole sent a minute to the Treasury requesting £50,o0o, which was granted on 26 May. Cole sprang into action.

He must have already done some groundwork in energising voluntary bodies, and encouraging the setting up of committees of manufacturers representing various trades and many towns throughout the country. The next stage was to "'ascertain by personal inquiries in the manufacturing districts . . . the willingness of producers to exhibit', as he told the Treasury.119 He started to stump the country on 22 June, visiting Coventry, where he was disconcerted to find himself ignored because the Godiva Fair was taking place. However, the mayor of Coventry got some manufacturers together the next day, and Cole went on to Birmingham, Wolverhampton, Lichfield, Stoke, Macclesfield and Sheffield, arousing enthusiasm for the exhibition. He undertook further provincial tours in the course of the year. And he travelled frequently to France.

This French adventure caused him to alter his domestic arrangements. Because he would have to spend much of 1855 in Paris, he decided to move his family to Boulogne, half way between Paris and London. This upheaval came quite soon after the birth of the Coles' ninth and last child, Granville Buller, on 5 January; Marian was now 42. Cole and Marian went to Boulogne on 6-9 April to find a house - even before it was finally confirmed that he was to run the British section at the exhibition  and the children went out there on 18 April. The house in Kensington Terrace was given up, Cole having set up a pied-a-terre in Gore House, which the department was now renting from the 185 1 commissioners. He slept there for the first time on 16 February. The second half of 1854 was a whirl, as Cole shuttled between Paris, London and the English provinces, with interludes to see his family at Boulogne.

$$
a _ { n } ^ { 4 } = \frac { 3 ^ { 4 } } { 4 ^ { 4 } } \times \frac { 1 + n } { 4 ^ { 4 } }
$$

Although an attempt had been made to ease some of the tensions in the department by reorganisation, there was also friction in the executive committee that ran the British section of the Paris exhibition. Cole, Redgrave and Playfair were at the head of this, and its secretary was Cole's close friend, Captain Henry Owen, who had helped with the Great Exhibition and had, since 18 February 1852, been an inspector of schools in the department.1 Cardwell took to doing exhibition busines directly with Owen, rather than with the other three senior officers, which put them nu on secondment froin the army, decided in January 1855 to go back to soldiering, in orr t ohis it or his coy ihen WarThis wasot the ny y i which the war affected Cole. Lord Aberdeen's coalition government fell on 29 January 185 5, after losing a vote of censure upon its feeble conduct of the war, and a Liberal government under Palmerston took over on 16 February. The prosecution of the war under Aberdeen had been chiefly in the hands of the Peelites, who were now discredited; and Cardwell, among others, stepped down on 23 February. He was succeeded as president of the Board of Trade by the vice-president, Lord Stanley, and Robert Lowe came in as the new vice-president. So, although the political complexion of the government was little changed, Cole had to adjust to the personalities of his new masters. The Crimean War must have been on everybody's mind throughout 185 5: Cole and his colleagues in Paris would have thought about it constantly and perhaps guiltily, but they might have remembered Prince Albert's comment when he gave the Paris project his blessing on 17 May 1854. With the war in mind, he said he hoped that the exhibition would be carried out well, 'as we were spending so much in destruction we ought to spend in Construction'.

Henry Owen was replaced on the exhibition team by a fellow Royal Engineer, Captain Francis Fowke (plate 36). After the exhibition, he was taken on by Cole's department, as were several others recruited for the Paris project: George Francis Duncombe and William Matchwick, colleagues of Cole from the record service, who became departmental clerks; Richard Thompson, son of Cole's old friend John Thompson, the wood-engraver, who in due course became an assistant director of the South Kensington Museum; and Philip Owen (plate 38). The latter was Henry Owen's younger brother; he became Cole's right-hand man, and eventually succeeded him as director of the South Kensington Museum.

The first stage of the team's work was to push paper around. They carefully bound up afterwards all the official forms and announcements that they sent out all over the country to attract exhibitors. 122 It was also necessary to set up a base in Paris. Cole went prospecting in early November 1854, and found a house at 14 rue du Cirque. When this was reported to Cardwell on 14 November, he cautioned that 'it should not be made too much of Club for Exhibitors'. Cole regarded it as a 'bureau',123 and that was how it was announced in a circular: a place to supply information about the price and availability of English products. 124 Nonetheless, the renting of the house fuelled suspicions that the organisers of the British section in Paris were simply bent on pleasure, rather than devoting themselves to official duties.125

By early 18 5 5 there was a provisional list of exhibitors,126 and goods could start to travel to Paris, where installation was supposed to take place between 15 January and i 5 March. Travel seems to have gone reasonably smoothly, but installation was a struggle. There were three main sites. The Palais de l'Industrie was built as a permanent hall for this and future exhibitions, but it at once proved too small, so a very long thin Annexe was built along the bank of the Seine nearby, the two buildings being joined by a covered way. The fine art section was in a third building not far away in the Avenue Montaigne.

On examining this last building the British contingent were at once alarmed. The 'huge timber and paper construction', as Redgrave described it, was 'separated only by two feet from a sugar refinery!—that most dangerous of manufactures; and the French do not even think it necessary to insure!' 'It seems overwhelming', lamented Redgrave, 'just as one is working fourteen hours a day, and serving upon committees, sometimes until ten o'clock at night.'127 Cole, however, was not overwhelmed by things like this. He set to and bothered the French (earning the designation 'ce terrible Cole')128 until they built, between the refinery and the exhibition hall, "a wall of great length, eet high, twering above both building

Redgrave set up the British fine art display. He was very glad that he had brought a gang of English workmen to help him. French labourers worked a seven-day week, but 'very easily', with a mid-day break, whereas 'our driving, reckless work for six days is far more than equal to their seven ... two Englishmen are worth three Frenchmen'. Redgrave himself supervised the hang of the British pictures, repelling with 'polite firmness' French attempts to push them into bad positions. He was surprised that the French officials left theirhang to the carpenters.30

The other buildings were, not unpredictably, behind schedule, so that exhibitors were held up and had to insert their exhibits as best they could. In this task they depended on French labourers. There were no cranes, so crates had to be unloaded from trucks either by human muscle-power or by the force of gravity.

If too heavy . . . to move, the packages were slid down a plank, and no precautions were taken to break the orc  theconcussion on reaching th round. I very heavy inded, the packages were unceremoniously thrust from the wagons. Packages of several tons in weight were thus unloaded. The damage done through this cause was very great; the loss to English exhibitors by breakage of glass alone amounting to several hundred pounds.131

The British found that space had been allotted to them in eight different spots in the Palais and the Annexe. There was only one entrance to the Annexe, at one end, and most of the British space was three-quarters of a mile away at the other end, so that many British crates had to be 'carried, or wheeled on trucks, the whole length of the building through the space allotted to other countries . . . to the great annoyance of our neighbours and with inconvenience to ourselves'. When the exhibits had been removed from the crates, the crates had to be taken all the way out again. Two weeks before the intended opening date, 1 May, two new ventilating tunnels were ploughed through the Palais, entailing considerable last-minute disruption132

Everything was so behindhand that the opening was suddenly postponed until 1 May, to the horror of all the foreigners who had booked their hotel rooms in anticipation of an opening on 1 May. When the exhibition did open, with a ceremony presided over by the emperor and empress, it was stil far from finished. 'Skilfullydisposed hangings hid deal counters and carpenter's tools' even in the central part, while elsewhere 'here and there certain stalls, if we may cal them so, display their contents, wholly or partially arranged .., standing amidst a wilderness of ye unopened packing cases'. However, the nave presented a 'gay effect', with a series of eye-catchers:

T h great lighthouse ornamented with Gerome's allegories of the maritime towns; the Eglih, Frecan rusi atury;the reat.Gobai gass;ebeu tiful carved altars; the French and Belgian church sculpture; the mysterious transit circle from Greenwich . . .; the Bohemian glass; the Berlin castings; the great carved birdcage; Osler's huge crystal candelabrum. . .133

The exhibition was inevitably compared with the 1851 Great Exhibition, and, predictably, there were widely varying views. One critic confessed to 'disappointment' when he remembered the Crystal Palace; he thought the French exhibition merely 'a hue bazar yiother cou muc  prai, wever  e solid construction of the Palais, which 'has enabled the architect to construct spacious staircases of solidmaterials and noble landing-places of great architectural beauty'15

The exhibits at the 1851 and 1855 exhibitions were also compared. One critic (and a taste reformer at that) could see no difference after only four years: 'Paris labours under the misfortune of treading too closely upon the heels of its English rival.'136 Matthew Digby Wyatt, however, with his highly developed grasp of design reform theory, had no difficulty, when examining the furniture, in seeing how all the countries had moved about in the aesthetic league table since 18 5 1. 'France and England had made most progress, Austria and Tuscany had lost most ground; the Papal states, Canada, Belgium, and Hamburg amongst the Hanse Towns, had steadily improved, and . . . the remainder had neither strikingly advanced nor receded.'137

After the completion of the installation of the exhibition during 'a long, cold srig' there might have seemed to be some prospect  relaxation or Col.But there was evidently a row between him and the department in Britain about the appointment of the British jurors, which Playfair had handled at the London end. A suggestion that Playfair should change places with Cole in Paris during the summer provoked an explosion from Cole. Playfair (who said that 'my preference strongly would be not to spend a month or two in Paris') wrote a careful, diplomatic letter on 1 May, followed the next day by a private note: 'Now that the thunder cloud has burst I hope the air is more enjoyable & less sultry over the Rue de Cirque. I felt by your recent letters that a cloud was gathering but I could not in the least comprehend why.'139 Cole stayed on in Paris, but he had ruffled feathers among the French, and Playfair recorded that 'I was sent over to Paris by the Government to act as peacemaker'. 140

Later in the summer, Queen Victoria and Prince Albert paid a visit to the exhibition, also attending the usual state balls and banquets. On 17 August the British contingent waited in the road for the Queen's procession to go by. 'We resolved,' recalled Redgrave the next day, 'to give the Queen three hearty English hurrahs as she passed. Philip Owen . . . was fugleman, and right heartily we gave tongue, so much so that we all cracked our voices, and are as hoarse as frogs today.'141

The exhibition closed in November. Cole could be pleased with himself on several counts. He brought the project in under budget. On 1 5 November 1854 he had told the Board of Trade's junior minister, Lord Stanley, that he thought he could get by with only two thirds of his £ 5o,ooo budget. 'That would be a work well done,' said Stanley. Cole's expenditure in fact totalled £39,259.142 He considered that 'as a whole, the British section, although necessarily much less in quantity, more completely represented the productions of the United Kingdom and its Colonies, than the Exhibition of 18 5 1'. 143 He did not leave a detailed aesthetic critique of design in the exhibition, but Redgrave did. Repeating what he had done in 185 1, he prepared a special 'Report on the present State of Design applied to Manufacture, as shown in the Paris Universal Exhibition', in which he rehearsed the department's rules of taste, compared art education in France and England, and gave a brief critique of design in each class of exhibits. His conclusion, which Cole probably shared, was that the

Paris exhibition 'showed evidence of a great general progress in English manufactures towards good taste, and that if we are still behind France in this respect we are behind no other European nation'.144 The exhibition also fulfilled Milner Gibson's tat via French tariff restrictions were reduced during and after the exhibition, and English exports to France rose.
