As Cole said, 'probably no other mode of attacking successfully an obstinate prohibitive system could have been devised, to which the French would have submitted so readily and so patiently'. 146

From time to time, Cole devoted thought to the museum. He visited French collections, and noted at least once (6 September 18 54) that he and Redgrave had been visiting 'curiosity dealers to make purchases'. Towards the end of the year, Herbert Minton tipped him off about the forthcoming sale of a collection in Toulouse. Minton knew about this from his colleague Léon Arnoux, a native of Toulouse who had trained at the art school there, and in 1849 became art director at Minton's factory in Stoke-on-Trent, where he remained for the rest of his life. The collection in Toulouse had been made by a lawyer, Jules Soulages, during the 183os when he was working in Paris. He was among the pioneer generation of French collectors of the decorative arts of the Middle Ages and Renaissance, and he had especially pursued Italian Renaissance material, while travelling in Italy. His collection might well be ranked alongside those of Alexandre du Sommerard, Pierre Révoil and Charles Sauvageot, and, like theirs, was celebrated among connoisseurs. As Cole would have known, Révoil's collection had been acquired by the Louvre in 1828, while du Sommerard's collection, bought by the French state in 1843, was open to the public as the Musée de Cluny, often held up before Britons as the ideal that the Marlborough House museum was striving to emulate. Sauvageot donated his collection to the Louvre in 1857, but it was still in its owner's apartment in 1855, where Cole and Redgrave saw it on 15 November, and longed to get their hands on it.147 Soulages had taken his collection with him when he retired to his birthplace, Toulouse, had kept it together, and now wanted to sel it en bloc. Thus the prospect opened before Cole of immensely strengthening the Marlborough House museum at a stroke, if he could secure this collection.

He set off on 4 October 18 5 5 to visit the collection, with his brother-in-law-to-be, Thurston Thompson, making part of the journey by boat along the great inland waterway formed by the River Garonne and the seventeenth-century Canal du Midi, which links the Atlantic to the Mediterranean and passes through Toulouse. 148 He spent twodays scruising the collectin, and orme the concusion that it was my duty to effect the purchase if possible for the nation'. Those are his own words in Fiy Years, where he adds his conviction that the collection would be of great use to manufacturers'149 Whether or not this was true, the collection certainly had immense antiquarian value, and, when he returned to work in Britain in 1856, Cole applied himself with relentless determination tosecuring it.

Heent s hei, 'ma is ndiffculLor Stany h minister, was 'very peremptory against including purchases at Toulouse in Estimates', i.in theeparte's buget, andeven Redgrave 'bjete tomy buying theToulue Mu'Rrave paps hah  y:8gust he was able el Cole that John Sheepshanks had undertaken to donate his collection of contemporary

British paintings to the museum, another substantial and important acquisition. Cole, denied support from the government for the acquisition of the Soulages collection, turned  as he was always eager to do  to private enterprise. He organised a consortium of distinguished people to loan money to a fund that would buy the collection, andoffer it to the nation at cost price; if it ultimately became necessary, the collection could be sold again to reover themoney lent.As soften, the key to Col's cmp was securing the support of Prince Albert. He undertook to subscribe £1ooo on 5 May, but insisted that his name should not appear in any transactions. Cole arranged for John Webb to value the collection and ship it back to England, for exhibition at Marlborough House. As the crates were unpacked in the autumn (disclosing, unfortunately, a few breakages, which Webb arranged to be mended),151 Cole gleefully noted (on 4 November) that the collection was 'coming out very strong'.

## \* \* \*

The Soulages collection continued to occupy Cole in 1856, and there were several other long-term issues that hovered over him as he settled back into routine work. At the very beginning of the year he was back in Boulogne, writing his report on the Paris exhibition, and obviously enjoying the opportunity to draw breath, for he writes in his diary a summary note about the pleasures of Boulogne, to cover the days up to I 1 January, when he left for England.

The Weather usually mild & clear, often very bright & Sunny. Boulogne gives walks for all Weathers. The grande Rue is always clean, & even in Wet is less wet than most streets: The ramparts for a short mile walk are excellent. The Pier is always animated. The Sands on the West are good. Then there is the Calais Road & to the Column across down land: & there is the [military] Camp an excellent walk in fine weather.

Back in Britain, his routine work was now dominated by tours to inspect schools: in Hampshire in January; in Cornwall and Devon in March; a wide sweep round the Potteries, Lancashire and Yorkshire in April; a long spell in Liverpool, followed by Wales, in June; to Alnwick and back through Lancashire, Cheshire and the Midlands in late August and early September.

After the French adventure, he had to reorganise his domestic arrangements in Britain, so that the family could return. His first move to solve this problem was to find a country cottage near enough to London for him to commute to work. On Sunday 3 February, he went to visit his cousin Philip Jacob at Stoke Fields near Guildford, and on Monday Mrs Jacob took him to look at Elm Cottage (plate 39) in the pretty old village of Shere, in a winding valley between Guildford and Dorking. The parish of Shere, Cole was later to record,

contains under 1,5oo inhabitants, many of whom live among the sand-hills at a distance from the little place, which, although within 3o miles of the metropolis, yield even blackcock to the sportsman, and once on a time gave cover to the Sussex smuggler, before the dawn of free trade. It has a church with architecture beginning with the Normans (I am not sure that a Saxon bit or two might not be found left in the tower), besides offering specimens of all the broad divisions of English architecture down to the days of modern church-wardenism.152

Cole liked the cottage, and on 18 February agreed to rent it for 5 years at £3o a year. Four days later Redgrave helped him choose wallpapers at Woollams in Marylebone, and on the weekend of 5-6 April Richard Thompson helped him move his furniture in. The family arrived the following week and lived here through the summer, Cole travelling up to town most days by train.153

At this time Cole took up photography, and made a series of photographs of the cottage, both its exterior and, more interestingly, its interior.154 These are preserved in an album called 'Marian F. Cole's Scrap Book', now in the V&A. They reveal (albeit in monochrome) Cole's taste in wallpapers, and the way the cottage was comfortably filled with pictures and bibelots, including some Felix Summerly art-manufactures. Also included are Cole's daughters and his servants, in informal, domestic poses (plate 4o). They must have had to stay still for the necessarily long exposures, but the photographs are as near to "snapshots' as was then possible.

A near neighbour in the country was Henry Drummond, a wealthy and eccentric Member of Parliament, who lived in the hall at Albury, the next village. Cole made friends with him, and the family often walked in his park. Drummond's principal achievement in life was as a founder and leader of the Catholic Apostolic Church. This was a millenarian sect, but unlike most such bodies, equipped itself with liturgy and ceremonial closely modelled on those of the Roman Catholic Church. Since it anticipated the imminent end of the world, it adopted no procedure for ordaining clergy in the future. As a result it still exists as a secular body, with property including several architecturally distinguished churches, but, having no priesthood, is virtually unable to function as a religious body. Cole attended Drummond's Catholic Apostolic church at Albury on Whit Sunday, 1 1 May 1856, taking note of the lighted lamps (a special feature of the sect's ritual), incense, chants, priests 'in dresses', and the practice of speaking with tongues (another special feature). In respect of the architecture and accoutrements, he observed, characteristically: 'ornament not very correct'.

Drummond was one of his coadjutors in a scheme to ginger up the local peasantry. A meeting was held on 5 November 1856, at which (Cole noted in his diary) Drummond was 'very droll'. Cole enlarged on this in Fifty Years. To begin with there was a polite contention over who should take the chair. The local vicar proposed Drummond, but he magnificently riposted, 'No, no, every cock on his own dunghill, so the vicar had to preside. Cole outlined his plans for a 'Labourers' Improvement Society', which would hold an annual country show. This provoked Drummond to deride the name 'Labourers' Improvement Society', mischievously surmising that the labourers might respond by creating a 'Landlords' Improvement Society', to improve 'us' and 'other classes who want improvement'. Nonetheless, he offered to help. The name was tactfully changed to the 'Tillingbourne Yearly Holiday Association', after the stream that ran through the village, and plans were laid for action in theesuing summer.155

Cole's trips round the country to inspect schools no doubt reassured him that his art education machine was ticking over adequately. Indeed, he would have been pleased to find that the system, intended to be so foolproof as to enable drawing tuition to be given even by teachers who could not practise the skill they imparted, acoming upo epectations. In Birigham  anuary he note: fairly taught by a willing master who c[oul]d not draw himself?.

In his first five years at the head of his department, Cole had established his authority, despite some organisational turbulence; had set up a nation-wide art education system and an innovative museum; and had repeated his triumph of $^ \textrm { T } 8 5 \ : \mathrm { I } \ : ,$ albeit on a smaller scale, by supervising the British part of the Paris exhibition in $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 5 5$ .But there was to be no resting on his laurels. He was aware that two big issues were looming over the department. As early as 24 January $^ \textrm { I } 8 5 6 ,$ Lord Granville, who, as president of the council, was in charge of the Education Department, warned him that owing to changes taking place in that department, it seemed likely that it and the Department of Science and Art would soon be 'united'. An order in council, on 25 February, confirmed the government's intention to pursue this policy. 156 However, it was not until the end of the year that further progress was made in this direction. The other looming issue was the impending removal of the department to South Kensington.

The move to South Kensington had emerged as a policy during 18 5 5. Once the South Kensington site had been secured by the 185 1 commissioners, Prince Albert tried to induce various institutions to set up their quarters there.' The National Gallery was his prime choice; and he also wanted almost roo learned societies to relocate on the site, as proposed in the Second Report of the commissioners, published in November 1852. Even before this, on 29 February 1852, he had expressed his wish that Cole's department would also move to the site. The department, however, escaped involvement for some time, during long-drawn-out negotiations with the National Gallery, which stoutly resisted attempts to remove it from Trafalgar Square. In 1853 and again in 1855, proposals issued from the government to build a barracks on part of the South Kensington site, and this no doubt spurred Prince Albert on to plant some buildings on the empty land, at any rate on the eastern spur that was not affected by the National Gallery proposals. Here, southwest of Holy Trinity Church, Brompton, the Department of Science and Art - its offices, schools and museum  was to be accommodated.

The prince would have liked to erect buildings according to the designs of Gottfried Semper, which he had commissioned in summer 1854. Semper had acquired a considerable reputation as an architect in Dresden when the upheavals of 1848 drove him - a wanted man after defending the revolutionary barricades - to take refuge in 1849 in France and then Britain.² Although he failed to find the work he hoped for in Britain, introductions to Edwin Chadwick and Henry Cole brought him employment in a minor capacity at the Great Exhibition, designing four of the foreign stands. This stimulated him in 1852 to publish Wissenschaft, Industrie und Kunst, a brief treatise on 'Science, Industry and Art: proposals for the development of a national taste in art at the closing of the London Industrial Exhibition'. When Prince Albert's scheme for a new educational institution became known, he offered his services to Cole, and, as has already been mentioned, was taken on to teach classes at the School of Design. In his lectures he explored ideas that were eventually to be expounded at length in Der Stil (186o-63). Cole had previously commissioned him to produce what was at first described as a 'catalogue raisonné of specimens of metal work for [the] Museum'' If the department hoped to obtain an acquisition 'wish-list' in the field of metalwork, or even a history of the metalworker's craft, it was disappointed, for Semper in fact produced a rambling, but exceedingly learned, treatise on museology. Semper hoped that the department would publish this, but his hopes were disappointed. A manuscript remains in the National Art Library (and another in the Austrian decorative arts museum in Vienna).

Semper eventually came to feel that his prospects would be better in a now more peaceful Europe, and resigned on 25 August 1855, when offered a professorship in Zurich.4 He went on to gain such an immense European reputation that some historians have assumed that he must have exercised a powerful influence upon the museum at Marlborough House. His biographer, however, sees the influence passing in the other direction, considering that Semper's spell in Britain 'transformed his life and brought him into contact with groups of people who, long after this association had ended, continued to influence his intellectual outlook' If Semper seems, then, to have made little impression, it is all the more worth noting that when Cole attended Semper's first lecture on 20 May 18 53, he noted in his diary that it was 'thoughtful & suggestive' Since Cole's diary entries are so very laconic, and so rarely deal with anything but practical matters, this entry may be interpreted as indicating some intellectual affinity between him and the German theorist. Semper might have left a solid legacy of bricks and mortar in South Kensington if his plans for a museum, commissioned by Prince Albert, had been carried out, but, when they were costed in summer 1855, they proved far too expensive. Prince Albert, as Redgrave reported to Cole, was disappointed not to be able to use Semper's plans, for he was 'urgent to do something at Kensington'.6

For want of anything better, a temporary iron building was run up, and in 1856 Cole was much involved in planning how the exhibits were to be installed. He did not like the iron building, but welcomed the prospect of expansion on the new site. Others, such as Robinson, were against the move to South Kensington.7 In Robinson's case this may well have been because he knew that the art museum would there be joined, perhaps even be obscured, by other collections. The development of the site purchased by the 185 1 commissioners was bound to be determined by their wishes in the matter  or rather, by the wishes of their chairman, Prince Albert. In their Second Report in 1852 they had outlined Prince Albert's scheme for South Kensington, and their Third Report, dated 12 April 1856, showed how implementation of it was proceeding. Progress was less advanced in devising teaching institutions than in the assembly of museum collections, which were at first conceived as accessories to the educational business of Prince Albert's 'Industrial University'. The shape of the collections was derived from the philosophical framework that the prince had conceived for the 185 1 exhibition. He divided the material to be shown there into four categories: (1) Raw Materials, which were transformed by (2) Machinery into (3) Manufactured Products, to which extra grace and value could be given by the application of (4) Fine Arts. As we know, this scheme was overlaid, for reasons of practicality, by a classification system in 3o sections, devised by Playfair. But, as Playfair remarked, 'the philosophical mind of the Prince Consort held tenaciously' to his scheme,8 and it re-emerged as the basis of the South Kensington project. Thus museum collections were sought in each of the four categories.

It became clear even in the course of the commissioners' Second Report (pp.3o-3 1) that Raw Materials and Manufactures could conveniently be grouped together, since raw materials were not very interesting unless you could see what was made from them. The commissioners further decided to divide raw materials and manufactures into three long-accepted categories: animal, vegetable and mineral. A collection of mineral materials and products already existed in the Museum of Practical Gology in Jermyn Street (which, being under the command of the Department of Science and Art, was pretty well within the grasp of the commissioners); and there was a collection of vegetable materials and products in the Museum of Economic Botany at Kew Gardens, which might, it was hoped, be incorporated in the South Kensington project. Since no collection of animal materials and products existed, the Society of Arts had encouraged its member (and secretary, 1852-3), Edward Solly, to create one in 1853. Exhibited in 1855, it had then been handed on to the commissioners with a view to its inclusion in the South Kensington project. A collection of machinery was annexed to the project when Prince Albert in 18 5 2 enjoined Bennet Woodcroft, assistant to the commissioners of patents, to form one.

The commissioners' Third Report describes other collections that had been drawn into the project: an educational collection, first formed by the Society of Arts as a special exhibition in 1854, and a 'Domestic Economy' collection shown by Thomas Twining at the Paris 1855 exhibition. This latter consisted of "those useful or necessary articles, which from their cheapness, appropriateness, and good workmanship are calculated to diffuse comfort in the dwellings of the humbler classes of the community, and tend to promote their physical well-being and intellectual development' (p.38). Art collections were to be supplied (since the National Gallery would not cooperate) by the Museum of Ornamental Art in Marlborough House. In addition, the Architectural Museum, a private body, had opened negotiations with Redgrave in autumn 1854 for space in the iron building for their collection of plaster casts of architectural detail and sculpture.9

There had been discussions about some of these collections in 1855: for instance, on I 2 July Playfair wrote to Cole to report 'rather a hot day yesterday in the Council of the Soc[iet]y of Arts' when the 'ultimate destination of the Trade Museum' was acrimoniously discussed. On Cole's return from Paris, he was entrusted with the task of putting the South Kensington museum together and got further involved with w   hcll since the Treasury was averse to putting money into South Kensington. At a meeting with hostile Treasury officials on 1 April, he was asked about 'Patents, Trades Mus; & Educat: Museum' and airily said that they 'were mere ideas at present'. He did take the opportunity, however, to argue that a move to more spacious quarters would benefit the Museum of Ornamental Art.

Assembling the new museum would be a physical task of some complexity. At the Great Exhibition the army had supplied manpower in the form of soldiers (Sappers') commanded by the Royal Engineers. The same expedient was adopted for South Kensington: the Department of Science and Art's Board requisitioned Sappers from the War Office on 1 2 July 1856.11 Cole had valued the assistance at the Paris exhibition of Captain Francis Fowke (an officer of the Royal Engineers), and had kept him on the payroll. On 22 September Fowke told Cole that he would prefer to throw in his lot with South Kensington, 'taking all risks', rather than return to the army, and on 29 November he was appointed architect and engineer to the department.12 Down on the South Kensington site, the new iron building was finished by the autumn, though on 22 September Cole noted that it was letting in the rain in 26 places. Still gently decaying on the site was a group of domestic buildings, once Brompton Park House and now subdivided into three dwellings. These were to be used for offices and for the Art School. To augment them, some wooden huts  which had been in use by the students at Somerset House - were re-erected in South Kensington. To link up the iron building and the old houses a new building (the junction') was projected; it would contain an entrance hall, library and lecture room as well as offices. This was cheaply designed in brick by James Pennethorne, and was completed early in $\mathtt { I 8 5 7 }$ A building containing refreshment rooms - an innovation in the museum world  was approved by Prince Albert on New Year's Eve, and finished in May $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 \varsigma _ { 7 }$ Yet another new building was wrung out of the reluctant government when John Sheepshanks donated his picture collection: the terms of this generous gift required in response the provision of a suitable building to house it. It was begun on 25 November 1856, and was ready when the new museum opened in 1857.

Since Cole's life was clearly about to be entwined with South Kensington, he set about looking for a house there, and on 18 December he and the family moved into 24 Onslow Square, not far from the museum. On Christmas Day, which was foggy, they had a big family party, with a magic lantern show and charades. Living here brought Cole into closer touch with Thackeray, who lived at number 36 from 1855 until 1862. 'A pleasant, bowery home', wrote his daughter Annie; 'our windows look out upon the elm trees of Onslow Square.'13 Thackeray and Cole were old friends, and so were the daughters of each family, who had played together when Thackeray lived in Young Street and the Coles at 1, The Terrace, Kensington High Street. Annie Thackeray later recalled the lanes of Kensington, "along which we used to go for straggling walks on early summer mornings with our playfellows, the Coles, who lived in the terrace close by. We used to start about six o'clock and bring home branches of hawthorn blossom to decorate our school-rooms and to remind us that it was May time.'14 At the start of 1857, Thackeray's two daughters, Annie and Minnie, were aged 19 and 16, of an age with the Cole girls, Laetitia or 'Tishy'(18), Henrietta or 'Hennie'(17), and Mary (I 5). Cole's diaries show that the families often visited each other or went walking together in Hyde Park, or to concerts. On the evening of 24 May, Cole took the Thackeray girls round the soon-to-be-opened museum in the dark. By this time the Coles were at 17 Onslow Square. Their lease of number 24 must have been a short one, for in early April Cole, lodging at 6 Pelham Villas, had had to hunt for a replacement. On January I 3 Cole acquired a new brother-in-law in the shape of Thurston Thompson, John Thompson's son, who married Marian's sister, Charlotte Bond, at Kensington Church. A large delegation of the Cole family was present, and the wedding breakfast was held at the Coles' house.

## \* \* \*\*

At the museum in Marlborough House, a last hurrah was raised with the display of the Soulages collection, from 5 December 1856 until 3 1 January 1857. The catalogue of the collection, by J. C. Robinson, contained an introduction signed by the three managers of the fund, Dudley Coutts Marjoribanks, company director, Matthew Uzielli, collector, and Cole. Presumably it was the latter who wrote it. It announced that, as with the Gherardini collection, the Soulages objects were loaned to the museum so that the public could decide whether they should be purchased. 'Whether the nation acquires the collection or not, is, in a pecuniary point of view, a matter of entire indifference to the subscribers of the guarantee; but they feel . . . that they have taken the most effectual means in their power of inviting the public to pass its own judgment, and of affording the Government sound evidence for its guidance.' Cole ended with a heavy hint. 'A government which represents the people, acts according to public opinion, and in this case it will probably rest in great measure with the public to decide whether or not the Soulages Collection will become the property of the Nation.'

The reaction from the public, so far as it can be gauged from press reports, was less than conclusive. On one side, The Times gave a hearty endorsement to the proposal to purchase the collection for the nation. If it were to be dispersed by sale at auction, it pointed out, 'nine-tenths of it would probably be withdrawn from the ken of those artists and manufacturers for whose special advantage it appears to have been brought to this country'. If placed permanently at Marlborough House, it would be accessible to the public along with the other treasures there, 'displayed with a zeal, liberality, and discretion which may be set in exceedingly favourable contrast to the system which has prevailed until very recently in the management of our other national collections'.15

The Morning Advertiser, however, was not so ready to applaud the museum's efforts. 'It is to be hoped that this country is not about to be inoculated with the fantastic fancy for bijouterie and trifling for which Continental Courts and Governments are so trivially conspicuous.' It conceded that the Soulages collection amounted to more than 'mere curiosities', but nonetheless asserted that 'it manifests a tendency to the revival of the barbarous, and it smacks of that relish for the ugly which is the resort of the arrogant few, who prefer notoriety and exclusiveness to art and beauty'.16 The Morning Chronicle also revealed some doubts: it considered, expressing its anguish through tortured syntax, that the collection would 'on the score of curiosity, be a very desirable, though not when viewed purely in an artistic light, a very valuable addition' to the museum. It felt that the objects 'sin too grossly against the canons of art to afford any great amount of pleasure to a mind of cultivated taste, or be of considerable benefit to a student of art'.17 In contrast, the Monthly Review was impressed by the many names of manufacturers on the subscription list, and accepted the argument that the collection would be useful to Bsh    c and methods, to the modern processes and arts connected with daily life'.18 Household Words shrewdly advised its readers to 'watch the visitors and listen to their comments. You will find that the raptures come from the connoisseurs', while ordinary people think that the collection is 'more curious than beautiful'.19

So Cole's stratagem failed to elicit a ringing support from the public. In the end the prime minister decided. 'What is the use of such rubbish to our manufacturers?' asked Palmerston, and declined to put up the money.20 Cole, however, refused to be discouraged. Having seized hold of the collection through exploiting private enterprise once, he resorted again to private enterprise, with an ingenuity that he took pleasure in recording in Fifty Years. An Art Treasures Exhibition (a huge festival of the fine arts, compensating for their exclusion from the Great Exhibition) was to be held at Manchester in 1857. Cole persuaded the committee in charge of this to buy the Soulages collection for inclusion in the exhibition. After the exhibition, it was agreed, the Department of Science and Art would rent from the committee parts of thecollection to be displaye in the museum, with an option to purchase them.Col hadsotte that the eparet had enje sinc1852 sannguthory objects and pay a rental', without reference to the Treasury, so, with the support of his ministers, he was able to play this wily trick despite Treasury disapproval. The Soulages problem kept raising its head through the next two years, but ultimately Col'  w u thealel annual instalments.

The display of the Soulages collection marked the end of the museum's occupation of Marlborough House, which had not, perhaps, provided ideal accommodation. 'Uncomfortable rooms', said one critic, 'in a dismal building never adapted or intende or anything the kind. Themus cosed i doorson 7February an Cole then worked fulltilt to set up the new museum in South Kensington.

His own position in his department was improved early in $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 \varsigma >$ That the department was to move from the Board of Trade to be under the committee of the Privy Council for Education had been foreshadowed at the start of $\mathrm { ~ \tt ~ I ~ } 8 5 6 .$ but nothing much happened until late in the year. On 6 December Cole, Redgrave and Playfair composed a 'private memorandum' on how they thought things should be managed in the new dispensation. Because the education committee would be at Whitehall and the Department of Science and Art at South Kensington, they argued that a degree of independence was necessary for the department. If all science and art business had to be referred to Whitehall, this 'would be very prejudicial' to the department, because (and here Cole's voice can surely be heard) 'for a long time to come the work of the Department must be one of invention, cautious experiment, and close observation of results. It is not an office of precedent or routine, and requires the undivided attention of a mind occupied with its mode of action.'22 Cole's mind, presumably.

If the government had really seriously wanted to bring its educational affairs within the control of a single, coherent, body, it might not have been keen on the department decamping to South Kensington. But the move was Prince Albert's wish, and could not be obstructed. Northcote and Trevelyan, together with Lord Granville, as lord president of the council, were called in to advise the Treasury on the forthcoming reorganisation, and they gave Cole the authority he wanted. First, the Science and Art Department was to have closer contact with ministers than the Education Department. For the latter was ruled by a committee of the Privy Council, which included not only the lord president and vice-president of the council, but also the Chancellor of the Exchequer, the Home Secretary, the president of the Local Government Board, the Secretary of State for War and the 1st Lord of the Admiralty. The Department of Science and Art was exempted from fealty to all these politicians. Its secretary was to be placed 'in direct relation with the Lord President and the Vice-President . . . without the intervention' of the committee, or of its secretary (a functionary who, Cole would have thought, had power without personal accountability, as he could always shelter behind the committee). The arrangement for the Department of Science and Art was fully in tune with Cole's belief in individual responsibility.23

Cole, it will be remembered, was at this point not the secretary of the department — Playfair had this position  but inspector general. The report, however, made a second important recommendation: that Cole should now become secretary, with Redgrave as art referee and inspector-general of art schools and Playfair as scientific referee and inspector-general of scientific schools. 'An exchange of offices' between Cole and Playfair 'would be for the public advantage'. Thus, in the game of departmental musical chairs, Cole emerged as winner, especially as the report also proposed raising his salary to £1,500.

The Department of Science and Art was henceforth ruled by a small board, which usually met weekly at South Kensington and was attended by the two ministers, the president and vice-president of the Privy Council (or often only one of them), together with the chief officers of the department. The board first met on 2 1 February 1857, and Cole recorded that the ministers - Lord Granville, with W. F. Cowper, MP, as vice-president  'seem disposed to leave details to us'. An ideal arrangement for Cole, therefore, but perhaps less propitious for the efficient administration of British education. Historians have regretted that 'successive governments made little or no effort to dovetail the work' of the Education Department and the Science and Art Department,24 so that these bodies remained 'joined, like Siamese twins, but separate in their operations'.25 Still, Cole was now unshakeably fixed in the saddle that he was to occupy for I 5 years. He had a little row with Richard Burchett on 6 March, and he must have felt some satisfaction at recording in his diary what he said to him: Told him plainly that so long as I continued at the Dept I intended to be Supreme in the Management.'

Criss-crossing the country to inspect schools now ceased to be part of Cole's job. The first half of 1857 he spent in shuttling between the Mall and South Kensington, as the installation of the new museum proceeded. On January 8 he was in the iron building with his team, Redgrave, Robinson, Fowke and Richard Thompson, to 'settle Museum points of Arrangement'. On 5 February, he, Robinson and Thompson were 'trying experiments in Colour on the Partitions'. The different sections of the museum were colour-coded for the sake of clarity, as the first guidebook points out, but wot  whi cr wo.ieny eat t up of the various sections to appropriate curators. He discussed the machinery section with Bennet Woodcroft on 9 February, and on 3 May 'called on Robinson & settled with him Arrangement of his part' of the Art Museum, where Cole had begun setting out purchases from the Paris exhibition on 1 5 April. On 7 May he looked at the Sheepshanks pictures, which had arrived in the museum the previous day and were Redgrave's special responsibility. On 12 January Richard Thompson, who had won his spurs at the Paris exhibition in 1855, was appointed to arrange the educational section (at £25 a month, with a competent assistant at £3 a week).26 He eventually became keeper of the education, food and animal products collections in 1859.27

The museum was to be provided with gas lighting to make it possible to open in the evening. Experiments were made on 3 March and 2 April, and on 29 April there was a demonstration for Prince Albert, the two ministers, and other notables. Gas lightin was istalle in the Sheeshanks gllery  1 June,the paine havig d work there only on 6 June. A lighting demonstration was provided for the press on

19 June, and Cole was quite surprised at how many reporters turned up. Whether they realised it or not, this was the first time in the world that a museum had been equipped with lighting. In setting up security arrangements, Cole consulted Panizzi of the British Museum about warding there, on 12 February. At South Kensington the galleries were to be warded at first by his detachment of Sappers, and on 21 February the board applied to the War Office for four more men and a subaltern. In return for working at the museum, the Sappers were to be taught architectural and freehand drawing, and photography, which would help them in their duties when they eventually eurne t he Royal Engines. On May  Cole made n rement with the General Omnibus Company that omnibuses would stop at the museum every half hour.

Among its visitor services, he was determined that the museum should have refreshment rooms. These had been provided to general satisfaction at the Great Exhibition, so it seemed appropriate to try them at South Kensington. Prince Albert, as chairman of the 185 1 commissioners, had 'settled to have refreshment rooms' on 29 December 18 56, and two days later Cole and Fowke presented plans to him which he promptly approved. The commissioners met on 14 February and agreed to take responsibility for the work. Cole noted that they decided to 'keep the management as I proposed in their own hands'. This was no doubt a speedier way of operating than involving the government, but it backfired on Cole, because, when the rapidly erected building was finished, the Office of Works objected that its timber construction had infringed the Metropolitan Building Act.29 The answer was for the commissioners to hand it over to the government, which was exempt from these building regulations.30 Thus, in opening the museum, Cole had to use his customary ingenuity in tackling a range of problems. No doubt he felt well rewarded when the Chancellor of the Exchequer toured the museum ("hastily') on 2 June, said that there had been great economy and that there was much to show for the money expended, and paid tribute to the 'organizing head' that had controlled the project.

The name 'South Kensington Museum' was officially adopted by the board on 21 May. Lord Granville had already, on Thursday 23 May, approved Cole's suggestion that the museum should open with a private view. Cole occupied the next Sunday morning drafting a justification for a 'Formal opening', by which he presumably meant a royal visit. Prince Albert paid an informal visit with the Prince of Wales and Prince Alfred on 8 June, and Cole was doubtless mortified to find that rain had penetrated the iron building in various places. Nonetheless, he wrote two days later to Grey, the prince's secretary, to 'urge' a royal opening. He settled the arrangements for opening the museum with Cowper on Saturday 13 June, and the next morning he was in his garden 'pondering over details of the opening'. All parties concerned in these arrangements seem to have cut it rather fine. The royal visit took place less than a week later, on the evening of Saturday 2o June, and was followed on the evening of Monday 22 by a private view, to which 2, 500 people came. Cole noted that everyone was surprised at the large number who attended: if he gave only a week's notice, perhaps this was indeed a surprising turnout. When the board met two days later, there was 'little or nothing said about [the] successful opening'. Cole doubtless noted this with some annoyance, but that evening - the first public opening on an evening - another 2,500 people came, which may have raised his spirits.

After the opening of the museum, the pace of Cole's life seems to have slowed down a little. There were some ongoing arrangements to be made (notably in relation to Bennet Woodcroft's ambition to have free admission to the Patent Museum every day), and some new initiatives, as when, on 2 1 July, the South London Railway suggested running a line to South Kensington. But the summer holiday period came round, and Cole often retreated (sometimes with paperwork to be done) to the rural calm of Shere. Here, he plunged again into the affairs of the Tillingbourne Association. He issued an 'Address to the Labouring Cottagers' in the Shere area, inviting them to submit their vegetables and fruit to the forthcoming show; also 'specimens of needlework done by grown persons, such as shirts, smockfrocks, and darning; knitting, netting, and garden work by young persons under 18 years of age; rustic work for seats, baskets, &c., for which Mr. Redgrave, R.A. has kindly consented to make some designs'.31 The first show was held on Saturday 25 July. Although basketwork designed by Redgrave seems to have been absent, there was a good display of vegetables, and 203 prizes were awarded.

Back at South Kensington after the vacation, Cole was occupied sometimes by day-to-day administration (such as inquiring into the theft of an ivory barometer from the animal products collection)32 and sometimes by advance planning for the commissioners' South Kensington site. Here the main expanse (usually referred to as the 'quadrangle') still lay undeveloped, for Prince Albert and his circle continued to hope that the National Gallery could be re-housed there. But a royal commission reported against their proposal in July, and it seemed as if they must acknowledge defeat. Cole was eager to answer back in a pamphlet. When he proposed this to Lord Granville on 6 August, Granville advised against attacking the commission. But Cole nonetheless devoted several days of his holiday at the beginning of September to writing a pamphlet, and on 17 September sent the draft to Lord Granville, who was at Osborne. The pamphlet was published, but anonymously.33

It does not attack the commission, though in the first paragraph Cole has some lumbering fun in exposing the conflicting testimonies of various witnesses. His main argument is concerned with new accommodation for the National Gallery. The commission made suggestions for expansion on the Trafalgar Square site, which, in Cole's computation, would cost a million pounds. He suggests a solution that would cost (in his computation) only £8o,ooo. His solution has three elements. First, he suggests that the Royal Academy should vacate its part of the Trafalgar Square building, which could then be used by the National Gallery. As Cole notes, everyone recognised that this would have to happen. His other two suggestions are more controversial. One is that, 'as fresh acquisitions were obtained' by the National Gallery, its 'less valuable speies might be drafte elswhere and belent or given to povincial galleries'. To induce the National Gallery and the British Museum to share their treasures more widely was a hobbyhorse of Cole's, but he never made much progress with it. However, it could be presented as a noble, altruistic aim: in the words with which Cole concludes the pamphlet, 'a system of distribution to the provinces of superfluous specimens would be established, which would exalt the national pictures from a merely metropolitan to a truly national use'.

Clthrn ws peap otealrPut ta Nal    , and needed new accommodation, he suggested that these should go to join the Sheepshanks paintings at South Kensington, where they might form a national gallery of modern painting. This arrangement would be similar to what Cole hoped would soon be settled between South Kensington and the British Museum. The latter, he believed, had agreed to 'the separation of ancient and mediaeval or Christian art, leaving the ancient art at the British Museum, and uniting its mediaeval acquisitions with those of the Science and Art Department at Kensington'. Cole argued that his 'proposal to keep the ancient paintings distinct from the modern, as indeed they are now, is quite an analogous proceeding'. Cole did not, in fact, ever achieve the desired share-out with the British Museum. But the Turner and Vernon paintings did spend some (rather uncomfortable) years at South Kensington, within Cole's empire. The pamphlet may have contributed to this outcome.

As well as trying to tidy up the 'National Gallery question' in this way, Cole became involved in discussions on how to develop the central site in South Kensington, a matter of some urgency now that the National Gallery was no longer part of the equation. With Redgrave and Fowke, he sat down to devise plans on 15 October, continuing to work on them on 22 and 24 October, and submitting them on 3 November to Prince Albert, who 'entirely approved of the principles' and asked for them to be 'further developed'. Already in July, Cole had been investigating the possibilities of a 'Music Hall' on the site: the opening salvoes of the campaign that would lead eventually to the Royal Albert Hall.34 By the end of the year, however, little progress had been made.

So far as art was concerned, Cole kept his eye in by various means. He helped Robinson set up a 'collectors' club', which first met on 18 February, and he continued to attend its meetings.35 He went with Robinson from time to time to see works of art at dealers' or auctioneers' premises. 36 And on 16 and 18 October, he was himself scrutinising prints and drawings that had been offered to the museum for purchase. By contrast with this aesthetic activity, he also derived some enjoyment from his own private army - the small group of Sappers now established on the South Kensington site. Captain Fowke, John Donnelly, Richard Thompson and R. G. Wylde formed a committee to organise games for the Sappers,37 and on 17 July, a cricket match was arranged between them and the office staff. On 15 July Cole played at quoits with them; on 22 July at trap bal; on 5 December at football. The department paid for a Christmas dinner for the Sappers,38 and Cole attended this on Christmas Day, as he was to do in ensuing years. Bestriding the broad territory between art and war, Cole continued to be a regular attendant at Society of Arts meetings throughout the year.

Perhaps the main issue on which he focussed in the latter part of 1 8 57 was his contribution to a series of lectures on the Department of Science and Art and its activities. Cole spent a lot of time in late October and early November drafting his lecture, which was delivered as the first of the series on 16 November 1857.39 The department, as he pointed out, had lately come under the new management of the Committee of Council on Education, and had moved to South Kensington, so that a reappraisal was appropriate; he obviously also felt that it was time to confront various criticisms that had been made of the department. First he squared up to those who 'have thought that the Science and Art Department is a new creation of the

Government, and have commented on the important item which its expenses make in the parliamentary estimates of the year'. He maintained, against this, that the department was 'rather a consolidation of institutions which have been long established, than the creation of any new ones', and provided a potted history of it (paragraphs I-8).

He staunchly defended the department's functions in providing education for poor people, citing in support Adam Smith and Archbishop Cranmer. He was very conscious that he must placate those who deprecated 'state interference' in education, or indeed, in any sphere. He himself believed that the state should not "weaken or supersede individual exertions, but, on the contrary, aid and stimulate them by doing only those things which must either be done by some central authority or would otherwise be left undone'. He refered to John Stuart Mill as proving 'that education is one of those things which it is admissible in principle that a Government should provide for the people'. As an example of a service provided by his department that could not be offered except 'by a Government agency', he cited the collection and distribution of reproductions (plaster casts or electrotypes) of works of art: this was to be an abiding passion of his. On the other hand, he repented of his earlier attempt to provide technical classes at the School of Design. This had not worked because manufacturers would not employ the craftsmen thus trained, and it was now time for the state to withdraw from the unsuccessful experiment. (The existing technical classes did migrate from Marlborough House to South Kensington, but within a year or two faded away.) Cole had some fun with statistics of costs and benefits in education, and concluded his defence of state interference (paragraphs 11-17) by arguing

that whilst democratic power in this country has increased, so a demand upon the Government to exercise certain new functions has increased also. As the people have felt their wants, and have had power to express them in Parliament, so the central authority has been called upon to administer to these wants . . .. This has been the case especially with . .. public education . .. Indeed, it is proved that as people become iteent ndreere they likely tman ublducaton n ill pay for it.

Ate descibing he epaent hools an cour instrction (paraphs 18-26), he turned to the museum.

He leha  a e M heterogeneous' was justified, and perhaps he conceded too much when, relishing a sharp phrase, he admitted that 'the Iron Museum is only to be regarded as a tempo-oH  eh fact that it had earned public approval. 'It was prudent at least to try the experiment, which has been fully justified by success.' He went at length into attendance figures, to the working class', he pronounced a rhapsody over his working-class visitors:

In theveni the workingmancme to this Musu om s ne r odimly lhtohe whol party when they frst observe the brilliant lghting inside the Musum show what a new, acceptable, and wholesome excitement this evening entertainment affords t al o them. Perhaps theevenigpening of ublic Muss may urnish a poweful antidote to the gin palace.

He sresed that people came because they were made welcomeand were offer easy access. In the galleries, 'arranged so clearly that they may woo the ignorant to examine them', all exhibits were labelled, so there was no need to buy catalogues. Indeed, the whole museum was like a book 'with its pages always open, and not shut' Furthermore, this accessible museum would not be content to limit its audience to London. 'Its destiny is rather to become the central storehouseor treasury of Science and Art for the use of the whole kingdom. As soon as arrangements are made, it is proposed that any object that can properly be circulated to localities, should be sent upon a demand being made by the local authorities.' The books in the library would be circulated as well as the exhibits in the museum (paragraphs 2742).

In his peroration (paragraph 43), Cole returned to the leitmotiv of his life, efficient and transparent administration. He revealed, with incredulity, that 'it has been feebly assr that e concl inormation On the cntrary, e c

there is not a single detail in the action of this Department . . . which does not invite the fullest publicity. Every purchase in the Museum and Library is publicly exposed, and may be criticised. Even the prices of the articles are published. The schools . .  are open to all, and . .  the works produced are publicly exhibited in town and country.

In short, the department 'maintains two principles of administration which are essential to all sound management': direct, individual responsibility and full publicity. 'Without these all corporate action must become corrupt and torpid, let the body consist of legislators, or local tradesmen, or mercantile adventurers.' Or, he might have added, the crew of civil servants, artists, scientists and soldiers, which now occupied South Kensington.

During the remainder of 1857 the lecture series continued: Redgrave on the Sheepshanks collection 'with a view to the formation of a national gallery of British art', Playfair on the 'scientific institutions in connexion with the Department', Burchett on the Central Training School for Art, Robinson on the art museum, and James Fergusson on 'A National Collection of Architectural Art'. These were all published, and constitute a sort of manifesto for the department. The series continued in 1858 with six lectures on the animal products collection (including one by T. H. Huxley), and a further six on other matters, including R. H. Soden Smith on the Art Library and John Bell on the modern sculpture that was exhibited in the museum for a few years. At these lectures, 'the Lecture Theatre has been always crowded, frequently inconveniently so', reported Cole. Some 3 5o seats were regularly reserved for working men.41

$$
\beta _ { i } ^ { \ast } = \beta _ { i } ^ { \ast } \qquad \beta _ { i } ^ { \ast } = \beta _ { i } ^ { \ast }
$$

By $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 5 8$ , then, the department seemed to have been rebuilt on satisfactory new foundations. Its annual report, or 'blue-book' was issued in the summer of 18 58, and was reviewed in The Times. Much of this notice concerned the South Kensington

Museum ('organized by Mr. Cole'), and it is tempting to think that Cole may have planted these paragraphs, or at least steered the journalist who wrote them. On the one hand, they criticise the iron building, which we know Cole disliked. Dismissing it as a woful [sic] failure', the notice admonishes: 'that "something must be done" is obvious'. On the other hand, the notice lavishes praise in terms such as Cole surely wished to hear.

The Museum at Kensington is a wonderful favourite of the public, considering its remote and far from central situation. 4o,ooo persons attend monthly, whereas at Marlborough-house, in the last year before the removal, the average monthly attendance was only 7,8oo . . . . It is the 'attraction' and not the 'site' that regulates the number of visitors, and the number who have visited the models of patented inventions at South Kensington has by 3o to 1 exceeded the number of visitors to the annual exhibition of the Society of Arts in the Adelphi - a central situation. The evening visitors exceed the diurnal in the ratio of s to I, and the working classes abound, appearing highly delighted with the Museum itself and the exhibition . . .. On the whole, the public manifest a worthy and laudable desire to use the facilities offered by the State for the study of science and art . . . and the removal of the department from Marlboroughhouse to South Kensington has proved signally successful.42

Cole did not rest on his laurels. The year 18 5 8 brought plenty of art school business to deal with, and a change of management. Palmerston's government fell in February, so the department said farewell to their ministers, Granville and Cowper, and welcomed in their place (in Lord Derby's minority Conservative ministry) Lord Salisbury as lord president and C. B. Adderley, MP, as vice-president. During the brief period of political manoeuvring that led to Palmerston's return to office in 1859, artistic and educational business seems to have proceeded as usual.

On 28 January, J. Shaw-Lefevre, now clerk of the Parliaments, met Cole and asked him 'why I did not give up the Arts as that had been done, & take to something new'. Cole does not note his own reply, but he must have had a sense that things in the South Kensington art world, far from being 'done', were moving forward smartly, especially the development of the site under Prince Albert's direction. On 9 February, Cole learned from Edgar Bowring that the prince liked his plans for 'dividing the ground' in the quadrangle. On 28 March, he discussed with the Horticultural Society the possibility of the society's managing the 'inner garden at Kengoanter he riasiethe or rhun  My, the laying out of the terrace on the quadrangle commenced the next day. On 16 July the prince gave his approval to the idea of involving the Horticultural Society, and appointed Cole, Redgrave and Fowke, with the architect Sidney Smirke, to take the project forward.43

In the meantime, the 185 1 commission, which in 185 2 had entered into a partnership with the government to develop the South Kensington site, decided that the partnership was hindering rather than helping them, and should be ended. Cole recorded a meeting on 13 April 1858 between Prince Albert, Edgar Bowring and himself at which this was decided, noting also that this was first occasion on which the prince asked us toit down! The partnerhp was fnally dissolved in anuary 1859, ut the decision in 18 58 no doubt gave extra impetus to Cole's site-planning efforts.

An increased sense that things were going his way might have stolen into Cole's consciousness when he heard on 2o May that Playfair was a candidate for the professorship of chemistry at Edinburgh. Playfair succeeded in obtaining this chair, as Cole heard on June 29. Fowke volunteered to replace Playfair as referee for science, but Cole and Redgrave conferred on 3o June, and decided that this post was not necessary. The administration of the scientific side of the department's business fell now to a young officer of the Royal Engineers, 24-year-old Lieutenant John Donnelly. It was later recalled that 'there was so little of a science branch of the department then that it was doubtful whether the post should be flled up after . . . Playfair went to Edinburgh'. Donnelly, however, 'was asked to carry on the work pro tem', and performed so successfully as to make it his life's mission.45

Another field in which Cole put down markers for the future was international exhibitions. At the council of the Society of Arts on 3 May 18 5 8 he took the chair and led a discussion on the possibility of holding another Great Exhibition in 1861, 10 years on from the first. This discussion led to a formal proposal to the council, which was discussed inconclusively on 1o March, Cole noting impatiently in his diary: 'No plan: Again adjourned'. He was seized with the idea of including music in this exhibition, a notion that had come to him after attending a performance of Samson (Handel's oratorio, presumably) with Marian, Hennie and Mary on 5 May. Dilke, however, who was chairman of the council of the Society of Arts in 1857 and '58, 'did not sympathize with the project for music'.46 Nor was Prince Albert's support unequivocal, when Cole discussed the idea of an 1861 exhibition with him on 20 March: 'Prince opposed, but agreed to receive proposals' from the Society of Arts. Nonetheless, Cole pushed on with further discussions on 22 and 24 March, and persuaded the Society of Arts council to commit itself to supporting a programme of 'decennial exhibitions' in principle, and an 1861 exhibition in particular, which 'should be an Exhibition of works selected for excellence, illustrating especially the progress of Industry and Art, and arranged according to classes, and not countries; and . . . should comprehend Music and also Painting, which was excluded in 185 1'. Dilke announced the decision to the society's general meeting on 14 April.47 Cole recorded that a council meeting on 5 May 'settled arrangements' for the exhibition, and he walked home with Dilke, both of them satisfied, no doubt, at having launched another project.

Cole ought, then, to have felt that the trajectory of his life was following a rising curve. But several family problems cropped up at this time. Of Cole's four brothers, Lindsay had died in 18 5 2, aged 42. His affairs had taken long to sort out, and seem to have ended in an arbitration of bankruptcy, which Cole had to settle on 13 July. Lindsay's son Wentworth, who, born in 1836, was the senior male in the younger generation of Coles, seems to have been in some difficulties, too, since Cole had already on 9 February been to the bank to 'sell out Wentworth's dividends' in order to cover a debt of £3oo which he had incurred in some speculation with his uncle Richard. Visiting his father in Kew (as he often did on Sundays) on 13 June, Cole discovered that his youngest brother, Charles, was 'in trouble about his debts', and (he learned two days later) was resigned to going through the insolvency courts. However, on 24 August, Charles was more optimistic that he could hold off his creditors. Worst of all was the case of Cole's brother William, who was chronically wayward. Cole, Charles and Wentworth had already, on $3 0$ January $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 \varsigma >$ , come to the conclusion that he should be 'kept in confinement', and on 5 June $\operatorname { I } 8 5 8$ Cole 'went to Winchester to arrange for William's removal to [a] Lunatic Asylum'. Paying for William's confinement (mostly in an establishment run by a Dr Finch at Salisbury) was thenceforward a steady drain on Cole's resources, until in 1865 William was sufficiently recovered to be let out again.

These problems must have been depressing for Cole, and it seems that he was not well himself. He reached the age of 5o on 15 July, after 1o years of unremittingly driven work, and was feeling the strain. On 27 April, his friend and doctor, Seymour Haden, had 'advised very good living & Iron' as a pick-me-up for Cole - which sounds like a (not unpalatable) prescription for stress  but when Cole consulted him again on 19 July, he suggested that Cole should take a month's holiday in Italy, and sent him to see Sir James Clark, the royal physician. Clark, three days later, told Cole that he must take a whole year's rest, and endorsed the suggestion of Italy. Cole sorted out his affairs, and just over a month later, on 28 August, set off for Italy.

He kept a diary of his trip, to which, when he had it bound up later, he gave a printed title page. This supplies a characteristically efficient summary of what transpired

A journal kept by Henry Cole, C.B., during his absence from England on sick leave, and relating to his travels in Northern Italy with Samuel and Richard Redgrave during September 1858;— his stay in Switzerland with Marian F. Cole, his wife, from the rt of October to the $6 ^ { t h }$ of December 1858; and his stay in Rome during January 1859; Naples, in February; and Florence, in February; and passage through France with his daughters Letitia Marian, and Mary Charlotte Cole, March MDCCCLIX.48

The first few pages, describing how he and the Redgraves crossed the Channel, seems to be a letter to Marian and the children, ending affectionately but with Victorian formality, Kisses to all. Henry Cole'. Thereafter Cole kept a proper journal, day by day, which he sent back to his children at home in instalments.

The three men travelled through France and over the Alps, wherever possible by rail. Railways were opening all over Europe at that moment; a hotelkeeper would tell Cole that 'his season . .  owing to railways was now [extended] a month later than it used to be' $\left( \mathrm { p . 7 5 } \right)$ . Among Cole's lively observations, encounters with food are often mentioned: 'hashed hare & fricandeau in tomatoes both dishes such as could hardly be got out of France' (f.5v), 'a new kind of soup with large pieces of bread in it & ground cheese to be sprinkled on them' (p.9). He always had a sharp eye for people. u  l   arT Lots of young stupid clownish fellows in training for the tonsure led about like recruits' (p.1o). 'Four strong minded Ladies who seemed travelling by themselves' (p. 17). 'Two Girls beating flax in the Stream - so tall  so lissome - no stooping & so e ay.. In Fehe  ead like any other ladies of London or Paris  All capitals at least produce the same kind ofupper classes' (p.39). At Milan he noted: 'Paris seemed an over-soldiered city  but Milan is much more so' (p.6o). His eye for scenery was equally keen, whether enjoying sunsets behind Alpie peaks or oting that the Arno was like pea sup o tc & muddy & frothy' (p.28).

Once in Italy, the three friends embarked on serious artistic tourism. Every evening Cole would plan the next day's programme (pp.534). Cole always responded well to buildings, giving detailed descriptions of, for example, the cathedrals at Pisa and Florence, and not necessarily taking the conventional view of the monuments he saw. Of St Mark's, Venice, he wrote: 'Ruskin has rhapsodized about its perfections  It seems much more what it really is, a vast collection of thefts from al parts compacted together as best they might be' (p.47). He noticed humble townscape as readily as ancient monuments. Painted villas in the suburbs of Genoa caused him to reflect that 'all the sorts of things which we abominate in England as shams  sham mouldings sham pilasters  sham windows are painted here as common as may be' (p.22). He seems to have preferred architecture to painting, and his references to picture galleries tend to be perfunctory. Professionally, as a museum curator, his field was not confined to art. Hence, while Redgrave 'was dwelling on the paintings' in the Pitti Palace at Florence, Cole 'went to the Museum of Nat: History. It is remarkable for a very fine collection of wax dissections', i.e. anatomical models (p.34).

The three friends scrutinised Pisa, Florence, Bologna, Mantua, Verona, Venice, Padua, and Milan at a great pace. Sometimes, they had to skimp and leave unexamined what was described in detail in the guidebooks. In Bologna, for example, they 'passed hastily through' the church of San Stefano, and Cole guiltily noted 'it is a subject for much study (see Murray)' (p.42). In Pisa, Cole's sightseeing was sabotaged for a while because he lost his spectacles. But he got another set from 'an optician who had been at the exhibition of London! So oddly things turn up' (p.29).

On 26 September they left Italy by the St Gotthard pass for Switzerland. On 28 September in Chaumont, the Redgraves set off back to London, and Marian Cole arrived to join Henry. The pace of life slackened now. Henry and Marian toured around, hardly noticing any art, taking walks and enjoying picnics: 'We made our luncheon in the train off a bottle of wine, some bread, pears & sausage flavoured with garlic  I relished it much' (p.67). At Vevey, they were disappointed in Philip Owen's 'pet Inn' (p.75), which was very crowded, and were happier in Lausanne. Here Cole joined an English library and read the English papers. 'I see the numbers keep up to 10,ooo at S. Kensington Museum' (p.8o). His memory thus jogged, he now kept on thinking about South Kensington. On 14 October he 'dreamt very distinctly about the designs for the quadrangle at S. Kensington'  that is, the main rectangle of the commissioners' site — and was convinced 'that any Gothic style was too cramped & that only a very free Italian style could be adopted' (p.8 1). After dreaming about the quadrangle again the next night, he sat down to 'draft a letter to the Prince about Kensington'. On I7 October, he only remembered that it was Marian's birthday (her 47th) because letters from home reminded him. 'She is a great blessing to us all', he dutifully noted (p.83).

On 2o October, feeling 'much better', he found himself 'designing a music hall for Kensington' (p.87). This project took hold of him: on 22 October his 'Music Hall with picture galleries' assumed 'grandiose dimensions' (p.88), and the next day he acknowledged that he must start again because his project 'would cost 7oo ooof and only about 7o.ooo± can be afforded' (p.89). Nonetheless, he worked on at the project and on 1 5 November he and Marian were 'writing out prospectuses of a Chorus Hall company' (p.1 18), which Cole arranged to have printed on the 22nd. Apart from this, time passed quietly at Lausanne, as the leaves fell from the trees, winter frosts began, and Cole sketched the snowy mountains. After a little more touring at the beginning of December, Henry and Marian reached Geneva where, on the 6th, they were joined by Tishy and Mary. They travelled on to Lyons and Arles, whence Marian left for home on the 1oth.

Cole and his daughters travelled to Marseilles, and then by boat, past Corsica, to Civita Vecchia, and on by coach to Rome. Having taken lodgings in Rome, they revelled in its luminous beauty. 'We have resolved to make drawings of Rome from our windows. The towers & roofs & gables show themselves in most picturesque lines - And the Colours & shadows are so clear & positive. From the window of our little dining room we overlook a garden of evergreens with a complete grove of orange trees studded with golden fruit' (pp.172-3). As Christmas approached, they noticed the customs of the season  the pifferari (shepherd bagpipers down from the hills), and the presepio and children's ceremonies at the church of Ara Coeli.

They undertook some serious artistic sightseeing. For Cole this had a special slant. He was on the lookout for features of Renaissance architecture that would be 'suggestive' for the gardens at South Kensington, and he commissioned photographs to take back to his designers in England.49 So eager was Cole in this quest that his companions 'were all on the alert to hear me say "highly suggestive", and often used the term themselves to tease him (p.29o). On 25 January he noticed a house being ornamented with sgraffiatura decoration, and 'mounted upstairs' to see at close quarters how this technique was executed: he was later to use it at South Kensington (p.289).

Cole was also on the lookout for what could be acquired for his museum's collections. While this was often a matter of noting objects that could be reproduced in the form of photographs or plaster casts, he also began to treat with dealers (p.244). In investigating the Campana collection, it occurred to him that, while in purchasing maiolica South Kensington would always be in rivalry with the British Museum, in the field of Italian sculpture there was no other museum in Britain with an interest, apart from South Kensington. He resolved to get J. C. Robinson to pursue this. (p.249) During their five-and-a-half week stay, Cole and his daughters tramped through countless churches, often attending service. It is clear that Cole had no more sympathy for Catholicism in religion than he had for the Gothic Revival in architecture. As a good English Protestant, he was often disparaging about Romanist ritual: so like I believe the ceremonies of the Egyptians & Pagans & very unlike the religion of the poor carpenter's son of Nazareth' (p.268). Eventually it was time to move on: The objects of interest & investigation are endless and one feels as if one had had only a glimpse at them' (p.291). Cole reflected that 'Rome is a sort of Museum belonging to the world'. Perhaps it was a sign of his recuperated energies that he could not forbear from jotting down some notes on how he would administer this museum so as to bring it up to scratch, before pulling himself up: 'but I suppose all this has been thought of and suggested again and again' (p.291).

The ep ow poe, by boat, toNapleWell enjy the enss o  u  a h  eo Borboni. On visiiPopei, his erreeurial zel aga arte isel,  he fntasize abut setting up a huge iteatnally-nd rchaological pre:

te whol iht  avate  yrs n Publi Muss  ll ct - p Hey hcsl (.1 made some purchases from dealers (p.323).

He and the girls then returned by sea to Civita Vecchia and on to Leghorn; and by train through Pisa and Lucca to Florence, relishing 'the ride by the side of the Appenines with their little Towns in the valleys & Monasteries and Castles on the heights & the snow in the distance' (p.34 1 ). Unwonted comfort awaited them in their Florence pensione: 'There is an air of entire Cleanliness & good management throughout  It made us all feel as though we had again reached Civilization' (p.342). By now Cole was so recovered that his mind was almost entirely on museum business. When he and his daughters visited San Miniato on 12 February, his museum director's acquisitiveness reasserted itself. This church, he noted, 'is now being cared for but a few years ago would have yielded a mine of wealth for a Museum' (p.345). Attending church on the next day, they met, with his wife, the collector Charles Drury Fortnum, whom Cole had encountered previously in connection with the Soulages collection. Two days later Fortnum introduced Cole to William Blundell Spence, an English painter resident in Florence, who chiefly operated as a dealer. 'A very desirable agent to have here & may be extremely useful', noted Cole, presciently (p.3 5 5). Every day for the next 1o days, Cole met either Fortnum or Spence, toured the antique dealers, and made numerous purchases for the museum.50 He was now thoroughly re-attuned to his museum work. He left Florence on 24 February, and travelled back through France to Britain, and to South Kensington.

# CHAPTER II Exercising Power with a Hidden Hand 1859-1862

Returning from his foreign break on Saturday 5 March, Cole went round the museum with Redgrave. 'All expressed their pleasure at my return.' A week later, at his first meeting with his ministers, he reported 'the highly effective state in which, after six months absence' he found the museum.' One of the first things he did, in the warm glow attending his return, was to invite the Robinsons to take tea on Monday 7 March, and to present to Mrs Robinson an enamelled chain which he had brought back as a present for her. Robinson, however, was chafing at his lack of advancement, and on Thursday 17 March Cole found himself having a 'long discussion with Robinson about his future prospects', which ended when 'I left the room', presumably in annoyance or impatience. The next day, Robinson 'asked if he might be called "Director" instead of Keeper' of the museum. These were portents of disagreements between Cole and Robinson that would grow over the next few years. But for the time being amicability was restored, and on 25 November Cole's daughter Tishy stood as godmother to the Robinsons' son Francis James.

During 1859 and 186o, Cole was concerned with three longish-term projects, all to some extent architectural. One was the continued enlargement of the museum. Cole had started to build out northwards from the iron building with the gallery for the Sheepshanks pictures, and, while he was away in Italy, Redgrave and Fowke had pressed ahead with galleries for the Turner and Vernon pictures, followed by a range running west-east along the northern edge of the site. It was now planned to build a range along the eastern edge of the site, thus creating a three-sided open courtyard. At some stage it occurred to Fowke, or Cole, or both, that this could be cheaply covered with a glass roof to form further galleries. Cole and Fowke were discussing this idea on 26 April 1859, and it was approved by the board on 7 May. It eventually resulted in the North and South Courts. These were to bring the museum much more space  space on a grander scale than the cramped earlier galleries had provided, and space that invited an elaborate scheme of interior decoration, which John Physick has described in detail.2

The second project that engrossed Cole was the development of the main space of the 1851 commissioners' site, generally known as the 'quadrangle'.3 As we have already seen, the Horticultural Society ('Royal' from December 186o) agreed in 1858 to create a formal garden here, and Cole, Fowke and Redgrave, with the architect Sydney Smirke, had been commissioned by the prince to advance the project. While Cole was away, the society and the commissioners had been negotiating a lease, but little else happened, and Cole now had to supply renewed impetus. In the first week of April 1859 there were repeated meetings of the team, along with Dilke and the prince, and by July 7 the grounds were 'nearly staked out'. The prince wantethe garden t be eclose by serircades,angradually hee werebuilt designs by Smirke (in the northern part of the site) and by Fowke (in the southern part), which took inspiration from the photographs of Italian architecture that Cole had gathered in Italy.4 At the end of 1859, W. A. Nesfield was commissioned to design the garden itself. The project went ahead more or less experimentally, with many meetings to mull over progress. Cole obviously found it frustrating that he was not really in charge. The prince's close interest in the smallest details slowed things up, and provoked Cole to private laments in his diary, such as 'all in confusion & no decisions'. On the other hand, when the prince did make up his mind, he could be exigent: 'wanted everything done at once as usual', grumbled Cole.

Cole's third project was the forthcoming International Exhibition, scheduled for 1861. Cole had energised the Society of Arts into proposing this in 1858, and the society had tried to arouse the interest of the 185 1 commissioners. Little progress had been made, however, in Cole's absence. He managed to secure a meeting with the prince less than a week after his return, on  March. The prince was cautiously supportive, requiring the society to gather expressions of interest from exhibitors, and to set up a guarantee fund, before the commission became further involved. In the face of Cole's enthusiasm, he conceded that he 'was disposed to take Moderate Evidence of the Chances of Success'; but he needed more reassurance. Cole started to bustle around raising funds, but in the spring war broke out between France and Austria, and, although it did not last long, it created sufficient alarm to convince everyone that the exhibition must be postponed until 1862. So this project went off the boil again.

When Cole resumed his seat at his desk in the Department of Science and Art, one problem that awaited him was what to do about science. At a board on 3 1 March, his senior Minister, Lord Salisbury, 'proved very desirous of doing something, & said he wd get rid of the name if nothing were done'. When discussion was renewed on 4 April, Cole recorded that there was no agreement. However, the young John Donnelly produced a scheme for expanding the provision of scientific education, which was adopted on 2 June, and which marked, in Donnelly's view 'a new era . . . in the working of this branch'.6 Lord Salisbury had no chance to see the results of the new policy, for Derby's short-lived Conservative government fell after a parliamentary defeat on 3 1 March. Palmerston formed a second administration on 18 June — but only after the premiership had been offered to Lord Granville who, though a favourite with the Queen, did not yet have the political weight to unite the various interests in the Liberal party. Palmerston gave back to Granville his previous post of president of the council, so once again he became Cole's minister.

His vice-president was Robert Lowe. Cole had briefly served under him when the Department of Science and Art was still part of the Board of Trade in 18 5 5, and when Lowe was beginning his ministerial career as vice-president of the board. When Palmerston returned to power in 1859, Lowe hoped for promotion to the cabinet, but had to be content with the vice-presidency of the Board of Education at the Privy Council. Though this was 'a subordinate office in a hard working and non-glamorous branch of the administration', it suited him perfectly, in the opinion of his biographer, since Lord Granville was busy as leader of the Liberals in the Lords and left the running of the department to Lowe; and Lowe 'discovered that the permanent officials he would be associated with in the Privy Council were the same kind of "true votaries" he had worked with so harmoniously at the Board of Trade'.7 Cole was a man of the same mould as Lowe. After his first board meeting with Lowe on 23 June he judged him to be 'very intelligent indeed'. Redgrave, too, was impressed: 'Mr. Lowe differs greatly from our late sub-chief, Mr. Adderley; Mr. Lowe is very shrewd, sees the real point at once, and decides on principle and not on detail.

As Granville noted, however, 'Bob Lowe . . . is disliked by many in the House'.9 Indeed, he was very widely disliked, on account of his uncompromising opinions, his combative assertion of them, and his refusal to suffer fools gladly. In this, he was not unlike Cole, though even more abrasive. Cole clearly admired him.
