He thought him 'very genial and witty', and noted down his droll phrases, such as a 'brogue strong enough to hang a hat on' or 'nonsense enough to fill a cart'. He relished Lowe's snubs, as when Lowe returned a paper on scientific education by Donnelly with the remark that he did so 'as the reprieved Convict did the Prayer book to the Chaplain with thanks having no further use for it'. No doubt, when Lowe told Granville that 'Cole is in an economical mood' and had cut his budget by £ 5oo, he smirked colludingly as Lowe continued: 'but I put it on again & the Treasury passed it. I gave Cole a blowing up and told [him] I wd not reduce the Estimates because the Treasury reduced Taxation'. 10 He must surely have relished that harsh quality in Lowe, which probably both Lowe and Cole thought of as clear-sighted intellectual honesty, but which was always apt to veer towards self-righteousness. As, for instance, when Lowe said: 'The Problem of Education was simply how to give the children of the Labouring Poor under I1 years of age such Education [as] shall enable them to walk in that path of life into wh: it shall please God to call them. Let us clear our Minds of all Cant, as Dr Johnson said.'11 Lowe and Cole probably excited the more high-handed tendencies in each other - which was stimulating for them while they had the same aims in view, but eventually proved explosive when they disagreed.

Cole's private life in 18 59 seems to have included more time for relaxation than he had allowed himself before his holiday. Though he occasionally worked in the evenings, he often dined out or entertained others for tea. He played whist, dominoes, trap and bowls with members of his family and others. On 24 May he noted that he had played cricket for the first time with 16-year-old Harry. He sang glees at home, and in the autumn often spent Wednesday evenings with a 'Chorus Meeting' that his daughters set up on 9 November. He had time for reading, and kept up with contemporary fiction: Trollope's Barchester Towers, Thackeray's The Virginians and George Eliot's Adam Bede.12 His military interests found a new outlet. In the spring there was a panic about the possibility of a French invasion, which led the government to approve the setting up of a Volunteer Force of civilians. South Kensington consequently created the First Middlesex Volunteer Engineer Corps.13 It was set up on 28 November: 'All unanimous & enthusiastic & about 70 joined at oCole was making argements r n offcial hat or the corps November, and drill began on 5 December. The commanding officer was MacLeod, and Cole was merely a footsoldier. But when Fowke told Prince Albert that Cole was a 'rear rank man in the Engineers', the prince replied: 'Then he will keep the front mmarcin1 Fowke us hi lsturivalle aciliyi isigtu ofmaterials in his buildings'in constructing for  a 'drill-shed, a structure with semi-circular ribs and a felt roof (it must have looked like a sort of prototype Nissenhut). Paxton told Cole 'that it was the neatest thing in cheap construction he had ever seen'.15

Cole paid a visit to Oxford in June, in which he not only visited the new University Museum and the University Galleries, but also sampled the fare (edible and intellectual, presumably) in University College, in the Senior Common Room and at High Table. If he came away with any thoughts of trying to obtain for his children the university education he had never had himself, there was not much he could do about it, because most of his children were daughters. His son Harry was of universityage, but he had chosen a military career, and was destined for the Royal Military Academy at Woolwich.16 Meanwhile, on 3 October he, Hennie and Mary signed on at the South Kensington Art School, where Tishy had preceded them, winning a prize for her tarsia (i.e. mosaic) work.17 Cole's son Alan, aged 13 in 1859, had attended Charterhouse as a day boy. Cole recorded in 1857 that 'altho' he has been at the head of the Class for a long time, he did not gain the prize, his competitor knowing a little more of Greek verbs'. Soon after, he noted: 'Alan averse to going to school'. Alan was therefore sent to a more progressive school in Chester, run by the Rev. Arthur Rigg and favoured by Cole's department.18 Here there were science classes, but after a while these were dropped, and in 1859 Alan started the new school year at Mr Browne's school in Kensington.19 The younger girls, Isabella and Rose (aged 9 and 7 in 1859), and little Granville (aged 5) would still have been under the supervision of a governess. Cole's extended family was extended further this year, when Marian's sister Fanny married. Fanny had been in Germany as companion to Lady Combermere since 1841, and found there a fiancé, Dr J.W. Appell, who came to England to marry her. He dined with the Coles on 3o August: 'This was our introduction to Dr App. who is Editor of a Literary & Art Paper at Vienna,' noted Cole. The marriage service took place on Saturday 3 September: 'Fine day & all passed off Cheerfully'.

## \*   \* \*

The year 186o was even more relaxed than 18 59. The family spent July and August at Shere. From 25 August until 6 September, Cole made a tour in Normandy with Harry and Alan: they kept diaries and made sketches, and, binding these up afterwards, gave the resulting volume to Marian on her 49th birthday on 17 October 186o.20 The trip aroused memories for Cole of a previous trip 'when I was about 18', and he found much changed. There were some things to complain about. The 'stink of stables and drains' caused him to get up in the night when staying in Dieppe, and apply 'a remedy with the Cologne bottle'. At Le Havre he disapproved of the 'absurd and indelicate' shorts that French men wore for bathing; Alan commented, 'It disgusted Papa - who you know prides himself on his plunging'. The party conscientiously visited historic monuments, recording such judgements as 'a fine specimen of massive Romanesque work' (of the Abbaye aux Hommes at Caen). They looked at the Bayeux Tapestry, and Harry noted that 'the whole is a good type of the uneducated state of the minds of females about I,ooo years ago'; no doubt he was here endorsing, without quite getting the tone right, his father's progressive perspective on the woman question.

Not long after the Normandy excursion, Marian and Hennie accompanied Cole on a trip to Scotland, staying for a while with Cole's friend Robert Napier, the marine engineer, at West Shandon. Here, on 18 and 2 1 September, Cole made some etchings. He had learned this craft earlier in the year at Shere from Seymour Haden, an accomplished practitioner. On 28 July he tried to etch a view of the cottage, but made a mess of the perspective. On 8 August Haden revised the etching and printed proofs (plate 39). Cole and Haden had another common interest. On Sunday 29 July they rambled round the Surrey countryside looking for sites on which they could build houses, a project that was to absorb them in 186o. For Cole there was again much singing in the family and drilling with the Volunteers. Cole seems to have had enough mental leisure to reflect on his achievements. Stimulated, perhaps, by what he called a 'Sortation of Papers &c.' on I5 January, he soon 'Commenced Catalogue of my writings', which occupied him during several evenings in March.

On the official side of life, the Horticultural Society's garden and the 1862 exhibition continued to loom, but neither project had urgency at this stage. If, then, action was in abeyance, the year 186o gave Cole several opportunities for utterances of policy. He testified before three parliamentary select committees: that on public institutions in March, and those on the British Museum and on the South Kensington Museum in July.

The purpose of the committee on public institutions was (to quote its full title) to "inquire whether it is in the Power of Parliament to provide, or of this House to recommend, further Facilities for promoting the Healthful Recreation and Improvement of the People, by placing Institutions supported by General Taxation within the reach of the largest Section of the Tax-payers, at Hours on Week Days when, by the ordinary customs of Trade, such persons are free from toil'. In other words, how to make museums more accessible to the working classes. Cole was the first witness to take the stand, on 1 March 186o, and had an easy ride, for it was acknowledged that the South Kensington Museum was in the lead in the accessibility stakes. All he had to do was to assert, with characteristic ebullience, that I think that at South Kensington we have got nearly to the extreme of public convenience', and describe the crowds of working people who visited the museum when it was open (as other museums were not) in the evenings.

He was able to reassure the committee that providing alcoholic refreshment for visitors was quite safe. By adding up alcohol consumed in the pioneering refreshment rooms at South Kensington and dividing it by the number of visitors, he was able to say that each person had upon the average, 2% drops of wine, '%ths drops of brandy, 1drops of bottled ale, and so on'. He also revealed how hard his staff had to work i y cme back again at 7o'lock and stay il past 1. I think the Coittee wil e opinion that they are pretty well worked.'21

Te q g e hat  o . one commentator said:

InLondonhereereasur rt numberenorsmusums ul - et and instruction; yet strange to say, they are all bolted and barred at the very tme when the manufactories, shops, and warehouses let loose their crowds of labourers, weary with monotonous toil, and athirst for some new scene where they may throw aside the burden of the day, and receive new strength and invigoration, by gazing on sights of grace and beauty.22

U oper

The committee on the British Museum was moved by William Gregory, MP, who later recalled that 'the main point of the enquiry was whether the natural history collections were to be removed elsewhere'. This was under consideration because the museum was so pressed for space. Gregory's committee was 'well received by the House, and with acclaim by the heads of departments in the Museum, with the exception of Mr. Panizzi, who could not bear any interference with this great establishment over which he ruled with absolute authority'. It eventually reported that the museum should expand on its present site, 'much to the annoyance of the Government, who were anxious to have Prince Albert's Kensington scheme carried out'  and thus supported the proposal to transfer the natural history collections to South Kensington — and to that of Panizzi, who was bent on getting rid of the scientific men, with whom he had no sympathy  of whom, indeed, he had expressed himself with sovereign contempt'.23 The committee's recommendations, vigorous in first draft, were watered down so as to become, in the words of Henry Layard, 'a positive insult to the common sense of the House of Commons and the public',24 and did not make much difference in the long run.

For Cole, testifying to the committee presented an opportunity for empire-building. He wanted to take over the British Museum's medieval holdings (assembled quite recently by Augustus Wollaston Franks), and had an agreement that he could do so with Panizzi, the director, who had no more sympathy with medieval antiquities than with natural history.25 When asked whether he wanted to establish a general collection of medieval art' at South Kensington, Cole bumptiously answered: 'If Parliament thought that Kensington would be a good situation for that purpose, having already a very large collection, it would not be difficult to add the little collection in the British Museum'.26 But although this was much talked about, it never happened.

For Cole the most important of the three committees was that on his own museum, which the department had agreed to as a means of countering parliamentary criticism. For Parliament was not sympathetic to artistic institutions. A philippic in the Art-Journal, urging the government to act with 'bold and generous hand' in order to 'raise the Arts to that high position which the tastes of the people and the exigencies of the nation now imperatively demand', attacked the obstructive attitude of Members of the Commons.

Of late . . . we regret to say, it has been made specially difficult for any paid official, however skilled, zealous and honest, to serve his country in the public promotion of the Arts. Whoever may have received parliamentary money is at once subjected to unmeasured and unscrupulous attack. Idle gossip and stale jokes are carefully compiled and elaborated for the amusement of the House.

No doubt Cole recognised himself in this portrayal of the persecuted arts administrator who 'is made the butt for party shots, and oft-times arraigned before the country as a culprit'. The writer thundered on:

There is scarcely an Art institution in the country which has not suffered repeated and inveterate attack from the pretended patriotism of so-called independent members . . . The modest estimates of the Government are thus often carried with difficulty, and its circumscribed plans and scanty generosity curtailed, and sometimes wholly defeated.

It was in such an atmosphere that Robert Lowe (who had aggressively remarked to Cole that 'if he was to have the trouble of a Committee he wd not "Spare the Philistines"') moved for the select committee on 19 June, to the accompaniment of the usual parliamentary noises. One MP grumbled that 'there was a tendency to unlimited expense at Brompton', while another urged that 'the question for the Committee to determine' should be 'whether the South Kensington Museum ought not to be done away with altogether'.27 The committee met for eight sessions in July.

The museum was under attack, so Cole retaliated. Redgrave recalled him putting up a characteristic performance under cross-examination. ´'He certainly loves strife, he courts a "spar," and always makes a good stand-up fight. Some of his hits have been most clever.'28 Cole was on the stand for two and a half days. In fact, much of what he said was simple description of the museum. After dealing with its administration, he reviewed each of its sections: the architectural museum, the educational collection, the animal products collection. In respect of the latter, Cole enlivened his testimony by showing specimens and discoursing learnedly on wool production and isinglass. He went on to speak of the food museum ('I should say that after the pictures, which is the most popular collection, the food collection is the next most popular') and the models and patents collection. It is important to remember that, only three years after its opening, the museum was (notwithstanding the rapid growth of its art galleries) still visibly a general educational museum covering a wide range of artistic and scientific subjects. Consequently, it had to be defended as such. But when the question was raised whether it had achieved enough success to give value for money (the committee carefully added up exactly what it had cost the nation), the ground shifted somewhat. The question then focussed on the ornamental art collections. Had they improved the quality of British manufactures?

A rather small group of witnesses from the manufacturing sector were rounded up to testify: three from Sheffield, one from Birmingham, and Colin Minton Campbell from Stoke-on-Trent. So far as they could, they spoke in South Kensington's favour. It was easier for them to praise the effect of their local art schools (which were supported, of course, by South Kensington) than to vindicate the usefulness of the distant South Kensington Museum. They tended to take the line that the museum directly affected public taste, and this subsequently affected manufacturers. As Colin Minton Campbell said, 'the public taste is improved, owing to the opportunities afforded of frequenting the South Kensington Museum, and also to the facilities for going abroad and visiting various collections, which obliges us to do different works which otherwise we would not have been induced to do'. Redgrave also took this anoay about the isplay  cina in themuseum:

I think the first result of this kind of exhibition is to make the public hunger after the ojes; I think then they go to the china shops and say, 'We do not like this or that; we have seen something prettier at the South Kensington Museum;' and the shop-keeper, wo hte,  at  ece  te, instigated by that demand, produces the article.29

This claim was unprovable, but Cole wanted to believe that it was true.

The committee pondered two grievances often voiced against the museum: that it was in the habit of bidding for purchases in competition with the British Museum, thus wasting public money, and that in maintaining its own photographic department it was unfairly undercutting commercial photographers. Both complaints were dismissed. The committee also considered the accommodation of the museum, and recommended expansion. Cole made sure of this. Although the report was ostensibly written by Lowe, as chairman of the committee, Cole recorded that it was he who drafted it - at Shere on 1 5 and 16 July, showing it to Lowe on the 18th. Moreover, his papers include a first printed draft, with further handwritten revisions by him, in which he considerably boosted the final section on buildings, introducing the phrase extreme urgency' and specifying the glazed court project as deserving of support.30 He was with Lowe again on 25 July when they 'settled revises', after which Cole 'sent Copy to ye Prince'.

The outcome of the inquisition was that the committee's report declared that the museum 'is exercising a beneficial influence, and . . . is fully deserving of continued Parliamentary support' (paragraph 17). The Art-Journal exulted that 'the repeated attacks made in Parliament upon the South Kensington Museum . . . have, we are glad to say, fairly broken down, and the charges now stand disproved'.31 A moment of triumph for Cole.

## \*     \*

Cole's life speeded up in 1861. In the domestic sphere, he and Haden were constantly involved in the building of their new country houses on adjoining sites that they had found at Witley, in Surrey. On 29 January, Cole noted that 'Harry began the plans of Witley House'. It was entirely characteristic of Cole not to commission designs from a professional architect, but to try to do it himself, or at least to keep it in the family. His son Harry would in the future train in the Royal Engineers and be involved in the design and construction of several buildings, including the National Training School for Music in South Kensington. But at this stage in his life Harry, however talented, could hardly have been capable of designing a house professionally, and much of the design work must have been done in the office of the contractor, John Kelk. Already Kelk had worked at South Kensington on the conversion of Brompton Park House, and on constructing the Sheepshanks and other galleries, and he was to be the contractor for the 1862 exhibition building. He and Cole were therefore on friendly terms. Cole was constantly visiting the site through the year as the house went up, and there was a topping-out ceremony on 19 October, with a feast in the local schoolroom. Haden was, already on I 2 June, regretting that the cost of the houses was 'rather beyond him', and he seems not to have gone ahead with his, Kelk building it as a speculation. Cole's house still exists: partly tile-hung in deference to the local vernacular style of architecture, it is basically of red brick with a picturesque irregular, gabled outline and tall chimneys.32

The main Cole family event in 186 1 was the engagement of Hennie. Her young man, J. F. Iselin, came on I6 February to tell Cole that 'he had formed an attachment to one of my daughters', and on the 2 I April he proposed to Hennie and was accepted. That evening he came to ask permission from Cole and Marian to marry their daughter. Young Alan, observing this incident, thought the business of asking the parents' permission was 'so awful' that he resolved to marry an orphan. Hennie's chance of marital happiness was not to be fulfilled. On 28 April 1 862, Cole noted in his diary that he told Iselin that 'unless he cd govern & controul his temper & manners, I cd not allow him to marry Hennie (see my letter)'. Poor Hennie never married, while Iselin, who had by then been appointed to the staff of the department, continued to work there.

The year 1 86 1 was a significant one in the history of English education, since it was then that 'payment by results' was established in elementary education.33 The chief movers of this change were Robert Lowe, and R. R. W. Lingen, secretary of the Education Department since 1849 (when, on the retirement of James Kay-Shuttleworth, he vaulted into the position over the assistant secretary, Harry Chester, an old crony of Cole's at the Society of Arts). Cole, however, played an important role in the background. Payment by results (i.e. making grants to schools proportionate to the success their pupils achieved in tests in reading, writing and arithmetic) has usually been deplored by educational historians and theorists. Matthew Arnold called it 'the heaviest blow dealt at civilization and social improvement in my time'.34 It is said to have reduced learning to a soul-destroying and mechanical process, to have degraded teachers from professionals into piece-workers, and to have turned the inspectorate into inquisitors. Nowadays, payment by results is enjoying a revival in esteem, and these judgements may seem harsh. Certainly, there is little reason to suppose that elementary education before the introduction of payment by results was enjoying a golden age.35

Therewer threefndmental ais behind the intduction o the new ystemr, inde, four, i neassumes a genuine desire on the part  i riginators toie education. Aside from this, an important aim was to reduce public expenditure. This may not seem a very noble ambition, but it must be remembered that, in the financial year 186o-6 1, the education budget amounted to almost a fifth of all public spending.3 A royal commission on 'the State of Popular Education in England and Wales' (the Newcastle Commission), set up in 1858 and reporting in 1861, had in part been s s after the Crimean War. Lowe would have been aware that many people wanted education spending to be more strictly controlled, and behind him loomed the Chancellor of the Exchequer, Gladstone, who regarded all unnecessary public expenditure as 'a great political, and above all, a great moral evil', and who discerned 'threatening circumstances arising from the heavy expenditure for education that had been going on for some years'.37

A second reason for introducing payment by results was to raise standards. The government endeavoured to ensure that the schools it funded were performing adequately by its system of inspection, but the Newcastle Commission had found it diful uha dvidualper sevi et anars.T judge performance by examination results seemed a more efficient, quantifiable method.38

The third reason for adopting a new system was not openly admitted, but is generally accepted as a potent factor in the thinking of all concerned. Most of the elementary schools in England, while supported by government grants, were actually run by 'voluntary bodies' associated with the churches - by the National Society representing the Anglican Church, and by the British and Foreign Society representing the dissenting sects. The two societies were at loggerheads with each other, but would not countenance education falling into the hands of anyone else, since they believed religious indoctrination to be the most important part of their work. State control of education, which naturally commended itself to politicians and civil servants, was regarded by the voluntary societies as opening the floodgates to a raging torrent of secularism. Earlier attempts to increase state control had been unsuccessful, though one advance in the war of attrition with the religious bodies was the introduction of scientific secondary education through Cole's department. The support of the Prince Consort for this greatly helped. Granville lamented that 'the present state of religious feeling' inhibited educational change, and praised Prince Albert's advocacy of scientific education as 'a godsend to the Government'.39 Cole knew very well that his department constituted the advance troops in the battle for secular education (though personally he was not irreligious, attending church regularly on Sundays, and conducting household prayers).40 It is interesting to overhear him and T. H. Huxley chatting in summer 186 1 in the midst of the preparations for payment by results: Huxley (who taught science in Cole's department) remarked that their efforts to improve scientific education 'were the most important ever taken against Parsonic influence'.41 Lowe, and behind him Granville, had to be careful in their public statements in the payment-by-results controversy to defer to the churches,42 for it would have been 'political suicide' to do otherwise,43 but there is no doubt that they too wished to reduce 'Parsonic influence'.

Religious opposition was still too strong to allow the government to take the risk of legislation. The cabinet were relying on Lowe to do something energetic, but agreed with him that an educational reform bill was impractical. It was decided that changes must be made by altering the internal rules of the Education Department, which could be done without reference to Parliament. Thus what emerged was not a bill but a 'Revised Code'. To many this stratagem seemed evasive, especially as the code was announced in a departmental minute on the day that Parliament was prorogued for the summer recess, after which the responsible ministers quickly went on holiday.44

The new system thus took the world by surprise, and was thought to be a wild move. A colleague of Granville's commented that 'people in general seem to think that you and Lowe rushed out, like Irishmen at a fair, to have a blow at something or somebody'.45 Historians, on the other hand, have found precedents: the idea had been aired before the Newcastle Commission,46 and might even be traced back to the first secretary of the Education Department, Kay-Shuttleworth.47 What Lowe needed, however, was not theoretical precedents but practical advice on how to proceed, and this Cole was able to provide, for his department had been operating a system of payment by results for a decade in its art schools. The system was mitigated by annual bonuses, which teachers could obtain as a result of possessing the various certificates of competence issued by the department, but their main earnings were controlled by results. When Donnelly set up the department's science schools (which expanded from 5 in 1859 t0 38 schools or classes with 1,330 pupils in 1861, reaching 799 schools with 34,283 pupils in 1870), he adopted a similar system for paying science teachers.48 So Lowe consulted Cole on Thursday 9 May: "asked me to prepare a Scheme for paying for results in writing, reading & Arithmetic'. The next day Cole talked it through with Donnelly, and on Sunday he wrote a brief on the subject for Granville, countering criticism from Kay-Shuttleworth.49 On Friday 17 May, Lowe discussed Cole's ideas with him 'and seemed disposed to try the experiment'. Cole unveiled his plan at a board meeting with Granville and Lowe on 3o May: Lowe 'thought my plan of results possible & even Lingen thought so, but p[re]ferred Inspection'. On I4 June Lowe passed to Cole the proposals that Lingen had prepared, using Cole's suggestions.

Cole thus influenced the plan for payment by results at the crucial moment, and then retired to a safe distance. A storm of protest broke over Granville and Lowe, and the government, alarmed, made various concessions that undermined Lowe's position In due course, his nemis were to gt their revenge, when he was acu of "editing" the reports of the inspectors of education in an unjustifiable manner, in order to make them appear more in harmony with his own views than they really were in regard to the effects of the Code and kindred matters'1 Although there was no substance in the accusations, enough mud stuck to embarrass him severely, and on 18 April 1864 he resigned from the vice-presidency. By this time, chickens were coming home to roost for Cole also.

In 186 1, however, Cole, turning aside from payment by results, was engrossed in the Royal Horticultural Society's garden and in preparing for the 1862 exhibition. During 186o, the building works around the garden2 had proceeded, albeit slowly: the arcades to the east and west, the conservatory to the north, and the buildings at the southern end which included a splendid committee room for the society abutting on the new buildings for the 1862 exhibition. Cole tried always to boost Fowke's work, and late in 186o was proposing to the Commissioners Fowke's design for a splendid entrance into the gardens from Exhibition Road. In his characteristically entrepreneurial way, Cole claimed that the costs of constructing this entrance could be covered by letting part of it as shops, and he opened negotiations with the contractor Kelk along these lines.53 The commissioners, however, were apprehensive: 'much alarmed at the fear of our friend Cole dipping his fingers into our nearly empty pockets to the tune of £10,ooo for entrances &c',54 and quashed the idea.

In 1861 the pace accelerated: there were now 1,o men at work on the site, where more architectural than vegetable, partly to make it attractive in winter as well as . I terns made of coloured gravel. Some found it rather fussy.

Littleramps were raised, and little slopes sliced off, with afiddling nicety of touch whic would have delight the peal ardener the sumer palace; and the ty declivit thus manufactured were tortured into curvilinear patterns, where a-sand, chopped coal, and pounded bricks, atoned for the absence of flower or shrub.

In this garden-architecture one periodical correctly espied 'the art-feeling that rules in the Museum': the garden was the forcing-ground for the South Kensington Renaissance style of architecture, which was to be fostered in Fowke's office (now enlarged by a talented recruit from the Sheffield School of Art, Godfrey Sykes) and applied in new buildings for the museum.

The garden was opened (though it was not quite complete) by the Prince Consort on 5 June 1861. It was to be his last public appearance in connection with the South Kensington project before his death in December. With hindsight we may find it poignant that, in his opening speech, he foresaw that the garden would become 'the inner court of a vast quadrangle of public buildings, . . where science and art may find space for development'. South Kensington did not turn out quite as he hoped. The gardens have disappeared, and while the site today is indeed occupied by institutions dedicated to science and art, these do not constitute an elegant quadrangle as he wished, but a formless muddle.

The matter that chiefly pre-occupied Cole in 1861 was the forthcoming International Exhibition. By the end of 1859 it had been rescheduled for 1862, and in 186o a guarantee fund was set up with five trustees. Cole urged the 185 1 commissioners to support his plan for decennial exhibitions (which he had pushed through the Society of Arts) by dedicating a permanent exhibition site at South Kensington. While willing to support the 1862 exhibition, the commissioners did not want to make a longer-term commitment, and there seemed every prospect of a row between the commissioners and the Society of Arts. Cole was so angry that, in a letter of 2 1 May 186o to the prince's secretary, General Grey, he denounced the 185 1 commissioners as 'traitors'.55 In the end, the trustees were turned into a new royal commission for the exhibition of 1862.

Prince Albert did not wish to be officially involved in the exhibition, and Cole followed his example, no doubt influenced by his awareness that the 'Public thought there was a job at [the ] bottom' of the South Kensington developments. It was maliciously believed that 'HRH had a large land speculation there and that I was his Scheming tool to get buildings &c to improve his property'.56 Lord Granville, who had been vice-chairman of the commissioners in 1851, gamely took on the chairmanship in 1862. A friend wrote to him: 'I think your Exhibition for 1862 a mistake. I do not believe that advances in arts and manufactures have been sufficiently marked to be appreciated by the common herd, and to be successful you must make impression upon them'. Granville conceded that '9,999 persons out of every 10,ooo are of your opinion', but held to his own view that the exhibition would be 'an absolute success'. He added revealingly: 'Nobody wished for it excepting Cole, and it is a great proof of the power of a strong will.

The other 1862 commissioners serving under Granville included two who were, like him, already members of the 185 1 commission: Thomas Baring, the banker, and Wentworth Dilke. The remaining two were the marquis of Chandos (who became the duke of Buckingham in the course of 1861); and Thomas Fairbairn, who had been chairman of the Manchester Art Treasures Exhibition in 1857 and who was the son of the eminent engineer, Sir William Fairbairn. Cole was éminence grise, a position likely to be frustrating for him and uncomfortable for others.

One of the matters in which Cole was most active was the exhibition building.58 This was intended to fill the remaining empty portion of the commissioners' 'quadrangle', south of the Royal Horticultural Society's gardens. It was only natural that Cole wanted the gardens and the exhibition building to form an architectural unity. He therefore contrived that Fowke should be consulted about planning the exhibition building from a very early stage, with the idea that, when it became necessary to request tenders for the exhibition building at the start of 1861, Fowke's drawings would be to hand. The commissioners seem not to have been altogether happy with what might have been regarded as a piece of cronyism, and were conscious that Fowke was not a professional architect but a military engineer. But Cole repelled their attempts to water down Fowke's contribution, by deploying a 'back-him-orsack-him' argument. When Granville asked Cole for his views, confidentially, on 15 January 186 1, he urged that the commission should either accept Fowke's plans and let him get on with it, or should find other people altogether to do the job. He resisted their attempts to introduce their own ideas ('Don't divide responsibility', was his cry, as always) and commented that Lord Chandos was an 'Amateur Architect'. It would perhaps have been difficult for him to reject this epithet if it had been bestowed on him, but nonetheless he boldly insisted that \*we [note the 'we'] wd have our professional status acknowledged or wd not act'. At a meeting on 3o January, Lord Chandos and Fairbairn did try to get rid of Fowke's plans, but it was too late - five days earlier Cole, Granville and Lowe had discussed at a departmental board how to formalise Fowke's connection with the exhibition, Lowe commenting sardonically that the arrangement should be 'one that cd be defended in ye House of Commons & "throw dust in the eyes" of critics. Cole suggested that Fowke's departmental salary should cease while he worked on the exhibition, but Fowke 'wished his connection with the Department not to be impaired'. However, on 28 January, Fowke 'gave up his opinion to mine', and on 2o March Cole was 'dictating Fowkes letter of terms for Professional Fee for building'Clearly, Cole acted as Fowke's manager, and no doubt he felt this was necessary owing to Fowke's 'sluggish and indolent temperament'.59

On the basis of Fowke's plans, tenders were sought in early February 1861, and after a good deal of bargaining between Cole and the contractors, the commission accepted a joint tender from John Kelk and Charles Lucas. Kelk was already contractor for the gardens at South Kensington, and was constructing Cole's new house at Witley. His close relationship with Cole led him to seek favours. Resentful at being 'always treated as a Tradesman' by the Commissioners, who seemed to despise contractors (always got the better of them & made them fee like Children', he wanted a baronetcy and, Cole noted, 'thought I cd get it for him'. He also asked Cole to 'get him named a Deplut]y Lieut[enant] by Lord Salisbury'. Cole did indeed ask Lord name down for both'.60

A feature of Fowke's design that was dear to Cole was a colossal concert auditorium, which would have been the realisation of his plans, conceived in Lausanne in 1858, for a 'Music Hall. At the tendering stage, however, all parties agreed (on 21 February) that this was too expensive to be worth risking, and must be abandoned. Granville wrote to a friend: 'we have been obliged, to our great regret, to excise a great Hall, which would have been the eighth wonder of the world'.61

Sinc atrst Cole adoffl sandingin theminisrationf thexhin, he obviously felt no inhibitions about writing (over Easter weekend of 1861) an article on it for the Cornhill Magazine, edited by his friend Thackeray. Although he revised this62 before it was published in July, the final product seems distinctly opinionated and outspoken. It starts with a very Cole-ian note. In preparing the exhibition, he says, 'government has not been asked to assist, and public taxes have not been drawn upon for subsidy'. While this is in a major key, he soon modulates to the minor, in acknowledging that 'all kinds of difficulties, apathy, hostility, hesitation, and timidity' have afflicted the project so far. He alludes to his own big ideas: the inclusion of paintings (for which there will be 'a gallery of nobler proportions and better lighted than the Louvre itself') and of music. The latter proposal seems not to have been taken up as enthusiastically as he wished, so he gives it a nudge: 'although the programme of the Commissioners names only "musical instruments," they may possibly mean to systematize them and make trials of them a source of attraction'.

Cole urges the commissioners to refrain from imposing 'an abstract idea of completeness' on the exhibition, as had been done in 185 1. Let the exhibition be open to whoever will take the trouble to contribute, and let the classification system be flexible. Pursuing his free-trade view, Cole disparages the awarding of prizes. Medals had been so widely distributed in 185 1 as to be meaningless, and at Paris in 1855 the medal-giving was a 'farce'. Let the best rise naturally to the top in a free market. 'Who . . . wants any other prize than public approbation and support?' But if there must be prizes, let them be awarded right at the beginning, "and then cause labels to be affixed to the productions exhibited, so that the public may test the decisions. This is already done at all agricultural, cattle, and flower shows'. Concluding with another personal theme, Cole calls on the commissioners not to "'attempt to combine both legislative and executive functions, and so annihilate that individual responsibility which is at the basis of all success'. This is Cole's penultimate sentence, and one almost expects that the last sentence will be: 'Leave it all to Henry Cole'. But he veers into a graceful final cadence: 'No cause so largely conduced to the eminent success of the Exhibition of 185 1 as that personal responsibility which its president the Prince Consort was pleased to assume.'63

Cole also put into print his opinions on the exhibition building  in a pamphlet published by Chapman and Hall, and quickly written between 1 2 and 29 July. It is a short, mainly descriptive discourse, but it is worth observing two points vigorously made by Cole. The first seems like an attempt to forestall criticism in the event of financial difficulty, by producing out of the hat yet again Cole's personal project for an endless series of exhibitions.

The buildings erected for the International Exhibition of 1862 . . . have been designed to serve not merely for that Exhibition, but for all future exhibitions - International, Colonial, and otherwise; and the legal arrangements necessary for preserving the buildings on the ground have been made . . . It was neither necessary nor expedient that the Exhibition of 1862 should be charged with the whole cost of the buildings. Enough will be paid by that Exhibition to render the remaining payments for the future purchase, and for the gradual completion of them, comparatively easy.

Here it is implied that the exhibition buildings may not achieve completion, and Cole goes on to make the point explicitly, in relation to the exterior elevations. 'As architectural fine-art works' the buildings 'must be considered incomplete at present. They are to be regarded simply as structures fulflling the first object of utility, and as a work for future completion and decoration'.64 The plainness of the exterior, Cole insisted, would soon be relieved by the application of sculptural decoration, Della Robbia ware, and mosaics. In September 1861 he went to enormous trouble to extract mosaic designs from all his artist friends.

When the structure was far enough advanced to be seen by the public, it aroused strong criticism. 'It has, almost with one voice, been condemned as ugly and featureless, and having no one element of grandeur but size.'6s So its designer was reprehended. 'For the past six months', remarked a journalist just after the opening, 'Capt. Fowke has been the best abused man in town.'66 But it was not only Fowke who came in for criticism. It was pointed out that

the building designed by Captain Fowke . . . springs from the very centre of a school which aspires to teach the true principles of designing art to an ignorant and benighted country. From the days when Marlborough House schoolmasters lectured us upon our barbarously-coloured carpets .. to this present period, when the South Kensington Museum gets two hundred thousand pounds at a time from a not very flourishing exchequer to enable it to teach its doctrines, we have been loudly told where to go to if we want to improve our taste. We have been carefully directed to the one existing college whose professors believe they possess the only true eye for harmony of form and colour, and whose missionaries, duly primed at head-quarters, are actively teaching South Kensington art throughout the country in local schools of design. This is not an organization to be treated tenderly because of its weakness or retiring disposition, and wh t ui mus  k z  wh Tu pared for the forthcoming International Exhibition is not so much the production of ol,  an u u getting educational value for our money.67

It  u hard.

One notable fight was at the Society of Arts on 4 December 1861, when Captain Fowke's assistant, Captain William Phillpotts, R.E., read a paper on 'The Building for the International Exhibition of 1862'. Hardly had he sat down when angry voices from the floor were heard demanding to know how Fowke had been appointed, why there had not been a competition, how the tendering process had been carried through, and other such provoking questions. Cole sprang up. He first contested the criticism that a professional architect should have been employed. 'What is an architect?', he asked, and went on to show that the designers of many of the world's great buildings could not claim architectural training. He alluded to Giotto (shepherd and painter), Brunelleschi (goldsmith), Michelangelo (sculptor), Leonardo da Vinci (mathematician, painter, engineer), Palladio (geometrician), San Gallo (sculptor), Wren (professor of mathematics), Vanbrugh ('no professional education as an architect'). He then claimed that 'real professionally-bred' architects did not always succeed, citing Sir Charles Barry - perhaps a rather cowardly choice since Barry had conveniently died in the previous year. He pointed out that Paxton, hero of 1851, was not an architect but a horticulturist. He then demanded whether the 1851 process should have been repeated in 1862. 'Was all Europe to be again invited? Were seven months to be lost? Was a building committee of the most eminent architects and engineers to be again appointed, to end in another horticulturist coming forward with another Crystal Palace?' He insisted that the 1862 exhibition picture gallery would be acknowledged by 'the whole of Europe' to be 'the finest ever seen'. After his barnstorming performance, Sir Thomas Phillips, chairman of the Society of Arts council, managed to lower the temperature with some conciliatory remarks, and the meeting closed.68 Cole was probably pleased with his intervention (he had brought Marian, Hennie and Iselin along to hear it), since the next day he was busy revising what he had said for publication in the society's journal.

Though Cole had no formal responsibility for planning the exhibition, he was quite ready to criticise those who did, notably the commissioners. Although there were only five of them, they often disagreed. The three old hands seem to have known how to get on, but Cole thought that Lord Chandos, who was a railway company director, 'ought to keep himself to Railways', and found Fairbairn "a stupid pigheaded fellow'. By July, Cole was fuming that commissioners' meetings were 'all talk & no progress'.69 On 13 July he 'exploded' about this to the commission secretary, and at a meeting on the 16th seems to have exploded over everybody: 'told Fairbairn that the work was not getting on well & was in Peril & that division against the Chair was intolerable, that Ld Ch: had never done what he undertook'.

Inevitably, Cole was drawn further into the administration of the Exhibition. At the start, Granville had tried to persuade him to take on this task. 'How can we engage you?' he asked on 21 November 186o, and suggested that the 'Department might be legitimately connected' with the Exhibition; he was prepared to authorise this. Again on 3 1 January 1861 he trailed the idea before Cole. Robert Lowe suggested Dilke as 'the man of sense to help Ld Gr.' but then concluded that he was 'too timid'. Dilke in turn sounded out Philip Cunliffe Owen, Cole's right-hand man in the museum.70 But the choice always came back to Cole. On 5 April Granville asked him quite confidentially if I wd take office under ye Comrs such duties as in 18 5 1. I said I was at his disposal'. The following day an offer was made to Cole, rejected, reshaped by Dilke, and accepted. Cole would 'take charge' of the allocation of space, the arrangement of exhibits, the catalogue and the juries, for a fee of £1,5oo. He would retain his position in the department but forgo three months' salary. Dilke trailed before him the prospect that the executive of the exhibition would, as in 1 85 1, 'push out' the commissioners. Cole nonetheless held to his policy of detachment. He insisted to Granville, on I 3 April, that he should not 'be named on the Staff, but if it were wished should propose being called "Consulting Officer

Lack of official status did not necessarily inhibit Cole, especially in matters to do with the building. It was at his behest that Octavius Hudson, who taught at the School of Art, was commissioned in October 186 1 to prepare a colour scheme for the interior. When, however, his trial samples were ready for examination in December, Cole found them 'not Satisfactory'.7l He persuaded Fowke to attempt a colour scheme,'72 but evidently this would not do either. So the decorator John Crace was brought in, and on 26 January was busy with colour experiments while the commissioners looked on. He triumphed in difficult conditions, providing a scheme in deep, rich colours that was, it has been remarked, much more mid-Victorian than the bright primary colours used by Owen Jones in 1851.73

After his appointment as consulting officer, Cole continued to keep his distance from practical work, while advising the commissioners at their meetings, as they discussed the allotment of space to exhibitors, a classification system, the provision of refreshments and the appointment of jurors.74 Who was to do the real work? The commissioners had a secretary, Francis Sandford, who had, until he took this job, been an assistant secretary in the Education Department under Lingen, and was to return there as secretary in due course, gathering up other responsibilities until he came to possess 'more administrative power over education than any other civil servant had ever done before or since in Great Britain'.75 He seems to have coped satisfactorily with secretarial duties but there was still a problem about getting things done. The commissioners decided to appoint a general manager. This was a Colonel Shadwell, who was introduced to Cole by Granville on 2 May. Granville had described Shadwell to Cole three days before in rather unprepossessing terms: he said that 'Shadwell did not look to have an Eagle eye', but that "therefore he thought that he would answer his purpose'. It was not long, however, before Cole was noting in his diary: 'Shadwell a failure'. In the opinion also of Cole's museum colleague, Richard Thompson, Shadwell was 'a failure & incompetent'.76 Shadwell was still 'pottering' around on 9 October, but four days later Cole recorded that 'even Fairbairn thought Shadwell a failure. Had talked of getting rid of him to Ld G and Mr B[aring]. Must be removed abroad'. On 3o November, Lord Granville was thinking of transferring the executive work to Fowke, but 'doubted if F.F. could be trusted with executive matters wholly'. By 2 December, acknowledging that Shadwell was 'worth nothing', he had in mind to entrust executive business to Fowke and Dilke jointly. Eventually, on 25 February 1862, Sandford was appointed manager in addition to his secretarial duties. Even this was unsatisfactory. After the exhibition had opened, Lowe still wanted to get Cole as manager, concluding that Sandford, though 'an excellent Secretary . . . had not the Special ability or experience for Manager'.

The administrative system responsible for preparing the exhibition was, then, somewhat ramshackle. The commissioners had three committees: the building committee, the finance committee, and a 'general committee of organization', which was the only place whereCole's name appeared. Sandford had a small secretariat, including his brother Herbert. Shadwell was backed up by a body of gentlemen who act as superintendents of classes, notably by Philip Cunliffe Owen as superintendent of the foreign division, and Richard Thompson as superintendent for reception and arrangement of goods. These two were drafted in from the museum to prop up

S nt aager  norpnhcalogue storekeeper, and a small number of clerks, complete the official staff.' Beyond these was a network of foreign and colonial commissioners, national committees, London trade committees, and local committees.77

These forces were working away, when in December there came a terrible blow. Prince Albert died on Saturday the 14th  of Gastric Fever', as Cole understood, 'having been ill about twelve days' (The exact cause of the prince's death remains uncertain.) Cole does not record his own reaction in his diary to the death of the prince. He does record that Lord Granville told him on 2 1 December that the Queen 'mentioned my name remarking that no one would be more sensible of his loss'. And, on 5 January, Lord Granville wrote to him 'to tell me of the Queens regard for me & her taking the Museum und[er] Her Special "patronage & protection"". The general response among the exhibition team to the dreadful news was to resolve that the show must go on. Kelk, for instance, volunteered to 'go on with the Exhibition at all risks & losses: said his workmen felt the Princes death.78

## \* \* \*

As the pace accelerated in the new year towards the opening of the exhibition, Cole (still not officially in charge) seems to have grown more bad-tempered. On 28 December 1861 he complained to Lord Granville about the commissioners. On 16 January 1862 he was gratuitously rude to Dilke: 'told Dilke he cd not paint a picture & could be no judge of Decorations'. His own men, Owen and Thompson, were busy laying out the exhibits in March (120 wagons were awaiting unloading on 29 March), and he was rude to them. 'Scolded Owen for wanting to alter Zollverein Passage.' 'Confusion in Machinery. Upbraided R. Thompson.' Thompson lost his temper too. 'Thompson refused to move the carpets & wd resign if moved.'79

In the last two months before the opening (scheduled for 1 May, as in 185 1) Cole and the commissioners seem to have spent an inordinate amount of time planning the ceremonial for the grand inauguration. There was the problem of what the wishes of the Queen would be, as she was in mourning for the prince. On 22 March, Lord Granville heard that she had agreed to a state ceremony, but she changed her mind only hours later. Then she changed it back again, since on 28 March Sandford was able to issue a minute confirming that she wished that the opening 'should bear as much as possible a character of a National Ceremony'.80 The duke of Cambridge was to represent her. Cole provided numerous drafts of the ceremony, only to see them altered by the commissioners. At a meeting on 28 March, when they 'wanted to alter every line & cut it to pieces', he could not restrain his indignation: 'I fired up & said they wd take the Testicles out of it'. In the end a ceremony was agreed. The exhibition opened on time, though only after last-minute tidying up by Thompson, Owen and soldiers from the Hon. Artillery Company. An orchestra of 398 players, and a chorus of 2,zoo singers, brought in from all over the provinces and 'piled . . . to a height of eighty feet, in the midst of which the ladies were happily ranged in the form of a cross, shining out with the sheen of an opal',81 raised very resounding echoes by singing the 'Hallelujah Chorus' and other music.82

The exhibition gained general approval, but there was one very obvious mistake: the principal nave was unduly cluttered with 'trophies'. At the opening, the procession of notabilities had much difficulty getting through all these, 'and appeared on the eastern dais in a limp and straggling manner'.83 Alexander Beresford-Hope commented that:

The Groves of Blarney were order and good taste in comparison with the conglomeration of telescopes, organs, light-houses, fountains, obelisks, pickles, furs, stuffs, porcelain, dolls, rocking-horses, alabasters, stearine, and Lady Godiva, which reduced the

Another critic wrote that 'every man seems to have shot his show case where he listed, and the consequence is that a more hideous jumble than is here witnessed never before was seen'.8 5 Remedial measures were necessary and Cole was called upon. On 5 May Granville 'drafted a Minute putting the arrangement of ye Nave in my hands'. Insisting that 'I must have everything at my disposal', Cole set to work next day, moving, banishing or scaling down the trophies. The statue of Lady Godiva went on 1 1 May. Of one trophy, Cole said that he 'cut off its head'.86 By 17 May, he was able to record: 'Nave clear except Chances lighthouse. Mintons fountain nearly done.'

Since, in discharging this task, Cole seemed at last to have taken charge of the exhibition, some suggested that he should remain in charge. On 17 May, Robert Lowe wrote to Granville:

Things are looking gloomy about the Exhibition. People who waited to see if abuse would go down are now open mouthed and the unfinished state of everything gives them only too good a text. It is time for a coup d'etat. I am convinced that there is so much discontent that the very people who would have united against Cole a month ago would now hail him as a Saviour. I write to state my honest conviction that nothing but making him dictator over everything can save you from a most mortifying reverse.87

Opposition to Cole was strong, however. Already, just before the opening, the duke of Buckingham had written to Granville in defence of the manager, Sandford, who was 'in a very uncomfortable state of mind' at Cole's interference and the commissioners' acquiescence in it. 'Our staff is unhinged at the most important moment,' Buckingham claimed, as a result of the apparent want of confidence implied by the placing of Cole in a position of superiority as to one part of the arrangement, and the vacillation of today'8 Granville did not take Lowe's advice. Cole noted on 19May that 'Ld Granville wd not appoint me Dictator as it wd be an admission of entire failure'. So Cole retired into the background again, and in July went off for a long summer holiday at Witley. His interventions had no doubt felt like the irruptions of a rogue elephant to hard-preed staff further down the lne, but his supporters'vsion of his contribution to the 1862 exhibition was that 'he saved that great undertaking from the natural consequence of the blunders and meddling and muddling of some high and mighty personages'.89 By the end, everyone knew that he was the kingpin of the exhibition, as witness some verses in Punch that address him as 'Old King Cole', one of the first uses of this nickname.90

The186 ehibition had bee publicis i dvance as ameans  showig what progress had been made since 181; Cole himself made this point in his Corill article. For thos intereste particularly in manufactured consumer goods, it offerd an opportunity to assess whether the English system of art and design education initiated after 185 1  the 'South Kensington' system - had really exercised a beneficial infuence. In April 186 a journalist, having described the South Kensington system, expanded on this question.

T hols  rt have owbet  years n  e seen whether our manufacturers have become less dependent upon foreign artists; wheer h esis which they execu whethr te wolen odcot , iron-castings, earthen mouldings, which proceed from our factories and workshops - e a higherand a purr taste, a justerpriation, and a moecratenowe of the true principles of decoration.

TheExhibition f 186, it is anticipated by persons best qualifed to form an - ion, will show that each one of our national products has reaped benefit from the organised system of Government instruction already described.'91

Not all observers came to this conclusion. Beresford-Hope, for instance, detected 'a great scene of disenchantment on all sides'. Design reformers, he thought, were disappointed that 'conventional trivialities' still had a hold on the public, while those who believed that nothing was wrong with British goods were disturbed by the growing signs of reform.92 Most people, however, accepted the view that British design had got better. It was in Cole's interest to prove that progress had been made, and he certainly tried. In April 1862 he drafted a proposal to the Society of Arts that they should publish a book entitled Reports on the Progress of the Arts and Manufactures of the World since 185 1, as shown by the International Exhibition of 1862.93 This was not adopted, but Cole took other measures. Already, on 8 March 1862, he had given to his colleague George Wallis (then agent for the sale of photographs at the museum, and a former art school headmaster) the task of reporting how far the designs of decorative manufactures in the exhibition had been produced by designers who had studied in the art schools.94 Wallis corresponded with 383 manufacturers and secured enough information to make a fairly positive report, which was published as an appendix to the Department of Science and Art's annual report for 1862. Cole also looked out for favourable testimony from others, and gathered frorn jury reports (especially the French jurors' comments) and elsewhere an anthology of comments, which were also published as an appendix to the annual report. He was even prepared to estimate the benefits that had accrued to the country in financial terms, on one occasion pointing out that during the decade between 185 1 and 1862 'the value of the export of manufactures likely to be influenced by art-instruction has increased from 29,244,779l. t0 43,799,501.'95 Though he could hardly have supposed that this meant much, it is one more example of his insistence that standards had been raised in British goods, and that it was South Kensington that had made the difference - a claim which the department always enthusiastically promoted.

Cole had a quiet time in the later part of 1862, living at Witley from July until the beginning of November, and commuting into town. He was much preoccupied, however, with efforts to ensure that the 1862 building should survive in perpetuity. He opned negotiations in all directions, but with little success. By the end of the year no solution was in sight; on I9 December Lowe told him that 'if the Govt did not buy the Exhibition building, he thought it must come down'. In order to boost the claims of the building, Cole got Fowke to produce designs for new façades. Fowke, after taking so much abuse, must have been reluctant to go into battle again. It is no wonder that Cole, in his diary entry for 14 January 1863, expresses some surprise that 'Fowke was stirred up into making a design for the exterior of the Ex: building!'. On 3 January Fowke showed Cole a sketch based on 'the Florence Duomo, very clever', while still expressing reluctance actually to supervise the recasting himself. With Sykes's help, Fowke worked up the sketch into a 'very striking' Renaissance design.96 Unexpectedly, the government took the bait, and would have bought the building, but in the face of a last-minute outbreak of furious criticism in a Commons debate on July, the purchase was voted down. It see toe Cole onfided toh y 'that the matter had been wholly mismanaged.' The Queen wrote to Palmerston that 'the folly'of the decision 'will bye and bye be repented of.98 But it was too late: demolition started on 6 July. The building was so well made that the stouter parts had to be blown up. Cole indulged his regrets by making sketches and prints of the massive ruins. Yet he soon revived 'Looking back dispassionately on the past, he remarked a few years later, I have learnt to think that this was a wise decision.'100

In the years since his sick leave, then, Cole had, while apparently keeping in the background, presided over the expansion of the museum, pushed hard for the development of the rest of the South Kensington site, influenced the momentous introduction of payment by results into the English education system, and swept everybody before him into the 1862 exhibition.

## Consolidating the Stronghold 1863-1867

If Cole's impact on the world around him in 1861-2 had been forceful, even painful for others, he was no less hard in his dealings in the years immediately following. As he turned back to the museum after the 1862 exhibition, he found himself facing the fiercest and most damaging revolt that had yet afflicted his kingdom, in the disaffection of J. C. Robinson.1 Robinson was 1 5 years younger than Cole, and, when Cole plucked him from obscurity to become curator of the museum, had virtually no qualifications for this post except an intense enthusiasm. He learned quickly, but the opportunities to do so were provided by Cole. The development of the museum as a historical collection of decorative art was Cole's policy, and the implementation of it by buying in bulk from existing collections - Bandinel, Bernal, Gigli-Campana, Soulages  was entirely a consequence of Cole's buccaneering approach. Robinson, for his part, took ful advantage of what proved to be for him a unique crash course in the connoisseurship of medieval and Renaissance art. His position in the museum brought him advantages, but soon his experience and knowledge proved a great asset to the museum. Cole backed him when in 1857 he started a 'Collectors' Conversazione', to create a network of private collectors, and was at his side at the early meetings. These meetings grew into the Collectors Club, later the Fine Arts Club, which remained a power-base for Robinson.2 As he gained experience and knowledge, the museum sent him on purchasing trips abroad: to Lyon in summer 1854, Paris in summer 1856, Dresden and Vienna in summer 1857, Rome and Florence in summer 1859, southern Italy in winter 186o and Aix-la-Chapelle and Florence in 1862. He was indefatigable in his search for works of art. Redgrave, when travelling with him, found antique shops 'unendurably tedious',3 but Robinson loved the chase. In describing his purchasing expeditions to Cole in 1858 he spoke of 'ransacking' towns, and he also used the word, almost 2o years later, to his occasional travelling companion, Sir William Gregory, MP.4 In 1859, in a letter from Italy to Cole that remarked in passing that 'the Austrians are ravaging the country without mercy', he wrote of his own purchasing feats: 'Florence has not had such a raking out as this within the memory of man' His collecting urge had a predatory edge.

As we have seen, he came to feel that his contribution to South Kensington deserved greater recognition than he was receiving. Cole was paid more than twice as much as Robinson. Cole, however, had a much wider range and heavier weight of responsibility than Robinson. The rational side of Robinson's mind could always recognise Cole's outstanding qualities as an administrator, but in Robinson's scale of values collecting and connoisseurship were far more important than administration, and he knew that in these fields he was far ahead of Cole. When Cole took a hand in purchasing, as he often did, Robinson could be roused to fury. A bitter moment came in 186 1. Robinson was authorised to go to Italy to study Renaissance sculpture and 'to inspect Mediaeval Exhibition at Florence'.6 But the funds for this were withheld from him, and instead Cole went to the International Exhibition in Florence, authorised to buy exhibits for the food, animal products, structural and educational divisions of the museum, as well as examples of contemporary design. This offended Robinson's sense of priorities, and he thought that Cole had bought 'a great number of objects of inferior nature'.7

Still, while Cole was busy with the 1862 exhibition, Robinson was given free rein to set up what proved to be almost a rival attraction. Fowke's new 'glazed court' in the museum was nearly finished. It comprised two courts, in fact. The North Court opened in April 1862, and Cole had worked with Robinson on laying out the exhibits over Christmas 1861. The South Court took longer to finish, with its elaborate decorative scheme. It was decided that it would open in 1862 with a loan exhibition of works of medieval and Renaissance decorative art from British private collections, organised by Robinson with help from his contacts in the Fine Arts Club. The exhibition, which showed over 9,o0o objects from 553 lenders,8 was a stunning demonstration of 'The Art Wealth of England' (this phrase was used as the title of a portfolio of photographs issued to accompany the exhibition). But the organisation of it left something to be desired. Exhibits were still being unpacked and arranged a month after the opening; the publication of most of the catalogue was considerably delayed; and the arrangement of exhibits on the ground was confusing, there being "an utter absence of anything like chronological order'.9

Nonetheless, the exhibition was very successful, to the extent that 'it was said that the merits of this Loan Collection were so great as to cause it to run the International Exhibition hard in attracting visitors'1Cole might well have felt a twinge of annoyance on reflecting that the new court in the museum, on which he had lavished such care, had been taken over by a troublesome subordinate for an exhibition which seriously rivalled Cole's own International Exhibition. In his annual report he made a point of saying that Robinson's exhibition contained 'for the most part almost all the objects of mediaeval art which were exhibited at the Society of Arts in 18 59 [a misprint for 185o], the collection of which was so greatly promoted by the exertions of H.R.H. the Prince Consort' 11 This latter exhibition had included Cole among its organisers, at a time when J. C. Robinson had not been heard of, so mentioning it is a way for Cole to steer some of the credit for Robinson's triumph in his own direction.

By early 1863 Cole and Robinson were seriously at odds, and the smallest thing could spark an explosion. The fatal sparks flew when they had a 'scene' on 30 January at which Robinson was 'very insolent'. In reply, Cole recorded, I told him to leve the room'Thereafter there was trouble all through February. Robinson's financial paperwork or the loan exhibition cred the disproval  the board, whic also looked askance at purchases made for the museum in Italy 'on his own responiy Furthermore it was just t this juncture that the museu comple aoher new gallery range, which included four residencs for offcials. Robinson hope o be allotted one of these, but was not, and took it as another slight. He was also iolve   iu bout ow wer  e payen he Nor Curt, eally the'Brulecichape the hape fo SantChiara Forece By early March, Robinson had resolved to challenge Cole before the board, though his other colleagues were obviously trying torestrain him. On 6 March, Cole noted that 'Robinson resisted all McLeods advice not to send his letter direct to Ld G. but he said he wd. Robinson bombarded Granville and Lowe with ltters until, on 3 March, Lowe cried in exasperation: 'His letters must be stopped.' But Robinson had thrown caution to the winds, and was now writing of Cole in very contemptuous terms.

I cannot think it any real disparagement of the Secretary of this Department, whose administrative talents and practical energy I hold in the highest respect, to say that his speciic knowledge of Art, is in my opinion not equal to the actual work of forming and technically directing a public Collection of such a varied and comprehensive nature as [South Kensington] . .. . Unfortunately Mr. Cole's selections were rarely either timely or judicious, made on the spur of the moment by accident or momentary impulse.13

This may well have had some truth in it, but it is not wise for an employee to say this kind of thing about his employer. Robinson went too far for the ministers to countenance his complaints.

Over Easter weekend, he had to decide whether to accept the terms that they now offered him. He was to cease to be in full-time employment at the museum as curator, but was to continue as a consultant on purchases, taking the title of 'Art Referee'. The ministers and Cole obviously wanted to retain Robinson's expertise for the museum; and, to make the new arrangement seem less of an expedient to deal with him personally, it was made part of a system whereby the department appointed a series of part-time referees in various fields, scientific as well as artistic. Robinson, after holding out for as long as he dared, accepted his fate. It is hard to see how the problem could have been solved in any better way, but Robinson had, to all intents and purposes, been sacked, and his alienation was to hit the museum hard. Cole won this dispute, but not without some damage to his reputation. The Art-Journal supported Robinson, claiming that 'it is to his indefatigable industry, no less than to his sound and matured knowledge, that we are mainly indebted for the value of the Museum. . . . He has the confidence of every collector in the kingdom'. Cole's abilities it regarded as 'not at all equal' to Robinson's and it sneered (perhaps deliberately using a military metaphor to mock Cole's known military sympathies) that 'it is not pleasant for a colonel to know that the corporal he commands is a better soldier than himself'.14

This affair came at a time when Cole was hard-pressed by other matters, not only in official but also in private life. His father, lately in poor health, was sinking in January 1863, and died on 1o February, while Cole was away on business in the eastern counties. It was not long since the death of the Prince Consort, and by the end of 1863 Thackeray was also to be dead. Particularly distressing must have been the fact that, as soon as the Robinson affair was over, Cole's eldest daughter, Tishy, had some kind of nervous breakdown. He found her on I 1 May 'wandering, Feverish & low'. The next day she was rambling incoherently, 'singing & acting with her arms & eyes shut'. She became violent, and, though drugged with chloroform, had a paroxysm of screaming on 16 May that lasted four hours. She calmed down eventually, and became capable of registering again what was happening in the world. On 27 May, as her mental haze lifted, she memorably remarked to Cole: 'You are always going to the Office, pulling the Strings like an old Harlequin.' By the middle of June she seemed to have recovered. She must have been close to the Robinsons, as godmother to their son, and may have been distressed by the implacable quarrel between Robinson and her father.

In Cole's official life, the question of payment by results cropped up again.15 The revised code, on which Cole had advised Lowe and Lingen, applied only to elementary schools supported by grants from the Education Department. It might have seemed that it would have no effect on the Department of Science and Art, which prided itself on already having a system of payment by results. In his annual report for 186o, indeed, Cole gave statistics showing how his department had applied payment by results over the previous five years, and, with a hint of complacency, noted: This statement may be useful and interesting to those who advocate the applicability of the system of paying on results to general education.'16 Lowe and Lingen, however, having removed direct government subsidy from the elementary sector, were now on the lookout for further anomalies. Their eye fell on various practices in the Science and Art Department that needed reform.

First was the department's involvement in teaching drawing in elementary schools. To spread the ability to draw throughout the nation was one of Cole's key policies when he took over in 1852, and, it will be remembered, he established a system whereby his department organised drawing classes in elementary schools. Lingen knew al about this, since he helped to set it up. He signed a letter of 20 August 1853, for instance, in which he said that 'the Lord President is desirous of giving all the effect in his power to the introduction of drawing as a regular part of elementary education''7 Cole had followed his principles in making payment for drawing instruction in elementary schools depend so far as possible on results, but felt that some further incentive was needed. This was provided by paying teachers according to the certificates they had obtained and giving prizes to children. 'From the outset,' remarks a modern historian, 'prizes and payments granted to these teachers seem to have proved so attractive that the Government became worried about both expendi-T  L in summer 1862, when they suggested that Cole abolish these incentives. A printed version of an exchange o views exists in Cole's Miscellanies,1 fro which it iscer that Lingen thought that the government should not give financial assistance to any 'extras' besides the three Rs in elementary schools: 'Music and Drill have their advocates as well as Drawing, not to mention all the varieties of Science'. Payments to schoolmasters for special subjects made them virtually employees of the state, 'which the Revised Code was meant to abolish'.

Theuy cut away, and Cole, Redgrave, and H. A. Bowler (senior inspector for art under Regrav) t0k cunter-argents t a metng n 17 July 186 with Lng nd Lowe. This confrontation 'amused Mr. Lowe', Cole noted. Lowe would certainly have been conscious that Cole was being hoist with his own petard. The Science and Art Deparent fund i possible toresst Linge'logic, anaccordingly iss a minute on 24 October $\texttt { I } 8 6 2$ ,changing the system of prizes and payments to one depending closely on results.20

Once payments for certificates had been abolished in elementary schools, they had to go in the department's art schools, too. The aim was to end all methods of payment to art masters except those made through the results system. It will be remembered that the department paid annual bonuses to teachers of $\mathcal { E } _ { \mathrm { { I O } } }$ for each of the department's certificates of competence that they held. This provision was abolished in a minute of $^ { 2 4 }$ February $\mathrm { ~ T ~ } 8 6 3$ On the same day another minute settled the fate of 12 art masters who had been in the system before Cole revised it in $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 5 3$ and were therefore still receiving salaries direct from the department. These men were superannuated, though they were allowed, if they wished, to go on working in the payment by results system. A third minute issued on 17 March outlined an exceedingly complicated method of payment linked to the 23-stage syllabus of the department, which was now subdivided into 6 1 sections. These administrative changes may have seemed necessary and tidy to Cole, Lingen and Lowe, but their impact on art masters throughout the country was painful. A modern scholar, after examining a number of cases of personal hardship, concludes that 'payment on results was now fully comprehensive, but the managers and masters had been provoked too far to expect them to submit without a great effort to obtain redress'.21

The 'revolt of the masters' was headed by John Sparkes, headmaster at the Lambeth School of Art, who founded the Association of Art Masters to co-ordinate opposition to the department. Sparkes eventually became headmaster of the South Kensington school, and looking back in later years temperately but accurately criticised payment by results for its 'tendency to destroy the elasticity of art teaching, and thus to cramp the genius of designers'. He saw 'no reason to suppose that the masters generally were unwilling that their remuneration should be gauged by the results of their work', but pointed out that 'the most efficient were discouraged by a system which made the best and highest kind of teaching unremunerative', while all of them 'were not unnaturally opposed to a change which had the effect of reducing their incomes'.22

Great resentment was felt on the part of provincial masters and management committees towards the bureaucracy in South Kensington. A statement from the Paisley School of Art asserted that, 'while the masters' salaries have been reduced' and would be further reduced by the new "uncertain system, styled payments on results', the number of officials in the central department had been increased, 'and their salaries enormously augmented'.23 A letter in the Manchester Guardian analysed the finances of the department and pointed out that from a budget of £68,401 16s. 2d. for art education the total expenditure on provincial schools was only £11,859 7s. $7 d .$ , leaving all the rest to be spent on South Kensington.
