'Is it not monstrous that more than five-sixths of the entire grant should be consumed in London?'24 It was not only to the London art schools that this money was directed, but also to the South Kensington Museum, for in his accounts Cole did not distinguish the museum from the schools. 'Mr. Cole's policy has hitherto been so to mix it up with the general system of education  to make it, in fact, part and parcel of the schools - as to render it difficult or impossible to separate one from the other when considering the money spent on both.'25 The money spent on buying exhibits for the museum was disguised under the head 'Examples, diagrams of science and art, &c.'. This aroused such furious comments as this, from Walter Smith, headmaster of the Leeds school:

Gorgeous courts arise at South Kensington, Venetian glass and majolica plates are purchased at fancy prices, whilst provincial Schools of Art, in important centres of manufactures, are crippled and curtailed; the Schools of Art and Schools of Design, throughout England, are to be made mere elementary drawing classes, in order that South Kensington may have a public curiosity shop.26

The fact that the museum had just completed a set of official residences, described as 'rooms for officers' in the accounts but 'construed to mean mansions for officers'27 by some vigilant critics, rubbed salt in the wound. And for many the problem was not just that South Kensington was an 'Augaean stable of jobbery and corruption, against whose doings the heads of nearly all the provincial and other schools are now loudly protesting'.28 The problem was Henry Cole himself, who had made South Kensington 'a snug place for one man to fill with his personal friends and nominees, however unfit they may be for the work given them to do'.29

## \*  \* \*

The provincial schools eventually, on 24 February 1864, organised a deputation to see Lord Granville, with many MPs in tow and Beresford-Hope as chief spokesman. Granville stonewalled against their criticisms, and hinted that the matter should be turned over to a select committee. On 8 March Sir Stafford Northcote successfully moved for one to be set up, and it took evidence during eleven sessions in April and May. As well he might, Cole seems to have been nervous of appearing before it, but Lowe (who was a member of the committee) said encouragingly, 'you gave excellent evidence before & will do so again'. Cole had the advantage that he and Northcote were old friends, and they conversed and corresponded about how the evidence should be presented.30

The committee saw 21 witnesses, mainly art school masters and industrialists. Cole and Redgrave appeared on the stand together to answer for the department. In 246 pages of evidence and a further 214 pages of documents in an appendix (including a sort of census of the art schools), the committee's report provided a detailed record of the working of the art education system. Buried in the evidence, at paragraph 4,3 13, is one of Cole's neatest mission statements. When asked to state the 'leading principles of your Department', he replied: 'To improve the taste and artknowledge of all classes of the community, having especial reference to the influence of that taste and knowledge upon the manufactures of this country.' That he chose this phrasing  stressing that the department aimed directly to influence taste, and thereby indirectly to improve manufactures - reveals, perhaps, that he knew that he was vulnerable in respect of two matters. One was the question of whether his art education system had actually had any effect on British manufactures. He always stoutly claimed that it had, but the committee remained agnostic, merely commentin that it had been 'struck with the conflict of opinion which prevails respectig the proper functions, and the actual value'of the art schools. The other problematic matter was the question whether the South Kensington Museum (which influenced taste)

had been privileged at the expense of the schools (which might be thought to influence manufactures). The committe concluded that 'the Museum is undoubtedly to be regarded as an instrument of Art Education, and its cost must be reckoned among the items  the Natinal Grants or the promotion that eucation' but it id not allow Cole to get away with his shifty accounting procedures. It recommended that 'a strict line is drawn between the sums voted for the purchase of works of Art, which are for the most part to be deposited at South Kensington, and those voted for the direct encouragement of the provincial schools', and the department in due course complied.

Cole's often repeated insistence that the schools would pay for themselves was dismissed by the committee: it was their 'opinion that it is improbable that the schools will ever become self-supporting upon the present system; nor do they think that the substitution of payment by results . . . will tend to make them so'. Furthermore, the committee concluded 'that the system of payment by results, introduced by the recent Minutes, is not well adapted to Schools of Art', and recommended a capitation system. Cole was, therefore, bruised in this enquiry. On the crucial question, however — whether there had been a 'breach of faith' with the art masters — the committee found in his favour.31 It concluded that there had not been a contract with the masters, and that the government was entitled to change its methods of administration in response to changing circumstances. Once again, then, Cole came out of a fight as winner, even if somewhat bloodied.

The department issued a reply to the committee, in which it more or less announced that it would go on as before,32 and Cole coasted along relatively smoothly in the years 1864-6. He had now achieved a position of enough eminence to pass from one meeting to another, taking decisions and launching projects, in the comfortable knowledge that well-trained subordinates and allies would take on the hard work of implementation.

One notable change in his private life occurred in 1863, when, on 18 July, he moved house into one of the newly constructed residences at the museum. Here he had his finger even more closely on the museum's pulse than when he lived previously in nearby Onslow Square. At weekends he often went to Ealing to visit his daughter Mary, who married George Bartley, a bright young man in the department, on 5 January 1 864. (Of this wedding Cole noted 'Passed off Cheerfully', the phrase he had used of the Appells' wedding in 1859: did he have fears, perhaps, that weddings would pass off miserably?) Mary promptly conceived a child, and Cole noted on 16 September: 'Mary had a boy prematurely, born at 12.3o at Ealing. Christened George Henry Cole Bartley. My first Grand Child.' The Bartleys, having chosen Ealing as their domicile, set about building a brand new house there, the first brick being laid on 4 March 1865.

Cole's son Harry, now commissioned in the Royal Engineers, was married in 1865, on 8 June, to Beatrice Trulock, the daughter of a Church of Ireland archdeacon. The Cole family went up to York for the wedding. 'Pretty well arranged ceremony' was Cole's comment on this occasion. Harry and Beatrice went on a honeymoon tour in the Lake District. In October Harry's service with the Engineers took him to India. The family made a trip to Paris and on 18 October said goodbye to him and Beatrice as they set off for Marseilles and, thereafter, Calcutta. On 23 April the following year came letters announcing the birth of Cole's second grandchild, Henry Walter George. While most of Cole's children were now grown up, the youngest three were still in their teens: Isabella (born in 185o), Rose and Granville. Cole noted on 10 March 1866 that 'Rose and Granville acted Robin Hood in their little Theatre', at which time they were 13 and ro respectively.

Christmas Day 1864 was particularly memorable, and moved Cole to make an unusually long diary entry:

All our eight Children at dinner which may never happen again. Harry going to India the next year & not likely to return again for seven years, if he has his health. My age 56 & though not ailing, beginning sensibly to wear out. Marian thank God better in health than she was a few years ago. The day passed so very cheerfully and happily.

Cole's apprehensions about his own health were to be reinforced on 21 July in the following year, when he experienced early symptoms of heart trouble: 'Awoke with a curious feeling in my chest & pulse very irregular, & heart sensible'. Nonetheless he continued to live at a great pace.

He was still carried away by enthusiasms, even when on holiday. In 1866, the family spent the summer at Shere again  for Witley had been given up in 1863, presumably being too expensive.33 After some days in Shere sketching and etching, Cole was roped in by the Bray family, the local lords of the manor, to help with a 'concert by the Gentry' that Mrs Bray was organising. After participating in vigorous chorus practice, Cole seems to have moved in and taken over the project, appointing himself 'Director' He stirred everyone up to startling effect.

The lord of the manor offered the use of timber for the platform, and directed its construction, a great printer in London contributed the programmes and placards, while a young lady undertook the decorations of the room, with the aid of a juvenile committee of assistants . . . and I cannot tell you how many hundred feet of roses and evergreens were made and suspended from the bare whitewashed walls, and several coronae lucis of a humble sort, producing an artistic effect which I have never seen surpassed for elegance and simplicity in any ballroom.34

Cole got an accompanist down from London, and, after witnessing choir rehearsals taken by local ladies, decided that a professional conductor was essential. His network of contacts was so good that he was able to engage Arthur Sullivan. He was pleased with his attempt to provide 'rational and attractiveamusement for his little village, and sent a long letter to The Times explaining to other villages how they could do it too.

In early summer 1865 Cole's likeness was taken by two of the most successful artists of the day. On 19 May he recorded that he went along to Julia Margaret Cameron's studio in Litte Holland House for a photographic portrait. Mrs Cameron ruffled her sir'hair toform a halo as was her wont, an Cole ecalle German Girl held n Umbrella over me'. On 5 and 13 June he sat for a pencil sketch by Samuel Laurence, commissioned by Annie Thackeray. In the sketch Cole looks smoother and thinner than in the photograph, but both artists catch the commanding glint in his eye.

Now that he was in his late 5os, and a well-known public figure, Cole seems to in country-house weekends. He had always stayed with close friends from time to time, such as with the Redgraves at their country cottage at Abinger. In the 186os, he quite often stayed with friends he had made among businessmen. We have already noted his visit in 186o to West Shandon, the house in Scotland of Robert Napier, the marine engineer. Joseph Whitworth, the armaments manufacturer, became a close friend, and Cole often stayed in his house in the Manchester suburb of Fallowfield, near his factory, or at Stancliffe House at Darley Dale in Derbyshire (fig.8). In 1868 Cole took his family to spend all of August and half of September at Darley Dale, in Fircliffe House,35 presumably a rented house, and spent much time with Whitworth, discussing army reform, ocean telegraphs, and plans for a new house that Whitworth was projecting. In July 1865 Cole had a long weekend at Whitmore Hall, Staffordshire, with Michael Hollins, the ceramic manufacturer.

Soon after, he went to stay with Lady Langdale at Eywood in Herefordshire, also visiting Lord Berwick at Attingham. In these more exalted circles, he participated in superior social pursuits such as 'a little Ball' and billiards.36 He and Redgrave were invited to stay with Lord Salisbury at Hatfield occasionally, ever since Salisbury had been their minister in 18 58, and Cole tended to note which of the many rooms in this vast Jacobean mansion were allotted to him ('Slept at top of House opposite the Church', 'Slept in English Tapestry room for the first time').37 In July 1865 Cole spent a weekend at Mentmore, the pseudo-Elizabethan mansion (designed by Paxton) in Buckinghamshire, where Baron Meyer de Rothschild was building up one of the several fabulous art collections belonging to various members of the

![](images/09825281c2471fc063ae7d1eb01b19397fe1c6d5c08c81d41e4af0b4f7856b70.jpg)  
Figure 8 Stancliffe, Darley Dale, Derbyshire, home of Cole's friend Joseph Whitworth: a pen drawing by Cole, August 1868.

Rothschild family. Cole returned there in 1866 (while playing games in the evening, someone mischievously said, 'A Sèvres vase was like Mr Cole because it was an ornament to any room' - and 'often sold'), 1868 and 1869.38 In the Mentmore circle was Lady Dorothy Nevill, kinswoman of Horace Walpole and inveterate collector, and Cole visited her at Dangstein, near Petersfield in Hampshire, in 1 869.39 Through her he met Lady Featherstonehaugh, who presided over the art collections of nearby Uppark. Cole learned that 'Sir Harry Featherstonehaugh took her from the Kitchen at 18 educated her & married her'. Despite her humble origin, she acquired aristocratic sensibilities, and could not endure the presence of radicals. Cobden and Lowe, for instance, were allowed to see round the house, but she would not receive them.40 Presumably she found Cole acceptable, since she came to the museum and he showed her round the china collection.41 Cole penetrated the literary élite in 1865 when he stayed for almost a week at Fryston, near Pontefract in Yorkshire, the residence of Monckton Milnes (Lord Houghton), the writer and politician. Here he met 'Mrs Gaskell & Daughter' and 'Mr Swinbourne, poet'.42 The up-and-coming Algernon Swinburne was a protegé of Lord Houghton and a frequent guest at Fryston. More informally, Cole occasionally visited A. H. Layard at Aldermaston Court.43 Cole's country-house visits, and his attendance at evening dinners in London in fashionable circles, perhaps made him a little dissatisfied with his own family circle. He had always paid visits to his most socially elevated relations, the Byngs, who lived at Quendon Hall, near Saffron Walden in Hertfordshire. When he was there on 21 May 1869, he noted: 'the place is pretty enough, but the complete selfishness & worldliness & vacancy of any thought is tiresome'.

In the department and the museum during the 186os significant changes followed Robinson's banishment to the fringes. George Wallis took over as full-time curator of the art collections. The words 'liberal' and 'progressive' have been applied to his earlier career as an art teacher at Manchester and Birmingham,44 but he was not an art historian or connoisseur  and perhaps if he had been he would have found it difficult to operate in the shadow of Robinson, who was still art referee. Cole did attempt, however, to bring into the Department some scholarly expertise, to compensate for the lack of Robinson's, when he appointed John Hungerford Pollen in February 1864 as 'Keeper of Catalogues'. Cole had first come across Pollen when on 3 1 July 1859 he 'dined with Thackeray in order to meet Mr Pollen, a late Fellow of Merton, become a Roman Catholic, & an artist, & see if he were a likely person for an Inspectorship'. He suggested employing Pollen to Granville and Lowe on 15 August 1859, but was met with anti-Catholic prejudice, for 'both objected to a Clergyman, who had become a pvt.' Like many Protestants, they used the word 'perv    nh  'e as 'like bad Manners'. However, Pollen's time came in 1864, and he was taken on, to edit a series of scholarly catalogues of the South Kensington art collections, and to oBooks on Art.The tortuous history of this will be taken up later.

Pollen gradually took a share in purchasing for the museum, according to his daughter, who recalled that his work at the museum 'necessitated an average of at leas two yeary je or the quisin  je by prase r ln At the sme time she recal he fte had private coisions to decorate hou wth p rather hard luck on Robinson that he was regarded with increasing suspicion by the department on the grounds that his activities as a private dealer (by means of which he now had to earn most of his living) were conflicting with the duties he still carried out for the museum as a consultant. It is true that he made things difficult for himself. Shortly after he was sidelined, Cole learned (on 12 April 1864) from a third party that a colleague stated that 'Robinson was waging an internecine war with me . . . he wanted to attack me in the Fine Arts Quarterly' Britain's first learned art history journal. 'But there was no doubt,' said the colleague, 'who was the stronger.' Only days later, on 21 April, Robinson wrote to the board to complain about Cole's purchases, and 'was told he must not be writing long letters against his superiors'. Robinson did himself no good by his angry letters. Cole noted, doubtless with a little malice, that he had heard that even Robinson's friend, William Gregory, MP, 'was obliged to write to tell Robinson not to bore him with Correspondence'. Gregory also admitted that Robinson alienated other collectors and connoisseurs because he 'pooh poohed anything not found by himself. 46 Cole, however, was not guiltless of harassing Robinson. There was the playing cards controversy. Some supposedly fifteenth-century French playing cards, offered to the museum by a dealer, were recommended for purchase by Cole, but dismissed as forgeries by Robinson. Piqued at this, Cole assembled a team of eminent experts to back him up, but scientific tests supported Robinson. 'What had essentially been an attempt by Cole to question Robinson's judgement on matters of art therefore ended as a vindication of it.'47 Intense mutual suspicion continued to fester between Cole and Robinson. In November 1 866 Cole was all for opening packing cases sent by Robinson from Spain to his private house, in the hope that evidence would be found of dishonest dealing.48 The situation could not be allowed to continue, and Robinson could not win. On 20 December 1866, after he had come to denounce another purchase, Cole recorded: 'I advised him not to run his head against a brick wall'. In 1867 Robinson's consultancy as a regular art referee was to come to an end, and from then on, he would have no special standing in the museum.

The other senior staff in the museum shifted around in the mid-186os. Following a recommendation from a Treasury staffing report,49 three assistant directors were appointed on 23 February 1866: Philip Cunliffe Owen, of administration; Richard Thompson, of arrangement; and Captain E. R. Festing (brought in from the Royal Engineers two years before) of works.50 This triumvirate remained as Cole's immediate backup until his retirement. A series of promotions on 1 December 1864 rewarded staff at a lower level, who had given loyal service from the early days: alongside Wallis, now keeper of art, Henry Sandham became keeper of the construction museum, W. Matchwick, keeper of the food museum, and R. Laskey, keeper of the art library, to be succeeded in 1866 by R. H. Soden Smith, previously keeper of the educational museum. A few younger men entered both the museum and the department during the 186os, but on the whole Cole's staff were growing older with him.

There were also alterations among his superiors. As we have seen, Robert Lowe resigned in April 1864, partly because he felt he had been put in a false position by accusations that he had 'mutilated' his inspectors' reports, but also partly because he felt that Palmerston had failed to reward his merits adequately. He was succeeded as vice-president by H. A. Bruce, Scottish-born but of a Welsh family, deriving great wealth from Welsh coalmines, and in return contributing much to the development of Welsh culture. Granville left him, as he had Lowe, a free hand in running the department, and Cole seems to have got on with him. In June 1866 the Liberal government fell, while struggling to bring in a second Reform Bill, and the Conservatives took power, again under Lord Derby. The lord president in the new government was the duke of Buckingham. This was the man whom Cole had found so difficult when he served as an 1862 exhibition commissioner. Lowe asked, 'How does Cole get on with the Duke? The Duke was the only man that ever put Ld Granville into a dancing passion'.51 The new vice-president was H. T. L. Corry (whom Cole found 'very timid')s2 until a reshuffle in March 1867 took him to the Admiralty and brought Lord Robert Montagu in his place, with the duke of Marlborough as lord president. Cole was no doubt sorry to cease working for Lord Granville, but remained in close touch with him. Granville, in a relaxed moment at the Paris Exhibition in 1867, confided to Cole that 'at first knowing me' at the Board of Trade, he 'thought me most disagreeable', but, 'as people after first disagreeable impressions married & grew fond', so it had been in his association with Cole.53

Cole did not know it, but approval was bestowed on him from an even higher level. On 14 March 1864, the Queen paid an informal visit to the museum, "which I feel it my duty to visit & watch over, though I feel miserably forlorne on all these occasions'. In her journal, she recorded that 'good Mr Cole, who with his rough, offhand manner, is so touchingly devoted to dearest Albert's memory, took me about, along the Gallery, through the beautiful new Courts, the decoration of which would have enchanted my beloved one'.

Educational and museological work proceeded successfully, then, in South Kensington. The Horticultural Gardens were in difficulty, however, and Cole was persuaded late in 1863 to take over as manager. His role, he noted on 2o December, was to be a 'Despot' and to 'rule the Council & keep them in order'. In the summer of 1864, an assistant came to help bear this load, Lt.-Col. Henry Scott, yet another inventive and enterprising officer of the Royal Engineers.54 Recommended by Edward DuCane, an Engineers officer whom Cole knew from the 185 1 Exhibition, Scott took this civilian secondment in order the better to care for his large family.55 He dealt diplomatically with the Royal Horticultural Society and left Cole free to push new construction work in South Kensington. Cole's plans to preserve the 1862 Exhibition building had failed, but the government had bought the site and were under obligation to erect something on it. In January 1864, the first commissioner of works, F. W. Cowper, announced a competition for designs for a new building, which would contain accommodation for the British Museum's natural history collections (to be moved from Bloomsbury) and for the Museum of Patents (already at hand in the Brompton Boilers), and would allow for expansion space for the other museum collections at South Kensington. Here was another chance for Fowke. In Cole's view, he had been 'patient', even 'apathetic', under the burden of his disappointment over the exhibition building, and 'it was with difficulty that he was spurred on' to embrace this new opportunity. However, Cole urged him on and he hurriedly submitted an entry. Cole told him on 28 January 'to begin with making his plan handsome and popular & unlike the Exhibition' of 1862.

At the end of April, all the submitted designs were exhibited, the identity of the it  y al panel. Cole looked at the designs on 25 April with a journalist from the Builder. The journalist 'pointed out a vile one as the work of ye Department', betraying low expectations of Cole and his non-professional designers. The journalist then singled out another as the best, and this, as Cole knew but could not say, was actually Fowke's. Others also expressed to Cole a preference for Fowke's plans "without knowing it'.57 As it turned out, the judges were of the same mind. To the consternation of the professional architects, Fowke won. One of the judges, the architect Tite, was said to have looked 'surprized & sore' when the identity of the winner was revealed, but another judge, Lord Elcho, congratulated Fowke, saying the 'profession were vapid & could not take a licking'58 Fowke's design was in a revived Renaissance style, with a dome and cupolas based on St Peter's, Rome. If his building had been constructed, virtually the whole South Kensington site would have been covered with constructions of coherent architectural style and texture, emanating from Cole's design office.

While waiting for further developments on the new building, Fowke busied himself on the continuing gradual expansion of the museum. His North and South Courts were open, forming, with their surrounding galleries, a solid block in the eastern part of the site. Further west, the range of buildings including the residences was complete, and now, linking these with the eastern block, another range was in preparation. This contained the lecture theatre, and, intended as the main entrance façade of the museum, had impressive bronze doors beneath a pediment supported on terracotta columns. The building was economically constructed of brick with terracotta details, using the techniques (and the sculptural talents of Godfrey Sykes) already tried out in the arcades in the Royal Horticultural Society's garden.

Fowke, however, began to flag again. On 8 May 1865, Cole 'talked very seriously to Fowke about his neglect of his office wh: he took very well'. But, as Cole later recalled, he 'had been working unusually hard at the drawing for the completion of the South Kensington Museum', and was 'much fatigued'. A summer visit to Switzerland was of no avail; 'he came back worse, with many alarming symptoms'.59 He went on sick leave to Eastbourne, where, on 19 September, Cole 'found him very weak, with expectoration'. He returned to his home in the residences, but two days later, on 4 December, 'whilst sitting in his chair, a blood-vessel broke. He exclaimed This is the end", and spoke no more'.60

Sykes, too, was ill. He had been on sick leave at Hastings. On 21 January 1866 Cole went with Marian to see him on his return to London. Though bedridden, he tried to be cheerful. 'He said he came away from the Jack Ketch of [a] Dr at Hastings who was always trying to execute him.' Cole regularly visited him, and noted the grim jokes with which Sykes combated his mortality. On 26 January he rejected his medicine, saying 'he had taken Nasty Stuff enough to bathe in'. He suffered from dropsy, and said that 'he was "too big for his Skin". On 17 February he was "gashed" as he said in twelve places', and though the dropsy went, he could not eat anything. He was very ill when Cole called on 23 February, and said he 'hoped Nature wd soon give in'. The next day, his humour failing, he lamented that he 'was dying an unmanly death'. He died on 28 February, aged only 4 1. 'For weeks', noted Cole, 'he had scarcely eaten anything.'

To lose both its principals must have been a terrible blow for Cole's design office. Nonetheless, the work went on, with Sykes's pupils, James Gamble and Reuben Townroe, continuing to design in Sykes's fluent South Kensington Michelangelesque manner, and Fowke's assistants, such as John Liddell and J. W. Wild, taking up his work. Cole rallied his forces. In early 1 866 his diaries often recorded that, first thing in the morning, he would set forth from his Residence with his dog Jim, to tour the building sites.61 Indeed, 'Cole was not only a daily visitor to the architects' rooms and the decorators' studios, but early in the mornings, soon after the workmen's bell had rung, his well-known figure would be seen clambering over bricks, mortar, and girders, up ladders, and about scaffolding'.62 And he lost no time in finding a successor for Fowke. For there was already in the South Kensington circle a soul mate of Fowke's, his fellow Royal Engineer, Lt.-Col. Henry Scott, who was managing the Royal Horticultural Society's garden. On 14 December 1865, having recorded their sorrow at Fowke's death, the board of the Science and Art Department promptly requested Scott 'to carry on works, vice Captain Fowke, deceased', and on 12 January following they appointed him director of new permanent buildings.63

A new project, which fell to Scott to implement, began in 1866: the provision of a building for the Royal School of Naval Architecture and Marine Engineering. The school had been created in early 1864, under the joint aegis of the Admiralty and of South Kensington (which was, it must be remembered, in charge of scientific as well as artistic education). Plans for a building got under way in summer 1866. On 4 August, while he was weekending at Shere, Cole occupied himself in 'designing W. Front of School of Naval Architecture'. How much Cole was actually involved in the design is impossible to tell. It seems that Fowke, before his death, had made some preliminary drawings, and other designers were involved later.64 The impressive façade relates oddly to the inconveniently shaped rooms behind it, so perhaps this may betray Cole's amateur hand. The fact that the façade is composed around terracotta columns identical with those designed by Sykes for the main museum façade may also have something to do with the strange proportions. The building was not complete until 1 874, by which time the School of Naval Architecture had removed to Greenwich, leaving the building to be used as Science Schools, which eventually became the Royal College of Science. It was the most massive building on the South Kensington site, and was regarded as the outstanding example of the South Kensington Reaissan Revival tyl nr rican teracotta. It ae ki contrast (as it still does) to the white neo-classical houses of neighbouring Princes Gate, displaying 'merits which put to shame the sham columns and entablatures, the flat and cold exterior, of the fashionable Gate . .  a sight good for eyes tired of the eternal stucco'.65

There was one notable erection in South Kensington that escaped Cole's control. The death of the Prince Consort in 1861 soon inspired his followers to create a memorial to him. Cole was quickly off the mark, with a proposal addressed to Lord Granville, and dated 3 1 December 186 1, in which he proposed the establishment of an 'Industrial University to be known throughout the world as the Albert University'. Its site would, not unexpectedly, be South Kensington.66 This suggestion, along with many others, fell by the wayside, as the cause was taken up by the lord mayor of London, William Cubitt, who started a fund for a monument. Designs were made by various architects and sculptors through 1862, and early in 1863 the Queen chose that of Sir Gilbert Scott. Cole had been involved in the discussions, since he was close to Sir Charles Phipps and General Charles Grey, Prince Albert's secretaries who now worked for the Queen. However, 'Cole's knack of provoking resentment had always made it desirable to keep him in the background', and 'a wish not to associate the memorial too openly with his "South Kensington" kingdom' has been attributed to those in control.67 Once Scott design was accepted, Cole resumed upon his own expertise in architecture and decorative arts to dispute with Scott, in an exchange of pamphlets, about how the historical precedents upon which Scott relidhe'Eleanor Croses and Italian Gothic shrine should beupdatin a modern work.68 Scott took no notice of Cole's suggestions.

In selecting the site of the Albert Memorial, 'opinion settled more or less sontaneously on the elevated ground north of the Kensington Road', according to one aiuhasvy

it is Cole who has determined it shall be where it is, and he has persuaded the Queen that the site is a 'revelation of ProvidenceCole says that if you take a line through the centre of the Exhibition of 185 1, and produce it, and then another line breadth-ways through the Exhibition of 1862, and produce that, the two will cut each other in the spot where the monument is to be placed.70

However this may be, Cole did not play a very large part in creating the Albert Mmal, andhis may be party pai by he c that hehadtwheem rials in view.

The memorial to the Great Exhibition of 185 1 was erected in the Horticultural Gardens.71 It is a statue of Prince Albert, on a lofty plinth, by Joseph Durham, and it had been in preparation since 1856. Cole had insisted that a model be made and put in place in a miniature mock-up of its proposed setting over a cascade, which was contrived in the museum garden. A watercolour survives showing Cole (with dog), the prince and others assessing the mock-up in May 186 1.72 The memorial was opened on 1 June 1863, while the big Albert Memorial did not open until 3 July 1872.

Cole's other memorial project was for what became the Royal Albert Hall. The idea of having a great concert hall on the South Kensington site was there from the beginning, in the prince's first notions. Cole was especially keen on it, and, as we have seen, devoted some time during his sick leave in 1858-9 to hatching schemes to achieve it. As preparations were made for the 1862 exhibition, it seemed likely that the concert hall would be realised as part of the exhibition building, but rising costs caused the abandonment of the great hall, as an economy measure. When, after the prince's death, proposals were made for memorials, Cole advanced the concert hall scheme again.

The story of its development has been well told elsewhere.73 Two strands of narrative have to be interwoven, the architectural and the financial. In both Cole was a leading actor. When the hall was more or less finished (on 20 January 1872), Cole was shown rough designs he had made for it in November 1863,74 and was pleased to find that they were 'curiously like what is now built'. Between 1863 and 1872, however, the history of European architecture was sifted for ideas and precedents. As a building type, the concert hall was comparatively undeveloped (though, as Marcus

Binney points out, London was 'much better served with large halls than any city on the Continent'), so there was plenty of scope for bright ideas. An amphitheatre had been Cole's first conception,75 but in 1862 several architects provided designs for a rectangular hall, while Matthew Digby Wyatt offered a circular one, in both Gothic and classical styles. Sir Gilbert Scott took Santa Sophia in Constantinople as a model for a domed hall, which he offered in Byzantine or Gothic styles (or both: 'early Gothic with a tinge of Byzantine'). Travelling abroad in 1863, Cole was impressed by the Spanish Hall in the Hradschin Palace in Prague. In 1864 the amphitheatre at Arles was suggested as a precedent, in 1865 that at Nimes. A Venetian treatment was discussed in 1865, and an exterior elevation combining the Colosseum and Bramante. In 1866 Greek influence jostled with the influence of the greater churches of East Anglia, while Cole was advancing the claims of the amphitheatre at Pula in Istria. Several tours were made by those involved in the design to study foreign precedents. The genesis of the Royal Albert Hall is a prime case of style-mongering, which could only have taken place in the mid-Victorian period, when rampant historicism and the rapid spread of railways supported such an approach to architecture.

What made the difference, however, was the involvement, from first to last, of Cole and his South Kensington design office, which managed to keep control of the project, and hold at bay other architects. Fowke made the first plans. Despite his sudden death in December 1865, his spirit lived on in his successor, Colonel Scott; and the Albert Hall, as realised, stands as the finest and largest monument to the South Kensington Renaissance Revival style, in brick and terracotta.

Nothing would have come out of the richly various architectural suggestions for the hall, if money had been lacking. That the project became financially viable was again owing to Cole. He had tried with little success to enthuse the 185 1 commissioners, who shrank from his 'visionary scheme'. Then came one of those formative moments, such as Cole liked to write into the story of his life, when he seized events by the scruff of the neck. Cole was on a train to Norwich, on 5 August 1864, when he wrote to General Grey: 'I have come to the conclusion that the only way to get the Memorial Hall done is to do it . . . . I don't intend to be beaten in this Matter.' He therefore started fundraising in his usual way, putting together subscriptions from the private sector, and rallying the support of the great and good. With General Grey's help, he succeeded in obtaining the backing of the Prince of Wales. This brought the commissioners round - unwillingly, with Lord Derby warning that Cole was one of the most generally unpopular men I know' and they offered a free sie at South Kensington and £50,000. On 6 July 1865, the Prince of Wales chaired a meeting at Marlborough House, at which some 8 celebrities endorsed 'the erection of a Great Central Hal, the want of which, for various purposes connected with Science and Art, has been long felt'.

A provisional committee was formed, with the prince as president, and Colonel Henry Scot  reary. Scott aredy ncharg themu' builin - gramme, soon slipped into the position of superintending architect. He had to start from the work of Fowke and his assistants, and proceed under the eyes of a committee of advice (consisting of the architects William Tite and Matthew Digby Wyatt, two engineers, and Richard Redgrave), so he probably did not contribute much persal architecturalnspiration, but heunoubtey how gret cpenc confidence in carrying the project through. The foundation stone of the hall was laid o May 18 y he uee who, Coen, was t fhe , but - ened up afterwards'.

Cole fitted this ceremony into one of his dashes back to England from Paris, where he was stationed from mid-February until mid-November 1867 in order to supervise the British section in the Paris Universal Exhibition. He brought the family (including the dog, Jim)77 to join him, in a house at 21 Champs-Élysées. Cole later had to protest at the imputation that 'I had 2o rooms & was living with my family at the public Xpense! which . . . was false'.78 Nonetheless, the impression was widespread that the British officials, occupying 'a newly-erected mansion in the Champs Elysées, ablaze inside with gilding from roof to basement, and for which a fabulous rent was paid', had established a luxurious lifestyle. The journalist Henry Vizetelly offered as proof: I remember, when calling on Sir Henry Cole one morning, being temporarily shewn into a room where several of these gentlemen had recently breakfasted, and noticing on the table a score or so of eggs, all the yolks of which had been carefully picked out. I learned afterwards that the young sybarites scorned to eat the whites.'7

Cole went back to London every month or so for meetings, or, on one occasion, for an important private occasion: the marriage of Minnie Thackeray to Leslie Stephen on 18 June at the church in Onslow Square. There was some doubt whether Cole would arrive in time. Herman Merivale Junior recorded:

It was a very quiet wedding in the early almost twilight morning, in a restful grey church in the Kensington district so much associated with the novelist. Very few of us were present and it was almost dark. Mr. Cole had volunteered to give the bride away but he was in Paris, and my father, who had been much with the daughters since their father died, was ready to take his place. But when the words 'Who giveth?' were spoken, a figure, until then unobserved, emerged from the shadow of a pillar and solemnly said: 'I do.' It was Henry Cole perfectly dressed for the occasion: how he had crossed the Channel was never known.80

Actually, his diary reveals that he reached London with a day to spare, and had aready tended

In Paris, Cole bustled round, organising anything and everything that fell to him as executive commissioner. When the Paris exhibition was officially announced early in 1865, the Queen appointed a royal commission to run the British section. The 24- year-old Prince of Wales was president of the commission, which included upwards of 8o noblemen and gentlemen, among whom the ever-faithful Granville was prominent. The hard work was deputed to the Department of Science and Art, so Cole acted as secretary to the commission and eventually as executive commissioner. Since he had three changes of ministers in 8657, he probably had lttle difficulty in keeing a firm hold on the business.

Cole's official report on the exhibition sets it neatly in context:

The Paris Exhibition differed in many respects from all previous universal exhibitions. It was at least four times as large as any former one. It embraced many new features in respect of the objects to be exhibited: besides the representation of modern works it comprised an exhibition of ancient works of art, even from a pre-historic period. It aimed at representing not merely the processes of manufacture, but the manners and customs of nations, and dancing, singing, various theatrical representations, sports and shows, were admitted within its scope. Shops were erected for sales, which were not merely permitted but encouraged throughout the Exhibition to the fullest extent, and various means were resorted to by the [French] Imperial Commission to increase their receipts, by placing charges hitherto unusual, both on native exhibitors and on foreign commissions.81

The hard-headed French provided only the shell of a building for the exhibition, and left foreign exhibitors to fit out their sections, to their own taste, with flooring, partitions, blinds, and decoration. Cole took the view that this might be acceptable to commercial exhibitors, who were in it for the money and could be expected to dig into their pockets. But it was a different matter to expect lenders of works of art, who were doing the exhibition a favour, to pay for the construction of their own art galleries: it was 'like inviting guests to come to see you in their best clothes, jewels, and velvets, and putting them in a temporary shed, and telling them to . . . make themselves comfortable if they can'.82 In the event, the conditions in the exhibition building turned out badly. The earthen floors were, it seems, constantly watered in order to keep down the dust, and the resulting mud was unpleasant, 'especially to ladies with a delicate chaussure'.83

The necessity of constructing some of the accommodation for the British section pushed the bill up considerably. Cole had at first been given a budget of £5o,000, which was what had been allowed for the Paris exhibition of 185 5. But in autumn 1866, 'when it appeared that various charges for buildings, fittings, maintenance, &c.' would have to be met, the probable costs leaped up to £136,ooo. The Treasury were fierce for reductions, and Cole, though supported in resistance by his minister, the duke of Buckingham, had to cut down his estimate to £128,315. Even after that, however, the Treasury only sanctioned £116,65o. When, in the end, Cole found that his total expenditure had been £120,1 54, he congratulated himself on keeping within his own revised estimate, even if he had exceeded the Treasury grant.84

In the exhibition, individual exhibitors had not only to fit out their own part of the building, but also to provide theirown motive power, which resulted in the construction of many boiler-houses in the spare ground around the central building in the Champs de Mars (plate 4I). The British boiler-house was a showpiece in itself. Known popularly as the 'mosque of Syed Osman', this 'light Eastern-looking building', made of terracotta, attracted visitors, who were able to 'lean over the rail and look down . . . without a suspicion of . . . dirt or stench . . . [on] the great boilers with makers'names affixed, glowing and singing'8 Cole had made a virtue of necessity, preferrng not to shut away his boilers but to draw attention to them. 'An open, cool, and self-ventilating structure was necessary, and so was a chimney shaft. The colonnaded temples of India furnished exactly the type of structure wanted for both house and shaft, and the British boiler-house was designed upon Syud Oosman's tomb of the fifteenth century, at Ahmedabad'.86 Thus South Kensington's practice of adapting historic design to contemporary purposes was given yet another ingenious twist  only slightly spoiled by Cole's admission that 'Italian terra-cotta and not Hindoo details were used for the sake of economy' Cole wrote the above comments in a report on terracotta at the exhibition. This was the only report that he personally contributed (apart from his general report) to the several volumes of reports on products at the exhibition (which were pre-printed in the Illustrated London News before being issued officially). Terracotta was almost an obsession with Cole at this exhibition.

Alongside the 'mosque' in the grounds was another British pavilion, in which 'the humourous element was pleasantly marked'. Known to the commissioners as the Test House' but to the French as 'Le Cottage Anglais' or 'Le Goddam', this was devoted to 'everything that could conduce to the internal decoration and external convenience of a dwelling house'. It was 'a very grotesquely incongruous pile of chimney-pots, cowls, gutters, drainpipes, ornamental tiles, terra-cotta mouldings, tessellated pavements, revolving shutters, ventilators, wire fences, improved cookingstoves, spring latchets, patent bolts, and door-handles "up-to-date"". 87 As well as displaying these everyday items, Cole was determined to exhibit the more dignified evidences of architecture and design at South Kensington: Godfrey Sykes's terracotta columns, and examples of mosaics, bronze doors and stained glass that had emanated from his design office. He met with great difficulty in obtaining a space for them, but in the end, they won a gold medal. It is, incidentally, a measure of Cole's unbounded, even absurd, pride in the South Kensington Museum that when he visited Versailles, it struck him that it 'is built of red brick, and recalls S.K.M.'.88

As representative of Britain, Cole often found himself at odds with the French organiser of the exhibition, Frédéric Le Play, an engineer who eventually achieved fame as a pioneer social scientist. When the opening moves towards the Exhibition were made in 1865, all was harmony: on Good Friday, 14 April, Cole went over preliminary plans with Le Play, and 'told him we shd be content with anything he proposed'. But by late 1866, French parsimony had caused relations to deteriorate. In a dispute on 3o November (about where to place the South Kensington terra-cottas, as it happened) Le Play 'got into a passion'. Cole recorded that he went 'white & shook his crooked finger till he spent himself'. Cole, of course, pressed ahead, regardless.

Le Play had applied Gallic logic to the layout of the exhibition building. As one commentator said, 'the great Paris Exhibition building is like an oval pie-dish, which the nations of the world have been invited to fill, each occupying a slice without prejudice to its neighbours'.89 The building was, in fact, a flattened oval, with two flat sides, and semicircular ends. Logically it should have been a circle, for the 'slice' system to work properly. It consisted of seven concentric rings of iron and glass halls. Each ring was devoted to a different kind of exhibit. The very central part was a garden. Then, in the smallest ring, came the history of labour, an innovation. Fine art came in the next ring, then the decorative arts. In the fourth ring from the middle came household equipment and furniture; then in the next ring textiles, jewellery, weapons and other manufactures. By far the biggest gallery was devoted to Machinery; and finally the outermost ring was devoted to food, with restaurants that spilled out into the surrounding park. If you followed any ring right round, you could have a comparative view of the entire world's production of, say, furniture; while if you crossed from circumference to centre, you could study the whole culture of a single nation. This was the theory, but of course, as Cole said, it was a 'fallacious theory, that every nation would exhibit an equal proportion of objects in each group'. 90 Often there was not room within a country's 'slice' for all its exhibits, and overflow objects were widely scattered, with 'no clue whatever by which to find them'.91 Not only did Cole consider the French attempt to impose an intellectual structure on the exhibition unsuccessful but also, as a seasoned exhibition designer, he disliked the shape of the building, being perturbed by the fact that 'the whole of the space assigned to Great Britain consisted of zones on the curve'. The effect of this 'was to prevent any lengthened views, to interfere with the arrangement of pictures, to prevent symmetrical laying out without great sacrifice of space, and to render the arrangements for working the machinery difficult and costly'.92

Another professional matter that elicited criticism from Cole was the jury system. He had argued after the Paris 1855 exhibition that juries were pointless, and now returned to the theme again, arguing that 'the jury judgment merely follows and confirms public opinion' — indeed, 'is often absolutely in arrear [sic] of public opinion, and even unjust'. Furthermore, it was impossible to assemble jurors with enough combined expertise to provide authoritative judgement on such a vast range of products as was always gathered at an international exhibition, from 'the make of a lady's corsets . . . to . . . the merits of a discovery in electro-magnetism'.93

Aside from such troublesome questions of policy and principle, Cole amused himse by getting up a subsidiary exhibition of his wn, on the English periodical press, 'ranging from the quarterlies and the half-crown and shilling magazines down to the humble catch-pennies of Seven Dials'. It was reported that all the London and all the provincial papers, the most rubbishing farthing ballads, all the almanacs, all the puffing pamphlets of advertising tailors and hatters, found a place in this unique collection' which, alas, did not survive.

As usual, Cole brought people from the department to help him run the British section of the exhibition. Philip Cunliffe Owen and Richard Thompson were his assistant executive commissioners. Captain Festing was superintendent of buildings and park. The accountant A. L. Simkins and his staff were there to look after the finances. From among the curators, Wallis, Worsnop, Matchwick and Sandham were on the strength. Donnelly and Bartley looked after juries and reports. Young Alan Cole and his cousin Wentworth were there, along with Redgrave's son Gilbert and his brother Samuel, and Frank Fowke, son of the deceased engineer.5 One might think that with such support Cole was in a position of unassailable power. But there were undercurrents. On 13 January 1867, Cole recorded that he had been told that people were 'agitating against me with English Exhibitors', and on April 29 Edwin Chadwick, visiting Paris, 'warned me of the sinister currents against me' Chadwick may have had in mind the complaints that had been raised in the House of Commons on 20 and 22 February about the rising costs of the exhibition. Mr Sergeant Gaselee had commented darkly that 'it appeared that there was some one behind the scenes who was enng h Bl olly how much money had gone into his pocket.

Moredamaging perhaps, were the remarks of Beresford-Hope, usually a supporter of cultural matters, who was himself among the very large band of exhibition commissioners. It seemed to him that the commissioners had been sidelined.

What had they done? They had been summoned half-a-dozen times to pleasant meetings in the Sheepshanks Gallery, where they sat round a horse-shoe table, admirably presided over by an illustrious personage, and where resolutions cut and dried had been submitted to their notice. These might be divided into two classes; some were too simple for consideration at al, while others were altogether ridiculous. In fact, they were treated by the authorities at South Kensington ..as the cats that were to pull the chestnuts out of the fire.96

Cole as usual survived such criticism, and in the end all was sweetness and light. At thecoth ibiton, h eiva tal fom B jrors p him for the 'order and excellent organization manifest in the British Section', and he responded to this 'very flattering letter' with a well-turned compliment: 'Though great the ability, and extensive the experience, and untiring the zeal of the gentlemen who assisted me in my executive duties, these were more than equalled by the fortitude and patience of the British Exhibitors, an army of volunteers, exceeding three thousand in number'.97

While championing British industry, Cole maintained an internationalist outlook. How could he not, since his whole life was bound up with international exhibitions? As his position in South Kensington became assured, he relished the opportunity to take part in international cultural exchanges, and sometimes to inaugurate international projects of his own. One such was the Universal Catalogue of Books on Art, at which John Hungerford Pollen was labouring away in South Kensington. Another was triumphantly clinched at the French International Exhibition, taking advantage of the presence in Paris of many of the crowned heads of Europe. This was a scheme for the exchange of reproductions of works of art.

Cole believed in the usefulness, for museums and art education, of reproductions, alongside genuine works of art. Reproductions in three dimensions, such as plaster casts and electrotypes, and in two dimensions, such as photographs, lithographs and etchings, were acquired by the museum and circulated to the art schools. To be able to study reproductions was especially helpful when the originals were far away from London. Getting hold of reproductions usually meant diplomatic negotiations, followed up with expeditions by craftsmen. One of the museum's biggest campaigns had been in 1864-5, when a cast had been made of the great Portico de la Gloria at Santiago de Compostella. 'The casting of this immense structure was an operation involving a sea voyage beset by numerous hazards - storms and fumigation against cholera included  as well as protracted and delicate negotiations with the ecclesiastical authorities.'98 It occurred to Cole that such projects would be easier if some kind of international co-operation could be established, and in 1864 he proposed an 'International Exchange of Copies of Works of Fine Art', a proposal that, with Lord Granville's help, was circulated worldwide by the British Foreign Office.

At the Paris exhibition, Cole had made sure that the South Kensington Museum was well publicised. He exhibited not only examples of the decorations used on the buildings, but also reproductions of works of art commissioned by the museum.

These were exhibited in the British section, 'where may be seen specimens of French, Italian, Spanish, Portuguese, German, Swiss, Russian, Hindoo, Celtic, and English art'. It occurred to Cole that he could give his scheme for the exchange of reproductions a boost by getting it ratified by the international celebrities who were in Paris at the time. Having drafted a 'Convention', he rushed round to the British Embassy on 3o May and got it signed, for Britain, by Prince Alfred. The next day, Princess 'Vicky', crown princess of Prussia, promised to obtain the crown prince's signature, which was added on 1 June. Rather embarrassingly the crown prince's signature took up the space intended for that of Prince Napoleon, but he too signed on 24 June. Some 'members of the reigning families throughout Europe' signed, representing Britain, France, Prussia, Hesse, Belgium, Russia, Sweden and Norway, Italy, Austria and Denmark. Each country was supposed to set up a commission to expedite exchanges. It does not appear that the grand plan proceeded much farther than this, but the convention does, at one, amusing, level, illustrate Cole's tirelessly roving eye for the main chance, while, at a more serious level, it surely represents the first formal attempt at international co-operation 'for the benefit of museums of all countries.99

Associated with this project was a universal inventory of works of art. The distribution by the Foreign Office in 1864 of Cole's proposal about reproductions elicited many catalogues of foreign collections, which Cole wanted to conflate. He announced his intention in Notes for a universal art inventory of works of fine art which may be found throughout the continent of Europe, for the most part in ecclesiastical buildings and in connexion with architecture: compiled for the use of the South Kensington Museum and the Schools of Art in the United Kingdom, published for the Science and Art Department in 1867. This, after its prefatory matter, contained, as a taster, an inventory of mosaics, which was reprinted along with an inventory of stained glass in the official first volume of the inventory (187o). The second volume (goldsmith's work and enamels; and ivories) and the third (metalwork) appeared in 1876, and the fourth (woodwork and sculpture) in 1879. The latter two volumes were announced as edited by Cole but compiled by H. Lindsay Cole, i.e. by Hennie, for whom, in her dependent spinsterhood, the task doubtless provided gentle but not unintellectual occupation.

While thus trying to establish the South Kensington Museum as the centre of the artistic world, Cole had to admit that it was far from complete. He confessed, about a year later, that the development of the museum was 'imperfect', and its 'destination ... somewhat ill defined'. Industrial art was well covered. Medieval and Renaissance art objects were abundant, and could easily be justified. 'It was found the British Museum rejected all the Art-objects which were not before the Christian era; so that there seemed to be something like a logical reason for taking charge [at South Kensington] of Art-objects after the Christian era. Accordingly a collection by various good fortune had been made.' But were the various other collections still appropriate? The education collection? The food collection ("which I hope will be removed')? The animal products collection ("which I think has no logical connexion with . . . the Kensington Museum')? Of the Patent Museum Cole opined that it 'starts from the wrong point of view'. On the other hand, he was keen on the construction collection, and on the newly arrived collection of ship models.

Perhaps Cole was feeling jaundiced on the day he made this judgement on the museum. He was appearing before a commission investigating whether Ireland should have its own independent Department of Science and Art, instead of enjoying the services provided by Cole's department. For once, Cole was defending the status quo (I am too much of a Conservative, particularly in Dublin, to attempt to revolutionize the present system, which in my opinion is working well), and so may have been unusually aware of its weaknesses. At any rate, he conceded that

SouKengon is a vey haphazar stiuti. It is lookeatrathe  the lgh pirate treading upon the toes of a great number of other institutions; and although, in the main, it has done things which other institutions have not done, it does not furnish, I should say, a logical model for the rection  anything that you would say ought to be like Tehhia

De i  Col antu  rivainheol few years, as his official career moved towards its close, he exerted himself to shore it up against further accidents and against assaults from those who wanted to dismantle it.

The Paris exhibition of 1867 gave Cole an opportunity to blow Britain's trumpet, but, as in 18 5 1, Britain's trumpet was felt to have been drowned by the clarion calls of other nations. Once again, Britain's products made a poor showing. 'As interpreted by Lyon Playfair and John Scott Russell, this reflected inadequate technical training of the industrial classes".101 In 185 1 better training in art had seee t be the answer. Now, better training in science was required. Most histories of English education see the upturn in scientific training in Britain as beginning here; the previous efforts of Cole's department are seen only as a small-scale prologue.102 The first step taken by the government was the 'Samuelson Committee', a parliamentary select committee (to which Cole and Donnelly testified) 'on the provisions for giving Instruction in Theoretical and Applied Science to the Industrial Classes', which sat in 1867-8, under the chairmanship of the industrialist, Bernhard Samuelson. It was soon followed by the 'Devonshire Commission', a royal commission 'on Scientific Instruction and the Advancement of Science' under the duke of Devonshire, which conducted wide-ranging investigations in 187o-75. Cole and Donnelly again testified, describing what South Kensington had already done, and urging the foundation of a central school for training science teachers at South Kensington.103

While Cole dutifully appeared at such enquiries, it was his younger colleague, Donnelly, who led the drive to expand the scientific side of the Department of Science and Art, with T. H. Huxley, a professor in the department, lending reverberant support.104 Cole was personally involved in negotiating a system of scholarships administered by the department and funded by his friend Sir Joseph Whitworth,105 but apart from this gave Donnelly a free hand. When he did speak in support, he was willing to adopt a characteristically trenchant tone. On one occasion, when pressed to agree that the study of Greek was more worthwhile than the study of science, he fiercely supported science, remarking: 'I think that in the state of the prejudice of the world for the Greeks, of whom, Heaven knows, we know very little after all, it is necessary that unpopular opinions should occasionally be expressed on the other side. I h  I for it f necessary. 104

Issues such as these would have re-occupied Cole's mind as he returned from Paris in late 1867 and resumed his busy round at South Kensington. He paused on Christmas Day, noting that 'at breakfast according to custom, each plate was covered with Xmas presents from one to the other', but was at work again on Boxing Day, which was foggy, causing numbers at the museum to drop. On Saturday 28 December, he briefly entertained the memory of 'our Wedding day', 34 years previously at St Martin's-in-the-Fields, turned back to 'Correspondence. Office. Museum. Works', and went to an operetta in the evening.

## CHAPTER I3 Supreme at South Kensington 1868-1874

The next peak of effort in Cole's life was to come in 1871: an International Exai Se. It re p sonal ascendancy. By then, however, the political climate was turning unfavourable, and, as he approached 6o, his energies (though not his aggressive tendencies) were running down.

In official life, Christmas 1867-8 saw the final parting of the ways with Robinson. His relations with Cole and the board got worse and worse, as he tried to assert an authority over purchases that he no longer possessed. His almost fanatical insistence was displayed in summer 1867 at a select committee set up to consider purchases from the Paris exhibition. Cole wanted to buy examples of contemporary design for the museum, but the department was already in bad odour for the high costs of the exhibition. So he resorted to a tried and tested ploy, a select committee of the House of Commons.1 A committee was established on 20 June, spent only two days taking evidence, on 28 June and 9 July, and quickly produced a report on 1 1 July, granting Cole £25,ooo. When Cole appeared before it on 9 July, he found 'all the members very friendly'. The other witnesses looked like a hand-picked bunch of Cole supporters: Redgrave, Donnelly and T. H. Huxley from the department; John Webb, the dealer; A. W. Franks and Reginald Stuart Poole from the British Museum; James Fergusson, the architectural historian; C. F. Hayward of the R.I.B.A; and J. C. Robinson. All testified in a way helpful to Cole, except for the last.

It is true that Robinson supported a special purchase grant for exhibition material, but his reasons, provided both in a written submission and in oral testimony, constituted an attack on Cole. A special grant was needed, argued Robinson, because the museum's annual purchase grant must not be used on modern material. It was his responsibility, he insisted (in heroic defiance of the true circumstances), to decide how the annual grant was to be spent, and he believed that it was 'vastly more important' to buy historic than contemporary art, 'because every year works of ancient art will become scarcer and scarcer, and indeed the time will come when they will be withdrawn from the market'.² Cole slipped over from Paris to appear as the last witness to the Committee, and Robinson was just ahead of him on the stand. Cole could not have been too pleased as he listened to Robinson's diatribe.3

By the end of the year, Robinson had fatally undermined himself. The Treasury staffing report of 1865 provided a means of getting rid of him. It had proposed (doubtless for reasons of economy) that the department should have on its permanent staff only generalist administrators, and that when it wanted particular expertise in science and art, it should buy in experts temporarily. Thus a salaried art referee (which was Robinson's position) was now anomalous and should be abolished. In setting out their intentions in a minute of 23 December $\mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 6 7 , { } ^ { 4 }$ the board could not refrain (though perhaps they should have) from criticising the wilful conduct of 'an Art Referee' on foreign purchasing expeditions. Anyway, Robinson's official ties with the department were cut. Cole heard from Macleod that Robinson 'thought it impossible to agree with me & was resigned to going'. Is there a hint here that Cole and Robinson had fought each other to a standstill? Cole, too, was thinking of going. He would be 60 on 1 5 July 1868, and on 5 December 1867 was asking about his pension prospects. However, he was still pretty keen to accept his friends' advice when they 'dissuaded me from Resigning as long as I was well'.5

In the official business with which Cole was concerned, another troubling matter was the Universal Catalogue of Books on Art, on which John Hungerford Pollen was working. The project got under way on 12 October 1865.6 The catalogue was compiled chiefly by copying entries from catalogues of existing libraries, notably the British Museum Library, the Bodleian at Oxford, and the library at South Kensington. But Cole wanted it to make waves in the international art community, so he recruited a 'Committee of Advice', which included 83 British scholars and 35 from abroad.7 He also aimed to publish the work provisionally and serially in some organ where it would be widely noticed and would elicit corrections and contributions from scholars in the field. Consequently it was arranged with The Times that instalments of the catalogue would appear in the advertisement supplement of that newspaper, at a total cost of £ 5,ooo, which soon rose to $\scriptstyle \mathcal { L } 6 , _ { 4 } 8 0 . ^ { 8 }$ Proof entries of the catalogue would thus come before the eyes of The Times' 65,ooo readers around the world, so it always seemed to Cole the best way of publishing.

Pollen started on letter A, working for 6d per entry.9 That he found the task somewhat harassing is suggested by a reproachful, if guarded, comment in his annual report for 1866: 'The discouragements, the adventures, shifts, and resources encountered or resorted to in the prosecution of the work would make up a history amusing buut plac the pas.st bat nr ulyper The Times on 8 May, but then politics intervened. On 1 3 May questions were asked in the House of Commons by Mr L. L. Dillwyn, followed by William Gregory and Beresford-Hope.'l In response to charges 'that the South Kensington department was playing into the hands of the "Times", ministers gave an assurance that the 'experiment' of publication in The Timnes would be suspended after the second instalment. On 17 May Parliament called for all the papers relating to the catalogue to be put before the House, and they were disclosed in a parliamentary paper published on I3 June. This happened while Cole was in Paris for the International Exhibition of 1867. Here he had made every effort to publicise the work of his department and its museum, and the first portion of the Universal Catalogue was exhibited among the museum's publications in Group 2, Class 6, number 44, with a brief essay by Pollen in the catalogue of the International Exhibition. When the jury awards were given, Pollen was 'very cock-a-hoop', as he wrote home, because 'my Universal Art Catalogue gets a Gold medal! 12 (In fact, the award  hors concours because Pollen and other members of the department were jurors and could not give themselves medals - was for the museum's whole display of publications, and not just Pollen's cgueThus while he proj increi ra i ws mom.

Since The Times was now proscribed, other ways of publishing were investigated. Eventually the catalogue resumed in the antiquarian monthly, Notes and Queries,13 where entries, starting from the beginning of A, began to appear in four-page supplements on 4 January 1868. The project advanced slowly, and Cole became edgy. Told Pollen', he recorded on 25 April 1868, I cd not be responsible for the present cost of Art Catalogue. Pointed out extravagant cost of corrections & compilation. Pollen was vidently tyig to etsgt every bok he atagued wih mable bibliographical professionalism  but Cole thought that 'examination of books must be postponed'. A month later, on 27 May, Cole chivvied Pollen again: 'told him he must deliver all his material of Art Catalogue to the office, so that it may be examined and checked'. Cole and his ministers were worrying about the imminent submission of their estimates to the scrutiny of Parliament, and their anxiety was justified.

When the estimates came before the Commons on 4 June, there were complaints about excessive expenditure, and Gregory moved that the grant for the catalogue be reduced from £2,500 to £1,000.14 Though this was defeated, it was embarrassing, as was the admission that the estimated total cost of the catalogue had gone up to £8,0oo. Cole set about investigating cheaper means of publication, evidently concluding that the department could no longer afford the luxury of printing the catalogue in proof in instalments so as to elicit help from the learned world. He found that it would be cheaper to have the thing printed in one go by the Stationery Office, and made moves to implement this change. In acting thus, he offended his minister.

The lord president of the council, in Lord Derby's third (Conservative) administration, was the duke of Marlborough. He had no special distinction as a politician, and must have owed his office in part to his social position. But he was robust enough to say no to Cole from time to time, and could be 'nasty'.15 On 1 1 July 1868, he rebuked Cole for going beyond his powers. He considered that it was for him, as minister, to make the decisions in response to pressure from MPs, and 'it was an improper act in a subordinate to communicate with them or take any action upon their views'. Having put Cole in his place, he loftily said that he would make no written record of his reproof, but Cole took care to make a private note of it, in which he also quoted his own reply: I said that during my offcial career of more than forty years I had never received such an admonition.'16

There then seems to have been a stand-off until the Conservative government fell in December 1868. Gladstone came in, appointing Lord de Grey and Ripon as president and W. E. Forster as vice-president. On 8 January 1869, Cole got them to agree to tell Notes and Queries, where the catalogue had hardly begun on letter C, that publication there was ended, and the Stationery Office would take over. However, there must have been contractual obligations to Notes and Queries, with whom protracted correspondence ensued through 1869. In the end, letters A to C, H to K and V to Z appeared through Notes and Queries, while D to G and M to U were published by the Stationery Office. The whole was eventually consolidated into two volumes, the first (A to K) issued in January 187o, and the second following in April 1870.

The publication of the catalogue prompted Mr. Dillwyn, an inveterate critic of South Kensington, to ask more questions in the Commons about the costs. Total expenditure had been £8,304, and it was expected that only £500 could be recouped by sales. 17 The project was, then, far from a shining example of the kind of administrative efficiency on which Cole prided himself. Doubtless Pollen was partly at fault on 5 June 187o, Cole was still grumbling about 'Pollen's rigmarole on Art Catalogue'  but no one emerges with credit. All the same, the Universal Catalogue, when it finally appeared, was a genuinely worthwhile achievement. And, as often in Cole's life, personal disagreements were later reconciled. When Cole and his critic William Gregory dined together on 3 1 December 187 1, just before Gregory departed to be governor of Ceylon, they 'had a laugh' about the Universal Catalogue.

Another piece of business that involved the Conservative ministers was the foundation of the Bethnal Green Museum, which opened in 1872 and has ever since been a branch of the South Kensington (later the Victoria and Albert) Museum. This project arose from the gradual enlargement of the main museum's buildings in South Kensington. By 1864, Fowke's extensions to the 1856 building had progressed to a point where it seemed possible to dispense with the original 'Boilers', which Cole had never liked. It had at the start been claimed as a virtue in the iron-framed Boilers that they were a temporary construction which could be dismantled and re-erected elsewhere. Consequently, the department offered them for re-use in other parts of London, 'to assist in the formation of district museums'.18 Only one London district showed an interest, the deprived East End borough of Bethnal Green, which had been lobbying for a museum since 185 1.19 A committee of local philanthropists secured a site, but they were not equal to starting up their own museum, as Cole had intended. If a museum building was to be raised on their site, they asked the department to stock and run it. At a meeting at South Kensington on 6 December 1866, the Conservative ministers, the duke of Buckingham and H. T. L. Corry, acknowledged that the department's original hopes of planting museums at all points of the compass in the London suburbs had 'not been realized', and took up the proposal from BelGrhat h bihocr national property'. 20

Getting the money for this fell to the duke of Marlborough and Lord Robert Montagu (who took over after a reshuffle in the Conservative government in March 1867), and funds were voted in the 1867 session of Parliament. The critical Mr Dillwyn, always eager to cut down South Kensington's grant, protested at the funding of a branch museum, on the grounds that everyone would want one. 'If every locality was to have its collection, the expense would be gigantic.' Lord Robert Montagu had to stress that the new museum in the East End (which at this point he was honouring with the adjective 'royal') was not a local museum. 'As to the Royal Museum at Bethnal Green, he might remark that it was no more a local institution because it was in Bethnal Green than the British Museum was a local institution from the circumstances of its being situated in Bloomsbury, or than Kew Botanical Gardens because they were in Kew.' Changing his adjective, he asked why the Bethnal Green should 'not be a national museum. He was at a loss to know.'21 On that basis, the Bethnal Green Museum was founded.

The duke of Marlborough approved plans on 2 February 1868, and the contract was let in October that year.22 On 24 March 1869 Cole noted that the building had proceeded up to the ground floor. On 2 November, decorative mosaics were under ceration, and y  August 8he iling s 'nearly nishe It time to consider the unning expense the museum, whic Cole did inpreaig estimates on 20 November 1870. On the 30 December, however, came an alarming setback: 'Treasury letter repudiating Maintenance of Bethnal Green' The political climate had altered for the worse, a development which would profoundly affect Cole's life. The further history of the Bethnal Green Museum must be postponed until the changes have been described.

Meanwhile, let us look at how Cole's energies surged beyond official life into various private projects. One was hymn singing, to which he devoted a short, sharp campaign in 1868. His chief motivation must have been simply a desire to galvanise Church of England congregations into 'singing up'. Although there was a considerable revival of Anglican Church music in the nineteenth century, which introduced robed choirs in raised chancels, the motivation for this largely sprang from the Oxford Movement and the rediscovery of medieval Christian ritual.23 Cole was 'no ritualist', 24 and kept his distance from those who 'seemed to think the position of the Choir more important than the music'.25 His motives were musical, though Robert Lowe teasingly claimed that he 'expected to see me prosecuted in the Ecclesiastical Court for violating the rites of the Church!'.26

We have seen that in 18 5 3 Cole kicked up a fuss about the standard of music in St Mary Abbots church, Kensington, which he then attended. On moving nearer the museum, he and his family transferred their allegiance to Holy Trinity, Brompton. He made no complaint about the music here, and it presumably helped that the vicar - the Rev. Dr W. J. Irons, a scholarly clergyman with many publications to his name - was, among other things, an expert on hymns and editor of a hymn book, Psalms and Hymns for the Church (1875). Cole hatched a scheme with Dr Irons to hold four Tuesday evening services (on 4 February 1868, and the following three Tuesdays) at Holy Trinity, Brompton, at which special musical programmes would be provided. Cole announced these occasions on posters and handbills.27 "Workmen with their Wives and Families and the Poor are especially invited to come and take part . .. . Pew Holders are earnestly requested to waive their rights on these occasions, so that all parts of the Church may be free.' Subscriptions were collected from the well-off by Captain Festing, while the public at large was informed that 'donations of one penny and upwards in aid of the Musical Church Services will be thankfully received at the doors of the church'. Rehearsals were held in the museum Refreshment Rooms and in the headquarters of the Ist Middlesex Engineer Volunteers at College Street, Fulham Road. At the services, the organ was 'accompanied as at St. Paul's Cathedral by Drums, Trombones and Trumpets'.
