The format of the service was Evening Prayer, with an anthem by a special choir, the psalms sung to Gregorian chant, and well-known hymns - in which 'the congregation are especially requested to join'.

After four Tuesdays, this experiment ceased. The periodical The Orchestra had warned that hymn singing might not be popular, and commented that "Squire Cole' (for it felt that this activity put him in this role) 'will be called upon for renewed dash and determination' to keep it going.28 Did he lose enthusiasm? And did four church services do any good apart from as an outlet for one form of Cole's overflowing selfexpression? He kept in his Miscellanies a parodic ballad (printed on a long sheet with woodcuts at the head), which tells how the 'King of Brompton' (thus promoted from mer 'Squire chivvies the vicar, along with his own family and staff, into taking part in the services. It ends:

But, as I mingled with the throng,

I heard a cynic say —,

'How many come to hear the song?

'How many come to pray?

Two more causes that Cole took up were pursued through the Society of Arts. One of these was army reform, which caught his attention in summer 1868. Ever since the disasters of the Crimean War, it had been obvious that the army needed reform, but it is not clear quite why Cole took the issue up at this time. Undeniably, the question was in the air: 'at present our army organization is in a state of worse than change - it is expectant of change'.29 This comment was made by a reviewer of Sir Charles Trevelyan's pamphlet The British army in 1868,30 which Cole read on 4 August. Expectations of change were soon fulflled, when Gladstone's government came in at the end of the year, and the secretary for war, Edward Cardwell (who had been Cole's minister at the Board of Trade in the early '5os), embarked upon a far-reaching programme of army reform. Cole spent a lot of time reading and writing about army reform during his summer holidays, and in the autumn published three letters in The Times over the pseudonym 'Lee Cromwell'.31 The ideas here expressed were presented in more coherent form in a speech that Cole gave to the Society of Arts on 19 February 1869.

His title indicates his approach: 'On the efficiency and economy of a national army, in connection with the industry and education of the people'. He saw the army in a wide social context, and also in an economic context: his main point was that a better army 'is not only compatible with, but ... can only be secured through, greatly reduced expenditure'. He thought that having a large standing army consisting of 'the scum of our population . . . the outcasts and roughs of the people' was inefficient. A smaller army, professionally trained rather than selected as cannon-fodder, would be better; and he held up as a shining example the Royal Engineers. 'Whether for officers or privates, you know with certainty that you can obtain a competent man for almost any kind of work in the Royal Engineers.' Alongside the permanent army, the rest of the male population should be ready to enlist in emergencies. Drill should be 'part of national education in every boys' school in the country', and, after they left school, able-bodied youths should serve as conscripts for a time, or should join the volunteers, in which they should remain for perhaps 2o years, with occasional training. Cole insisted that many industrialists 'in the North of England . . . assure me that some such system would in no way interfere with the labour market and the operatios of labour, but greatly benefit them both' Furthermore, he argued that the money saved by his scheme, which would cost only half what the existing army cost, could be "appropriated to national objects more productive than war, such as the education of the people, and the promotion of those things that advance Arts, Manufactures, and Commerc He saw the army, then, as serving the ends o civilisaton.

This broaannhihil  aw o ehing more peestrian:thetechingdril inschools.Coleganival prioT of Arts was successful and discussion went on through two further meetings of what ub t o sure from General Grey, the Queen's private secretary, to back away from army reform. As he later recalled, he 'promised Gen: Gray not to agitate more as he particularly requested me not to do so, because my Influence w[oul]d be damaged' 35 Army reform was anathema to the commander-in-chief, who, since he happened to be the duke of Cambridge, Queen Victoria's cousin, enjoyed royal support. So Gray's warning meant that if Cole continued to lobby for this cause, he would forfeit the favour f the Queen. Cole let the subject drop.

A thircaue that he took up in the late 18 was 'The London Cab Questin', a it was known in the Society of Arts, where, as usual, Cole first ventilated his thoughts. On 3 1 January 1867, he gave an extempore speech, asserting that 'we had in London the meanest cabs in the world', and seeking suggestions for improvement. His own suggestion was a version of free trade: London cabs had been compelled since 1853 to observe a fixed tariff of fares, and Cole argued that if they were allowed to set their own prices, they would invest more in providing a better service. In discussion the point was also made that they were disadvantaged by the government tax that they had to pay.37

This tax was removed in the budget of 1869, which prompted the society to take up the cause again. In May 1869, a deputation went to see the home secretary, led by the chairman of council. He urged a change in the cab licensing law on the grounds (doubtless the resonant phrase was drafted for him by Cole) that 'the principles upon which the cab system in London were based were utterly unsound, and diametrically opposed to the first truths of political economy'. 38 Having thus impressed its view on the government, the society called a conference, attended by many cab-drivers and chaired by Cole. Recalling his earlier discourse, Cole remarked that 'many of the views which he then advocated had since received pretty generally the sanction of public opinion'. A detailed and fascinating discussion ensued of fare zones, different classes of cabs, cab-stands, and licensing.39 The society's lobbying for free trade in cabs met with success in the Metropolitan Public Carriages Act of 1869, which allowed cab proprietors to fix their own prices. But (comments the historian of the London cab), 'results were disappointing, and in March 1871 the flexible fare scheme was abandoned, having failed to meet its objective'.40

Besides lobbying, the society tried to improve cabs by offering in 1869 a series of prize medals for better cab designs  an initiative that, they later had to admit, 'has not proved fruitful'.41 Undeterred, they offered cash prizes to the same end in 1871, but out of 16 entries none was judged worthy of a prize.42 Cab designs did gradually improve in the 187os, and the society's competitions may have helped by drawing attention to some of the faults that needed to be corrected.43 Nonetheless, Cole's efforts with 'The Cab Question' were less successful than he might have wished.

In the summer of 1868 the Cole family decamped, for the months of August, September and some of October, to Darley Dale, where they enjoyed the company of Whitworth and explored Derbyshire. Cole also improved the moment by giving lessons to his younger children - on Shakespeare's As You Like It, on Phaedrus (the author of the fables, presumably), on architecture, even on the army, which was preoccupying him at the time.44 Hardly was this summer break over than Cole set off on what was almost another holiday, though officially it was a business trip. Cole and Scott had obtained the board's approval on 28 July to visit 'Malta, Palermo, Venice, &c. for purpose of seeing architectural adaptation of mosaics'.45

It is not clear why they needed to do this at this juncture, for mosaic had already been much used in the decoration of the museum, and there was no shortage of experts on the history of mosaic in Cole's circle: Matthew Digby Wyatt and A. H. Layard had both published on the subject.46 But Cole had recently been compiling, with Hennie and others, the first part (published in 1867) of the Universal Inventory of Works of Art, which was devoted to mosaics. He would therefore have enhanced his knowledge of where the best examples were to be found; and presumably he also scanned much of the literature listed in the bibliography for this part of the Inventory (published in the 187o edition, pp. xxi-xxiv). Scott, perhaps, was anticipating the use of mosaic on the Royal Albert Hall exterior, and wanted to look at precedents. At any rate, Cole and Scott, together with Tishy and Isabella Cole, Samuel Redgrave, Mrs Scott and a Mr Roberts, who was a friend of Scott, set off on 9 October for a nine-week foreign tour.

Crossing the Channel on 9 October from Folkestone, they advanced towards Italy through France and Switzerland. Then over the Alps via the Splügen pass - 'most remarkable Engineering especially on South side. Very Sharp Curves: Immense blocks of rocks at Sides of valley'  and on through North Italy, arriving in Venice on Sunday 18 October. As so often happened to English travellers in the Victorian period, they kept meeting their compatriots: Frederick Poynter and his wife at Basel (on 1 1 October), the Scott Russells and their daughter Cherry at Zurich (the next day), Charles Trevelyan in Venice (on 18 October) and G. H. Lewes with 'Miss Evans' in Naples (on 8 November).

At Venice, the party was met by Layard, who guided Cole and Scott round the mosaics in St Mark's and elsewhere, and took them to Salviati's works in Murano. On 23 October they moved on to Padua, and spent a day sightseeing there. They examined the Arena Chapel, and Cole noted in his diary that he wanted 'to buy it with the view of preserving it'. He acted on this audacious ambition, writing to Layard to suggest "apparently in all seriousness'  as a modern art historian incredulously writes - 'that Layard and he should approach Count Gradenigo of Venice with an offer to purchase the Arena Chapel . . . and remove it, Giottos and all, to South Kensington!.47 Cole and Scott then moved on to Bologna for a few days. From here they made a day trip on 26 October to Ravenna, where Cole found the mosaics 'much finer than the later Byzantine in Venice' From Bologna they proceeded on the 29th to Florence, where they stayed with the dealer William Blundell Spence, an old friend of Cole from his visits in 1859 and 1861. Spence now occupied the Villa Medici in Fiesole, where guests could lounge upon the terraces, 'looking down on every side, on what is thought to be the garden of the world, with Florence and its uul hsacTyF were spent in revisiting the major sites. It seems that the ladies of the party then stayed in Florence, while Cole, Scott and Redgrave went on via Perugia and Assisi (which Cole found 'most picturesque, but lonely & desolate') to Rome, and, after a single day mosaic-hunting, proceeded to Naples. A week's stay there included a visit to see Roman mosaics at Pompeii on 1o November. Then came the voyage to

Palermo (id not sleep well, tho'not ick, where the mosaic hunt scoured the Capella Reale, the Martorana, and the cathedral at Monreale.

The mosaic team returned via Naples to Rome, and put in a couple of days' conscientious research before being joined by the ladies on 23 November. On the 29th they tried to visit the Sistine Chapel, but Cole was 'nable to get in a frock ct They saw the pope passing by, and an 'English priest pulled me to make me kneel'. From Rome the party returned via Spoleto to Florence for another week there, and then started the homeward journey via Milan and Turin to France, and through Macon and Paris to London. On crossing the Channel they met Lord Robert Montagu. He might have been expected to remember that, as Cole's minister, he had authorised Cole to take his official trip, but he breezily accosted Cole with the question, 'Had a trip to Paris?' When Cole told him 'I had been to Palermo to see Mosaics he asked "Have you brought any?"

Cole had, in fact, been looking at mosaics with a view to obtaining copies for the use of the museum and art schools. He compiled a journal of the expedition  a businesslike record in words and sketches of works of art and ornament seen, but enlivened with two little watercolour sketches of Vesuvius erupting.49 He and Scott submitted a report, describing their 'plan for illustrating in the South Kensington Museum the history of the art of mosaic working, and for supplying by the illustrations thus obtained part of the decorations of the new buildings'. They recommended that paper facsimiles should be made, and 'think there will be no great difficulties in finding competent persons to undertake the work', suggesting that the firm of Salviati should be commissioned,50 as indeed they were.51 Cole also submitted Notes of objects in Italy suitable for reproduction by various methods'.52 During the Italian trip, Cole took the opportunity to make purchases for the museum: at Florence (an 'Urbino Table' on 3 I October), Naples (a 'silver table top' from the dealer Castellani on 9 November), Rome (27 November) and Milan (from the dealer Bastini on 9 December). Among the 5o or so items that he bought were Hispano-Moresque vases, a maiolica inkstand, bronze mortars, pietra dura portraits, a marriage tray, and the 'leather scabbard for Caesar Borgia's sword'.53

The breadth of Cole's artistic sympathies can be measured by the fact that he was at this time trying to boost the museum's coverage not only of Byzantine art but also of Indian art, especially Indian architecture. Again one asks why he turned to that subject at this moment. Indian decorative art had been highly esteemed at the museum from its earliest days, when it purchased much contemporary Indian material from the Great Exhibition, and at South Kensington this material was exhibited in a gallery specially decorated by Owen Jones in Indian style. But the subject does not seem to have concerned Cole much until the mid-186os. His eyes might, perhaps, have been opened to Indian sculpture and architecture by the published work on Indian architecture of James Fergusson (who had lectured at South Kensington in 1857 and had been manager for a couple of years of the rival Crystal Palace in Sydenham) and particularly by a lecture given by Fergusson at the Society of Arts on 19 December 1866. Leading off in the question session after the lecture, Cole remarked that 'his own impression was that they knew very little indeed of India, excepting what they read in price currents of grey shirtings, twills, cotton twist, indigo, &c.; of the people of India they knew very little; and of their architecture still less'. He went on to show, however, that he himself was not so ignorant as this might suggest, when he announced that it was planned to exhibit photographs of Indian architecture in the 1867 Paris exhibition. (They appeared, oddly enough, in the history of labour section.) He also advocated that plaster casts should be made of Indian sculpture and architecture.54

It seems altogether likely that what stimulated Cole's interest in India was that Harry had been there since late 1865. Cole put up a proposal, based on a memorandum expertly drafted by Fergusson, to his board for making casts of Indian architecture, through the good offices of the India Office and using British government servants working in India.5s Not surprisingly, the chosen agents were to be Harry and the Royal Engineers. Equally predictably, shady dealing was suspected. On 29 July 1868, Cole was challenged by Sir Erskine Perry (an old India hand, once chief justice in Bombay) 'who asked Would it not be thought a job if Harry was applointe]d to the Architectural Casting. I asked why? If it was the cheapest public arrangement. & he had no answer'. The most splendid fruit of Harry's efforts was the cast of the massive, 3o-foot-high gateway of the Sanchi Tope (burial mound). 'Twenty-eight tons of materials, chiefly plaster-of-Paris ... were drawn by bullocks one hundred and eighty miles; and in a year's time three full-sized casts of the magnificent structure were completed without a flaw  which is marvellous, considering the extremely fine and complicated character of the carvings.'56 One of these casts dominated first the Cast Courts and then the Indian galleries of the South Kensington Museum until it was smashed up in the 1950s.57

The South Kensington Museum was thus impelled to reach out towards India by Cole, assisted by Harry, who in 1874 published a Catalogue of the Objects of Indian Art already belonging to the museum. This, he claimed in an 'Introductory Notice', was the first work to attempt a general account of Indian art. India then reached out to the museum. The East India Company's museum collection, accommodated from 1869 in the newly built India Office in Whitehall, was in search of a better home. In 1874 it moved to the Eastern Galleries at South Kensington, galleries built by the 185 1 commissioners for a series of exhibitions in 1871-4. In 1879 this Indian collection was handed over to the South Kensington Museum, where it was re-arranged. 'And lo! London awaked one morning to find that it had a new and splendid institution . .. . There is no university in the world where one can learn so much about India.58

These developments occurred after Cole's retirement in 1873, but he paved the wy h  .e s Near Eastern art, putting up to his board on 8 May 1868 a proposal from A. H. Layard that Briticonsuls hould b t to wor colleting in Per n Trk: 'not . . . expensive curiosities, but such common objects as illustrate the habits and life of the people, and their tastes and skill in manufacture'.

## \* \*\*

Although Cole at the age of sixty was contemplating retirement, his energy and breadth of vision seem to have been unimpaired. But at the end of 1868 the Conrvativ vent l n Gtoerst isty ook ve For hxt five years 'science and art', as Cole was later to put it, were 'like the toad under the harrow'.61 Gladstone believed in reducing public expenditure, and this policy was fiercely implemented by his Chancellor of the Exchequer, Robert Lowe. It was surprising that Lowe made a comeback into the cabinet. After the controversy over his alleged 'mutilation' of his inspectors' reports in 1864, he had retired, discomfited though not in disgrace, to the backbenches, where he might have remained. But he returned to prominence in the debates on the Second Reform Bill of 1867. To this he was implacably opposed, on what can only be regarded as élitist grounds. As 'the orator of a crisis of anger, distrust and fear',62 he became unofficial leader of the opposition campaign and alienated many of his Liberal colleagues. Nonetheless, he emerged from his defeat over this issue with his credit as a parliamentarian so enhanced that Gladstone took him back into government. Lowe had remarked in a letter: 'I wish they would make me Chancellor of the Exchequer. I think I possess the faculty for saying No as well as anyone, and in that and not in super finance lies the real secret of financial prosperity.'63 He got his wish, doubtless because Gladstone needed someone who liked saying 'No'.

At first Lowe seemed pleasant enough to Cole. The man who had agreed with him (on 17 August 1865) that the control of the British Museum must be wrested from its trustees seemed still to be of the same mind on 27 February 1869: 'was in favour of passing ... Brit Mus: over to the Government'. When, on the 4 January 1869, 'Lowe . . . hoped I wd keep my Estimates Lowe', no doubt both he and Cole chuckled sympathetically at the joke. But Lowe meant it with a vengeance. When the Department of Science and Art went to the Treasury to discuss their estimates on 8 April, they had a nasty surprise. 'Mr Forster said Lowe had altered our Estimates without consultation & if it were allowed Treasury must conduct the Department.' Protests were of no avail. On 13 April the 'Estimates were printed & issued with Lowe's changes. Everybody angry.' On 28 April, Cole's former Conservative minister, the duke of Marlborough, rubbed salt in the wound: 'said Disraeli wd not cut Art down like Lowe'.

By 25 June Lowe had stopped all building works at South Kensington: 'Mr Forster . . . said he cd Sympathize with my Agony at the stoppage'. Next day Forster angrily protested at Lowe's penny-pinching - 'Lowe wd make economy stink in the nostrils' - and on 1o August, supported by Lord de Grey, he stiffened his resolve to 'get all he cd out of the Treasury'. Cole and Forster laboured over the next year's estimates in December 1869. De Grey and Forster tried to stand up for the department: 'both stiff about the Treasury, & its authority', Cole noted on 17 March 1870. There were occasional successes: on 25 March de Grey sought out Gladstone himself to protest 'about reducing our purchase vote' and 'came back triumphant not to be reduced'. But there was no victory over Lowe's biggest threat, which had come on 3 1 October 1869, when he decreed that the building work at South Kensington was henceforward to be under the control of the Office of Works. There were strenuous negotiations in the early months of 1 870.64 It was even thought, on 24 February, that the Queen might intervene on behalf of South Kensington. But it was no use. The Office of Works did take over. In his annual report, dated 3 1 March 187o, Cole had to make the best of it, exerting such pressure as he could on the Office of Works. The museum was now terribly crowded, he claimed; there was no room to display new donations; readers in the library had to stand. 'I venture to submit the opinion', he pronounced portentously, but rather hollowly, that it is of great public urgency that the New Buildings should proceed at a much greater rate than heretofore.'65

This was not the end of Lowe's repression. What particularly rankled with the Department of Science and Art was that Lowe had once supported them. In the early 186os, when a spending minister as vice-president of the council, Lowe had encouraged Cole to grab as much money as he could from the Treasury. Now that he was chancellor, it was a different story: 'Lowe of '69 abused Lowe of 6o', as Forster remarked.66 Or, as Lord de Grey said, when Lowe reversed his position on purchasing the Castellani collection, 'Lowe's actions in 63 wd not affect Lowe in 1870'.67 Lowe was prepared ruthlessly to change his mind, and his allegiances. His former permanent secretary, G. E. Hamilton, after Lowe had parted with him, told Cole that 'Lowe's characteristic feature was Treachery'.68

Lowe was offending people right and left. Some felt that he was simply malign. A lady told Cole (on 18 August 187 1) that 'Lowe had said in her presence that "he did not care if England became a fourth rate power, provided his Exchequer was full". Cole's former minister, H. A. Bruce, said that 'Lowe erected brick walls to knock his head against'.69 Others tried to detect in his methods an intellectual and political logic. Walter Bagehot wrote subtly: 'The quality of his mind is to put everything in the most lively, most exciting, and most startling form . . . . The middle principles, in which most minds feel most reality and on which they find it most easy to rest . . . have no charms for him. He likes to go back to the bone, to the abstract.'70 A somewhat less respectful newspaper article, attributing to Lowe 'a keen, forcible narrow mind, a scornful and cynical temper, and a faculty of epigrammatic expression', regretted that he reasons rigidly to results which offend common sense and a larger reason'71 Whatever the quality of his reasoning capacity, Lowe's political stance (some people thought) must have sprung from 'a fixed prejudice against expenditure so strong as to overrule his sense of Ministerial subordination, and even Ministerial decency. When Parliament differs from him, Parliament is in the wrong, and passive resistance becomes his duty.'72

The Department of Science and Art struggled to make headway against this oppressor. As estimates were prepared on 24 March 1871, Forster 'observed that it must be seen that we do not give in', and the next day he promised that 'when Ld de Grey returns we will have it out with the Treasury'. But by now Cole's personal relations with Lowe had come into the equation. Lowe was heard to say on I 5 April that there was 'internecine war' between them. And Scott told Cole that 'Gladstone wi Low  the at'n r ea  th  when Coe w pondering retrement again, Forster was able to rouse him by suggesting that 'Lowe might jump at getting rid of me'.73

Lowe's opposition hit the department hard where the Bethnal Green Museum was concerned. By the end of 1870, the new museum building, begun with the support of the Conservatives, was almost ready to open. But on 3 December 1870, when the Liberals were back in power, Cole received a 'Treasury letter repudiating Maintenance of Bethnal Green'. Although a month later, on 31 January 1871, the Treasury gave in ab[ou]t refusing to maintain Bethnal Green', Lowe stalled the project through 1871. On 21 October, Cole learned that Lowe had tried to hand back the museum to the locals who had at first clamoured for it but later entreated the department to run it. They declined the proposed gift. Lowe tried again, as Cole learned on 31 January 1872, offering the museum building to the locals as a 'Technical University', repudiating the promises of previous governments, and saying that he 'wd not give a shilling to make it one of King Cole's Hens & chickens'. Cole parleyed with the Opposition, approaching his former Conservative minister H. T. L. Corry, who 'said the Conservatives would take it up'.

The department was determined to open the museum in 1872, for on 28 December 1871 Cole found it 'getting dirty & neglected'. But the Treasury would allow only £2,ooo per year for running costs.74 Pressure was kept up as the 'Treasury shuffled', and eventually Cole learned on 26 April that $ { \mathcal { L } } _ { 4 } , 5 5  { \mathrm { o } }$ had been conceded. The atmosphere in the department must have been tense, since on the following day Redgrave pleaded with Cole 'to avoid vanquishing Lowe any more as he was so spiteful and powerful'. A royal opening was arranged. Lowe would not approve £ 5oo for this purpose, so Ripon (Lord de Grey and Ripon became marquis of Ripon in 1871) and Forster undertook to pay the costs from the department's budget, Lowe refraining from comment as he was now in passive resistance mode  'the case is not before me'.76

The museum had been filled with the food collection, the animal products collection and other material from South Kensington, which occupied the lower floors permanently, and with Sir Richard Wallace's collection (as yet without a London home), which occupied the upper floor on a temporary basis. The former collections were judged, from the perspective of fashionable London, to be appropriate for 'the long-neglected toiling masses at the East-End', while the Wallace collection rendered the museum 'worthy of a pilgrimage . . . from any distance by every lover of art'.77 A popular ballad, circulated at the opening of the museum, offered an alternative perspective:

The rich should ponder on the fact,

'Tis labour has built up,

A mountain of prodigious wealth,

And filled his golden cup,

And surely workers who have toiled,

Are worthy to behold,

Some portion of the treasures won,

And ribs of shining gold.78

Cole would have sympathised with the ballad. He would later recall that when the museum in the East End was first suggested, it was stated that the valuables would be greatly damaged by the rough people who inhabit that part of the metropolis'. But 'I trusted the poor people'. No damage was done and the museum was greatly appreciated.79

The museum was opened on 24 June 1872 by the prince and princess of Wales, with appropriate music and ceremonial. As usual this was fixed up at the last minute, Cole grumbling on 22 June that the plans were 'altering every hour'. In the event all went well at an occasion that was a personal triumph for Cole over the political system. He marched in the procession in his court suit $( \hbar g . 9 )$ . When, thus attired, he first emerged from his bedroom on the great day, his dog, Jim, was stupefied. 'Seeing me in Uniform, he stood motionless at the top of the Stairs instead of rushing down as he does every Morning.'

In the course of the preparations for the opening of the new museum in the East End, Ripon had chaffingly said to Cole, 'You must be made the Duke of Bethnal Green'. To which Cole replied, 'I can't take that title as I am engaged for another. Duke of Brompton Boilers.'80 This reminds us that Cole's chief commitment was to South Kensington, and he achieved his greatest ascendancy there in the early 1870s. The museum was never far from his thoughts. He enlarged the collections through buying trips abroad. He kept an eye on the displays to see that everything was up to scratch - for instance, complaining to Owen on 4 January 1870 about the labelling.

![](images/d34584701bd931074f2de87ed396ec6d65f67417dca782da4ecc48006afc0ad7.jpg)  
Figure 9 The royal procession at the opening of the Bethnal Green Museum in 1872, caricatured in Fun, 6 July 1872. Cole, in court dress, is on the second row, in front of the chimney sweep.

He was particularly concerned with the new buildings, until control of them was wrested from him by Lowe. The lecture theatre range, with its refreshment rooms downstairs and ceramic gallery upstairs, was reaching completion towards 1870, and in the theatre itself Cole plunged into acoustic trials in 1869. On 12 May there were audible and visible demonstrations of the varieties ofmusical pitch directed by Profesor Guthrie' on  June a trial ofvoice directed by Mr. Arthur Sullivan'and Ju tril  by Mr. El nll this the did not have particularly good acoustics, and eerily foreshadowed similar problems with the Royal Albert Hall.

It was with the Royal Albert Hall and the gardens that Cole was particularly concerned at this time. Under Scott's supervision the hall was smoothly rising. A crucial moment came on 1 1 May 1870, when the scaffolding was removed from beneath the great iron roof. Many feared it would collapse, but it settled gently and stayed firm. The Queen came on 3 December. 'Seemed satisfied with the Hall,' wrote Cole, 'but not very expressive.' As armies of carpenters and decorators, not to mention organ builders, swarmed over the hall, Cole became caught up in an absorbing design problem - the construction of seats for the body of the hall. He first worked on his 'Xperimental Chair for Hall' on 14 December, continuing on the 19th and 2oth. He and Scott tried to get into the Prince of Wales Theatre on 4 January to examine theatre seats, but were turned away. On 6, 7 and I 1 January he attended 'experiments' and 'trials' of what he described as his 'rotating chair'. Later he was to publish an account of it, now calling it a 'Roundabout Chair'.82 The rotating seats enabled the audience to swivel to face either the orchestra or the central space, and eased the passage of people along the rows. They survived until replaced by a modern version in the hall's refurbishment in 19992000.

Once the scaffolding had come down in the interior, it was possible to assess the acoustics. Cole's tame conductor, Sir Michael Costa, had professed himself 'highly satisfied with sonority of Hall' on 5 December $8 7 0 ,$ and on 17 December thought the 'sonority splendid'. But on 1 1 February 1871 Cole and Costa noticed that a distinct echo could be heard from the balcony, and by the 18th it was obvious that there was a problem: 'Echoes in Hall very curious'. The day before the opening Costa's disappointment with the sound was so great that he said he was 'broken hearted'. After the opening, Scott tried to remedy the echo, but it has always remained a flaw in the hall, even though modern acoustic devices have reduced it.

The official opening was planned for 29 March. It was a race against time to get the work finished. Cole visited on the evening of the 28th, and found 'candles burning every where', as workmen laboured through the night. The next day there was much litter and confusion, which, as Cole laconically recorded, had to be 'left so'. The ceremony went well, however. It was an important moment for Cole. He proceeded at a fast pace through life, driving on from one project to another and always seizing the main chance. There was hardly time to savour one triumph before cranking up the next. The project for a concert hall was unusual because Cole held to it over a long period. As he recorded in his diary after the opening, 'it had been my aim since $8 5 8 ^ { \ ' }$ . So it was not only the Queen who found the occasion 'émotionnant'. She noted in her diary that 'good Mr. Cole was quite crying with emotion and delight'.83

The hall, splendid as it was, had a struggle to achieve success as a venue for performances. Its situation was a disadvantage, as it still is. Although transport links between South Kensington and elsewhere are not bad, they are not well adapted to the swift assembly and dispersal of large concert audiences. The main problem for the hall sprang, however, from the financial arrangements that Cole made in order to raise enough cash to build it. To attract subscriptions, he sold many seats on 999- year leases. This meant that a concert promoter could never hope to attract a full house. Some I,3oo seats out of the total of 6,ooo were permanently reserved; and these included many of the best seats. The problem of the 'seat-holders', though mitigated by various measures in later years, haunted the hall. Its management was regularised soon after the opening. Hitherto run by a provisional committee, it was given a new constitution on 25 May 1872. The seat-holders now formed a corporation, while management was in the hands of an 18-man council. The honorary secretary of the council was John Donnelly, but paid help was brought in, in the shape of Henry Cole's nephew Wentworth Cole, who ran the hall until his death in 190o.

## \*  \* \* \*

In the weeks after the hall opened, Henry Cole was busy with a new international exhibition, sited partly in the hall itself. After the Paris exhibition of 1867, Cole was among those who thought that international exhibitions, though a good thing, were beginning to get out of hand in their size and variety. Some practices needed reform, and Cole promulgated some ideas before leaving Paris.84 Back home, he contrived to lu a his n ae patr.Th te te Sy  Ar as not involved, and Cole drew into association three other bodies with which he was concerned in South Kensington: the 18 5 I commissioners, the Royal Albert Hall provisional committee, and the Royal Horticultural Society. The commissioners were, as usual, cast in the role of funding agency, while the other two bodies were, in one way or another, suppliants for aid. Cole had always envisaged the Royal Albert Hall as both a performance space and an exhibition space. It has never managed to establish itself in the latter function (since architecturally it is only too obviously shaped for the former), but it was natural that Cole wanted to try it out as an exhibition hall. The Royal Horticultural Society's gardens were in perpetual financial crisis, and bringing them into a scheme for exhibitions seemed one way of relieving their plight. Cole conceived a scheme for inserting small annual exhibitions into the arcades that formed the eastern and western borders of the gardens, and into ranges of new, twostoried galleries built on the outer sides of the arcades. With much effort, the commissioners were induced to construct these new galleries at their expense (plate ). Cole was poised to undertake the administration of the programme of exhibitions he proposed, but the commission were wary of him by now and tried to give the job to Scott. He, however, refused to displace 'one to whom I owe so much and who has shown such generosity to me', and so Cole remained in charge as superintendent of the exhibitions, while Scott acted as secretary.86

C ntetnaeiits intomal roupshatcul b ealt wih utilitarian products were classified in a system that would last for 1o years and could the ba.alrng he harhe fistfuls as they fl.Among major themes, pottery was scheduled fr 1871, cotton for 1872, precious metals for 1876, iron and hardware for 1879, and railway plant for 188c. Here and there in the scheme were to be found toys and games (1871), cooking (1873), bookbinding (1874), electricity (1876), straw manufactures (1879) and boots and shoes (188o). The principle of these exhibitions was selection. Exhibits would be solicited, but, when submitted, they would be subject to the judgement of expert committees, and only the best would be chosen.

Among various explanations of this approach, one by Redgrave's son, Gilbert, has quite a modern consumerist ring. Publicity of all kinds, he says, 'is a special feature of the nineteenth century:. Commercial prosperity and success depends at the present day, to a very large extent, upon such publicity and display; and advertising has become quite an institution among us.' Advertising, however, is often dishonest. Exhibitions, on the other hand, should be valued by contributors as a means of reaching the truth, because they offered 'chances . . . of contrasting their works with those of their fellows, and thus finding out their defects and shortcomings'. The 1871 exhibition would assist this process of discrimination through its 'selective character'. Whereas at past exhibitions, manufacturers had sent in whatever they liked, in this exhibition 'nothing will be admitted unless it is remarkable for merit of some kind. This weeding out, if we may so term it, . . . constitutes a valuable innovation'.88

Notwithstanding this high-minded approach, concessions were made to commerce. Some exhibitors, notably the French (who insisted on having a separate building of their own), were permitted to make direct sales of material from their stalls, and all exhibitors were allowed to display prices beside their products. This caused criticism, and a demonstration by West End tradesmen protesting that their custom was being taken away. Cole was fully in favour of the display of prices, arguing that it 'seems to be entirely in accordance with the wishes and interests of the public at large', even though 'intermediate agents dislike it'. He was also in favour of direct sales at the exhibitions. 'The more the public at large learn to appreciate works of Science and Art, the more they desire to possess them, and the greater should be the facilities of purchase.'90

Disappointingly, the participation of foreigners in the 1871 exhibition was less than expected, and it was said that 'the term "international" might be almost omitted from the programme'.9"1 The arrangement of the exhibition also aroused criticism. It occupied 'the central chamber and gallery of the Albert Hall, [and] four spacious galleries on either side of the . .. Gardens, together with several adjacent buildings' - including the French annexe - and this accommodation inevitably seemed scrappy, especially when compared with 'the marvellous structure of 1851'.92 Because the exhibition was widely dispersed around the perimeter of the South Kensington site, rather than tightly packed, it proved a wearisome experience for many visitors:

When the working man and his family had got safely within the walls, he found himself lost in a perfect maze of roundabout passages, through which his wife and himself patiently tramped, each carrying a young pledge of connubial affection, and the two in combination dropping three or four more at their skirts . . . . At least one-half of the visit was occupied in trudging through the corridors.93

exhibition, causing W. E. Forster to remark that Cole was 'the only Despot that c[oul]d have made a procession walk a mile'.

Although the layout of the exhibition may have left much to be desired, its setting around the gardens (to which the Royal Horticultural Society, normally jealous of its members' privileges, gave free access to exhibition visitors) added a touch of class. The exhibition went down well with the fashionable world, and it was said that 'the main staple of the season's visitors' consisted of 'the gaily-dressed young ladies and their attendant cavaliers'.95

To provide guidance to visitors to the exhibition, Cole started a sort of small daily paper, entitled The Key. Although a new issue was printed daily  on a rotary printing machine which was itself an exhibit6  the only regularly changing content was the programme of musical concerts in the Royal Albert Hall. The rest changed weekly, or even more infrequently. In the versions printed for the exhibitions of 18724, the contents settled into an almost unchanging brief guide, but there was greater variey in 1871. At first it seemed as if The Key might be a sort of Court Circular for King Cole of South Kensington. The opening editorial, a kind of fanfare, must have been written by him.

South Kensington, incomplete as it is, has already done enough to stamp its character and insure the success of its destiny. What has been done? South Kensington has created buildings, marking a new epoch in architecture, which have been erected with unexampled economy, at a cost of three-quarters of a million pounds sterling. South Kensington has established gardens which Lord Chancellor Bacon would have approved; it has instituted a new Museum for the Arts and Sciences which millions consult and frequent, and to which is allied more than a thousand schools and classes for teaching the sciences and the arts bearing on productive industry, counting their hundred thousand students and more in all parts of the United Kingdom.97

The 1871 exhibition was a social and financial success. Some 1,142,154 people attended, and gross receipts were £76,433. 16. 11, of which £17,671. 3. 1. was prof-T   u, but administering them continued to be a significant part of Cole's work. At the same time, he did contrive to take more periods away from his desk, refreshing himself with family holidays and official trips abroad.

His family holiday in 1870, extending from 13 August to 26 September, was taken on the Celtic fringe. First the Coles went to the mid-Wales spa town of Llandrindod Wells, where, on 19 August, Cole visited the Pump House and 'drank some saline water for the first time'. Taking the waters soon became a habit. There were walks in the countryside and picnics, while Cole occupied his mind with drafting policies and rules for the 1871 exhibition, and following developments in the Franco-Prussian War. After a few days visiting Whitworth's factory in Manchester, Cole rejoined his family at Shrewsbury, and on 12 September they moved on to stay at Church Stretton, which was conveniently placed for healthy walks up the Long Mynd. Cole and Marian had a room with a charming view, but 'my snoring so disturbed Marian that I obtained her kind permission to take Hennie's room on Wednesday night'. Cole spent some time, on 14 and 15 September, in revising an article by Harry on

Kashmir architecture (and, back in London on the 28th, discussed with Harry the placing of the great plaster cast of the Sanchi gate). Scott came to stay at Church Stretton, and, after he and Cole had examined the Roman remains of Viroconium at Wroxeter on 2o September, 'got the idea of making light bricks' Marian and Rose left for London on 23 September, and Cole made an expedition to Whitworth's house at Stancliffe, where, revisiting after an interval of two years, he admired the landscaping ('a good deal of undoing, & cutting the Rocks about') and the 'little pigs' which were Whitworth's latest enthusiasm.

As well as this family holiday, Cole paid a visit to Spain in April. The official purpose of this and most of his other foreign trips was to 'note objects for reproduction', while taking the opportunity to make purchases.9 Cole crossed the Channel on 8 April, with Alan and Colin Minton Campbell. They travelled via Paris, Bordeaux and Biarritz, reaching Vittoria in Spain on 12 April. Energetic art sightseeing followed in Burgos ('to Cathedral before breakfast') and Valladolid ('c[oul]d not see the Silver Custodia in Sacristy, being passion week'). In Madrid, they were looked after by the ubiquitous Layard, who was now British ambassador in Madrid. As usual they met other English people abroad, this time Lady Charlotte and Mr Schreiber, collectors of ceramics and later notable benefactors to the South Kensington Museum; and the young Charles Dilke, son of Cole's recently deceased ally at the Society of Arts, Wentworth Dilke. Cole went to a bullfight on 18 April, Easter Monday, noting in his diary: 'Disgusting Cruelty & little Skill. On the following Friday he assisted in a gentler seasonal pursuit, when he 'painted Eggs for Mrs Layard['s] party of Children'.

Among other purchases, he 'bought priest's dresses for £4o, also Cabinet of Tortoiseshell' on 19 April, and 'Spanish Arab Tiles' on 22 April. More importantly, Layard introduced him to the distinguished art-historian, curator and collector 'Mr Riaño' (Juan Facundo Riaño y Montero),100 whom he enlisted in the service of the museum. Riaño was appointed 'professional ArT ReFEREE in SPAIN' with a brief to suggest purchases, especially of 'ancient Spanish jewellery'. 'To submit REPORT monthly, and to receive 5l. 5s. therefor. To be allowed first class railway fare, and 20 francs per diem when obliged to leave Madrid.'101 Riaño served the museum well, compiling a Catalogue of the art objects of Spanish production in the South Kensington Museum (1872), and a volume on The industrial arts in Spain (1879) in the museum's 'Art Handbook' series. After their spell in Madrid, Cole's party travelled home via Saragossa, Pamplona, Biarritz, Bordeaux and Paris, crossing the Channel on 29 April. Cole was home at 5.30, had his hair cut, and 'found all quite well'.

## \*    \*

The year 187 I, as we have seen, was a year of triumph, and Cole was able to note down in his diary many tributes from the great and good. Of course, there were always some who, like the 185 1 commissioner Edward Thring, disparaged 'Cole's Great Touting Schemes'. (Although Cole recorded that 'Thring said ... that I stamped upon his Guts', Thring nonetheless respected Cole enough to propose later that 'we should be good friends again & forget all our differences'.)102 But others treated him with considerable deference (even if tinged with mockery). Lord Granville, who had often been Cole's boss, said that 'I had ruled over him', and Cole's current minister, Lord de Grey and Ripon, 'said he always obeyed my orders'. Even the king of the Belgians told Cole on one occasion, 'I shall obey as you are accustomed to be obeyed'. 103 The speaker of the House of Commons congratulated Cole, in the hearing of the Lord Chancellor and other dignitaries, on the fact that 'you have now a great Kingdom'. Cole was often known as a 'real Despot & King', even to royalty themselves: Prince Oscar of Sweden (one of the princes whom he had recruited for his scheme of international exchange of reproductions) twitted him with being 'a despot', and the Prince of Wales, too, called him 'most despotic'.104 Presumably the use of this word was a genial way of fending Cole off when he became too insistent.

When Beresford-Hope, after watching Cole's estimates get through the Commons without opposition in 1871, urged Playfair, 'You sh[oul]d ask the Queen to make Cole a Duke', this was no doubt a joke. But Cole seems to have played an almost avuncular role in relation to Queen Victoria's various sons and sons-in-law. Edward, Prince of Wales, and Alfred, duke of Edinburgh, were often involved in his schemes, as was Prince Christian, the German husband of Princess Helena, who resided with her in England. Contacts with Princess 'Vicky', Crown Princess of Prussia, were also good: for instance, after the Franco-Prussian War, Cole felt able to urge the Crown Prince of Prussia that it was 'time to attend to Industry & not to fighting'. The Queen's regard for Cole is revealed in the following anecdote. Visiting the 187 1 exhibition, she noticed little Harry, then aged four. He 'took off his hat in a timid way, & she asked who he was, & learning he was my Grandson shook his hand & told her sons &c. He is Mr Coles Grandson'.105

Cole was generally acknowledged as 'the very genius loci of South Kensington' - or, more satirically, as the 'Plenipotentiary' of the (mockingly renamed) 'Department of Aesthetics and Commonplace' 106 The most powerful tributes to him are those given through gritted teeth by writers who disapprove of him but cannot deny his achievements. Take this from the Daily News of 1 May 1871:

South Kensington and Mr. Cole have been as well and heartily abused as most other fnly vhat  hould heiat  p  cding ti thing that Mr. Cole and his associates have been doing during the last twenty years in the domain they have conquered, and upon which they have founded a little empire of their own, under the nominal protectorate of a body of Royal Commissioners whose authoriy s not very clearly define.Mr. Cole s perhaps a little too Impeial in his ways and works for English taste; but . . . he has done great things for industrial art and culture in this countryand .he has assertedin the bet manner the true genius nd character of England as a sanctuary of civilization.

M give Cole his occasionally uncivilised methods. Work such as had been done at the Department of Science and Art required 'more than ordinary perseverance and decision of character', remarked the Architect, obviously thinking of Cole though not naming him. It went on: 'Prejudice occupies so strong a position amongst what Mr.

Disraeli calls "the sublime instincts of an ancient people," that anything short of Cromwellian or Napoleonic firmness would fail in the attempt to establish so unprecedented innovation. Caesarism accordingly must be allowed for' 107 If, as is implied here, Cole is to be ranked in toughness with Cromwell, 108 Napoleon and the Roman Caesars, it is all the more remarkable that at the height of his triumph he seemed to be suffering from a stress-related illness. On 4 February 187 1 he 'called on Dr Christian, saw Dr Seton about Itching Symptoms'. His general health seems to have been good. At a check-up on 10 October 1870, he had been told that 'the Heart worked well', though he also had some 'Stomach sensations', which were said to result from 'Rheumatism on the Ribs'. But his skin trouble, now described as 'Exeme' (he always had trouble spelling the word), persisted, despite restorative weekends at Shere (delightfully peaceable . . . a chorus of Nightingales', quite blissful a lovely delicious warm day'19 and he eventually resorted to a German spa to combat it.

It looks as if he obtained some sick leave from his board. Already in 187o, he had been granted permission to visit 'places in Germany, indicated by the CRowN PRINCEsS OF PRussIA, and report on objects for reproduction' and 'to take a clerk'. Now, he was 'granted leave of absence to take the waters in Germany and afterwards to visit places', as prescribed in the previous permission. He was also granted an 'imprest of or purcase f desirable bjects1Taking Henie as his clerk, and travelli also with Marian, Rose and Granville, he set off on 8 August 187 1 via Rotterdam and Cologne to Bad Kissingen in northern Bavaria. While the rest of the family went straight to the spa, he and Granville made a diversion to Luneburg to examine, on 12 August, the collection of town silver in the Rathaus: 'All London c[oul]d not show such a Collection as this little Republic'. (In 1874 the Luneburg silver was to move to become the central treasure of the German Industrial Art Museum in Berlin.) To get from Luneburg to Kissingen, Cole travelled overnight in a First Class carriage. 'Seats make beds', he noted on 13 August. There was no food on the train, however, nor at the station, and he 'washed in Soda Water in a little tumbler'.

At Kissingen he embarked on vigorous consumption of the water, carefully noting how many glasses he took each day. By 22 August he recorded: 'Exzema decidedly less'. On 28 August he tried bathing in the waters, but next day his doctor 'advised no repetition of the Bath, which seemed to deaden the action of my heart'. He occupied his spare time in visiting the curiosity shops in Kissingen, and, by contrast, in literary work. Thomas Love Peacock, his earliest patron, to whom he owed his first opportunities as a journalist, had died in 1866. On 30 June 187 1 Cole agreed with the publisher Richard Bentley to produce a new, commemorative edition of Peacock's works, with biographical introduction, on which he worked intermittently through the next few years.

"As his stay at Kissingen neared its end, he found 'Exema breaking out afresh' on his hands on 9 September. All his doctor could do was to predict that it would 'get well soon'. The party moved off via Bamberg to Nuremberg, where they stayed from 1 2 to 18 September, visiting Dürer's house, the Germanisches Nationalmuseum, and the great churches of St Lawrence and St Sebald. Here Cole treated with the dealer Pickert, who offered a batch of objects worth £350,111 including (for £1 50) an elaborately worked sixteenth-century silver cup that had formerly belonged to the Nuremberg

Goldsmiths' Guild, and was attributed by Pickert to Wenzel Jamnitzer. The attribution was challenged (by Redgrave among others) when Cole brought it back to England,112 and it is now regarded as a 'masterpiece' made by an apprentice or journeyman to gain admission to the guild. From Nuremberg, Cole's expedition proceeded to Würzburg, Darmstadt, Mainz, Cologne and Aachen. Here Cole renewed contact (on 24 and 26 September) with the textile collector Dr Bock, who offered the museum a collection of lace, bought for £92.113 The journey went on to Essen, Dortmund, Hanover, Magdeburg and Berlin, pausing there from 2 to 7 September. Cole visited the Gewerbemuseum to see the recently acquired Minutoli collection (6 September), and, not forgetting that he was professionally concerned with science as well as art, called on 3 September at the chemical laboratory of Professor August Hofmann, whom he had known in London in the 18 5os when his department took over the Royal College of Chemistry, which Hofmann then directed. After Berlin there were stops at Erfurt, Cassel and Cologne, before the crossing from Ostend to Dover. Cole then had a fortnight at Folkestone to recover from the trip, with a three-day visit to Lord Granville at Walmer Castle.

By now he had definitely resolved to retire, and set about telling everyone. First he told his ministers on 26 October. Forster 'objected to my going', and Lord Ripon said meaningfully, 'That is a large question'. On 3 November Cole talked over his resignation with Scott, who 'at first said "No" then "Yes". When, on 8 November, Cole told MacLeod, 'he said he expected I sh[oul]d die in harness & little else', while Redgrave responded to the news by 'piteously' pleading to be allowed to retire also. Owen and Thompson were told on 27 November, to which Thompson cried, 'Impossible'. As it turned out, retirement at this juncture was impossible, because the department was under the shadow of scandal. This crisis had arisen in June, and must have troubled Cole to some extent during the holiday when he tried to suppress his eczema.

The crisis was about money. The department was allowed to maintain an unusually large balance of ready cash, because it had to pay weekly wages to many staff. Receipts from museum activities were also paid into the balance. From this turbulent pool of cash it was not difficult for a cunning accountant to drain some away for his own purposes. It turned out that the departmental accountant, A. L. Simkins, had been doing just this. Simkins's accounts were regularly checked by Mr Vine of the Audit Offce, who in June 187 1 noticed a minor discrepancy. Not regarding this as important, he made an appointment to discuss it with Simkins in three days time. Simkins, however, concluded that he had been detected, and fled  never to be seen again (so far as can be determined from the official sources available). His 'defalcations wee eventually reckoned at 7,715.7.1 which s roughly £8, today's money.

On 8 June, Vine had warned Cole that he thought the department should be held responsible 'for not investigating Simpkins balances', and the department quickly got its defence down on paper. Cole was offcially the accounting offier of the department, but he took the line that it was impossible to expect him, as head of an organisation, to double-check the work of all his subordinates in detail. He pointed to the fact that he had earlier criticised the financial systems of the department and autun 1871 that the Treasury hit back. In a letter of 1 November, Lingen, the permanent secretary, wrote on behalf of the lords of the Treasury: 'The conclusion to which my Lords come, after . .going minutely into the case is, that Mr. Cole must be removed from the position of accountant. They cannot assume the responsibility of continuing him in that position under section 22 of the Exchequer and Audit Act, 18.1 In effect, the Treasury was telling the departent: sack Cole or there will be no more money. It is difficult not to believe that there was some personal animus in this. Cole was already on bad, and worsening, terms with Robert Lowe, Chancellor of the Exchequer, and Lingen, though previously a colleague of Cole's, was now Lowe's committed right-hand-man. At any rate, it was clear that the Treasury was determined to make Cole the scapegoat for the embarrassing affair.

Cole was nervous enough to seek advice from Lord Granville (who was confined by his gout to a sofa) and Donnelly.117 His ministers rallied behind him, Forster authorising him on 14 December to prepare a full explanatory statement on behalf of the department.118 In New Year 1872, there was a public relations battle, as each side tried to ensure that its own version of events prevailed. Forster 'expressed sorrow I sh[oul]d be so tormented', wrote Cole. 'I said as my Conscience was clear I was like Mark Tapley [in Dickens's Martin Chuzzlewit], feeling very Jolly about it.' By now, the Audit Commission was taking a neutral stance between the department and the Treasury, and was prepared to publish all the papers, as the department wanted. 119 On 13 January, Mr Ryan of the Audit Office assured Cole that 'to keep within the Law every thing must be settled on 15th', so the matter went up to the Prime Minister, Gladstone, who decided that the papers should be printed and granted the department's request that the matter be referred to the Commons Public Accounts Committee. This was a partial victory for the department. 'So much gained,' said Forster, with satisfaction, on 15 January, and next day it was reported that 'Lowe washed his hands of the case'. However, the hearings before the Public Accounts Committee were yet to come, so Cole suspended his resignation, and Lord Ripon "agreed that my resignation c[oul]d not take place now or even soon after the Report of Commons on Acc[oun]ts if unfavourable to the Department'. 120 So Cole soldiered on, putting his shoulder behind the 1872 exhibition, the opening of Bethnal Green, and the foundation of a new museum at Nottingham.

The Public Accounts Committee met to consider the 'defalcations' on 24 April, and beheld (as The Times reported wonderingly) 'a sort of triangular duel between three public departments'121 as spokesmen for the Treasury, the Audit Office and South Kensington each tried to put the blame on the others. Cole found the committee 'fair & impartial', and Forster came along to make a vigorous statement on his behalf. There was further detailed examination of the affair on 8 May, at which Cole tried to defuse the rancour with a gracious sentiment: 'I gave a parting hope that the system w[oul]d be reformed & good come out of Evil.' When the committee issued its report on 17 May, it sternly regretted that the accounting system at South Kensington had been 'notoriously bad for many years', but then (as such investigations into British public life tend to do) distributed blame pretty equally to all concerned. It did concede, however, that 'the appointment of an officer other than a skilled accountant to sign and render the appropriation account under the Act, cannot carry with it any obligation upon the officer appointed to be answerable for the correctness of the bookkeeping so far as that involves knowledge of a technical character'. So Cole escaped personal liability, and no doubt many other senior civil servants who were required to approve accounts prepared by their subordinates heaved a sigh of relief.

In summer 1872 Cole made another foreign tour, in which taking the waters was combined with the search for reproducible objects and purchases. This time the chosen spa was Bad Homburg, near Frankfurt, which was described to Cole by the Prince of Wales on 27 July as 'a nice but naughty place'. With Hennie, he crossed from Dover to Calais on 14 August. Arriving in Frankfurt, he devoted 20-2 1 August to visits to galleries and dealers, and to examination of new plans for Frankfurt's sewers. At Homburg, from 2 1 August to 7 September, he conscientiously imbibed the waters, and mingled with English people, including the Playfairs, Kelk the builder, Sir Anthony de Rothschild and the duke of Cambridge.

Serious art tourism began on 7 September, when Cole and Hennie proceeded to Marburg, Hildesheim, Braunschweig ('old vestments but none so fine as what we have'), Halberstadt, Leipzig and Nuremberg. Here Cole renewed contact with the dealer Pickert, buying ironwork, playing cards and pipes.122 In Munich from 18 to 23 September, Cole visited the Bavarian National Museum and encountered Augustus Wollaston Franks of the British Museum, and the connoisseurs Sir Coutts and Lady Lindsay. On 17 September, Cole noted: 'Hennie rather feeble'. Perhaps the remorseless pace of the art tourism was getting her down. But Cole was insatiable. On the 2 Ist, he recorded with annoyance in Munich: 'sight seeing here . . . impeded by the closing between 1 2 & 2 and places are only open on few days of the week'. On 30 September, trying to visit the Louvre in Paris, he was vexed to find it 'closed on Monday!.

In Munich, on 19 September, he went to hear Wagner's opera Lohengrin. 'Screeching discords. Much use of Trumpets. Very fine & affecting climaxes. Story not intelligible. Left after 3 hours.' The previous year, in Kissingen, he had sampled Tannhäuser, which he found 'very complex & confused', though perhaps he can be excused since at the time he was suffering from 'symptoms of Diarrheoah'. 123 The journey continued through Augsburg, Ulm, Stuttgart, Heidelberg, Strasbourg and Nancy, and ended with a spell in Paris, from 28 September to 2 October. Here, on 1 October, Cole revisited the Cluny Museum, once considered the inspiration and model for South Kensington. Cole clearly thought he had now outstripped it in museological professionalism. 'The Museum looking very well & tidy, as a picturesque arrangement, but diffcult to study. No labelling, references inaccurate, catalogue incomplete.'124 Cole and Hennie sailed from Boulogne on 3 October on a ferry that had been 'recently covered over' so that 'none [were] wetted by the sea who did not lk it In spi hs pro, Cole cugt a col and ws 'eean in eall Saturday 5 October.

Now that the Simkins scandal was out of the way, Cole again tried to retire. He submitted his resignation on 8 November 1872, but Lord Ripon again made him hang on. While Ripon assured him (on 29 November) that 'I might have perfect confidence that Lowe did not intend to behave ill about my pension', it was not until May 1 873 that the mists cleared. Cole sent in his final resignation on 2 1 May, and the board accepted it on the 26th, 'strongly recommending' to the Treasury Cole's claim to a special pension, and 'remarking on great value of services rendered'. 125 Cole slackened off work in summer 1873. Indeed, when he wanted some old files on 12 June, MacLeod demurred, and Cole had to insist that 'I was not yet defunct & that my Key was not officially delivered to a successor & that I w[oul]d be responsible for what I ordered'On 14 July he began to clear his office, on the 24th he started house hutgr ewouldhav relquis is ffl reence, nhedepartm the museum at the end of the month.

However, it was not a happy departure, since yet another crisis loomed. Though Lowe did prove liberal over Cole's pension,126 he found means to strike a further unexpected blow against the museum. On 7 July Lord Ripon cautiously revealed to Cole what was about to happen: 'told me what Cabinet contemplated ab[ou]t the Museum & asked for my objections'. Lowe, who had in former times applauded Cole's attempts to prise the administration of the British Museum away from its trustees into government control (see page 245), had persuaded the cabinet to agree that the South Kensington Museum should be handed over from government control to those same, infamous trustees. On 22 July Cole discovered his deputy, MacLeod, in conference, in his room at South Kensington, with the director of the British Museum, Winter Jones. The atmosphere must have been electric. Winter Jones, understandably, 'seemed hazy' and 'flinched at saying that Lowe had sent him'. MacLeod hastened to explain that Lord Ripon had authorised the conference. Cole said, doubtless with all the forcefulness for which he was renowned, that 'Lowe w[oul]d never succeed in substituting Trustees for Parliamentary Responsibility'.

Playfair subsequently discovered 'that the [British Museum] Trustees consented very unwillingly to take charge of S.K.: and that in fact the only member of the Ministry who was keen for it was Lowe'.127 Furthermore, it was not long before Lowe himself suffered another reverse. Owing to a scandal in which Lowe was only peripherally involved, Gladstone had a small reshuffle in August 1873, and transferred him to the Home Office. However, his policy, having been adopted, remained a threat. Cole had arranged to go on holiday immediately on his retirement, so he had to let go of the matter for a time. On 3 1 July he and Alan set off to Bad Neuenahr, near Bonn. Cole left only brief notes in his diary on this holiday. He took the waters, of course, noting that the pump room was 'hot but not so steamy and depressing as at Kissingen'. At first, he usually worked on his Peacock edition in the mornings. Later, tiredness caught up with him and he tended to do 'nothing all day, but daudle about'. Reading Middlemarch ('rather spun out') occupied a lot of time. He did, however, encounter Playfair, and they drew up a scheme to save the museum. Playfair, now an MP, consented to take the struggle to Parliament. 128 Cole was back in London on 2 September, having for once had a real holiday, without any artistic sightseeing. On 3 September he was in the museum, to the consternation of his former staff: 'General surprize at my presence & calling thro the Pipe', he noted, the last clause obviously referring to an in-house audio-communication system in those pretelephone years. On 5 September, he lunched with Macleod and 'told him I sh[oul]d fight for S.K.M. till the death & that I had no further link of allegiance except to the Museum itself'.

A committee was set up to advise on the implementation of Lowe's proposal. On one side, this included MacLeod and Donnelly from the Department of Science and

Art, together with Sir Francis Sandford, the senior civil servant at the Education Department, the 'twin' of the Science and Art Department, under the same ministers. From the British Museum came the principal librarian (i.e. director), John Winter Jones; Charles Newton, keeper of Greek and Roman antiquities; and Augustus Wollaston Franks, who had built up the British Museum's holdings of medieval and Renaissance works of art and was marked out as the most suitable man to take charge of South Kensington if and when it came under the aegis of the British Museum. Donnelly surreptitiously kept Cole informed about what the committee was up to.

Meanwhile, Cole started to agitate. As usual he turned to the Society of Arts. It must have seemed to him that he could achieve most through this agency if he reanimated efforts it had already made to improve the administration and development of museums in general. The South Kensington issue might then be overtaken and sidelined by the impetus of other issues. Cole had been prominent in an agitation on 'Free Libraries and Museums' that the society had conducted in 1869. This assessed the results (disappointing) of Ewart's Museums Acts of 1845 and 18 50, and urged the foundation of more museums. 129 More recently, during Cole's holiday, the Society had been pursuing another campaign that served his purpose. This related to the Bethnal Green Museum. Resentment at the Treasury's attitude to this new museum had not been confined to Cole. At a celebration dinner in October 1872, at which Cole was present, angry voices from Bethnal Green had railed against 'the greedy and selfish policy of the men who now disgrace the Government of a great country', 'the people-hating Administration now in power', 'the parsimonious measures, the trickery, and the bad faith of a "Liberal" Government'.130 To capitalise on this feeling, the society had rather cheekily asked Gladstone (in a letter of 3 July 1873) to accept a deputation that would argue, on the basis of the Bethnal Green Museum's undoubtedly impressive attendance figures, to 'submit to Parliament the policy so essentially national of voting increased means to facilitate the establishment of museums, libraries, and galleries of Science and Art in large centres of population'. Gladstone tried to divert the deputation elsewhere. But the society, deploying a well-calculated high moral tone, asserted that their cause 'was of vast importance to the education, general cultivation, and social welfare of the people', and therefore was 'strictly within the consideration of the Prime Minister, rather than of a department of the Government'.

Gladstone reluctantly undertook to receive a letter. The society wrote to him in a yet higher tone, urging that all publicly subsidised museums and galleries should be put in the charge of a responsible Minister of State' Since their suggestions were matters 'not only of general policy, but involve some new principles of administration, large financial considerations, the reform of old institutions, &c.', they request-r  u se isr he  hy remarked that

they could not hide from themselves the knowledge they possessed of the several departmental diffculties which attended the pening f the Bethnal Green Musum, atha hyha beemian, tr r  nh estimates for 18712, of the opposition which the Treasury, as lately administered, had in 1866, for conducting the Bethnal Green Museum.

Glastone was able to escape with a briacknowlegement f the letter, 'thectents of which he will not fail to make known to his colleagues' But he had been effectively pushed into a corner.

This correspondence provided a useful weapon with which to counter the government's policy for South Kensington, and it was reprinted (doubtless through Cole's intercession) in the Society of Arts journal of 3 1 October 1873. It was also reprinted in the scientific journal Nature on 30 October. 131 Perhaps we can detect Cole again here, using his department's scientific connections. The scientific community harboured some resentment against the British Museum trustees for what they saw as the trustees' neglect of the natural history collections, and were thus disposed to take South Kensington's side. On 6 November, Nature, in a magisterial editorial, urged scientific learned societies to lobby the prime minister to create a museums minister. And in relation to South Kensington it pointed out that to transfer a museum already under ministerial control 'to irresponsible trustees is a step worthy of the Middle Ages'. It concluded: 'While the South Kensington system represents everything that is best in the way of progress, so much, to say the least, cannot be urged in favour of the present management of the British Museum.'132

It was not only the Bethnal Green battle that was taken up by the Society of Arts. It reprinted in its journal excerpts from a parliamentary speech of 1862, drafted by Cole though delivered by Lord Henry Lennox, on 'Irresponsible Boards'.133 The journal also began to print a regular list of visitor figures at nationally funded museums. Since the British Museum refused to disclose its attendances, this showed it up in a bad light.14 Furthermore, the society girded itself for a new struggle by setti up on 1 October 1873 a standing committee on national museums and galleries, to consider their contribution to public education. This issued a brief manifesto, in which the main point was the same as had been advanced in the Bethnal Green agitation: the necessity to press on the government the desirability of Parliament 'voting increased means to facilitate the establishment of museums, libraries, and galleries of Science and Art in large centres of population', and the concomitant necessity of placing national museums under the direct control of the government rather than under trustees.135 Signatories to the manifesto included such South Kensington worthies as Cole, Huxley, Playfair and Tyndall, and the list had grown to over 2oo by the end of the year.136 Playfair was to be figurehead of this committee, but had to withdraw when he accepted a job in government as Postmaster General.137 This was a blow to the campaign, but the committee carried on.

The committee quite cleverly chose to make its first mark by investigating (on 10 January 1974) the Patent Museum. Cole had lost no sleep over this for a decade or more, but suddenly it seemed timely to emphasise 'the miserable treatment this museum was receiving', as Cole himself put it when a deputation from the society visited the Lord Chancellor on 17 January 1874. It was headed by A. J. Mundella, an experienced radical working-class politician, and a fairly new member of Parliament (from 1868), who was especially concerned with popular education and was to become a Liberal minister of education. When Mundella asked for more funding, the Lord Chancellor hastily said that he 'had no control' over this and that it was a matter for the Chancellor of the Exchequer. 'That was no doubt so,' countered Mundella smoothly, 'but it was understood that the Patent Museum was under the control of the Commissioners of Patents, of whom his lordship was the head'. Thus another member of the government was successfully embarrassed.138

After this the committee asked Mundella to put down a motion in the Commons, and decided to organise a conference of provincial Mayors. Mundella, hardly surprisingly, was briefed by Cole (on 1 5 March), but when the motion came up on 24 March discussion was postponed. 139 The conference was held on 20 May 1874, and featured Joseph Chamberlain of Birmingham as main speaker. The purpose of all this agitation was to stimulate a current of public opinion in favour of greater state involvement in running museums, in the hope that the proposal to give South Kensington to the British Museum would be seen to be inconsistent with this. The public would then conclude, as the Daily News put it, that "a Department which has hitherto distinguished itself by its quick apprehension of the public interest' could not be 'placed under another which in comparison with it may be described as stagnant, without the accompaniment of strong and numerous protests

As well as setting in train this large outflanking movement, Cole also addressed the problem head on. A speaking engagement at the prizegiving at the Hanley School of Art on 20 October 1873 gave him an opportunity for a rip-roaring oration. He started with pious invocation, pointing out to the students that they were part of 'a comprehensive and national system of science and art applied to productive industry, the establishment of which the nation owes to the wise forethought of the late Prince Consort'. He ended on the same note: 'I offer to you, and all interested in Science and Art instruction, my humble services, if I can be any use during the rest of my life, to preserve from the hands of the ignorant spoiler your privileges, and the institution which the Prince Consort founded.' In between, he gave a history of the growth of the South Kensington project, deploying copious statistics (in 1852 'there was no [art] teaching in schools for the poor, now there are 194,500 children taught drawing', etc.), and especially defending the museum as a source of inspiration for design: 'It is simple savage ignorance, and priggish pedantry, not to recognise the absolute necessity of examples of art, easily consultable' in museums. He derided the trustees of the British Museum: they were always good for a laugh, being such an odd group, including the Archbishop of Canterbury, the 1st Lord of the Admiralty, the Colonial Secretary ("with the Ashantee war on his hands') and the Attorney-General ('hardly recovered from his Tichborne labours'). He painted a terrifying picture of what would happen to a Hanley student trying to use the British Museum ("does not understand the labelling, as he has not been taught Greek and Latin'), comparing it with the relaxed welcome he would receive at South Kensington. 'Art and science students throughout the country! I appeal to you. Are you going to permit this work of the Prince Consort's - matured and organised with great attention and the labour of years to be destroyed, and this insult to his memory to be perpetrated?'

Cole had the speech reprinted in the Society of Arts journal on 31 October 18i  wiely oie Not i ceva ly cannot be right',commented one journal, for the retiring Director still a public servant  to appeal to the people in the name of the dead Prince against the Queen's responsible ministers in a question affecting a change of management in a public iiution!But Co ot  s.

Soon after, he made an even more reckless speech at the opening in London of the re-constituted City and Spitalfields School of Art on 20 November 1873. He shared the platform with George Goschen, 1st Lord of the Admiralty. Despite the presence of a government minister, Cole launched a furious attack on Robert Lowe. It will be remembered that by now Lowe had been moved from the Treasury to the Home Of Cee a man when he was down. Perhaps he was rattled at losing Playfair from the Society of Arts commite.That vry day, Forster had said to him goomily, 'You have lost yr leader in Playfair,' and had predicted that since the government had 'committed itself with the Trustees it would find it 'ifficult to retreat At any rate, Cole lashed ut.

After delivering a hymn of praise to South Kensington, his anger against Lowe seems to have run away with him. The various accounts of his speech in the press143 suggest that he was spluttering almost incoherently as (disclaiming any political animus  'I am speaking only of science and art.
